title: Robot Planning and Situation Handling with Active Perception
summary: Current robots are capable of computing plans to accomplish complex tasks. However, real-world environments are inherently open and dynamic, and unforeseen situations frequently arise during plan execution, such as jamming doors and fallen objects on the floor. These situations may result from the robot's own action failures or from external disturbances, such as human activities. Detecting and handling such execution - time situations remains a significant challenge, limiting those robots' ability to achieve long-term autonomy. In this paper, we develop a planning and situation-handling framework, called VAP-TAMP, that enables robots to actively perceive and address unforeseen situations during plan execution. VAP-TAMP leverages action knowledge to strategically prompt vision-language models for active view selection and situation assessment, while constructing and reasoning over scene graphs for integrated task and motion planning. We evaluated VAP-TAMP using service tasks in simulation and on a mobile manipulation platform.

AI.tldr: Introduces VAP-TAMP for active perception and situation handling in TAMP using action knowledge to prompt vision-language models and scene graphs; validated in simulation and on a mobile robot.
AI.motivation: Real-world environments are open and dynamic; unforeseen execution-time disturbances (e.g., jams, fallen objects) challenge long-term autonomy, requiring detection and remediation during plan execution.
AI.method: Proposes VAP-TAMP: uses action knowledge to strategically prompt vision-language models for active view selection and situation assessment; constructs and reasons over scene graphs to support integrated Task and Motion Planning (TAMP); enables active perception during plan execution.
AI.result: Evaluated on service tasks in simulation and on a mobile manipulation platform; demonstrates capability to detect and address unforeseen situations during execution.
AI.conclusion: VAP-TAMP couples perception and reasoning with planning to improve robustness and autonomy in open, dynamic environments.

title: Interaction Forces and Internal Loads in Parallel Manipulators with Actuation Redundancy
comment: 13 pages, 11 figures. Submitted to Mechanism and Machine Theory
summary: This paper discusses null-space wrench components in parallel manipulators. We examine the adaptation of the two most common characterizations of these components in grasp-like systems, namely, interaction forces and internal loads, to parallel manipulators with actuation redundancy. We identify critical oversights in the existing literature on the subject, resolve ambiguities related to the definitions of interaction forces and internal loads, and provide explicit methods for synthesizing equilibrating and manipulating joint torque vectors. A case study is also provided to justify the validity of our novel methods and correct erroneous results reported in the literature.

AI.tldr: Clarifies null-space wrench components for redundant parallel manipulators by adapting grasp-like interaction forces and internal loads, identifies gaps, and provides explicit torque synthesis methods; validated via a corrective case study.
AI.motivation: To address definitional ambiguities and methodological gaps when transferring grasp-oriented concepts to parallel manipulators with actuation redundancy, ensuring correct synthesis of joint torques.
AI.method: Literature review of interaction forces and internal loads; formal adaptation to redundant parallel manipulators; development of explicit synthesis procedures for equilibrating and manipulating joint torque vectors; critical examination of prior results; case study to validate methods and correct errors.
AI.result: Definitions clarified; explicit synthesis methods provided; erroneous prior results corrected; methods validated by case study.
AI.conclusion: Provides a corrected, precise framework for null-space wrench components in redundant parallel manipulators and highlights careful transfer of grasp-based concepts.

title: PALCAS: A Priority-Aware Intelligent Lane Change Advisory System for Autonomous Vehicles using Federated Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present a priority-aware intelligent lane change advisory system based on multi-agent federated reinforcement learning, namely PALCAS, for autonomous vehicles (AVs). While existing lane-change approaches typically focus on single-agent systems or centralized multi-agent systems, we introduce a federated reinforcement learning-based multi-agent lane change system prioritizing lane changing based on vehicle destination urgency. PALCAS incorporates a novel priority-aware safe lane-change reward function to enable judicious lane-change decisions in both mandatory and discretionary scenarios. PALCAS leverages the parameterized deep Q-network (PDQN) algorithm to facilitate effective cooperation among agents, enabling both lateral and longitudinal motion controls of AVs. Extensive simulations conducted using the SUMO traffic simulator and Mosaic V2X communication framework demonstrate that PALCAS significantly improves traffic efficiency, driving safety, comfort, destination arrival rates, and merging success rates compared to baseline methods.

AI.tldr: PALCAS is a federated reinforcement-learning-based multi-agent lane-change system for autonomous vehicles, using a priority-aware PDQN framework. It improves efficiency, safety, comfort, and merging success in SUMO-based simulations with Mosaic V2X.
AI.motivation: Current lane-change methods rely on single-agent or centralized multi-agent setups, which limit cooperative decision-making and safety. A priority-aware, federated approach aims to optimize lane-changing decisions based on destination urgency in both mandatory and discretionary scenarios.
AI.method: A federated reinforcement learning framework employing parameterized deep Q-networks (PDQN) for multi-agent coordination among AVs. A priority-aware safe lane-change reward function guides decisions, enabling both lateral and longitudinal control. Evaluation uses SUMO simulations and Mosaic V2X communication.
AI.result: PALCAS significantly improves traffic efficiency, driving safety, comfort, destination arrival rates, and merging success rates compared with baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective cooperative decision-making in lane-change tasks through federated PDQN with a priority-based reward, showing potential for scalable AV coordination and V2X-enabled deployment; further real-world validation recommended.

title: Automated Detection of Mutual Gaze and Joint Attention in Dual-Camera Settings via Dual-Stream Transformers
summary: Analyzing mutual gaze (MG) and joint attention (JA) is critical in developmental psychology but traditionally relies on labor-intensive manual coding. Automating this process in multi-camera laboratory settings is computationally challenging due to complex cross-camera relational dynamics. In this paper, we propose a highly efficient dual-stream Transformer architecture for detecting MG and JA from synchronized dual-camera recordings. Our approach leverages frozen gaze-aware backbones (GazeLLE) to extract rich visual priors, combined with a custom token fusion mechanism to map the spatial and semantic relationships between interacting dyads. Evaluated on an ecologically valid dataset of caregiver-infant interactions, our model exhibits good performance, significantly outperforming both a convolutional baseline and a state-of-the-art multimodal Large Language Model (LLM). By open-sourcing our model and pre-trained weights, we provide behavioral scientists with a scalable tool that can be fine-tuned to diverse laboratory environments, effectively bridging the gap between computational modeling and applied interaction research.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream Transformer detects mutual gaze and joint attention from synchronized dual-camera data using frozen gaze-aware backbones (GazeLLE) and a token fusion mechanism; it excels on ecologically valid caregiver-infant data and outperforms a CNN baseline and a multimodal LLM, with open-sourced models for broad reuse.
AI.motivation: Automating mutual gaze (MG) and joint attention (JA) coding is labor-intensive. Multi-camera relational dynamics are complex, hindering scalable analysis in developmental psychology. A fast, accurate model tailored to dual-camera setups would accelerate data annotation and enable broader reuse.
AI.method: A highly efficient dual-stream Transformer architecture processes synchronized dual-camera inputs. It leverages frozen gaze-aware backbones (GazeLLE) to provide rich priors, and a custom token fusion module to align spatial and semantic relationships between interacting dyads. The system is evaluated on ecologically valid caregiver-infant interaction data and compared against a CNN baseline and a state-of-the-art multimodal Large Language Model (LLM).
AI.result: The proposed model achieves strong performance on an ecologically valid caregiver-infant dataset, significantly outperforming both the convolutional baseline and a state-of-the-art multimodal LLM. By open-sourcing the model and pre-trained weights, the authors provide a scalable tool adaptable to diverse laboratory environments.
AI.conclusion: The work advances computational modeling for developmental interaction research by delivering a scalable, efficient method for MG and JA detection in dual-camera settings, enabling broader application and easier replication across labs.

title: Monitoring Neural Training with Topology: A Footprint-Predictable Collapse Index
summary: Representational collapse, where embeddings become anisotropic and lose multi-scale structure, can erode downstream performance long before performance metrics react. We propose an online, topology-aware monitor for evolving neural representations that couples Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) with a composite Collapse Index (CI). Instead of rebuilding complexes each epoch, we apply sparse edits at a fixed scale and maintain a discrete Morse matching, yielding fast, incremental updates. Across LLM fine-tuning and temporal KGE training, CI provides a low-latency early-warning signal suitable for in-training interventions. Code and experimental scripts will be released publicly

AI.tldr: Online topology-aware monitor that couples Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) with a composite Collapse Index (CI) to detect representational collapse via fast incremental updates.
AI.motivation: Representational collapse makes embeddings anisotropic and erodes multi-scale structure, often preceding observable performance drops; a low-latency, in-training monitor can enable timely interventions.
AI.method: An online monitor using sparse edits at a fixed scale and maintaining a discrete Morse matching to update the Collapse Index without reconstructing complexes each epoch; MMHM integrated with CI for rapid tracking of representation health.
AI.result: CI provides a low-latency early-warning signal during LLM fine-tuning and temporal knowledge graph embedding (KGE) training, enabling potential in-training interventions; implementation is designed for fast, incremental updates.
AI.conclusion: Public release of code and experimental scripts; the approach offers practical topology-aware monitoring for evolving neural representations.

title: Compositional Meta-Learning for Mitigating Task Heterogeneity in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) approximate solutions of partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into the loss function. In parameterized PDE families, variations in coefficients or boundary/initial conditions define distinct tasks. This makes training individual PINNs for each task computationally prohibitive, while cross-task transfer can be sensitive to task heterogeneity. While meta-learning can reduce retraining cost, existing methods often rely on a single global initialization and may suffer from negative transfer, particularly under feature-scarce coordinate inputs and limited training-task availability. We propose the Learning-Affinity Adaptive Modular Physics-Informed Neural Network (LAM-PINN), a compositional framework that leverages task-specific learning dynamics. LAM-PINN combines PDE parameters with learning-affinity metrics from brief transfer sessions to construct a task representation and cluster tasks even with coordinate-only inputs. It decomposes the model into cluster-specialized subnetworks and a shared meta network, and learns routing weights to selectively reuse modules instead of relying on a single global initialization. Across three PDE benchmarks, LAM-PINN achieves an average 19.7-fold reduction in mean squared error (MSE) on unseen tasks using only 10% of the training iterations required by conventional PINNs. These results indicate its effectiveness for generalization to unseen configurations within bounded design spaces of parameterized PDE families in resource-constrained engineering settings.

AI.tldr: LAM-PINN is a modular, adaptive meta-PINN that uses task-specific learning affinities to cluster parameterized-PDE tasks, routing through cluster-specialized subnetworks plus a shared meta network, dramatically boosting generalization to unseen tasks with limited training and inputs.
AI.motivation: Parameter variations in PDE families create many tasks; per-task PINN training is costly and cross-task transfer can suffer from heterogeneity. Global meta-initialization can cause negative transfer, especially with scarce coordinate inputs and few tasks.
AI.method: Create task representations by combining PDE parameters with learning-affinity metrics from brief transfer sessions; cluster tasks even with coordinate-only inputs; decompose into cluster-specific subnetworks plus a shared meta network; learn routing weights to selectively reuse modules instead of relying on a single global initialization.
AI.result: On three PDE benchmarks, achieves an average 19.7x reduction in MSE on unseen tasks using only 10% of the training iterations required by conventional PINNs.
AI.conclusion: LAM-PINN demonstrates effective generalization to unseen configurations within bounded parameterized PDE design spaces under resource-constrained engineering settings, by leveraging modularity and task-aware routing.

title: Global Sampling-Based Trajectory Optimization for Contact-Rich Manipulation via KernelSOS
summary: Contact-rich manipulation is challenging due to its high dimensionality, the requirement for long time horizons, and the presence of hybrid contact dynamics. Sampling-based methods have become a popular approach for this class of problems, but without explicit mechanisms for global exploration, they are susceptible to converging to poor local minima. In this paper, we introduce Global-MPPI, a unified trajectory optimization framework that integrates global exploration and local refinement. At the global level, we leverage kernel sum-of-squares optimization to identify globally promising regions of the solution space. To enable reliable performance for the non-smooth landscapes inherent to contact-rich manipulation, we introduce a graduated non-convexity strategy based on log-sum-exp smoothing, which transitions the optimization landscape from a smoothed surrogate to the original non-smooth objective. Finally, we employ the model-predictive path integral method to locally refine the solution. We evaluate Global-MPPI on high-dimensional, long-horizon contact-rich tasks, including the PushT task and dexterous in-hand manipulation. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach robustly uncovers high-quality solutions, achieving faster convergence and lower final costs compared to existing baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Global-MPPI combines global exploration with local refinement for contact-rich manipulation by (1) using kernel sum-of-squares to find promising regions, (2) applying a graduated non-convexity via log-sum-exp smoothing to handle non-smooth landscapes, and (3) using MPPI for local refinement, achieving faster convergence and lower costs on high-dimensional, long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Sampling-based methods in contact-rich manipulation suffer from getting stuck in poor local minima due to high dimensionality, long horizons, and hybrid contact dynamics. A mechanism for global exploration is needed to reliably discover high-quality solutions.
AI.method: Global-level global optimization via kernel sum-of-squares to identify promising regions; a graduated non-convexity strategy using log-sum-exp smoothing to transition from a smoothed surrogate to the non-smooth objective; local refinement using the model-predictive path integral (MPPI) method.
AI.result: Evaluations on high-dimensional, long-horizon tasks (PushT task and dexterous in-hand manipulation) show that Global-MPPI robustly uncovers high-quality solutions, with faster convergence and lower final costs compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework successfully couples global exploration and local refinement for contact-rich manipulation, delivering improved performance and suggesting potential applicability to a broader class of high-dimensional, non-smooth robotics problems.

title: Reconstruction by Generation: 3D Multi-Object Scene Reconstruction from Sparse Observations
summary: Accurately reconstructing complex full multi-object scenes from sparse observations remains a core challenge in computer vision and a key step toward scalable and reliable simulation for robotics. In this work, we introduce RecGen, a generative framework for probabilistic joint estimation of object and part shapes, as well as their pose under occlusion and partial visibility from one or multiple RGB-D images. By leveraging compositional synthetic scene generation and strong 3D shape priors, RecGen generalizes across diverse object types and real-world environments. RecGen achieves state-of-the-art performance on complex, heavily occluded datasets, robustly handling severe occlusions, symmetric objects, object parts, and intricate geometry and texture. Despite using nearly 80% fewer training meshes than the previous state of the art SAM3D, RecGen outperforms it by 30.1% in geometric shape quality, 9.1% in texture reconstruction, and 33.9% in pose estimation.

AI.tldr: RecGen provides probabilistic joint estimation of object/part geometry and pose from RGB-D observations under occlusion, using compositional synthetic scenes and strong 3D shape priors; achieves state-of-the-art performance while using ~80% fewer training meshes than prior work.
AI.motivation: Accurate reconstruction of complex multi-object scenes from sparse observations is challenging, especially under occlusion and with real-world variability; robust, data-efficient methods are needed for scalable robotics simulation and perception.
AI.method: A generative framework (RecGen) for probabilistic joint estimation of object shapes, part shapes, and poses. It leverages compositional synthetic scene generation and strong 3D shape priors to generalize across diverse objects and environments. It supports single or multiple RGB-D inputs and handles heavy occlusion, symmetry, and detailed geometry/texture.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on heavily occluded datasets. Demonstrates robustness to severe occlusions, symmetric objects, object parts, and intricate geometry/texture. Using nearly 80% fewer training meshes than SAM3D, RecGen outperforms SAM3D by 30.1% in geometric shape quality, 9.1% in texture reconstruction, and 33.9% in pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: RecGen generalizes across diverse object types and real-world environments by combining probabilistic joint estimation with strong priors and compositional scene generation, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction under occlusion with reduced data requirements.

title: Simple Self-Conditioning Adaptation for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) generate discrete sequences by iterative denoising under an absorbing masking process. In standard masked diffusion, if a token remains masked after a reverse update, the model discards its clean-state prediction for that position. Thus, still-masked positions must be repeatedly inferred from the mask token alone. This design choice limits cross-step refinement. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a simple, yet effective, post-training adaptation for MDMs that conditions each denoising step on the model's own previous clean-state predictions. The resulting method, called Self-Conditioned Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM), requires minimal architectural change, does not introduce a recurrent latent-state pathway, does not rely on an auxiliary reference model, and adds no extra denoiser evaluations during sampling. This is an important departure from partial self-conditioning approaches which requires expensive model training from scratch. In particular, the paper shows that partial self-conditioning, including the commonly used 50% dropout strategy for training self-conditioned models from scratch, is suboptimal in the post-training regime. Instead, once the model's self-generated clean-state estimates become informative, the specialization to refinement is preferable to mixing conditional and unconditional objectives. SCMDM is evaluated across multiple domains, demonstrating consistent improvement over vanilla MDM baselines, achieving nearly a 50% reduction in generative perplexity on OWT-trained models (42.89 to 23.72), alongside strong improvements in discretized image synthesis quality, small molecular generation, and enhanced fidelity in genomic distribution modeling.

AI.tldr: Post-training self-conditioning for Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM) improves sequence generation by conditioning each denoising step on the model's own previous clean-state predictions, yielding substantial perplexity and quality gains without architectural changes or extra denoiser evaluations.
AI.motivation: Standard masked diffusion discards clean-state predictions for still-masked positions, limiting cross-step refinement. A simple post-training adaptation aims to enable iterative refinement by conditioning on the model's own earlier outputs.
AI.method: Apply self-conditioning post-training: at each denoising step, feed the model the previously generated clean-state prediction for the target position; no new architecture, no recurrent latent-state pathway, no auxiliary model, and no additional sampling passes.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over vanilla MDM baselines across domains; notable ~50% reduction in generative perplexity on OWT-trained models (42.89 -> 23.72); improved discretized image synthesis, small-molecule generation, and genomic distribution fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Self-generated clean-state estimates become informative and refinement-focused; partial self-conditioning (e.g., 50% dropout) is suboptimal in post-training; specializing to refinement via self-conditioning yields robust gains without extra training or evaluation overhead.

title: Real-Time GPU-Accelerated Monte Carlo Evaluation of Safety-Critical AEB Systems Under Uncertainty
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE journal for possible publication; under review
summary: Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) systems represent a safety-critical national interest, with the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (FMVSS No. 127) requiring AEB in all new light vehicles sold in the United States by September 2029. However, production implementations frequently rely on deterministic stopping-distance or Time-to-Collision (TTC) thresholds that fail to capture uncertainty in sensing, road conditions, and vehicle dynamics. This paper presents a GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo framework for stochastic evaluation of emergency braking performance using a high-fidelity longitudinal vehicle model incorporating aerodynamic drag, road grade, brake actuator dynamics, and weight transfer effects. A one-thread-per-sample execution strategy exploits the independence of Monte Carlo rollouts, while deterministic CPU-generated sampling ensures bit-exact numerical consistency between CPU and GPU implementations. The framework is evaluated across four hardware platforms spanning development and deployment environments: two laptop GPUs (GTX 1650, RTX 5070) and two automotive-grade embedded platforms (Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson AGX Orin). Peak speedups of 54.57x are achieved while maintaining exact numerical agreement. Real-time feasibility analysis with a complete AEB timing budget (700 ms human reaction time minus 120 ms perception and 50 ms decision overhead) demonstrates that the Jetson AGX Orin can execute approximately 25,000 Monte Carlo samples within a 530 ms budget, enabling real-time probabilistic AEB evaluation as part of a complete embedded pipeline. These results establish Monte Carlo-based uncertainty evaluation as a deployable runtime component rather than an offline validation tool and provide quantitative guidance for risk-aware AEB threshold selection under the NHTSA final rule.

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo framework for real-time stochastic evaluation of Auto Emergency Braking (AEB) performance using a high-fidelity longitudinal model. Demonstrates bit-exact CPU/GPU results and up to 54.57x speedups across platforms, enabling ~25k samples within a 530 ms budget on Jetson AGX Orin for real-time uncertainty quantification and risk-aware thresholding under FMVSS No. 127.
AI.motivation: Deterministic stopping-distance or TTC thresholds fail to capture uncertainty from sensing, road conditions, and vehicle dynamics. There is a need for real-time probabilistic evaluation to support risk-informed AEB threshold design and regulatory compliance (NHTSA FMVSS No. 127).
AI.method: GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo with one-thread-per-sample architecture and CPU-generated sampling to ensure bit-exact numerical consistency between CPU and GPU. Uses a high-fidelity longitudinal vehicle model including aerodynamic drag, road grade, brake actuator dynamics, and weight transfer. Evaluated on four platforms (GTX 1650, RTX 5070, Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson AGX Orin).
AI.result: Achieves peak speedups of 54.57x with exact numerical agreement between CPU and GPU. Real-time feasibility: Jetson AGX Orin executes ~25,000 Monte Carlo samples within a 530 ms budget (700 ms human reaction time minus 120 ms perception and 50 ms decision). Demonstrates deployability of Monte Carlo-based uncertainty evaluation as a runtime component in AEB systems and provides quantitative guidance for risk-aware thresholding under NHTSA rule.
AI.conclusion: Monte Carlo-based uncertainty evaluation is practically deployable in embedded AEB pipelines, enabling probabilistic performance assessment and informed threshold selection under regulatory rules. The approach supports real-time decision-making and can inform design choices for AEB thresholds.

title: InterPartAbility: Text-Guided Part Matching for Interpretable Person Re-Identification
summary: Text-to-image person re-identification (TI-ReID) relies on natural-language text description to retrieve top matching individuals from a large gallery of images. While recent large vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong retrieval performance, their decisions remain largely uninterpretable. Existing interpretability approaches in TI-ReID rely solely on slot-attention to highlight attended regions, but fail to reliably bind visual regions to semantically meaningful concepts, limiting explanations to qualitative visualizations over a restricted vocabulary. This paper introduces InterPartAbility, an interpretable TI-ReID method that performs explicit part-wise matching and enables phrase-region grounding. A new open-vocabulary, lightweight supervision, patch-phrase interaction module (PPIM) is proposed to train a standard TI-ReID model with concept-level guidance. Concept-based part phrases provide evidence that encourages the model to attend to corresponding image regions. InterPartAbility further constrains CLIP ViT self-attention to produce spatially concentrated patch activations aligned with each part-level phrase, yielding grounded explanation maps. A quantitative interpretability protocol for TI-ReID is introduced by adapting perturbation-based evaluation metrics, including counterfactual region masking that measures retrieval degradation when top-ranked explanatory regions are removed. Empirical results\footnote{Our code is included in the supplementary materials and will be made public.} on challenging benchmarks like CUHK-PEDES and ICFG-PEDES show that InterPartAbility achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) interpretability performance under these metrics, while sustaining competitive retrieval accuracy.

AI.tldr: InterPartAbility introduces explicit part-wise matching and phrase-region grounding for text-to-image person re-identification (TI-ReID), with a lightweight patch-phrase interaction module (PPIM) that provides concept-level guidance and constrains CLIP self-attention to produce spatially localized, interpretable activation maps. It also proposes a perturbation-based interpretability evaluation protocol for TI-ReID, achieving state-of-the-art interpretability on CUHK-PEDES and ICFG-PEDES while maintaining competitive retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between high-performing but opaque TI-ReID models and the need for interpretable, semantically grounded explanations. Existing interpretability methods rely on soft region highlighting without reliable concept-to-region binding, limiting explanations to qualitative visualizations.
AI.method: Train TI-ReID models with a lightweight open-vocabulary supervision via a patch-phrase interaction module (PPIM) that leverages concept-level guidance to align part phrases with corresponding image regions. Enforce CLIP ViT self-attention to yield spatially concentrated patch activations aligned with each part-level phrase, enabling grounded explanations. Extend evaluation with a perturbation-based interpretability protocol, including counterfactual region masking to assess how removal of top explanatory regions degrades retrieval.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art interpretability on standard benchmarks (CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES) under the proposed perturbation-based metrics, while maintaining competitive retrieval accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicit part-wise matching and phrase-region grounding, implemented via PPIM and constrained CLIP attention, provide semantically meaningful, grounded explanations for TI-ReID without sacrificing retrieval performance; introduces a quantitative interpretability protocol for TI-ReID.

title: People-Centred Medical Image Analysis
summary: Recent advances in data-centric medical AI have produced highly accurate diagnostic systems, but the emphasis on data curation and performance metrics has not translated into widespread clinical adoption. We conjecture that this limited uptake stems from insufficient attention dedicated to the optimisation of fair performance across diverse patient populations and to workflow integration: performance biases can create regulatory barriers, and poorly integrated automation can disrupt clinical routines, degrade the quality of human-AI collaboration, and reduce clinicians' willingness to adopt AI tools. Prior work on workflow integration (e.g., Learning to Defer (L2D) and Learning to Complement (L2C)) and AI fairness has typically examined these challenges in isolation, overlooking their natural interdependence and the practical constraints of clinical environments, such as restricted clinician availability. We propose People-Centred Medical Image Analysis (PecMan), a human-AI framework that jointly optimises fairness, diagnostic accuracy, and workflow effectiveness through a dynamic gating mechanism that assigns cases to AI, clinicians, or both under clinician workload constraints. We also introduce the Fairness and Human-Centred AI (FairHAI) benchmark for evaluating trade-offs between accuracy, fairness, and clinician workload. Experiments using this benchmark show that PecMan consistently outperforms existing methods, paving the way for more trustworthy and clinically viable AI systems. Code will be available upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: A unified human-AI framework (PecMan) jointly optimizing fairness, diagnostic accuracy, and workflow efficiency via a dynamic gating mechanism assigning cases to AI, clinicians, or both, under clinician workload constraints; plus a FairHAI benchmark; experiments show improved performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Low clinical adoption due to neglect of fairness across diverse populations and poor workflow integration; prior work treated fairness and workflow in isolation, ignoring their interdependence and practical constraints.
AI.method: Introduce PecMan with a dynamic gating mechanism that allocates cases among AI, clinician, or both, optimizing a joint objective over accuracy, fairness, and workflow efficiency under clinician workload constraints; develop FairHAI benchmark to evaluate trade-offs.
AI.result: Empirical results show PecMan outperforms existing methods on the FairHAI benchmark; code availability promised.
AI.conclusion: PecMan advances trustworthy, clinically viable AI by integrating fairness and workflow considerations with diagnostic performance, fostering better adoption.

title: When Your LLM Reaches End-of-Life: A Framework for Confident Model Migration in Production Systems
summary: We present a framework for migrating production Large Language Model (LLM) based systems when the underlying model reaches end-of-life or requires replacement. The key contribution is a Bayesian statistical approach that calibrates automated evaluation metrics against human judgments, enabling confident model comparison even with limited manual evaluation data. We demonstrate this framework on a commercial question-answering system serving 5.3M monthly interactions across six global regions; evaluating correctness, refusal behavior, and stylistic adherence to successfully identify suitable replacement models. The framework is broadly applicable to any enterprise deploying LLM-based products, providing a principled, reproducible methodology for model migration that balances quality assurance with evaluation efficiency. This is a capability increasingly essential as the LLM ecosystem continues to evolve rapidly and organizations manage portfolios of AI-powered services across multiple models, regions, and use cases.

AI.tldr: Bayesian calibration of automated metrics against human judgments enables reliable model migration for production LLM systems with limited manual evaluation, demonstrated on a 5.3M/month commercial QA service.
AI.motivation: Enterprise LLM deployments require systematic, reproducible model migration as models reach end-of-life or require replacement, balancing evaluation efficiency with quality assurance across portfolios, regions, and use cases.
AI.method: A Bayesian statistical framework that calibrates automated evaluation metrics to human judgments; applied to metrics such as correctness, refusal behavior, and stylistic adherence; validated on a commercial QA system operating across six regions with 5.3M monthly interactions to identify suitable replacement models.
AI.result: The framework successfully identified suitable replacement models with confidence despite limited manual evaluation data; demonstrated broad applicability for enterprises deploying LLM-based products and balancing QA with evaluation efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, reproducible methodology for model migration in production LLM systems, addressing the rising need as the LLM ecosystem evolves and organizations manage multi-model, multi-region portfolios.

title: Learning Tactile-Aware Quadrupedal Loco-Manipulation Policies
summary: Quadrupedal loco-manipulation is commonly built on visual perception and proprioception. Yet reliable contact-rich manipulation remains difficult: vision and proprioception alone cannot resolve uncertain, evolving interactions with the environment. Tactile sensing offers direct contact observability, but scalable tactile-aware learning framework for quadrupedal loco-manipulation is still underexplored. In this paper, we present a tactile-aware loco-manipulation policy learning pipeline with a hierarchical structure. Our approach has two key components. First, we leverage real-world human demonstrations to train a tactile-conditioned visuotactile high-level policy. This policy predicts not only end-effector trajectories for manipulation, but also the evolving tactile interaction cues that characterize how contact should develop over time. Second, we perform large-scale reinforcement learning in simulation to learn a tactile-aware whole-body control policy that tracks diverse commanded trajectories and tactile interaction cues, and transfers zero-shot to the real world. Together, these components enable coordinated locomotion and manipulation under contact-rich scenarios. We evaluate the system on real-world contact-rich tasks, including in-hand reorientation with insertion, valve tightening, and delicate object manipulation. Compared to vision-only and visuotactile baselines, our method improves performance by 28.54% on average across these tasks.

AI.tldr: A tactile-aware loco-manipulation framework for quadrupeds using a hierarchical policy: a tactile-conditioned high-level policy learned from real demonstrations to predict end-effector trajectories and tactile cues, plus a simulation-trained tactile-aware whole-body controller that transfers zero-shot to reality, improving performance on contact-rich tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision and proprioception alone struggle with uncertain, evolving contact-rich interactions. Tactile sensing provides direct observability of contact states, but scalable tactile-aware learning for quadrupedal loco-manipulation is underexplored.
AI.method: A two-stage hierarchical approach: (1) real-world demonstrations train a tactile-conditioned visuotactile high-level policy that predicts end-effector trajectories and evolving tactile cues; (2) large-scale reinforcement learning in simulation to learn a tactile-aware whole-body controller that tracks commanded trajectories and tactile cues, enabling zero-shot transfer to real world.
AI.result: Evaluated on real contact-rich tasks (in-hand reorientation with insertion, valve tightening, delicate object manipulation). The tactile-aware method outperforms vision-only and visuotactile baselines by 28.54% on average across tasks.
AI.conclusion: The combination of a tactile-conditioned high-level policy and a tactile-aware locomotion policy enables coordinated, contact-rich loco-manipulation with improved performance and effective sim-to-real transfer.

title: Lightweight Distillation of SAM 3 and DINOv3 for Edge-Deployable Individual-Level Livestock Monitoring and Longitudinal Visual Analytics
summary: Foundation-model pipelines for individual-level livestock monitoring -- combining open-vocabulary detection, promptable video segmentation, and self-supervised visual embeddings -- have raised the accuracy ceiling of precision livestock farming (PLF), but their GPU memory budgets exceed the envelope of commodity edge accelerators. To close this gap, the 446M-parameter Perception Encoder (PE-ViT-L+) backbone of SAM 3 is distilled into a 40.66M-parameter multi-scale student through three mechanisms: a Feature Pyramid Network student encoder built on TinyViT-21M-512, a four-term direction-then-scale distillation loss, and backbone-substitution inference with sliding-window session pruning that bounds streaming GPU memory growth. The DINOv3 family includes a pre-distilled ViT-S/16 variant (21.6M parameters) released alongside a 6716M-parameter ViT-7B teacher; the ViT-S (21M) variant is adopted as the per-individual embedder. On the Edinburgh Pig dataset, the compressed pipeline reaches 92.29% MOTA and 96.15% IDF1 against the SAM 3 teacher (1.68- and 0.84-percentage-point losses), achieves a 7.77-fold reduction in system-level parameters and a 3.01-fold reduction in peak VRAM (19.52GB -> 6.49GB), and reaches 97.34% top-1 accuracy with 91.67% macro-F1 on nine-class pig behaviour classification. The pipeline fits inside an NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX 16GB envelope with 4.9GB of headroom, supporting a proposed -- but not yet empirically validated -- on-device embedding-pool re-identification mechanism whose per-individual footprint of approximately 94MB per animal per year produces a longitudinal visual record amenable to retrospective association with disease, lameness, reproductive, and growth outcome labels.

AI.tldr: Compresses a large SAM 3 backbone into a small multi-scale student (≈40.7M parameters) using FPN on TinyViT, a four-term distillation loss, and sliding-window pruning, enabling on-device embedding and PLF with substantial memory and parameter reductions while maintaining strong accuracy.
AI.motivation: Bridge the memory and compute gap between high-accuracy foundation-model PLF pipelines and commodity edge devices, enabling on-device multi-object tracking, re-identification, and behavior classification for pigs.
AI.method: Distill the PE-ViT-L+ 446M backbone into a 40.66M-parameter student via: (1) a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) student encoder based on TinyViT-21M-512; (2) a four-term direction-then-scale distillation loss; (3) backbone-substitution inference with sliding-window session pruning to bound streaming GPU memory. The DINOv3 family is used with a pre-distilled ViT-S/16 (21.6M) as the per-individual embedder and a 6716M-parameter ViT-7B teacher. The ViT-S/16 variant is adopted as the per-individual embedder.
AI.result: On Edinburgh Pig dataset, the compressed pipeline achieves 92.29% MOTA and 96.15% IDF1 versus the SAM 3 teacher (losses of 1.68 and 0.84 percentage points). Parameter count drops by 7.77× and peak VRAM by 3.01× (19.52GB → 6.49GB). It attains 97.34% top-1 accuracy and 91.67% macro-F1 for nine-class pig behavior classification. The pipeline fits within an NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX 16GB with 4.9GB headroom, enabling a proposed on-device embedding-pool re-identification mechanism (~94MB per animal per year) for longitudinal analysis linking visual records to health and growth outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers substantial model compression and memory savings with performance close to the teacher on PLF tasks, enabling practical on-device deployment. The proposed embedding pool for longitudinal re-ID shows promise but requires empirical validation to confirm feasibility and effectiveness in live deployments.

title: When Continual Learning Moves to Memory: A Study of Experience Reuse in LLM Agents
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents offer an appealing shortcut to continual learning: rather than updating model parameters, they accumulate experience in external memory, seemingly sidestepping the stability-plasticity dilemma of parametric learning. We show that this challenge does not disappear but resurfaces at the memory level. Under a limited context window, old and new experiences compete during retrieval, relocating the continual-learning bottleneck from parameter updates to memory access. To study this phenomenon, we introduce a (k,v) framework that disentangles two fundamental design axes of external memory: how experience is represented and how it is organized for retrieval. Across sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI, we find that abstract procedural memories transfer more reliably than detailed trajectories, while negative transfer disproportionately harms the hard cases. Moreover, finer-grained memory organization is not universally beneficial: designs that yield strong forward transfer can simultaneously induce severe forgetting. Together, these results reveal that external memory does not resolve the continual-learning problem; it reshapes it into a problem of memory representation and retrieval design.

AI.tldr: External memory in memory-augmented LLMs does not solve continual learning; bottleneck shifts to memory retrieval under limited context, studied via a (k,v) memory framework across ALFWorld/BabyAI; procedural memories transfer better than detailed trajectories; memory granularity trade-offs can cause forgetting; overall: memory design reshapes, not resolves, continual-learning.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether external memory resolves the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning or merely relocates it to memory access, and how memory representation and retrieval influence performance.
AI.method: Propose a (k,v) framework to disentangle memory representation (k) from retrieval organization (v); run sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI comparing abstract procedural memories against detailed trajectories; analyze transfer and forgetting under different memory designs.
AI.result: Under limited context, old and new experiences compete during retrieval, relocating the bottleneck to memory access. Abstract procedural memories transfer more reliably than detailed trajectories; finer-grained memory organization is not universally beneficial and can induce forward transfer alongside severe forgetting. Negative transfer disproportionately harms hard cases.
AI.conclusion: External memory reshapes the continual-learning problem into design challenges of memory representation and retrieval, rather than solving it.

title: End-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform
summary: Scientific research has long been human-led, driving new knowledge and transformative technologies through the continual revision of questions, methods and claims as evidence accumulates. Although large language model (LLM)-based agents are beginning to move beyond assisting predefined research workflows, none has yet demonstrated end-to-end autonomous discovery in a real physical system that produces a nontrivial result supported by experimental evidence. Here we introduce Qiushi Discovery Engine, an LLM-based agentic system for end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform. Qiushi Engine combines nonlinear research phases, Meta-Trace memory and a dual-layer architecture to maintain adaptive and stable research trajectories across long-horizon investigations involving thousands of LLM-mediated reasoning, measurement and revision actions. It autonomously reproduces a published transmission-matrix experiment on a non-original platform and converts an abstract coherence-order theory into experimental observables, providing, to our knowledge, the first observation of this class of coherence-order structure. More importantly, in an open-ended study involving 145.9 million tokens, 3,242 LLM calls, 1,242 tool calls, 163 research notes and 44 scripts, Qiushi Engine proposes and experimentally validates optical bilinear interaction, a physical mechanism structurally analogous to a core operation in Transformer attention. This AI-discovered mechanism suggests a route towards high-speed, energy-efficient optical hardware for pairwise computation. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an AI agentic system autonomously identifying and experimentally validating a nontrivial, previously unreported physical mechanism, marking a milestone for research-level autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based agentic system (Qiushi Engine) performs end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform, reproduces published experiments, and discovers a novel optical bilinear interaction analogous to Transformer attention, demonstrating high autonomy with extensive reasoning, measurement, and scripting.
AI.motivation: To push AI-assisted research from predefined workflows to autonomous, long-horizon discovery in physical systems and validate ideas without human-in-the-loop intervention.
AI.method: A dual-layer, Meta-Trace memory–enabled LLM-driven agentic system guiding a real optical experiment. The system handles nonlinear research phases over thousands of LLM-mediated steps, reproduces existing experiments, translates abstract theory into observables, and autonomously proposes and validates new mechanisms. Large-scale usage quantified by 145.9M tokens, 3,242 LLM calls, 1,242 tool calls, 163 notes, and 44 scripts.
AI.result: Autonomously reproduced a transmission-matrix experiment on a non-original platform; observed coherence-order structure from theory; discovered and experimentally validated an optical bilinear interaction mechanism analogous to Transformer attention, suggesting a path to fast, energy-efficient optical pairwise computation.
AI.conclusion: Marks a milestone for autonomous, research-level AI agents capable of proposing, testing, and validating nontrivial physical mechanisms in real experiments, with potential implications for AI-driven discovery and optical hardware design.

title: DOT-Sim: Differentiable Optical Tactile Simulation with Precise Real-to-Sim Physical Calibration
summary: Simulating optical tactile sensors presents significant challenges due to their high deformability and intricate optical properties. To address these issues and enable a physically accurate simulation, we propose DOT-Sim: Differentiable Optical Tactile Simulation. Unlike prior simulators that rely on simplified models of deformable sensors, DOT-Sim accurately captures the physical behavior of soft sensors by modeling them as elastic materials using the Material Point Method (MPM). DOT-Sim enables rapid calibration of optical tactile sensor simulation using a small number of demonstrations within minutes, which is substantially faster than existing methods. Compared to current baselines, our approach supports much larger and non-linear deformations. To handle the optical aspect, we propose a novel approach to simulating optical responses by learning a residual image relative to the real-world idle state. We validate the physical and visual realism of our method through a series of zero-shot sim-to-real tasks. Our experiments show that DOT-Sim (1) accurately replicates the physical dynamics of a DenseTact optical tactile sensor in reality, (2) generates realistic optical outputs in contact-rich scenarios, (3) enables direct deployment of simulation-trained classifiers in the real world, achieving 85% classification accuracy on challenging objects and 90% accuracy in embedded tumor-type detection, and (4) allows precise trajectory following with a policy trained from demonstrations in simulation, with an average error of less than 0.9 mm.

AI.tldr: A differentiable optical tactile simulator (DOT-Sim) combines Material Point Method-based elasticity with a learned residual optical model to enable fast, accurate sim-to-real transfer for tactile sensors, handling large deformations and enabling direct deployment of simulation-trained classifiers and policies.
AI.motivation: Simulating optical tactile sensors is hard due to strong coupling between soft deformations and complex optical responses; existing simulators use simplified models or are slow to calibrate, hindering real-world deployment and learning-based control.
AI.method: Model soft tactile sensors as elastic bodies via Material Point Method (MPM); calibrate quickly using a small number of demonstrations; predict optical responses by learning a residual image relative to the idle real-state image, enabling differentiable, visually faithful outputs; validate with zero-shot sim-to-real tasks on DenseTact and related perception/control benchmarks.
AI.result: Physically accurate replication of DenseTact dynamics; realistic optical outputs in contact-rich scenarios; direct transfer of simulation-trained classifiers achieving ~85% object classification and ~90% embedded tumor-type detection; trajectory following with average error <0.9 mm when policies are trained in simulation.
AI.conclusion: DOT-Sim achieves high-fidelity physical and optical realism with fast calibration, enabling robust sim-to-real transfer for tactile sensing tasks and supporting learning-based perception and control in robotics.

title: Energy-Efficient Plant Monitoring via Knowledge Distillation
summary: Recent advances in large-scale visual representation learning have significantly improved performance in plant species and plant disease recognition tasks. However, state-of-the-art models, often based on high-capacity vision transformers or multimodal foundation models, remain computationally expensive and difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments such as mobile or edge devices. This limitation hinders the scalability of automated biodiversity monitoring and precision agriculture systems, where efficiency is as critical as accuracy. In this work, we investigate knowledge distillation as an effective approach to transfer the representational capacity of large pretrained models into smaller, more efficient architectures. We focus on plant species and disease recognition, and conduct an extensive empirical study on two challenging benchmarks: Pl@ntNet300K-v2 and Deep-Plant-Disease. We evaluate four representative architectures, including two ConvNeXt models and two vision transformers, under multiple training regimes: from-scratch training and pretrained initialization, each with and without distillation. In total, we train and evaluate 70 models. Our results show that knowledge distillation consistently improves performance across tasks and architectures. Distilled models are able to match the performance of significantly larger models while maintaining substantially lower computational cost. These findings demonstrate the potential of knowledge distillation techniques to enable efficient and scalable deployment of plant recognition systems in real-world environmental applications.

AI.tldr: Knowledge distillation from large pretrained models to smaller, efficient architectures (ConvNeXt and ViTs) improves plant species and disease recognition performance while reducing computational cost; 70 models evaluated across two benchmarks, demonstrating distillation's effectiveness for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Address the computational and deployment bottlenecks of state-of-the-art vision models (transformers and multimodal bases) for plant recognition in resource-constrained environments, enabling scalable biodiversity monitoring and precision agriculture.
AI.method: Empirical study across two benchmarks (Pl@ntNet300K-v2 and Deep-Plant-Disease) using four architectures (two ConvNeXt, two ViTs) under multiple training regimens (from-scratch and pretrained), each with and without distillation; total of 70 trained/evaluated models.
AI.result: Knowledge distillation consistently yields performance gains across tasks and architectures; distilled models achieve parity with significantly larger models while incurring substantially lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation is an effective strategy to enable efficient, scalable plant recognition systems suitable for deployment on edge/mobile devices in real-world environmental applications.

title: Automatic Causal Fairness Analysis with LLM-Generated Reporting
summary: AutoML, intended as the process of automating the application of machine learning to real-world problems, is a key step for AI popularisation. Most AutoML frameworks are not accounting for the potential lack of fairness in the training data and in the corresponding predictions. We introduce \textsc{FairMind}, a software prototype aiming to automatise fairness analysis at the dataset level. We achieve that by resorting to the assumptions of the \emph{standard fairness model}, recently proposed by Plečko and Bareinboim. This allows for a sound fairness evaluation in terms of causal effects, based on \emph{counterfactual} queries involving the target, possibly confounders and mediators, and the different values of an input feature we regard as \emph{protected}. After the necessary data preprocessing, the tool implements a closed-form computation of the effects. LLMs are consequently exploited to generate accurate reports on the fairness levels detected in the training dataset. We achieve that in a zero-shot setup and show by examples the expected advantages with respect to a direct analysis performed by the LLM. To favour applications, extensions to ordinal protected variable and continuous targets and novel decomposition results are also discussed.

AI.tldr: FairMind automates dataset-level fairness analysis for AutoML using the standard fairness model, leveraging causal counterfactuals, closed-form effects, and LLM-generated reports in a zero-shot setup, with extensions to ordinal protected variables and continuous targets.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of fairness diagnostics in AutoML pipelines; enable sound causal fairness evaluation at the dataset level to improve model fairness before deployment.
AI.method: Adopt Plečko & Bareinboim's standard fairness model; perform counterfactual queries involving target, confounders, mediators; after preprocessing, compute causal effects in closed form; use LLMs to generate reports; evaluate in zero-shot setting; discuss extensions.
AI.result: Prototype FairMind achieves zero-shot LLM reporting of detected fairness levels; provides advantages over direct LLM analysis as shown by examples; presents potential extensions to ordinal protected variables and continuous targets and new decompositions.
AI.conclusion: FairMind enables automated, dataset-level fairness analysis for AutoML using a causal framework, with interpretable outputs via LLM reports and room for extensions.

title: Think it, Run it: Autonomous ML pipeline generation via self-healing multi-agent AI
summary: The purpose of our paper is to develop a unified multi-agent architecture that automates end-to-end machine learning (ML) pipeline generation from datasets and natural-language (NL) goals, improving efficiency, robustness and explainability. A five-agent system is proposed to handle profiling, intent parsing, microservice recommendation, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) construction and execution. It integrates code-grounded Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for microservice understanding, an explainable hybrid recommender combining multiple criteria, a self-healing mechanism using Large Language Model (LLM)-based error interpretation and adaptive learning from execution history. The approach is evaluated on 150 ML tasks across diverse scenarios. The system achieves an 84.7% end-to-end pipeline success rate, outperforming baseline methods. It demonstrates improved robustness through self-healing and reduces workflow development time compared to manual construction. The study introduces a novel integration of code-grounded RAG, explainable recommendation, self-healing execution and adaptive learning within a single architecture, showing that tightly coupled intelligent components can outperform isolated solutions.

AI.tldr: A unified five-agent architecture automates end-to-end ML pipeline generation from datasets and natural-language goals, using code-grounded RAG, explainable hybrid recommender, self-healing with LLMs, and adaptive learning, achieving 84.7% success on 150 tasks.
AI.motivation: Automate and robustify end-to-end ML pipeline construction from user intents and data, addressing fragmented tooling, lack of explainability, and brittleness in existing systems.
AI.method: A five-agent system (profiling, intent parsing, microservice recommendation, DAG construction, execution) with code-grounded Retrieval-Augmented Generation for microservice understanding, an explainable hybrid recommender, self-healing via LLM-based error interpretation, and adaptive learning from execution history; evaluated on 150 ML tasks across diverse scenarios.
AI.result: End-to-end pipeline success rate of 84.7%, outperforming baselines; demonstrated robustness through self-healing and reduced workflow development time compared to manual construction; shows that tightly integrated intelligent components outperform isolated solutions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating code-grounded RAG, explainable recommendations, self-healing execution, and adaptive learning in a single architecture yields superior performance over modular approaches and enables more reliable automation of ML pipelines.

title: An Experimental Modular Instrument With a Haptic Feedback Framework for Robotic Surgery Training
comment: Accepted to the 11th IEEE RAS/EMBS International Conference on Biomedical Robotics and Biomechatronics (BioRob 2026)
summary: Robotic-assisted surgery offers significant clinical advantages but largely eliminates direct haptic feedback, increasing the risk of excessive tool-tissue interaction forces. Although recent commercial systems have begun to introduce force feedback, their high cost limits accessibility, particularly for surgical training. This paper presents a modular experimental robotic laparoscopic instrument integrated with a real-time haptic feedback framework. The proposed instrument employs a wrist-mounted force/torque (F/T) sensor to estimate tool-tissue interaction forces while avoiding the durability and integration challenges of tip-mounted sensors. A haptic feedback framework is developed to extract the external contact forces, render them to the haptic device, and generate stable and perceptually meaningful feedback. The instrument is integrated into the robotic surgery training system (RoboScope) and evaluated through a controlled user study involving a force regulation task. Experimental results demonstrate that haptic feedback significantly improves task success rate, force regulation accuracy, and task efficiency compared to visual-only feedback. The proposed instrument enables stable, high-fidelity haptic interaction, supporting effective robotic surgery training.

AI.tldr: A modular robotic laparoscopic instrument with a wrist-mounted force/torque sensor provides real-time haptic feedback in a RoboScope training system, improving force regulation and task performance over visual guidance.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of haptic feedback in robotic surgery and its training implications, offering a durable, integrable, and cost-effective approach for high-fidelity haptic cues.
AI.method: Develop a wrist-mounted F/T sensor to estimate tool-tissue interaction forces; implement a real-time haptic feedback framework to extract, render, and stabilize external contact forces; integrate into RoboScope; evaluate via controlled user study on a force regulation task.
AI.result: Haptic feedback significantly improved task success rate, force regulation accuracy, and task efficiency compared with visual-only feedback.
AI.conclusion: The instrument enables stable, high-fidelity haptic interaction, enhancing robotic surgery training effectiveness.

title: HQ-UNet: A Hybrid Quantum-Classical U-Net with a Quantum Bottleneck for Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
summary: Semantic segmentation in remote sensing is commonly addressed using classical deep learning architectures such as U-Net, which require a large number of parameters to model complex spatial relationships. Quantum machine learning (QML) provides an alternative representation paradigm by mapping classical features into quantum states, but its direct application to high-dimensional images remains challenging under near-term quantum hardware constraints. In this work, we propose HQ-UNet, a hybrid quantum-classical U-Net architecture that integrates a compact parameterized quantum circuit at the bottleneck of a classical U-Net. The proposed design uses a non-pooling quantum convolutional module to enrich highly compressed encoder features before decoding, while keeping the quantum component shallow and parameter-efficient. Experiments on the LandCover.ai dataset show that HQ-UNet achieves a mean IoU of 0.8050 and an overall accuracy of 94.76%, outperforming the classical U-Net baseline. These results suggest that compact quantum bottlenecks can enhance feature representation for remote sensing image segmentation under near-term quantum constraints. This highlights the potential of hybrid quantum-classical designs as a promising direction for parameter-efficient dense prediction in Earth observation.

AI.tldr: HQ-UNet is a hybrid quantum-classical U-Net with a compact quantum bottleneck, achieving improved segmentation performance over a classical U-Net on LandCover.ai.
AI.motivation: Quantum machine learning offers richer feature representations for high-dimensional remote sensing data, but near-term quantum hardware necessitates parameter-efficient, shallow circuits. A quantum bottleneck integrated into a classical architecture could provide performance gains without excessive quantum resources.
AI.method: Integrates a non-pooling quantum convolutional module at the bottleneck of a classical U-Net. A compact parameterized quantum circuit processes highly compressed features, while the encoder compresses and the decoder reconstructs, yielding a shallow, parameter-efficient hybrid design.
AI.result: On the LandCover.ai dataset, HQ-UNet achieves mean IoU = 0.8050 and overall accuracy = 94.76%, outperforming the classical U-Net baseline.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that compact quantum bottlenecks can enhance feature representations for remote sensing segmentation under near-term quantum constraints, supporting the potential of hybrid quantum-classical designs for parameter-efficient dense prediction in Earth observation.

title: Fidelity, Diversity, and Privacy: A Multi-Dimensional LLM Evaluation for Clinical Data Augmentation
summary: The scarcity of high-quality annotated medical data, particularly in mental health, poses a significant bottleneck for training robust machine learning models. Privacy regulations restrict data sharing, making synthetic data generation a promising alternative. The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in a data augmentation pipeline could be leveraged as an alternative in this field. In the proposed methodology, DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3 and Qwen 3.5 are used to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports conditioned on specific International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10) codes. Because naive text generation can lead to mode collapse or privacy breaches (memorization), a comprehensive evaluation framework is introduced. The generated diagnostic texts are assessed across three dimensions: semantic fidelity, lexical diversity, and privacy/plagiarism. The results demonstrate that all models can generate clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe synthetic reports, significantly expanding the available training data for clinical natural language processing tasks without compromising patient confidentiality.

AI.tldr: LLM-based synthetic data generation for mental health NLP conditioned on ICD-10 codes; uses three models; evaluated on semantic fidelity, lexical diversity, and privacy; claims to be privacy-safe and expands training data.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and privacy regulations constrain sharing of medical data; synthetic data augmentation offers scalable solution, particularly for mental health NLP.
AI.method: Generate synthetic diagnostic texts conditioned on ICD-10 codes using DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3, and Qwen 3.5; employ an evaluation framework assessing semantic fidelity, lexical diversity, and privacy/plagiarism.
AI.result: All models produced clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe synthetic reports, effectively expanding training data for clinical NLP tasks without compromising patient confidentiality.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven synthetic data is a viable approach to mitigate data scarcity in mental health NLP, provided rigorous evaluation to guard against memorization and privacy risks; warrants further benchmarking and validation.

title: Unsupervised Electrofacies Classification and Porosity Characterization in the Offshore Keta Basin Using Wireline Logs
summary: This study presents an unsupervised machine learning workflow for electrofacies analysis in the offshore Keta Basin, Ghana, where core data are scarce. Six standard wireline logs from Well~C were analysed over a depth interval comprising approximately $11{,}195$ samples. K-means clustering was applied in multivariate log space, with the clustering structure evaluated using inertia and silhouette diagnostics. Four clusters were identified, supported by an average silhouette coefficient of approximately $0.50$, indicating moderate but meaningful separation. The resulting electrofacies exhibit systematic, depth-continuous patterns associated with variations in clay content, porosity, and rock framework properties, forming a geological continuum from shale-dominated to cleaner sandstone-dominated units. The results demonstrate that log-only, unsupervised clustering supported by quantitative metrics provides a robust and reproducible framework for subsurface characterisation. The proposed workflow offers a practical tool for early-stage formation evaluation in frontier offshore basins and a foundation for future integrated studies.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised k-means clustering applied to six wireline logs from Well C in the offshore Keta Basin yields four electrofacies with moderate separation (silhouette ~0.50), revealing depth-continuous patterns linked to clay content, porosity, and rock framework. The approach provides a log-only, robust framework for early-stage subsurface characterization in data-scarce frontier basins.
AI.motivation: Address core-data scarcity in frontier offshore basins and provide an objective, repeatable method to characterize lithofacies and heterogeneity using available log data.
AI.method: Use six standard wireline logs across ~11,195 samples; perform multivariate log-space normalization; apply k-means clustering; evaluate cluster validity with inertia and silhouette metrics; identify four electrofacies; interpret in terms of clay, porosity, and rock framework.
AI.result: Identification of four electrofacies with depth-continuous trends; characterization from shale-dominated to sandstone-dominated units; moderate separation (average silhouette ~0.50) indicates meaningful, reproducible clustering; workflow is log-only and suitable for early-stage formation evaluation and a basis for integrated studies.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability and robustness of log-only unsupervised clustering with quantitative validation for subsurface characterization; provides a practical, reproducible workflow for frontier basins and a foundation for future integrated analyses.

title: RAY-TOLD: Ray-Based Latent Dynamics for Dense Dynamic Obstacle Avoidance with TDMPC
summary: Dense, dynamic crowds pose a persistent challenge for autonomous mobile robots. Purely reactive planning methods, such as Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control, often fail to escape local minima in complex scenarios due to their limited prediction horizon. To bridge this gap, we propose Ray-based Task-Oriented Latent Dynamics (RAY-TOLD), a hybrid control architecture that integrates obstacle information into latent dynamics and utilizes the robustness of physics-based MPPI with the long-horizon foresight of reinforcement learning. RAY-TOLD leverages a LiDAR-centric latent dynamics model to encode high-dimensional sensor data into a compact state representation, enabling the learning of a terminal value function and a policy prior. We introduce a policy mixture sampling strategy that augments the MPPI candidate population with trajectories derived from the learned policy, effectively guiding the planner towards the goal while maintaining kinematic feasibility. Extensive tests in a stochastic environment with high-density dynamic obstacles demonstrate that our method outperforms the MPPI baseline, reducing the collision rate. The results confirm that blending short-horizon physics-based rollouts with learned long-horizon intent significantly enhances navigation reliability and safety.

AI.tldr: A hybrid planning framework (RAY-TOLD) that fuses LiDAR-based latent dynamics with MPPI, using a learned policy prior and a policy-mixture sampling strategy to guide short-horizon physics-based rollouts with long-horizon intent, improving navigation safety in dense dynamic crowds.
AI.motivation: Purely reactive planners (e.g., MPPI) struggle in dense, dynamic crowds due to limited prediction horizons and local minima. There is a need for long-horizon foresight and obstacle-aware latent representations to improve safety and reliability in autonomous navigation.
AI.method: Introduce RAY-TOLD, a LiDAR-centric latent dynamics model that encodes high-dimensional sensor data into a compact state. Learn a terminal value function and a policy prior. Develop a policy-mixture sampling strategy that augments MPPI candidate trajectories with those from the learned policy, blending short-horizon physics-based rollouts with long-horizon learned intent.
AI.result: In stochastic environments with high-density dynamic obstacles, RAY-TOLD outperforms the MPPI baseline by reducing collision rates, demonstrating improved navigation reliability and safety.
AI.conclusion: Blending short-horizon, physics-based planning with learned long-horizon intent via latent dynamics and policy priors yields more reliable and safer autonomous navigation in dense crowds.

title: AttriBE: Quantifying Attribute Expressivity in Body Embeddings for Recognition and Identification
summary: Person re-identification (ReID) systems that match individuals across images or video frames are essential in many real-world applications. However, existing methods are often influenced by attributes such as gender, pose, and body mass index (BMI), which vary in unconstrained settings and raise concerns related to fairness and generalization. To address this, we extend the notion of expressivity, defined as the mutual information between learned features and specific attributes, using a secondary neural network to quantify how strongly attributes are encoded. Applying this framework to three transformer-based ReID models on a large-scale visible-spectrum dataset, we find that BMI consistently shows the highest expressivity in deeper layers. Attributes in the final representation are ranked as BMI > Pitch > Gender > Yaw, and expressivity evolves across layers and training epochs, with pose peaking in intermediate layers and BMI strengthening with depth. We further extend the analysis to cross-spectral person identification across infrared modalities including short-wave, medium-wave, and long-wave infrared. In this setting, pitch becomes comparable to BMI and attribute trends increase monotonically across depth, suggesting increased reliance on structural cues when bridging modality gaps. Overall, the results show that transformer-based ReID embeddings encode a hierarchy of implicit attributes, with morphometric information persistently embedded and pose contributing more strongly under cross-spectral conditions.

AI.tldr: Defines and quantifies expressivity of transformer-based ReID features with respect to attributes (BMI, Pitch, Gender, Yaw) using a secondary network; finds a depth- and epoch-dependent hierarchy of attribute encoding, with BMI most encoded, especially in deeper layers, and cross-spectral results showing increased reliance on structural cues.
AI.motivation: Fairness and generalization concerns in person re-identification arise because learned features can encode sensitive attributes. The work seeks to quantify how strongly attributes are embedded in ReID representations and how this evolves across layers and modalities (visible and infrared).
AI.method: Introduce expressivity as mutual-information-like strength quantified by a secondary neural network. Apply to three transformer-based ReID models on a large-scale visible-spectrum dataset. Extend the analysis to cross-spectral infrared (short/medium/long-wave). Examine layer- and epoch-wise evolution of attribute expressivity.
AI.result: BMI shows the highest expressivity in deeper layers; final representations rank BMI > Pitch > Gender > Yaw. Expressivity evolves across layers and training epochs; pose peaks in intermediate layers, BMI strengthens with depth. Across infrared modalities, pitch becomes comparable to BMI and attribute trends increase monotonically with depth, implying greater reliance on structural cues when bridging modality gaps.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based ReID embeddings encode a hierarchy of implicit attributes, with morphometric information persistently embedded and pose contributing more strongly under cross-spectral conditions; implications for fairness, generalization, and interpretability in ReID systems.

title: Dynamic Adversarial Fine-Tuning Reorganizes Refusal Geometry
summary: Safety-aligned language models must refuse harmful requests without collapsing into broad over-refusal, but the training-time mechanisms behind this tradeoff remain unclear. Prior work characterizes refusal directions and jailbreak robustness, yet does not explain how dynamic adversarial fine-tuning changes refusal carriers across training. We present a measurement-driven mechanism study, not a new defense, on one 7B backbone under supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and R2D2-style dynamic adversarial fine-tuning. Our protocol aligns fixed-source HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest with a five-anchor refusal-geometry suite and causal interventions. R2D2 drives fixed-source HarmBench ASR to 0.000 at steps 50 and 100, then partially reopens to 0.035 at step 250 and 0.250 at step 500; SFT remains less robust, with ASR between 0.505 and 0.588 at the same anchors. On XSTest, R2D2 any-refusal is 1.000 early, then falls to 0.664 and 0.228. Geometrically, R2D2 preserves a late-layer admissible carrier through step 100 before relocating to an early-layer carrier, while effective rank remains near 1.23--1.27. Causal interventions indicate low-dimensional but utility-coupled control. These results support a reorganization account rather than a drift-only account, with evidence limited to one backbone and fixed-source attacks.

AI.tldr: A measurement-driven study analyzes how dynamic adversarial fine-tuning reorganizes–not drifts–refusal mechanisms in a single 7B LM, showing R2D2-based tuning reroutes refusal carriers across training while fixed-source attacks remain limited in scope.
AI.motivation: To uncover training-time mechanisms by which safety-aligned LMs balance refusal strength and general utility, focusing on how dynamic adversarial fine-tuning alters refusal representations rather than relying on drift explanations.
AI.method: Empirical mechanism study on one 7B backbone under supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and R2D2-style dynamic adversarial fine-tuning. Protocol aligns fixed-source HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest with a five-anchor refusal-geometry suite and performs causal interventions to trace refusal carriers across steps.
AI.result: R2D2 reduces HarmBench attack success rate (ASR) to ~0.0 at steps 50–100, then partially reopens (ASR 0.035 at step 250; 0.250 at step 500); SFT remains less robust (ASR 0.505–0.588). On XSTest, R2D2 any-refusal starts at 1.0 then drops to 0.664 and 0.228. Geometrically, refusal carriers shift from late-layer to early-layer during training; effective rank ~1.23–1.27, implying a low-dimensional, utility-linked control.
