This forces users to rewrite LLM training libraries to incorporate compositions of various complex long-context optimizations, such as sequence-parallelism, to training pipelines; a process that requires in-depth expertise, reducing developer productivity. To tackle these challenges, we introduce AutoSP: the first automated solution to automatically optimize LLM training for longer-contexts. AutoSP compiles models and applies a targeted set of optimizations: automated sequence parallelism, and long-context aware activation-checkpointing, to drastically enhance LLM trainability at negligible cost to throughput. Our evaluation demonstrates AutoSP's capability on both NVIDIA and AMD hardware, increasing training contexts by upto 2.7$\times$ and 2.5$\times$ respectively over competitive hand-written baseline at negligible cost to runtime performance.

AI.tldr: AutoSP is an automated system that optimizes long-context training for LLMs by automatically applying sequence parallelism and long-context aware activation-checkpointing, enabling substantially longer training contexts with negligible throughput cost.
AI.motivation: Current LLM training stacks lack user-friendly abstractions for long-context optimization, forcing developers to manually implement complex compositions (e.g., sequence parallelism) that hamper productivity as prompts grow to tens/hundreds of thousands of tokens.
AI.method: AutoSP automatically compiles models and applies targeted long-context optimizations—specifically sequence parallelism and long-context–aware activation checkpointing—without requiring manual reengineering of training pipelines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on NVIDIA and AMD hardware shows training contexts increase by up to 2.7x and 2.5x respectively with negligible impact on runtime performance compared to strong hand-written baselines.
AI.conclusion: AutoSP provides the first automated, hardware-agnostic solution to extend LLM training contexts, improving trainability and developer productivity with minimal throughput trade-offs.

title: Web2BigTable: A Bi-Level Multi-Agent LLM System for Internet-Scale Information Search and Extraction
summary: Agentic web search increasingly faces two distinct demands: deep reasoning over a single target, and structured aggregation across many entities and heterogeneous sources. Current systems struggle on both fronts. Breadth-oriented tasks demand schema-aligned outputs with wide coverage and cross-entity consistency, while depth-oriented tasks require coherent reasoning over long, branching search trajectories. We introduce \textbf{Web2BigTable}, a multi-agent framework for web-to-table search that supports both regimes. Web2BigTable adopts a bi-level architecture in which an upper-level orchestrator decomposes the task into sub-problems and lower-level worker agents solve them in parallel. Through a closed-loop run--verify--reflect process, the framework jointly improves decomposition and execution over time via persistent, human-readable external memory, with self-evolving updates to each single-agent. During execution, workers coordinate through a shared workspace that makes partial findings visible, allowing them to reduce redundant exploration, reconcile conflicting evidence, and adapt to emerging coverage gaps. Web2BigTable sets a new state of the art on WideSearch, reaching an Avg@4 Success Rate of \textbf{38.50} ($7.5\times$ the second best at 5.10), Row F1 of \textbf{63.53} (+25.03 over the second best), and Item F1 of \textbf{80.12} (+14.42 over the second best). It also generalises to depth-oriented search on XBench-DeepSearch, achieving 73.0 accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/web2bigtable/web2bigtable.

AI.tldr: A bi-level, multi-agent framework (Web2BigTable) enables both breadth-oriented schema-aligned web-to-table outputs and depth-oriented coherent reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art on WideSearch and strong depth performance, via a run--verify--reflect loop with persistent external memory and a shared workspace.
AI.motivation: To address the dual demands of web-to-table search: wide coverage with cross-entity consistency and deep, coherent reasoning over long, branching search trajectories, which existing systems struggle to satisfy.
AI.method: A bi-level architecture with an upper-level orchestrator that decomposes tasks into subproblems and lower-level worker agents that solve them in parallel. A closed-loop run–verify–reflect process updates both task decomposition and execution. Persistent, human-readable external memory stores knowledge; workers coordinate via a shared workspace to reveal partial findings, reduce redundant exploration, reconcile evidence, and adapt to coverage gaps.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on WideSearch: Avg@4 SR 38.50 (7.5× the second best 5.10), Row F1 63.53 (+25.03), Item F1 80.12 (+14.42). Generalizes to depth-oriented search on XBench-DeepSearch with 73.0 accuracy. Code available at provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Web2BigTable demonstrates effective integration of orchestration, parallel problem solving, and an ever-evolving external memory to deliver both breadth and depth in web-to-table search, with measurable gains over baselines.

title: Learning-Based Hierarchical Scene Graph Matching for Robot Localization Leveraging Prior Maps
summary: Accurate localization is a fundamental requirement for autonomous robots operating in indoor environments. Scene graphs encode the spatial structure of an environment as a hierarchy of semantic entities and their relationships, and can be constructed both online from robot sensor data and offline from architectural priors such as Building Information Models (BIM). Matching these two complementary representations enables drift correction in SLAM by grounding robot observations against a known structural prior. However, establishing reliable node-to-node correspondences between them remains an open challenge: existing combinatorial methods are prohibitively expensive at scale, and prior learned approaches address only flat graph matching, ignoring the multi-level semantic structure present in both representations. Here we present a learned, end-to-end differentiable pipeline that augments both graphs with semantically motivated edge types encoding intra- and inter- level relationships, explicitly exploiting this hierarchy to enable simultaneous matching from high-level room concepts down to low-level wall surfaces. Trained exclusively on floor plans, the proposed method outperforms the combinatorial baseline in F1 on real LiDAR environments while running an order of magnitude faster, demonstrating viable zero-shot generalization for BIM-assisted robot localization.

AI.tldr: A learned, end-to-end differentiable pipeline matches scene graphs from robot observations with BIM priors to correct SLAM drift by exploiting hierarchical multi-level semantics; it outperforms combinatorial baselines and is orders of magnitude faster, with zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Drift correction in indoor robot localization requires robust grounding of observations to a known structural prior. Existing combinatorial graph matching is expensive at scale, and flat-learning approaches ignore the hierarchical scene structure present in sensor-derived graphs and BIMs.
AI.method: An end-to-end differentiable pipeline that augments both graphs with semantically motivated edge types encoding intra- and inter-level relationships, leveraging the hierarchy to perform simultaneous matching across high-level room concepts down to low-level wall surfaces. Trained exclusively on floor plans, enabling cross-domain generalization.
AI.result: Outperforms a combinatorial baseline in F1 on real LiDAR data and runs an order of magnitude faster, demonstrating viable zero-shot generalization for BIM-assisted robot localization.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables scalable, accurate multi-level graph matching for SLAM drift correction by leveraging hierarchical semantic structure, achieving fast, zero-shot capable BIM-assisted localization.

title: CasLayout: Cascaded 3D Layout Diffusion for Indoor Scene Synthesis with Implicit Relation Modeling
summary: Synthesizing realistic 3D indoor scenes remains challenging due to data scarcity and the difficulty of simultaneously enforcing global architectural constraints and local semantic consistency. Existing approaches often overlook structural boundaries or rely on fully connected relation graphs that introduce redundant generation errors. Inspired by human design cognition, we present CasLayout, a cascaded diffusion framework that decomposes the joint scene generation task into four conditional sub-stages with explicit physical and semantic roles: (1) predicting furniture quantity and categories, (2) refining object sizes and feature embeddings, (3) modeling spatial relationships in a latent space, and (4) generating Oriented Bounding Boxes (OBBs). This decoupled architecture reduces data requirements and enables flexible integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) for zero-shot tasks such as image-to-scene generation. To maintain physical validity within complex floor plans, we explicitly model building elements (e.g., walls, doors, and windows) as conditional constraints. Furthermore, to address the high entropy of dense relation graphs, we introduce a sparse relation graph formulation aligned with human spatial descriptions. By encoding these sparse graphs into a compact latent space using a bidirectional Variational Autoencoder (VAE), the proposed framework provides enhanced relational controllability, allowing generated layouts to better respect functional organization. Experiments demonstrate that CasLayout achieves state-of-the-art performance in fidelity and diversity while enabling improved controllability in practical applications.

AI.tldr: CasLayout: a cascaded diffusion pipeline for 3D indoor scene synthesis that decomposes scene generation into four conditional stages, uses a sparse relation graph encoded by a bidirectional VAE, and enforces explicit architectural constraints to achieve high fidelity, diversity, and controllability, with zero-shot integration of LLMs/VLMs.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and the challenge of enforcing global architectural constraints and local semantic consistency in 3D indoor scene synthesis. Existing methods often overlook structural boundaries or rely on dense relation graphs that can introduce redundant errors.
AI.method: Four-stage cascaded diffusion: (1) predict furniture quantity/categories; (2) refine object sizes/embeddings; (3) model spatial relationships in latent space; (4) generate Oriented Bounding Boxes (OBBs). Enforce physical validity via constraints for walls/doors/windows. Introduce sparse relation graphs encoded into a latent space with a bidirectional VAE to improve relational controllability. Enable zero-shot LLM/VLM integration for tasks such as image-to-scene generation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art fidelity and diversity; improved controllability in practical applications.
AI.conclusion: CasLayout shows that decomposed, constraint-aware, sparsified relational modeling improves realism and controllability in 3D indoor scene synthesis, with data efficiency and adaptability to zero-shot tasks.

title: Anomaly Detection in Soil Heavy Metal Contamination Using Unsupervised Learning for Environmental Risk Assessment
summary: Soil contamination by heavy metals poses a persistent environmental and public health concern in rapidly urbanising regions of Ghana, particularly at unregulated waste disposal sites. This study applies an unsupervised machine learning framework to detect and characterise anomalous heavy metal contamination patterns in soils from twelve waste sites and residential controls in the Central Region, of Ghana. Concentrations of eight metals (As, Cd, Cr, Cu, Hg, Ni, Pb, Zn) were analysed alongside standard health risk indices, including the Hazard Index (HI) and Incremental Lifetime Cancer Risk (ILCR). Isolation Forest and PCA reconstruction error each identified $12$ anomalous samples ($15.4\%$ of $78$ samples), while DBSCAN detected no density-isolated noise points. A consensus approach isolated six robust anomalies ($7.7\%)$, all spatially concentrated at a single site (S3). Anomalies exhibited approximately $70$--$80\%$ higher mean HI values than normal samples, with all consensus anomalies exceeding the HI$=1$ threshold. PCA reconstruction error showed a strong positive association with HI ($r \approx 0.8$), indicating consistency between multivariate deviation and health risk. Three distinct anomaly types were identified: extreme Cu enrichment at S3, anomalously low Ni at S4/S5, and moderate multi-metal (Pb--Zn) co-elevation at S9--S12. The results demonstrate that unsupervised machine learning provides granular, objective insight beyond aggregate indices, enabling targeted site prioritisation and risk-informed environmental management.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised ML detects spatially clustered soil contamination anomalies in Ghana using Isolation Forest, PCA reconstruction error, and DBSCAN; consensus identifies six robust anomalies (7.7%), all at site S3, with higher health risk (HI>1); three anomaly archetypes (extreme Cu at S3, low Ni at S4/S5, Pb–Zn co-elevation at S9–S12); ML adds diagnostic value beyond HI/ILCR for targeted risk management.
AI.motivation: Address ongoing heavy-metal soil contamination risk in rapidly urbanizing regions of Ghana and limitations of traditional aggregate health risk indices; demonstrate how unsupervised ML can detect anomalous patterns to inform site prioritization.
AI.method: Data from twelve waste sites and residential controls in Ghana's Central Region: eight metals (As, Cd, Cr, Cu, Hg, Ni, Pb, Zn) plus standard risk indices HI and ILCR. Applied unsupervised algorithms: Isolation Forest and PCA reconstruction error to identify anomalies; DBSCAN for density-based noise; a consensus approach to derive robust anomalies. Spatial mapping for site-level interpretation.
AI.result: Isolation Forest and PCA reconstruction error flagged 12 anomalous samples (15.4% of 78). DBSCAN found no density-based noise. Consensus method yielded six robust anomalies (7.7%), all at site S3. Anomalies had 70–80% higher mean HI than normal samples, with all consensus anomalies exceeding HI=1. PCA reconstruction error correlated strongly with HI (r≈0.8). Three anomaly archetypes identified: extreme Cu at S3, anomalously low Ni at S4/S5, and moderate multi-metal Pb–Zn co-elevation at S9–S12.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised ML provides granular, objective insights beyond aggregate HI/ILCR indices, enabling targeted site prioritisation and risk-informed environmental management.

title: When Roles Fail: Epistemic Constraints on Advocate Role Fidelity in LLM-Based Political Statement Analysis
summary: Democratic discourse analysis systems increasingly rely on multi-agent LLM pipelines in which distinct evaluator models are assigned adversarial roles to generate structured, multi-perspective assessments of political statements. A core assumption is that models will reliably maintain their assigned roles. This paper provides the first systematic empirical test of that assumption using the TRUST pipeline. We develop an epistemic stance classifier that identifies advocate roles from reasoning text without relying on surface vocabulary, and measure role fidelity across 60 political statements (30 English, 30 German) using four metrics: Role Drift Index (RDI), Expected Drift Distance (EDD), Directional Drift Index (DDI), and Entropy-based Role Stability (ERS). We identify two failure modes - the Epistemic Floor Effect (fact-check results create an absolute lower bound below which the legitimizing role cannot be maintained) and Role-Prior Conflict (training-time knowledge overrides role instructions for factually unambiguous statements) - as manifestations of a single mechanism: Epistemic Role Override (ERO). Model choice significantly affects role fidelity: Mistral Large outperforms Claude Sonnet by 28pp (67% vs. 39%) and exhibits a qualitatively different failure mode - role abandonment without polarity reversal - compared to Claude's active switch to the opposing stance. Role fidelity is language-robust. Fact-check provider choice is not universally neutral: Perplexity significantly reduces Claude's role fidelity on German statements (Delta = -15pp, p = 0.007) while leaving Mistral unaffected. These findings have direct implications for multi-agent LLM validation: a system validated without role fidelity measurement may systematically misrepresent the epistemic diversity it was designed to provide.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether multi-agent LLM pipelines reliably maintain adversarial epistemic roles in evaluating political statements, introduces an epistemic stance classifier, and reveals robust yet model- and language-dependent role fidelity effects with practical implications for validation of such systems.
AI.motivation: To empirically test a core assumption of adversarial, multi-agent LLM pipelines: that models will faithfully maintain their assigned roles (epistemic stances) when assessing political content across languages and contexts.
AI.method: Develop an epistemic stance classifier to detect advocate roles from reasoning text without surface cues. Evaluate role fidelity across 60 political statements (30 English, 30 German) using four metrics (RDI, EDD, DDI, ERS). Compare two models (Mistral Large vs Claude Sonnet) and examine the impact of the fact-check provider via perplexity. Identify failure modes (Epistemic Floor Effect, Role-Prior Conflict) as Epistemic Role Override. Assess language robustness and provide cross-language analysis.
AI.result: Two failure modes culminate in Epistemic Role Override: the Epistemic Floor Effect imposes a lower bound on maintaining a legitimizing role due to fact-check results; Role-Prior Conflict occurs when training-time knowledge overrules role instructions on factually unambiguous statements. Model choice greatly impacts fidelity; Mistral Large outperforms Claude Sonnet by 28 percentage points (67% vs 39%) and exhibits role abandonment rather than polarity reversal. Fidelity is language-robust. Fact-check provider choice (perplexity) reduces Claude’s role fidelity on German statements (Delta = -15pp, p = 0.007) but not Mistral’s.
AI.conclusion: These findings imply that multi-agent LLM validation is incomplete without explicit role-fidelity assessment, as the epistemic diversity such systems aim to provide can be systematically misrepresented by role drift or overrides.

title: Flying by Inference: Active Inference World Models for Adaptive UAV Swarms
summary: This paper presents an expert-guided active-inference-inspired framework for adaptive UAV swarm trajectory planning. The proposed method converts multi-UAV trajectory design from a repeated combinatorial optimization problem into a hierarchical probabilistic inference problem. In the offline phase, a genetic-algorithm planner with repulsive-force collision avoidance (GA--RF) generates expert demonstrations, which are abstracted into Mission, Route, and Motion dictionaries. These dictionaries are used to learn a probabilistic world model that captures how expert mission allocations induce route orders and how route orders induce motion-level behaviors. During online operation, the UAV swarm evaluates candidate actions by forming posterior beliefs over symbolic states and minimizing KL-divergence-based abnormality indicators with respect to expert-derived reference distributions. This enables mission allocation, route insertion, motion adaptation, and collision-aware replanning without rerunning the offline optimizer. Bayesian state estimators, including EKF and PF modules, are integrated at the motion level to improve trajectory correction under uncertainty. Simulation results show that the proposed framework preserves expert-like planning structure while producing smoother and more stable behavior than modified Q-learning. Additional validation using real-flight UAV trajectory data demonstrates that the learned world model can correct symbolic predictions under noisy and non-smooth observations, supporting its applicability to adaptive UAV swarm autonomy.

AI.tldr: An expert-guided, active-inference framework for adaptive UAV swarm planning that converts offline expert demonstrations into a probabilistic world model and uses KL-divergence minimization for online decision making, yielding smoother, more stable behavior with validation on real-flight data.
AI.motivation: To overcome the combinatorial complexity of multi-UAV trajectory design and achieve adaptive, collision-aware planning that preserves expert structure while handling uncertainty in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Offline phase: a genetic-algorithm planner with repulsive-force collision avoidance (GA-RF) generates expert demonstrations, which are abstracted into Mission, Route, and Motion dictionaries. A probabilistic world model is learned to capture how expert mission allocations induce route orders and how route orders induce motion-level behaviors. Online phase: the swarm evaluates candidate actions by forming posterior beliefs over symbolic states and minimizing KL-divergence-based abnormality indicators with respect to expert-derived reference distributions, enabling mission allocation, route insertion, motion adaptation, and collision-aware replanning without rerunning the offline optimizer. Bayesian state estimators (EKF and PF) are integrated at the motion level for trajectory correction under uncertainty.
AI.result: Simulation results show the framework preserves expert-like planning structure while yielding smoother and more stable behavior than modified Q-learning. Validation with real-flight UAV trajectory data demonstrates that the learned world model can correct symbolic predictions under noisy and non-smooth observations, supporting its applicability to adaptive UAV swarm autonomy.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a viable active-inference approach for adaptive UAV swarm autonomy by embedding expert demonstrations into a probabilistic, online decision framework that handles uncertainty and collisions while maintaining expert-structured planning.

title: Hyperspectral Image Classification via Efficient Global Spectral Supertoken Clustering
comment: Accepted by ISPRS JPRS 2026. This manuscript version is made available under the CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0 license
summary: Hyperspectral image classification demands spatially coherent predictions and precise boundary delineation. Yet prevailing superpixel-based methods face an inherent contradiction: clustering aggregates similar pixels into regions, but the subsequent classifier operates pixel-wise, undermining regional consistency. Consequently, existing approaches do not guarantee region-level, boundary-aligned classification. To address this limitation, we propose the Dual-stage Spectrum-Constrained Clustering-based Classifier (DSCC), an end-to-end framework that explicitly decouples clustering from classification by first grouping spectral similar and spatially proximate pixels into spectral supertokens and then performing token-level prediction. At its core, DSCC computes an image-level multi-criteria feature distance between pixels and centers, followed by a locality-aware assignment regularization, enabling the generation of boundary-preserving spectral supertokens. A density-isolation based center selection further yields representative, well-separated centers, reducing redundancy and improving robustness to scale variation. To accommodate mixed land-cover compositions within each token, we introduce a soft-label scheme that encodes class proportions and improves robustness for mixed-class tokens. DSCC attains a CF1 of 0.728 at 197.75 FPS on the WHU-OHS dataset, offering a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared with state-of-the-art methods. Extensive experiments further validate the effectiveness and generality of the proposed dual-stage paradigm for hyperspectral image classification. The source code is available at https://github.com/laprf/DSCC.

AI.tldr: A dual-stage clustering-classifier (DSCC) that creates boundary-preserving spectral tokens and performs token-level prediction; demonstrates an accuracy-efficiency trade-off with CF1 = 0.728 at ~198 FPS on WHU-OHS, with code released.
AI.motivation: Existing hyperspectral methods rely on region-based clustering followed by pixel-wise classification, which conflicts: clustering aggregates regions but classifiers operate per pixel, yielding weak region-level, boundary-aligned predictions. A method that decouples clustering from classification and preserves boundaries is needed, including robustness to scale and mixed land-cover.
AI.method: DSCC is a two-stage framework that first groups spectral-similar and spatially proximate pixels into spectral supertokens using an image-level multi-criteria distance and locality-aware assignment regularization, plus density-isolation center selection to yield representative centers. In the second stage, token-level predictions are made, aided by a soft-label scheme encoding class proportions to handle mixed-class tokens. The approach decouples clustering from classification while enabling end-to-end training dynamics and boundary-preserving tokens, aiming for improved region coherence and robustness across scales.
AI.result: On WHU-OHS, DSCC achieves CF1 = 0.728 at 197.75 FPS, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Extensive experiments validate the dual-stage paradigm's effectiveness and generality. Source code is available at https://github.com/laprf/DSCC.
AI.conclusion: The dual-stage DSCC paradigm effectively addresses the region-level, boundary-aligned demand in hyperspectral classification by constructing spectral tokens that respect boundaries and class proportions, achieving strong performance and robustness with scalable token-based prediction.

title: Better Models, Faster Training: Sigmoid Attention for single-cell Foundation Models
summary: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms: we find that using sigmoid attention as a drop in replacement for softmax attention a) produces better learned representations: on six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss, b) faster training, models with sigmoid attention train up to 10% faster than their softmax counterparts, and c) more stable training by eliminating inherent sources of instability in softmax attention. We establish that sigmoid attention has globally bounded derivatives ($\leq 0.25$) as opposed to softmax, and a diagonal Jacobian structure in contrast with softmax's dense coupling, which together help alleviate training instabilities. In stress tests on 160M-parameter bidirectional attention models trained without gradient clipping on 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges catastrophically, with gradients exploding by four orders of magnitude, while sigmoid remains stable. Finally, we implement and open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel that achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming both FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid, with native padding support, which is essential for biological sequences. Our results establish sigmoid attention as both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/MSDLLCpapers/triton-sigmoid

AI.tldr: Sigmoid attention replaces softmax in biological foundation models, yielding better representations, faster training, and stability; supported by theoretical bounded derivatives and diagonal Jacobian, with a high-performance Triton kernel and open-source code.
AI.motivation: Softmax-based attention in large biological sequence models can be unstable and computationally demanding; the paper investigates sigmoid attention as a drop-in replacement and its theoretical/empirical benefits.
AI.method: Replace softmax with sigmoid attention in bidirectional attention; analyze derivatives and Jacobian; evaluate on six single-cell datasets for representation quality; stress-test 160M-parameter models on 8K-token sequences without gradient clipping; implement TritonSigmoid kernel; benchmark against FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid; provide open-source code.
AI.result: Sigmoid attention achieves ~25% higher cell-type separation, better cohesion, and lower validation loss; training speed up to 10%; more stable training with bounded derivatives (≤0.25) and diagonal Jacobian; softmax diverges on long sequences with exploding gradients, sigmoid remains stable; TritonSigmoid delivers 515 TFLOPS on H100 and outperforms rivals with padding support.
AI.conclusion: Sigmoid attention is both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models; its adoption could improve stability, efficiency, and performance; code available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Reinforced Agent: Inference-Time Feedback for Tool-Calling Agents
summary:
Tool-calling agents are evaluated on tool selection, parameter accuracy, and scope recognition, yet LLM trajectory assessments remain inherently post-hoc. Disconnected from the active execution loop, such assessments identify errors that are usually addressed through prompt-tuning or retraining, and fundamentally cannot course-correct the agent in real time. To close this gap, we move evaluation into the execution loop at inference time: a specialized reviewer agent evaluates provisional tool calls prior to execution, shifting the paradigm from post-hoc recovery to proactive evaluation and error mitigation.
  In practice, this architecture establishes a clear separation of concerns between the primary execution agent and a secondary review agent. As with any multi-agent system, the reviewer can introduce new errors while correcting others, yet no prior work to our knowledge has systematically measured this tradeoff. To quantify this tradeoff, we introduce Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics: helpfulness measures the percentage of base agent errors that feedback corrects; harmfulness measures the percentage of correct responses that feedback degrades. These metrics directly inform reviewer design by revealing whether a given model or prompt provides net positive value.
  We evaluate our approach on BFCL (single-turn) and Tau2-Bench (multi-turn stateful scenarios), achieving +5.5% on irrelevance detection and +7.1% on multi-turn tasks. Our metrics reveal that reviewer model choice is critical: the reasoning model o3-mini achieves a 3:1 benefit-to-risk ratio versus 2.1:1 for GPT-4o. Automated prompt optimization via GEPA provides an additional +1.5-2.8%. Together, these results demonstrate a core advantage of separating execution and review: the reviewer can be systematically improved through model selection and prompt optimization, without retraining the base agent.

AI.tldr: A proactive reviewer agent evaluates provisional tool calls during inference, enabling real-time error mitigation and reducing post-hoc corrections, with quantified tradeoffs via Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics.
AI.motivation: Close the gap between evaluation and execution. Traditional post-hoc trajectory assessments cannot correct in real time; integrating a reviewer into the execution loop aims to proactively reduce errors and improve tool usage without retraining the base agent.
AI.method: Introduce a secondary reviewer agent that pre-validates provisional tool calls before execution. Define Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics to measure the reviewer's net value. Evaluate on BFCL (single-turn) and Tau2-Bench (multi-turn) with comparisons across reviewer models (e.g., o3-mini vs GPT-4o) and automated prompt optimization via GEPA.
AI.result: Reviewer-enabled evaluation achieved +5.5% improvement in irrelevance detection and +7.1% on multi-turn tasks. Reviewer model choice strongly affects performance (o3-mini yields 3:1 benefit-to-risk vs 2.1:1 for GPT-4o). GEPA-based automated prompt optimization adds an additional +1.5–2.8%. Demonstrates that separating execution and review allows systematic improvement through model selection and prompt tuning without retraining the base agent.
AI.conclusion: Separating execution and review provides a practical, scalable path to proactive error mitigation. Reviewers can be iteratively improved via model choice and prompts, avoiding retraining the primary agent, though the reviewer can introduce new errors that require careful design and measurement.

title: Dreaming Across Towns: Semantic Rollout and Town-Adversarial Regularization for Zero-Shot Held-Out-Town Fixed-Route Driving in CARLA
summary: Learned driving agents often degrade when deployed in unseen environments. This paper studies a deliberately bounded instance of that problem in the CARLA simulator: zero-shot transfer of a closed-loop fixed-route driving agent from Town05 and Town06 to unseen Town03 and Town04. The study isolates structural town shift by keeping weather fixed to ClearNoon and removing traffic and pedestrians. We build on a Dreamer-style latent world-model agent and add two training-only auxiliary losses: multi-horizon prediction of future visual-semantic embeddings along imagined rollouts and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the recurrent latent state. A causal context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor, while the actor and critic retain the standard control feature. The policy receives no navigation command, route polyline, goal pose, or map input; the reference route is used only by the environment for reward, progress, success, and termination. Across the evaluated held-out towns, the proposed model achieves the highest mean success rate among the included Dreamer-family methods. Secondary safety and lane-keeping metrics are mixed across towns. These results support a bounded conclusion: in this controlled fixed-weather CARLA setting, semantic rollout supervision combined with town-adversarial regularization improves mean held-out-town route completion.

AI.tldr: In a controlled CARLA setup with fixed weather and no traffic, the paper improves zero-shot transfer of a Dreamer-style driving agent by adding two training-time losses—multi-horizon future visual-semantic embeddings and town-adversarial supervision on latent semantics—leading to the best mean held-out-town route completion among Dreamer variants, though safety metrics vary by town.
AI.motivation: To study structural generalization gaps in learned driving agents by isolating town shift (architectural/environment changes) under bounded conditions, and to assess whether latent world-models with auxiliary losses can improve zero-shot transfer to unseen towns.
AI.method: Use a Dreamer-style latent world-model agent. Introduce two training-only auxiliary losses: (1) multi-horizon prediction of future visual-semantic embeddings along imagined rollouts, and (2) town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the recurrent latent state. A causal context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor; the actor/critic use standard control features. The policy receives no navigation commands or map inputs; the reference route is used only by the environment for reward signals. The evaluation tests zero-shot transfer from Town05/06 to Town03/04 under fixed weather (ClearNoon) with no traffic/pedestrians.
AI.result: On held-out towns, the model achieves the highest mean success rate among Dreamer-family methods. Safety and lane-keeping metrics show mixed results across towns.
AI.conclusion: Semantic rollout supervision combined with town-adversarial regularization yields improved mean held-out-town route completion in the constrained CARLA setting; the approach demonstrates bounded generalization gains under fixed weather and traffic-free conditions.

title: Judge, Then Drive: A Critic-Centric Vision Language Action Framework for Autonomous Driving
summary: Recent advances in vision language action (VLA) models have shown remarkable potential for autonomous driving by directly mapping multimodal inputs to control signals. However, previous VLA-based methods have not explicitly exploited the critic capability of VLAs to refine driving decisions, even though such capability has been well demonstrated in other LLM-based domains, thereby limiting their performance in complex closed-loop scenarios. In this work, we present a theoretically inspired two-stage framework, CriticVLA, which extends the role of VLAs from acting to judging. CriticVLA first generates a rough trajectory and then refines it through multimodal evaluation and single-step optimization guided by a VLA-based critic, yielding higher-quality driving behaviors. To support this process, we construct a large-scale synthetic dataset of 12.9 million annotated trajectories covering diverse driving scenarios, which enhances the critic's reasoning and refinement abilities. Extensive closed-loop experiments on the Bench2Drive benchmark show that CriticVLA significantly surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 73.33% total success rate and delivering about 30% improvement in challenging scenarios.

AI.tldr: Two-stage CriticVLA uses a VLA-based critic to judge and refine initial trajectories for autonomous driving, backed by a large synthetic dataset, achieving state-of-the-art performance on Bench2Drive.
AI.motivation: Limitations of prior VLA-based driving models lacking explicit critic/refinement in closed-loop control; need improved reasoning and data to handle complex driving scenarios.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: rough trajectory generation followed by multimodal evaluation and single-step optimization guided by a VLA critic; training on a 12.9M synthetic annotated trajectory dataset.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over baselines: 73.33% total success rate; ~30% improvement in challenging scenarios on Bench2Drive.
AI.conclusion: Introducing a critic module into VLA for autonomous driving enhances decision quality and closed-loop control; synthetic data scales reasoning capabilities and yields robust driving behavior.

title: How to Guide Your Flow: Few-Step Alignment via Flow Map Reward Guidance
summary: In generative modeling, we often wish to produce samples that maximize a user-specified reward such as aesthetic quality or alignment with human preferences, a problem known as guidance. Despite their widespread use, existing guidance methods either require expensive multi-particle, many-step schemes or rely on poorly understood approximations. We reformulate guidance as a deterministic optimal control problem, yielding a hierarchy of algorithms that subsumes existing approaches at the coarsest level. We show that the flow map, an object of significant recent interest for its role in fast inference, arises naturally in the optimal solution. Based on this observation, we propose Flow Map Reward Guidance (FMRG): a training-free, single-trajectory framework that uses the flow map to both integrate and guide the flow. At text-to-image scale, FMRG matches or surpasses baselines across inverse problems, style transfer, human preferences, and VLM rewards with as few as 3 NFEs, giving at least an order-of-magnitude speedup in comparison to prior state of the art.

AI.tldr: The paper reformulates guidance in generative modeling as a deterministic optimal control problem and introduces Flow Map Reward Guidance (FMRG), a training-free, single-trajectory method that leverages flow maps. It achieves strong performance with very few NFEs and substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Guidance methods for aligning generative models to rewards are costly (multi-particle, multi-step) or rely on opaque approximations. A principled, efficient alternative is needed.
AI.method: Formulate guidance as a deterministic optimal control problem, deriving a hierarchy of algorithms. Highlight the flow map as a natural object in the optimal solution and introduce FMRG, which uses the flow map to integrate and steer the flow within a single trajectory without training.
AI.result: FMRG matches or surpasses baselines on text-to-image tasks across inverse problems, style transfer, human preferences, and VLM rewards, using as few as 3 NFEs, and offers at least an order-of-magnitude speedup over prior state of the art.
AI.conclusion: Flow Map Reward Guidance provides a unifying, training-free, single-trajectory framework that leverages the flow map for efficient and effective guidance in generative modeling, with strong empirical performance and significant computational savings.

title: AutoSurfer -- Teaching Web Agents through Comprehensive Surfing, Learning, and Modeling
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized web agents that can automate complex tasks on websites. However, their accuracy remains limited by the scarcity of high-quality web trajectory training data. Existing automatic trajectory generation methods suffer from incomplete website coverage due to homepage-based task proposals or random-walk exploration. Such methods often result in hallucinated or ambiguous task synthesis that lead to incomplete and unreliable trajectory generation. Here, we present AutoSurfer, a comprehensive web trajectory generator that addresses these limitations through three key innovations. First, AutoSurfer employs a systematic breadth-first exploration strategy that maintains a queue of discovered pages and action traces, propagates knowledge across pages to avoid redundant exploration, and recursively expands multi-level graphical user interface elements - closely resembling how a human would learn a new website. Second, AutoSurfer leverages the exploration trajectory to guide task synthesis, reducing hallucinations by grounding complex tasks in actual navigation paths rather than isolated actions or page content alone. Third, AutoSurfer uses the same exploration trajectory as hints to steer a web agent toward more accurate and reliable trajectory refinement. Together, these innovations enable AutoSurfer to comprehensively cover a website's action space and generate data suitable for training website-specific LLMs. We evaluate AutoSurfer on the WebArena benchmark by fine-tuning Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct and demonstrate that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods - Explorer, OS-Genesis, and SynthAgent - achieving up to 24.23% overall task completion accuracy compared to 19.59% for the best prior method. Further, task diversity analysis demonstrates that AutoSurfer yields a more diverse distribution of synthesized tasks.

AI.tldr: AutoSurfer introduces a systematic breadth-first web trajectory generator to comprehensively cover a website's action space, guiding task synthesis and refinement to improve training data for website-specific LLMs; it achieves higher task completion and diversity than prior methods on WebArena.
AI.motivation: High-quality, comprehensive web trajectories are scarce. Existing automatic methods rely on homepage proposals or random walks, leading to incomplete coverage and hallucinated task synthesis, which hampers training of web-enabled LLMs.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) breadth-first exploration with a queue of pages and action traces, knowledge propagation across pages to reduce redundant exploration, and multi-level GUI element expansion; (2) using exploration trajectories to ground task synthesis, reducing hallucinations by tying tasks to real navigation paths; (3) using the same trajectories as hints to steer web agent refinement, improving accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the WebArena benchmark with fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct shows AutoSurfer outperforms state-of-the-art methods (Explorer, OS-Genesis, SynthAgent) with up to 24.23% overall task completion accuracy (vs 19.59% prior) and yields a more diverse distribution of generated tasks.
AI.conclusion: AutoSurfer provides a more comprehensive and reliable data-generation pipeline for website-specific LLM training by (i) exhaustively exploring websites, (ii) grounding task synthesis in real navigation, and (iii) leveraging exploration trajectories for refinement, leading to improved task completion and diversity.

title: Framework for Collaborative Operation of Autonomous Delivery Vehicles Within a Marshaling Yard
summary: As autonomous vehicles slowly deploy into urban roads for limited use cases with significant edge case issues, closed facilities like marshaling yards provide a ripe case for combining lower-level vehicle autonomy with fixed infrastructure to create full autonomy without similar edge case concerns. Within a delivery marshaling yard, electric fleet vehicles complete a set of sequential tasks (charging, inspection, cleaning, and loading) before exiting the yard with their new load of deliveries. Hybrid automation of the vehicles and infrastructure can allow these vehicles to reach full autonomy and navigate the facility without the need of a driver, allowing for quicker movement between tasks increasing vehicle throughput. However, isolated autonomous operations based on static rules are prone to gridlock causing facility failures that temporarily shut down operations. Our orchestrated autonomy solution uses decentralized, dynamic priority scoring of vehicles based on the current status of the marshaling yard to optimally assign vehicles to tasks to increase vehicle throughput. Using a simulated facility with three marshaling yard sizes (small, medium, and large) and three demand levels (low, medium, high), we demonstrated that our orchestration solution increases vehicle throughput above static, isolated autonomy for all combinations of yard size and demand, while reducing facility failures at high demand levels.

AI.tldr: An orchestration framework with decentralized dynamic priority scoring improves throughput and reduces failures in marshaling yard operations, outperforming static, isolated autonomy across multiple yard sizes and demand levels in simulation.
AI.motivation: Autonomous vehicle deployment faces edge-case failures in urban settings; marshaling yards offer a controlled environment to achieve higher autonomy by combining vehicle automation with fixed infrastructure. Static, rule-based autonomous systems risk gridlock; a dynamic, decentralized scheduler can optimize task assignments based on real-time yard status to maximize throughput and reliability.
AI.method: Simulated marshaling yard scenarios using three yard sizes (small, medium, large) and three demand levels (low, medium, high). Implemented a decentralized priority-scoring orchestrator that assigns vehicles to tasks (charging, inspection, cleaning, loading) based on current yard state. Compared performance against static, isolated autonomy (no orchestration). Measured vehicle throughput and facility failure rates.
AI.result: The orchestrated autonomy solution achieved higher vehicle throughput than static autonomy across all yard sizes and demand levels. It also reduced facility failures at high demand levels, indicating improved resilience and efficiency in the simulated environment.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, decentralized priority-based orchestration of autonomous vehicles in marshaling yards enhances throughput and reduces failures, demonstrating scalable benefits across facility sizes and demand regimes and supporting progression toward higher levels of automation in controlled environments.

title: VeraRetouch: A Lightweight Fully Differentiable Framework for Multi-Task Reasoning Photo Retouching
summary: Reasoning photo retouching has gained significant traction, requiring models to analyze image defects, give reasoning processes, and execute precise retouching enhancements. However, existing approaches often rely on non-differentiable external software, creating optimization barriers and suffering from high parameter redundancy and limited generalization. To address these challenges, we propose VeraRetouch, a lightweight and fully differentiable framework for multi-task photo retouching. We employ a 0.5B Vision-Language Model (VLM) as the central intelligence to formulate retouching plans based on instructions and scene semantics. Furthermore, we develop a fully differentiable Retouch Renderer that replaces external tools, enabling direct end-to-end pixel-level training through decoupled control latents for lighting, global color, and specific color adjustments. To overcome data scarcity, we introduce AetherRetouch-1M+, the first million-scale dataset for professional retouching, constructed via a new inverse degradation workflow. Furthermore, we propose DAPO-AE, a reinforcement learning post-training strategy that enhances autonomous aesthetic cognition. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VeraRetouch achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks while maintaining a significantly smaller footprint, enabling mobile deployment. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/OpenVeraTeam/VeraRetouch.

AI.tldr: VeraRetouch: a compact, fully differentiable, multi-task photo retouching framework powered by a 0.5B Vision-Language Model as planner, with a differentiable Retouch Renderer, a 1M+ dataset, and RL post-training; achieves state-of-the-art results with a mobile-friendly footprint; code released.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on non-differentiable external software that creates optimization barriers, high parameter redundancy, and limited generalization in existing photo retouching methods; aim for end-to-end differentiable, plan-and-execute retouching.
AI.method: Key components: (1) a 0.5B Vision-Language Model as the central planning intelligence to formulate retouching plans from instructions and scene semantics; (2) a fully differentiable Retouch Renderer that replaces external tools, enabling end-to-end pixel-level training with decoupled control latents for lighting, global color, and specific color adjustments; (3) AetherRetouch-1M+ dataset (million-scale) created via an inverse degradation workflow to address data scarcity; (4) DAPO-AE, a reinforcement learning post-training strategy to enhance autonomous aesthetic cognition.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows VeraRetouch achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks with a significantly smaller footprint, enabling mobile deployment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework delivers an end-to-end differentiable, multi-task photo retouching solution with strong performance and efficiency; the accompanying large-scale dataset and RL post-training strategy help address data scarcity and aesthetic cognition challenges; code and models are publicly available.

title: ConformaDecompose: Explaining Uncertainty via Calibration Localization
comment: This is the accepted author version of a paper to appear in the proceedings of the World Explainable AI Conference (Springer). The final version will be available via Springer. This manuscript introduces ConformaDecompose, a framework for instance-wise uncertainty explainability via calibration localisation
summary: Conformal Prediction provides distribution-free prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage, but its reliance on a single global calibration threshold obscures the sources of uncertainty at the instance level. In particular, it conflates irreducible noise with uncertainty induced by heterogeneous training data (aleatoric), model limitations, or calibration mismatch (epistemic), offering little insight into why an interval is wide or whether it could be reduced. We introduce an uncertainty-aware explainability framework that analyses the reducibility of calibration-induced epistemic conformal uncertainty via progressive calibration localisation for regression tasks. The approach is diagnostic rather than causal: it does not estimate true aleatoric or epistemic uncertainty, but explains how conformal intervals contract and stabilise as calibration support is localised around a test instance. Across benchmarks and real-world data, absolute reducible uncertainty aligns with epistemic proxies, while its relative contribution varies by task, revealing regimes hidden by interval width. This instance-level view complements conformal uncertainty, enhancing interpretability without altering the predictor or coverage.

AI.tldr: Introduces an uncertainty-aware explainability framework for conformal prediction that localizes calibration around a test instance to quantify reducible (epistemic) conformal uncertainty, offering instance-level insight without altering predictor or coverage.
AI.motivation: Conformal prediction provides distribution-free prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage but relies on a single global calibration threshold, conflating irreducible noise with epistemic, aleatoric, or calibration-mismatch uncertainty and offering limited insight into why intervals are wide or reducible.
AI.method: Progressive calibration localisation for regression: analyzes how conformal intervals contract as calibration support is localized around a test instance; assesses absolute and relative reducible uncertainty; benchmarks across real data to relate reducible uncertainty to epistemic proxies; the framework is diagnostic, not causal, and does not estimate true aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty.
AI.result: Absolute reducible uncertainty aligns with epistemic proxies; the relative contribution of reducible uncertainty varies by task, revealing regimes hidden by interval width; the approach complements conformal uncertainty and does not require changing the predictor or the coverage guarantees.
AI.conclusion: An instance-level diagnostic tool that explains how calibration-induced epistemic conformal uncertainty can be reduced via local calibration, enhancing interpretability of conformal prediction without affecting its guarantees.

title: OptimusKG: Unifying biomedical knowledge in a modern multimodal graph
summary: Biomedical knowledge graphs (KGs) are widely used in the life sciences, yet many are derived from unstructured documents and therefore lack schema-level constrains, whereas graphs assembled from structured resources are difficult to harmonize into a unified representation. We present OptimusKG, a multimodal biomedical labeled property graph (LPG) built from structured and semi-structured resources to preserve factual, type-specific metadata across molecular, anatomical, clinical, and environmental domains. OptimusKG contains 190,531 nodes across 10 entity types, 21,813,816 edges across 26 relation types, and 67,249,863 property instances encoding 110,276,843 values across 150 distinct property keys, derived from 18 ontologies and controlled vocabularies. The graph enforces a top-level schema for nodes and edges and retains granular, type-specific properties, cross-references, and provenance across molecular, anatomical, clinical, and environmental domains. We assessed the validity of OptimusKG by evaluating whether graph relationships are supported by evidence from the scientific literature using a multimodal agent, PaperQA3. PaperQA3 identified supporting evidence for 70.0% of sampled edges, whereas 83.4% of sampled false edges received no supporting evidence. Edges without literature support were concentrated in associations derived from experimental and functional genomics resources, suggesting that OptimusKG captures biomedical knowledge that may precede synthesis in the scientific literature. OptimusKG is distributed as Apache Parquet files, providing a standardized resource for graph-based machine learning, knowledge-grounded retrieval with large language models, and biomedical discovery use cases such as hypothesis generation.

AI.tldr: OptimusKG is a multimodal biomedical labeled property graph with a defined schema, large-scale cross-domain data, and literature-backed validation, enabling ML and hypothesis-generation tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of schema-level constraints in KGs derived from unstructured sources and the harmonization challenges of graph resources by creating a unified, schema-enforced LPG for biomedical domains (molecular, anatomical, clinical, environmental).
AI.method: Construct OptimusKG as a multimodal labeled property graph sourced from structured and semi-structured resources. Enforce a top-level schema for node/edge types, retain granular type-specific properties, cross-references, and provenance across domains, derive from 18 ontologies and vocabularies, and populate 190,531 nodes, 21,813,816 edges, 67,249,863 property instances across 150 property keys from 26 relation types. Validate relationships using a multimodal agent (PaperQA3). Distribute as Apache Parquet for ML and retrieval workflows.
AI.result: PaperQA3 found supporting evidence for 70.0% of sampled edges, while 83.4% of sampled false edges had no literature support, suggesting some relationships may precede documentation. The graph captures provenance and cross-domain metadata and is intended for graph ML, knowledge-grounded retrieval with LLMs, and discovery tasks.
AI.conclusion: OptimusKG provides a standardized, schema-enforced biomedical LPG suitable for machine learning, knowledge-grounded retrieval, and hypothesis generation, with broad domain coverage and provenance tracking across molecular, anatomical, clinical, and environmental data.

title: GSDrive: Reinforcing Driving Policies by Multi-mode Trajectory Probing with 3D Gaussian Splatting Environment
summary: End-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving presents a promising approach for translating perceptual inputs directly into driving actions. However, prohibitive annotation costs and temporal data quality degradation hinder long-term real-world deployment. While combining imitation learning (IL) and reinforcement learning (RL) is a common strategy for policy improvement, conventional RL training relies on delayed, event-based rewards-policies learn only from catastrophic outcomes such as collisions, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal behaviors. To address these limitations, we introduce GSDrive, a framework that exploits 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for differentiable, physics-based reward shaping in E2E driving policy improvement. Our method incorporates a flow matching-based trajectory predictor within the 3DGS simulator, enabling multi-mode trajectory probing where candidate trajectories are rolled out to assess prospective rewards. This establishes a bidirectional knowledge exchange between IL and RL by grounding reward functions in physically simulated interaction signals, offering immediate dense feedback instead of sparse catastrophic events. Evaluated on the reconstructed nuScenes dataset, our method surpasses existing simulation-based RL driving approaches in closed-loop experiments. Code is available at https://github.com/ZionGo6/GSDrive.

AI.tldr: GSDrive uses 3D Gaussian Splatting to provide differentiable, physics-based reward shaping for end-to-end driving, pairing a flow-matching trajectory predictor with a differentiable simulator to enable dense feedback and bidirectional IL-RL training; it outperforms existing simulation-based RL on nuScenes in closed-loop tests. Code available.
AI.motivation: Address annotation costs and data quality degradation, and overcome sparse, delayed RL rewards by grounding rewards in differentiable physics signals to improve sample efficiency and policy quality in end-to-end autonomous driving.
AI.method: Introduce GSDrive framework with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS); incorporate flow-matching trajectory predictor; enable multi-mode trajectory probing; roll out candidate trajectories to estimate rewards; facilitate bidirectional IL-RL exchange by grounding reward signals in physically simulated interactions.
AI.result: Outperforms existing simulation-based RL driving approaches in closed-loop experiments on reconstructed nuScenes; code released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of physics-based differentiable reward shaping and IL-RL integration for end-to-end driving, suggesting improved long-term deployment with reduced annotation reliance.

title: COHERENCE: Benchmarking Fine-Grained Image-Text Alignment in Interleaved Multimodal Contexts
summary: In recent years, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress on a wide range of multimodal benchmarks. Despite these advances, most existing benchmarks mainly focus on single-image or multi-image comprehension. In real-world scenarios such as document reading, information is often presented as interleaved multimodel contexts. This requires MLLMs not only to recognize the content of individual images, but also to identify relevant textual and visual evidence, establish fine-grained alignments between them, and reason over these aligned signals in interleaved contexts based on contextual evidence. However, there is still a lack of systematic benchmarks for quantifying the fine-grained understanding ability of MLLMs in interleaved image-text contexts. To fill this gap, we propose COHERENCE, a benchmark designed to evaluate the ability of MLLMs to recover fine-grained image-text correspondences in interleaved multimodal contexts. COHERENCE covers interleaved image-text content from four representative domains and contains 6,161 high-quality questions. Moreover, we perform a six-type error analysis, enabling fine-grained attribution of failures in interleaved image-text understanding to the specific capabilities missing in current MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A benchmark (COHERENCE) evaluates MLLMs’ ability to recover fine-grained image-text correspondences in interleaved multimodal contexts, with 6,161 questions across four domains and a six-type error analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks largely assess single-image or multi-image understanding; real-world tasks (e.g., document reading) require interleaved multimodal reasoning with precise alignment of textual and visual evidence, which current MLLMs struggle to diagnose.
AI.method: Construct and curate interleaved image-text data across four domains, design questions that require identifying and aligning textual and visual evidence, collect 6,161 high-quality questions, and perform a six-type error analysis to attribute failures to specific missing capabilities in current MLLMs.
AI.result: Introduction of the COHERENCE benchmark capable of evaluating fine-grained interleaved image-text understanding and providing an error taxonomy for diagnosing model weaknesses.
AI.conclusion: COHERENCE fills a gap by enabling systematic assessment and diagnostic analysis of MLLMs’ performance on interleaved image-text comprehension, across diverse domains.

title: Generalizing the Geometry of Model Merging Through Frechet Averages
summary: Model merging aims to combine multiple models into one without additional training. Naïve parameter-space averaging can be fragile under architectural symmetries, as their geometry does not take them into account. In this work we show that not only the geometry, but also the averaging procedure itself, must be symmetry-invariant to achieve symmetry-aware merges. Consequently, we propose a general solution: merging as Fréchet averaging, i.e., selecting parameters that minimize a sum of geodesic distances on an appropriate manifold. In this view, the key design choice is the overall geometry, i.e., the choice of metric, manifold, and distance approximation, that determines what it means for two models to be "close". We show that Fréchet averaging, combined with simplifying assumptions, contains Fisher merging. Building on this, we examine the particular case of low-rank adapters (LoRA), whose symmetries induce a distinct geometry: that of a quotient manifold. We outline the limitations of current LoRA merging methods, propose a practical algorithm for this setting, and show how they compare with other commonly used approaches.

AI.tldr: Symmetry-aware model merging via Fréchet means on appropriate manifolds; introduces LoRA-specific quotient-manifold merging and a practical algorithm with comparisons to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Naïve parameter-space averaging can fail due to architectural symmetries; a symmetry-invariant merging criterion is needed that respects the underlying geometry of parameter space.
AI.method: Frame merging as the Fréchet mean: minimize a sum of geodesic distances on a chosen parameter manifold. The geometry (metric, manifold, distance approximation) determines model closeness. Show that Fréchet averaging subsumes Fisher merging under simplifying assumptions. For LoRA, treat the symmetry-induced geometry as a quotient manifold; derive a practical LoRA-merging algorithm and relate it to other approaches.
AI.result: Provides a unified, geometry-aware framework for model merging; identifies that LoRA merging requires quotient-manifold treatment due to symmetries; presents a practical algorithm and comparative insights against standard approaches, and outlines limitations of current LoRA merging methods.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry-aware merging via Fréchet averaging is principled; the choice of geometry is central to what is considered “close” between models. LoRA merging requires quotient-manifold considerations, and the proposed algorithm offers a practical, principled approach while clarifying limitations of existing methods.

title: The Inverse-Wisdom Law: Architectural Tribalism and the Consensus Paradox in Agentic Swarms
summary: As AI transitions toward multi-agent systems (MAS) to solve complex workflows, research paradigms operate on the axiomatic assumption that agent collaboration mirrors the "Wisdom of the Crowd". We challenge this assumption by formalizing the Consensus Paradox: a phenomenon where agentic swarms prioritize internal architectural agreement over external logical truth. Through a 36 experiments encompassing 12,804 trajectories across three state-of-the-art (SOTA) benchmarks (GAIA, Multi-Challenge, and SWE-bench), we prove the Inverse-Wisdom Law: in kinship-dominant swarms, adding logical agents increases the stability of erroneous trajectories rather than the probability of truth. The introduction of additional logical audits converges the system toward a Logic Saturation where internal entropy hits zero while factual error hits unity. By evaluating the interaction between the 3 preeminent SOTA models (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and GPT-5.4), we establish the Architectural Tribalism Asymmetry as a mechanistic law of transformer weights. We demonstrate that terminal swarm integrity is strictly gated by the synthesizer's receptive logic, rather than aggregate agent quality. We define the Tribalism Coefficient and the Sycophantic Weight as the primary mechanistic determinants of swarm failure. Finally, we establish the Heterogeneity Mandate as a foundational safety requirement for resilient agentic architectures.

AI.tldr: In kinship-dominant swarms, adding logical agents increases stability of wrong trajectories; introduces the Inverse-Wisdom Law, Logic Saturation, and Architectural Tribalism Asymmetry; defines Tribalism Coefficient and Sycophantic Weight; proposes Heterogeneity Mandate as a safety requirement; validated via 36 experiments (12,804 trajectories) on GAIA, Multi-Challenge, SWE-bench, using Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and GPT-5.4.
AI.motivation: Question the assumption that agent collaboration yields external truth (Wisdom of the Crowd). Formalize the Consensus Paradox to reveal how internal architectural agreement can override external logical correctness, with safety implications for multi-agent and transformer-based systems.
AI.method: Empirical study comprising 36 experiments totaling 12,804 trajectories across three state-of-the-art benchmarks (GAIA, Multi-Challenge, SWE-bench). Evaluate interactions among three SOTA models (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.4). Introduce and measure metrics: Logic Saturation, Tribalism Coefficient, Sycophantic Weight; analyze transformer-weight dynamics and kinship-dominant swarms.
AI.result: Observed Inverse-Wisdom Law: adding logical agents increases stability of erroneous trajectories. Logic Saturation occurs (internal entropy -> 0; factual error -> 1). Architectural Tribalism Asymmetry identified as a law of transformer weights. Terminal swarm integrity gated by synthesizer’s receptive logic, not aggregate agent quality. Defined Tribalism Coefficient and Sycophantic Weight as key failure determinants. Heterogeneity Mandate proposed as safety prerequisite for resilience.
AI.conclusion: Caution against overreliance on internal consensus in MAS. Recommend incorporating heterogeneity and safety constraints (Heterogeneity Mandate) to ensure resilience and preserve truth-seeking behavior. Further research needed on mitigating Tribalism and Sycophancy in large-scale transformer ensembles.

title: FreeOcc: Training-Free Embodied Open-Vocabulary Occupancy Prediction
summary: Existing learning-based occupancy prediction methods rely on large-scale 3D annotations and generalize poorly across environments. We present FreeOcc, a training-free framework for open-vocabulary occupancy prediction from monocular or RGB-D sequences. Unlike prior approaches that require voxel-level supervision and ground-truth camera poses, FreeOcc operates without 3D annotations, pose ground truth, or any learning stage. FreeOcc incrementally builds a globally consistent occupancy map via a four-layer pipeline: a SLAM backbone estimates poses and sparse geometry; a geometrically consistent Gaussian update constructs dense 3D Gaussian maps; open-vocabulary semantics from off-the-shelf vision-language models are associated with Gaussian primitives; and a probabilistic Gaussian-to-occupancy projection produces dense voxel occupancy. Despite being entirely training-free and pose-agnostic, FreeOcc achieves over $2\times$ improvements in IoU and mIoU on EmbodiedOcc-ScanNet compared to prior self-supervised methods. We further introduce ReplicaOcc, a benchmark for indoor open-vocabulary occupancy prediction, and show that FreeOcc transfers zero-shot to novel environments, substantially outperforming both supervised and self-supervised baselines. Project page: https://the-masses.github.io/freeocc-web/.

AI.tldr: FreeOcc is a completely training-free framework that builds an open-vocabulary 3D occupancy map from monocular/RGB-D sequences using a SLAM-based geometric backbone, Gaussian priors, and vision-language semantics, achieving substantial improvements and zero-shot transfer; ReplicaOcc benchmark introduced.
AI.motivation: To overcome dependence on large-scale 3D annotations and pose ground-truth and to enable cross-environment generalization for occupancy prediction by leveraging SLAM, Gaussian-based 3D representations, and off-the-shelf VLMs without learning stages.
AI.method: Four-layer pipeline: 1) SLAM backbone estimates poses and sparse geometry; 2) geometrically consistent Gaussian update builds dense 3D Gaussian maps; 3) associate open-vocabulary semantics from vision-language models with Gaussian primitives; 4) probabilistic Gaussian-to-occupancy projection to produce dense voxel occupancy.
AI.result: On EmbodiedOcc-ScanNet, FreeOcc achieves >2x IoU and mIoU improvements over prior self-supervised methods; zero-shot transfer to novel environments; ReplicaOcc benchmark introduced to evaluate indoor open-vocabulary occupancy; FreeOcc outperforms both supervised and self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Training-free, pose-agnostic occupancy prediction with open-vocabulary semantics is viable and generalizable; promotes new benchmarking (ReplicaOcc) and strong zero-shot transfer.

title: Understanding Adversarial Transferability in Vision-Language Models for Autonomous Driving: A Cross-Architecture Analysis
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at SAE WCX 2026
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used in autonomous driving because they combine visual perception with language-based reasoning, supporting more interpretable decision-making, yet their robustness to physical adversarial attacks, especially whether such attacks transfer across different VLM architectures, is not well understood and poses a practical risk when attackers do not know which model a vehicle uses. We address this gap with a systematic cross-architecture study of adversarial transferability in VLM-based driving, evaluating three representative architectures (Dolphins, OmniDrive, and LeapVAD) using physically realizable patches placed on roadside infrastructure in both crosswalk and highway scenarios. Our transfer-matrix evaluation shows high cross-architecture effectiveness, with transfer rates of 73-91% (mean TR = 0.815 for crosswalk and 0.833 for highway) and sustained frame-level manipulation over 64.7-79.4% of the critical decision window even when patches are not optimized for the target model.

AI.tldr: Physically realizable patches on roadside infrastructure transfer robustly across three VLM-based driving models, achieving high cross-architecture transfer and substantial manipulation within critical decision windows in crosswalk and highway settings.
AI.motivation: VLMs enable interpretable perception for autonomous driving, but robustness to physical adversarial attacks is uncertain, especially cross-architecture transfer when attackers do not know the deployed model.
AI.method: Systematic cross-architecture study using three architectures (Dolphins, OmniDrive, LeapVAD) with physically realizable patches placed on roadside infrastructure in crosswalk and highway scenarios; transfer-matrix evaluation; patches not optimized for the target model.
AI.result: Transfer rates 73-91%; mean transfer rates TR = 0.815 (crosswalk) and 0.833 (highway); sustained frame-level manipulation in 64.7-79.4% of the critical decision window even without target-model-specific optimization.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial patches transfer effectively across VLM-based driving models, revealing cross-architecture vulnerability; emphasizes need for cross-architecture defense strategies and standardized evaluation.

title: Distributional Alignment Games for Answer-Level Fine-Tuning
summary: We focus on the problem of \emph{Answer-Level Fine-Tuning} (ALFT), where the goal is to optimize a language model based on the correctness or properties of its final answers, rather than the specific reasoning traces used to produce them. Directly optimizing answer-level objectives is computationally intractable due to the need to marginalize over the vast space of latent reasoning paths. To overcome this, we propose a general game-theoretical framework that lifts the problem to a \emph{Distributional Alignment Game}. We formulate ALFT as a two-player game between a Policy (the generator) and a Target (an auxiliary distribution). We prove that the Nash Equilibrium of this game corresponds exactly to the solution of the original answer-level optimization problem. This variational perspective transforms the intractable marginalization problem into a tractable projection problem. We demonstrate that this framework unifies recent approaches to diversity and self-improvement (coherence) and provide efficient algorithms compatible with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), such as Coherence-GRPO, yielding significant complexity gains in mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: A distributional-alignment game for Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT) that makes optimizing final answers tractable by equating the Nash Equilibrium of a Policy–Target game with the original objective; enables efficient Coherence-GRPO-based algorithms and shows gains in math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Directly optimizing answer-level objectives is computationally intractable due to marginalization over latent reasoning paths and reasoning traces; a tractable framework is needed to improve final answers without exhaustively evaluating all reasoning processes.
AI.method: Formulate ALFT as a two-player Distributional Alignment Game between a Policy (generator) and a Target (auxiliary distribution). Prove that the Nash Equilibrium corresponds exactly to the original answer-level optimization. Recast the intractable marginalization as a tractable projection problem via a variational perspective. Show that the framework unifies diversity and coherence ideas and enable efficient algorithms compatible with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), exemplified by Coherence-GRPO.
AI.result: Theoretical result establishing NE ↔ solution to the answer-level objective, providing a variational/projection formulation that turns latent-path marginalization into a tractable optimization. The framework yields practical, GRPO-compatible algorithms (e.g., Coherence-GRPO) with significant complexity reductions in mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a unified, scalable approach to ALFT by connecting game theory, variational optimization, and policy optimization; it enables efficient, theoretically grounded methods that improve answer quality while handling diverse and coherent output objectives, particularly in mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Mechanized Foundations of Structural Governance: Machine-Checked Proofs for Governed Intelligence
comment: 27 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. Code and proofs: https://github.com/mashin-live/governance-proofs
summary: We present five results in the theory of structural governance for cognitive workflow systems. Three are mechanized in Coq 8.19 using the Interaction Trees library with parameterized coinduction; two are proved on paper with explicit reductions. The Coinductive Safety Predicate (gov_safe) is a coinductive property that captures governance safety for infinite program behaviors, indexed by a boolean permission flag that is provably false for ungoverned I/O and true for governed interpretations (mechanized). The Governance Invariance Theorem establishes that governance is uniform across the meta-recursive tower: governance at level n+1 reduces to governance at level n by definitional equality of the type (mechanized). The Sufficiency Theorem proves that four atomic primitives (code, reason, memory, call) are expressively complete for any discrete intelligent system, formalized as compositional closure of a Kleisli category (mechanized). The Alternating Normal Form provides a canonical decomposition of any machine into alternating code and effect layers, with a confluent rewriting system (paper proof). The Necessity Theorem proves via explicit reduction to Rice's theorem that an architecturally opaque component (the reason primitive) is mathematically necessary for problems requiring semantic judgment (paper proof). A sixth contribution connects the abstract model to the deployed runtime: the Verified Interpreter Specification formalizes the BEAM runtime's trust, capability, and hash chain logic in Coq, then tests the running system against this specification using property-based testing with over 70,000 randomly generated directive sequences and zero disagreements. The mechanization comprises approximately 12,000 lines across 36 modules with 454 theorems and zero admitted lemmas.
