AI.tldr: A cohesive formal theory of governance for cognitive workflow systems, largely mechanized in Coq with a runtime-verified BEAM interpreter; five formal results (three mechanized, two paper proofs) plus a runtime connection, totaling ~12k lines of formalization, 454 theorems; aims to ensure safety, expressivity, and alignment between abstract model and execution.
AI.motivation: Address safety and expressivity in governance of infinite, complex cognitive workflows, and provide a rigorous bridge from formal theory to deployed runtime.
AI.method: Formalization in Coq 8.19 using Interaction Trees and parameterized coinduction for a coinductive safety predicate (gov_safe); governance invariance across meta-recursive levels via definitional equality; sufficiency of four primitives via Kleisli category compositional closure; alternating normal form with confluent rewriting; explicit reductions to Rice's theorem for necessity of the reason primitive; a sixth artifact connects to BEAM runtime through a Verified Interpreter Specification and property-based testing (70k+ random directives) to validate runtime against the specification.
AI.result: Five formal results (three mechanized, two paper proofs): (1) gov_safe coinductive safety predicate; (2) Governance Invariance Theorem (n+1 reduces to n); (3) Sufficiency Theorem (four primitives complete for discrete intelligent systems, Kleisli closure); (4) Alternating Normal Form (canonical code/effect decomposition with confluent rewriting, paper proof); (5) Necessity Theorem (reduction to Rice's theorem showing necessity of the reason primitive). Sixth contribution: Verified Interpreter Specification tying abstract model to BEAM runtime, tested with property-based testing; runtime had zero disagreements. Mechanization spans ~12,000 lines, 36 modules, 454 theorems, zero admitted lemmas.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a robust, multi-faceted foundation for governance in cognitive workflows, combining machine-checked formal proofs with runtime verification. It demonstrates safety, invariance, expressivity, and a credible pathway from theory to practice, while also revealing areas for scalability and broader runtime integration.

title: Design and Characteristics of a Thin-Film ThermoMesh for the Efficient Embedded Sensing of a Spatio-Temporally Sparse Heat Source
comment: 45 pages, 13 figures, 63 references, under review in Sensors and Actuators A: Physical
summary: This work presents ThermoMesh, a passive thin-film thermoelectric mesh sensor designed to detect and characterize spatio-temporally sparse heat sources through conduction-based thermal imaging. The device integrates thermoelectric junctions with linear or nonlinear interlayer resistive elements to perform simultaneous sensing and in-sensor compression. We focus on the single-event (1-sparse) operation and define four performance metrics: range, efficiency, sensitivity, and accuracy. Numerical modeling shows that a linear resistive interlayer flattens the sensitivity distribution and improves minimum sensitivity by approximately tenfold for a $16\times16$ mesh. Nonlinear temperature-dependent interlayers further enhance minimum sensitivity at scale: a ceramic negative-temperature-coefficient (NTC) layer over 973--1273~K yields a $\sim14{,}500\times$ higher minimum sensitivity than the linear design at a $200\times200$ mesh, while a VO$_2$ interlayer modeled across its metal--insulator transition (MIT) over 298--373~K yields a $\sim24\times$ improvement. Using synthetic 1-sparse datasets with white boundary-channel noise at a signal-to-noise ratio of 40~dB, the VO$_2$ case achieved $98\%$ localization accuracy, a mean absolute temperature error of $0.23$~K, and a noise-equivalent temperature (NET) of $0.07$~K. For the ceramic-NTC case no localization errors were observed under the tested conditions, with a mean absolute temperature error of $1.83$~K and a NET of $1.49$~K. These results indicate that ThermoMesh could enable energy-efficient embedded thermal sensing in scenarios where conventional infrared imaging is limited, such as molten-droplet detection or hot-spot monitoring in harsh environments.

AI.tldr: ThermoMesh is a passive thin-film thermoelectric mesh sensor that detects sparse heat sources via conduction-based imaging using linear or nonlinear interlayer resistive elements, achieving high localization accuracy and low NET in 1-sparse operation; nonlinear interlayers (VO2 MIT, ceramic NTC) substantially boost sensitivity, enabling scalable, energy-efficient embedded thermal sensing.
AI.motivation: Enable energy-efficient, in-sensor thermal sensing in environments where infrared imaging is limited, by leveraging thermoelectric junctions with interlayer resistors for simultaneous sensing and compression.
AI.method: Numerical modeling of linear and nonlinear interlayers in 16x16 to 200x200 mesh; evaluation on 1-sparse datasets with boundary-channel noise at 40 dB SNR; comparison across linear, ceramic NTC, and VO2 interlayers; metrics: range, efficiency, sensitivity, accuracy; performance measures include minimum sensitivity, localization accuracy, MAE, NET.
AI.result: Linear interlayer flattens sensitivity distribution and raises minimum sensitivity ~10x for 16x16 mesh. Ceramic NTC (973–1273 K) yields ~14,500x higher minimum sensitivity than linear at 200x200; VO2 MIT across 298–373 K yields ~24x improvement. On synthetic data: VO2 case achieves 98% localization accuracy, MAE 0.23 K, NET 0.07 K; ceramic-NTC: MAE 1.83 K, NET 1.49 K; suggests practical potential for in-sensor thermal sensing in harsh environments.
AI.conclusion: ThermoMesh shows promise for scalable, energy-efficient thermal sensing with enhanced sensitivity using temperature-dependent interlayers; requires validation with experimental data and real-world heat sources; could outperform infrared imaging for certain applications.

title: Sparse-View 3D Gaussian Splatting in the Wild
summary: We propose a 3D novel sparse-view synthesis framework for unconstrained real-world scenarios that contain distractors. Unlike existing methods that primarily perform novel-view synthesis from a sparse set of constrained images without transient elements or leverage unconstrained dense image collections to enhance 3D representation in real-world scenarios, our method not only effectively tackles sparse unconstrained image collections, but also shows high-quality 3D rendering results. To do this, we introduce reference-guided view refinement with a diffusion model using a transient mask and a reference image to enhance the 3D representation and mitigate artifacts in rendered views. Furthermore, we address sparse regions in the Gaussian field via pseudo-view generation along with a sparsity-aware Gaussian replication strategy to amplify Gaussians in the sparse regions. Extensive experiments on publicly available datasets demonstrate that our methodology consistently outperforms existing methods (e.g., PSNR - 17.2%, SSIM - 10.8%, LPIPS - 4.0%) and provides high-fidelity 3D rendering results. This advancement paves the way for realizing unconstrained real-world scenarios without labor-intensive data acquisition. Our project page is available at $\href{https://robotic-vision-lab.github.io/SaveWildGS/}{here}$

AI.tldr: A reference-guided sparse-view 3D synthesis framework for unconstrained real-world scenes, combining diffusion-based view refinement with a sparsity-aware Gaussian field to generate high-fidelity renderings from sparse, distractor-rich image collections, outperforming prior methods on standard metrics.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of unconstrained real-world 3D novel-view synthesis from sparse, distractor-containing image collections, where existing methods either rely on constrained views or dense image collections and still struggle with artifacts.
AI.method: Introduce reference-guided view refinement using a diffusion model with a transient mask and a reference image to improve 3D representation and reduce artifacts. Handle sparse regions in the Gaussian field by pseudo-view generation and a sparsity-aware Gaussian replication strategy to amplify Gaussians where data is missing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets show the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing methods, with PSNR improvements of 17.2%, SSIM improvements of 10.8%, and LPIPS improvements of 4.0% (lower is better). The method yields high-fidelity 3D renderings in unconstrained scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables high-quality 3D rendering from sparse, unconstrained image collections, reducing labor-intensive data acquisition in real-world settings; a project page is provided for further details.

title: Context-Aware Graph Attention for Unsupervised Telco Anomaly Detection
summary: We propose C-MTAD-GAT, an \emph{unsupervised}, \emph{context-aware} graph-attention model for anomaly detection in multivariate time series from mobile networks. C-MTAD-GAT combines graph attention with lightweight context embeddings, and uses a deterministic reconstruction head and multi-step forecaster to produce anomaly scores. Detection thresholds are calibrated \emph{without labels} from validation residuals, keeping the pipeline fully unsupervised. On the public TELCO dataset, C-MTAD-GAT consistently outperforms MTAD-GAT and the Telco-specific DC-VAE, two state-of-the-art baselines, in both event-level and pointwise F1, while triggering substantially fewer alarms. C-MTAD-GAT is also deployed in the Core network of a national mobile operator, demonstrating its resilience in real industrial settings.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised, context-aware graph-attention model (C-MTAD-GAT) for anomaly detection in multivariate time series from mobile networks, achieving state-of-the-art performance with unsupervised threshold calibration and robust real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Tackle unsupervised anomaly detection in multivariate time series from mobile networks by integrating graph attention with lightweight context embeddings to improve detection accuracy and reduce false alarms without requiring labeled data.
AI.method: Propose C-MTAD-GAT that fuses graph attention with context embeddings, includes a deterministic reconstruction head and a multi-step forecaster, and uses threshold calibration from validation residuals in a fully unsupervised pipeline; evaluates on TELCO dataset and demonstrates deployment in a national operator Core network.
AI.result: Outperforms MTAD-GAT and DC-VAE on TELCO in both event-level and pointwise F1 scores while triggering substantially fewer alarms; validates practical robustness through real-world deployment in a national operator's Core network.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers accurate, unsupervised anomaly detection in enterprise mobile networks, with effective thresholding and demonstrated industrial viability.

title: The Two Boundaries: Why Behavioral AI Governance Fails Structurally
summary: Every system that performs effects has two boundaries: what it can do (expressiveness) and what governance covers (governance). In nearly all deployed AI systems, these boundaries are defined independently, creating three regions: governed capabilities (the only useful region), ungoverned capabilities (risk), and governance policies that address non-existent capabilities (theater). Two of the three regions are failure modes. We focus on the governance of effects: actions that AI systems perform in the world (API calls, database writes, tool invocations). This is distinct from the governance of model outputs (content quality, bias, fairness), which operates at a different level and requires different mechanisms. We present a formal framework for analyzing this structural gap. Rice's theorem (1953) proves the gap is undecidable in the general case for any Turing-complete architecture that attempts to govern effects behaviorally: no algorithm can decide non-trivial semantic properties of arbitrary programs, including the property "this program's effects comply with the governance policy." We define coterminous governance: a system property where the expressivenessboundary equals the governance boundary. We show that coterminous governance requires an architectural decision (separatingcomputation from effect) rather than a governance layer added after the fact. We show that structural governance under this separation subsumes separate governance infrastructure: governance checks become part of the execution pipeline rather than a second system running alongside it. We propose coterminous governance as the testable criterion for any AI governance system: either the two boundaries are provably identical, or risk and theater are structurally inevitable. Proofs are mechanized in Coq (454 theorems, 36 modules, 0 admitted).

AI.tldr: Formalizes the governance gap for AI effects, proves undecidability via Rice's theorem, and advocates coterminous governance achieved by architecture separation; validated by a large Coq proof (454 theorems, 36 modules, 0 admitted).
AI.motivation: To address the gap between expressiveness and governance boundaries in AI systems and provide a testable criterion for governance effectiveness.
AI.method: Develops a formal framework distinguishing governance of effects from governance of outputs; applies Rice's theorem to show undecidability; defines coterminous governance; demonstrates that only with separation of computation and effects can governance be embedded; mechanized proofs in Coq.
AI.result: Shows that coterminous governance either occurs (architecture with integrated checks) or the risk/theater regions are structurally inevitable; governance infrastructure can be subsumed into execution pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Coterminous governance is a testable criterion for AI governance systems; architecture must separate computation from effects to provide verifiable governance; Coq proofs provide machine-checked confirmation.

title: FlexiTac: A Low-Cost, Open-Source, Scalable Tactile Sensing Solution for Robotic Systems
summary: We present FlexiTac, a low-cost, open-source, and scalable piezoresistive tactile sensing solution designed for robotic end-effectors. FlexiTac is a practical "plug-in" module consisting of (i) thin, flexible tactile sensor pads that provide dense tactile signals and (ii) a compact multi-channel readout board that streams synchronized measurements for real-time control and large-scale data collection. FlexiTac pads adopt a sealed three-layer laminate stack (FPC-Velostat-FPC) with electrode patterns directly integrated into flexible printed circuits, substantially improving fabrication throughput and repeatability while maintaining mechanical compliance for deployment on both rigid and soft grippers. The readout electronics use widely available, low-cost components and stream tactile signals to a host computer at 100 Hz via serial communication. Across multiple configurations, including fingertip pads and larger tactile mats, FlexiTac can be mounted on diverse platforms without major mechanical redesign. We further show that FlexiTac supports modern tactile learning pipelines, including 3D visuo-tactile fusion for contact-aware decision making, cross-embodiment skill transfer, and real-to-sim-to-real fine-tuning with GPU-parallel tactile simulation. Our project page is available at https://flexitac.github.io/.

AI.tldr: FlexiTac is a low-cost, open-source plug-in tactile sensing module with densely instrumented flexible pads and a multi-channel readout, enabling real-time sensing and data-driven robotics across rigid and soft grippers.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, manufacturable tactile sensing that can be mounted on diverse end-effectors and integrated into modern tactile learning pipelines.
AI.method: A three-layer laminate tactile pad (FPC-Velostat-FPC) with integrated electrodes on flexible PCBs, sealing to allow compliance; a compact, low-cost readout board streams multi-channel data at 100 Hz to a host over serial; modular and adaptable to fingertip pads and larger mats; supports real-to-sim-to-real tactile learning (3D visuo-tactile fusion, cross-embodiment transfer, GPU-accelerated tactile simulation).
AI.result: Demonstrates dense tactile sensing signals, real-time streaming, and compatibility with contemporary tactile learning workflows; scalable fabrication with improved throughput and repeatability; adaptable to various gripper geometries; project page provided for implementation details.
AI.conclusion: FlexiTac provides a practical, scalable, open-source tactile sensing solution that can be readily integrated into diverse robotic platforms and modern learning-based control frameworks.

title: Softmax-GS: Generalized Gaussians Learning When to Blend or Bound
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D GS) is widely adopted for novel view synthesis due to its high training and rendering efficiency. However, its efficiency relies on the key assumption that Gaussians do not overlap in the 3D space, which leads to noticeable artifacts and view inconsistencies. In addition, the inherently diffuse boundaries of Gaussians hinder accurate reconstruction of sharp object edges. We propose Softmax-GS, a unified solution that addresses both the view-inconsistency and the diffuse-boundary problem by enforcing a softmax-based competition in overlapping regions between two Gaussians. With learnable parameters controlling the strength of the competition, it enables a continuous spectrum from smooth color blending to crisp, well-defined boundaries. Our formulation explicitly preserves order invariance for any two overlapping Gaussians and ensures that the output transmittance remains unchanged irrespective of the extent of overlapping, preventing undesirable discontinuities in the rendered output. Ablation experiments on simple geometries demonstrate the effectiveness of each component of Softmax-GS, and evaluations on real-world benchmarks show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving both reconstruction quality and parameter efficiency.

AI.tldr: Softmax-GS introduces a softmax-based competition between overlapping 3D Gaussians in Gaussian Splatting to fix view inconsistency and diffuse boundaries, with learnable competition strength; preserves order invariance and constant transmittance; achieves state-of-the-art results with ablations and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting relies on non-overlapping Gaussians, causing artifacts and blurry edges when overlaps occur; a unified mechanism is needed to allow overlap while maintaining stable rendering and sharp boundaries.
AI.method: Apply a softmax-based competition in overlapping regions between Gaussians. Include learnable parameters that control competition strength, enabling a continuum from smooth color blending to crisp boundaries. Prove order invariance for any two overlapping Gaussians and ensure output transmittance is unaffected by the degree of overlap.
AI.result: Ablation studies on simple geometries validate the contribution of each component. Real-world benchmarks show state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and improved parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Softmax-GS provides a unified framework to address both view-inconsistency and diffuse boundaries in 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling a tunable spectrum from smooth to sharp rendering while preserving core physical properties, and achieving superior performance.

title: Preserving Temporal Dynamics in Time Series Generation
summary:
Time-series data augmentation plays a crucial role in regression-oriented forecasting tasks, where limited data restricts the performance of deep learning models. While Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have shown promise in synthetic time-series generation, existing approaches primarily focus on matching marginal data distributions and often overlook the temporal dynamics that naturally exist in the original multivariate time series. When generating multivariate time series, this mismatch leads to distribution shift and temporal drift, thereby degrading the fidelity of the synthetic sequences.
  In this work, we propose a model-agnostic Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)-based framework to mitigate distribution shift and preserve temporal dynamics in synthetic time series. We provide a theoretical analysis of how conditional generative models accumulate deviations under sequential generation and demonstrate that the MCMC algorithm can correct these discrepancies by enforcing consistency with empirical transition statistics between neighboring time points.
  Extensive experiments on the Lorenz, Licor, ETTh, and ILI datasets using RCGAN, GCWGAN, TimeGAN, SigCWGAN, and AECGAN demonstrate that the proposed MCMC framework consistently improves autocorrelation alignment, skewness error, kurtosis error, R$^2$, discriminative score, and predictive score. These results suggest that synthetic time series consistent with the original data require explicit preservation of transition laws rather than solely relying on adversarial distribution matching, thereby offering a principled direction for improving generative modeling of time-series data.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic MCMC framework improves fidelity of synthetic multivariate time series by enforcing empirical transition consistency, addressing temporal drift not captured by marginal GAN-based matching.
AI.motivation: Time-series forecasting with limited data benefits from augmentation, but GAN-based synthesis often matches only marginal distributions and neglects temporal dynamics, causing distribution shift and temporal drift when generating multivariate sequences.
AI.method: Propose a model-agnostic Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework that enforces consistency with empirical transition statistics between neighboring time points. Provide theoretical analysis of how conditional generative models accumulate deviations under sequential generation and show MCMC can correct these discrepancies. Apply to existing generators (RCGAN, GCWGAN, TimeGAN, SigCWGAN, AECGAN).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Lorenz, Licor, ETTh, and ILI datasets demonstrate consistent improvements in autocorrelation alignment, skewness error, kurtosis error, R^2, discriminative score, and predictive score across the tested models.
AI.conclusion: Preserving transition laws is crucial for faithful synthetic time-series generation; the proposed MCMC framework offers a principled, model-agnostic route to improve time-series generative modeling by enforcing empirical transition statistics.

title: Learning Rate Engineering: From Coarse Single Parameter to Layered Evolution
summary: Learning rate scheduling has evolved from the single global fixed rate of early SGD to sophisticated layer-wise adaptive strategies. We systematize this evolution into five generations: (Gen1) global fixed learning rates, (Gen2) global scheduling, (Gen3) parameter-level adaptation, (Gen4) layer-level differentiation, and (Gen5) joint layer-time scheduling. We trace the fundamental motivation behind each transition, showing how the shift from one-size-fits-all to tailoring by layer and time addresses the impossible trinity of transfer learning: lower layers require small updates to preserve general knowledge while higher layers need large updates to adapt to new tasks. Building on this taxonomy, we propose Discriminative Adaptive Layer Scaling (DALS), a unified framework that integrates phase-adaptive cosine scheduling, depth-aware Grokfast gradient filtering, and LARS-style trust ratios into a single coherent optimizer. We benchmark 18 strategies including three DALS variants across all five generations on five datasets: synthetic, CIFAR-10 (from scratch), RTE, TREC-6, and IMDb (fine-tuning). On synthetic, DALS achieves the best accuracy at 98.0%, while DALS-Fast reaches 90% in just 3 epochs. The cross-dataset analysis reveals striking regime-dependent patterns -- no single strategy wins across all regimes. Critically, STLR+Discriminative, the ULMFiT champion, catastrophically fails on from-scratch tasks (43.6% on TREC-6 from scratch vs. 96.8% with RAdam), confirming that directional decay biases are harmful without pretrained features. DALS avoids either extreme, achieving the best synthetic result while maintaining competitive fine-tuning performance.

AI.tldr: A five-generation taxonomy of learning-rate scheduling is proposed, with Discriminative Adaptive Layer Scaling (DALS) as a unified optimizer that combines phase-adaptive cosine scheduling, depth-aware gradient filtering, and LARS-style trust ratios. Extensive benchmarking across 18 strategies and five datasets shows regime-dependent performance; no single method dominates. DALS excels on synthetic data and maintains competitive fine-tuning, while some pretrained-based methods (e.g., STLR+Discriminative) fail in from-scratch tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the transfer-learning trilemma by moving from one-size-fits-all updates to layer- and time-aware updates, enabling lower layers to remain stable while higher layers adapt, thereby unifying diverse LR strategies under a coherent framework.
AI.method: Systematize learning-rate scheduling into Gen1–Gen5 generations; introduce DALS that fuses phase-adaptive cosine scheduling, depth-aware Grokfast gradient filtering, and LARS-style trust ratios; benchmark 18 strategies (including 3 DALS variants) across five datasets (synthetic, CIFAR-10 from scratch, RTE, TREC-6, IMDb fine-tuning).
AI.result: DALS achieves 98.0% accuracy on synthetic data; DALS-Fast attains 90% in 3 epochs; cross-dataset analysis reveals regime-dependent patterns with no universal winner; STLR+Discriminative (ULMFiT) catastrophically underperforms on from-scratch tasks (43.6% on TREC-6 vs 96.8% with RAdam); DALS provides strong synthetic performance while remaining competitive in fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The taxonomy clarifies the evolution of LR scheduling and positions DALS as a robust, unified optimizer across regimes. Yet performance remains regime-dependent; future work should investigate broader tasks, ablations of DALS components, computational costs, and strategies to further close the gap between from-scratch and pretraining-based settings.

title: RopeDreamer: A Kinematic Recurrent State Space Model for Dynamics of Flexible Deformable Linear Objects
summary: The robotic manipulation of Deformable Linear Objects (DLOs) is a fundamental challenge due to the high-dimensional, non-linear dynamics of flexible structures and the complexity of maintaining topological integrity during contact-rich tasks. While recent data-driven methods have utilized Recurrent and Graph Neural Networks for dynamics modeling, they often struggle with self-intersections and non-physical deformations, such as tangling and link stretching. In this paper, we propose a latent dynamics framework that combines a Recurrent State Space Model with a Quaternionic Kinematic Chain representation to enable robust, long-term forecasting of DLO states. By encoding the DLO as a sequence of relative rotations (quaternions) rather than independent Cartesian positions, we inherently constrain the model to a physically valid manifold that preserves link-length constancy. Furthermore, we introduce a dual-decoder architecture that decouples state reconstruction from future-state prediction, forcing the latent space to capture the underlying physics of deformation. We evaluate our approach on a large-scale simulated dataset of complex pick-and-place trajectories involving self-intersections. Our results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a 40.52% reduction in open-loop prediction error over 50-step horizons compared to the state-of-the-art baseline, while reducing inference time by 31.17%. Our model further maintains superior topological consistency in scenarios with multiple crossings, proving its efficacy as a compositional primitive for long-horizon manipulation planning.

AI.tldr: A latent dynamics model for deformable linear objects that uses a recurrent state-space model with a quaternionic kinematic chain to enforce physical constraints, featuring a dual-decoder architecture, achieving substantial accuracy and speed gains on simulated long-horizon manipulation tasks with self-intersections.
AI.motivation: DLO manipulation is high-dimensional and non-linear; existing data-driven methods struggle with self-intersections and non-physical deformations; a physically-constrained latent representation is needed for robust long-horizon prediction and planning.
AI.method: Encode DLO as a sequence of relative rotations (quaternions) forming a quaternionic kinematic chain; employ a Recurrent State Space Model to predict latent states; use dual-decoder: one for state reconstruction, another for future-state prediction; train to decouple latent dynamics from observation reconstruction.
AI.result: 40.52% reduction in open-loop prediction error over 50-step horizons versus SOTA baseline; 31.17% faster inference; improved topological consistency in crossing scenarios; demonstrated efficacy as a compositional primitive for long-horizon manipulation planning.
AI.conclusion: The quaternion-based manifold constraint and dual-decoder latent dynamics yield physically consistent long-horizon predictions of DLO states and improve planning robustness for contact-rich manipulation.

title: Context as Prior: Bayesian-Inspired Intent Inference for Non-Speaking Agents with a Household Cat Testbed
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Animal Workshop
summary:
Many agents in real-world environments cannot reliably communicate their goals through language, including household pets, pre-verbal infants, and other non-speaking embodied agents. In such settings, intent must be inferred from incomplete behavioral observations in context-rich environments. This creates a core ambiguity: observable behavior is often noisy or underspecified, while context provides strong prior information but can also induce brittle shortcut predictions if used naively.
  We present CatSignal, a Bayesian-inspired probabilistic framework for multimodal intent inference that models spatial context as a prior-like constraint and behavioral observations as evidence. Rather than treating context as an ordinary input feature, our method uses a context-gated Product-of-Experts formulation to compute posterior-like intent distributions from context, pose dynamics, and acoustic cues. We instantiate this formulation in a household cat setting as a focused proof-of-concept for intent inference in non-speaking agents.
  Under Leave-One-Video-Out evaluation on a multimodal domestic cat dataset, the proposed prior-guided fusion achieves the best overall accuracy of 77.72%, outperforming feature concatenation (71.83%) and stronger late-fusion baselines. More importantly, it substantially reduces context-driven shortcut failures in ambiguous cases. While simpler fusion strategies remain competitive in Macro-F1 and selective prediction, the proposed model provides the strongest overall accuracy and the best suppression of context-based shortcut collapse.

AI.tldr: CatSignal uses a context-gated Product-of-Experts to fuse context and behavioral cues for probabilistic intent inference in non-speaking agents, showing improved accuracy and reduced shortcut failures.
AI.motivation: Many real-world agents cannot reliably express goals through language; intent must be inferred from incomplete behavioral observations in rich contexts. Context can provide strong priors but may cause brittle shortcut predictions if used naively, motivating a principled fusion approach.
AI.method: Treat context as a prior-like constraint and combine context, pose dynamics, and acoustic cues via a context-gated Product-of-Experts to produce posterior-like intent distributions. Evaluated as a focused proof-of-concept in a household cat setting with Leave-One-Video-Out (LOVO) evaluation on a multimodal domestic cat dataset.
AI.result: Achieves best overall accuracy of 77.72% under LOVO, outperforming feature concatenation (71.83%) and stronger late-fusion baselines, and substantially reduces context-driven shortcut failures. Macro-F1 and selective prediction are competitive with simpler fusion strategies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prior-guided fusion via a Product-of-Experts provides a principled, robust fusion mechanism for multimodal intent inference in non-speaking agents, offering strong overall accuracy and reduction of context-induced shortcut collapses.

title: Remaining Useful Life Estimation for Turbofan Engines: A Comparative Study of Classical, CNN, and LSTM Approaches
summary: Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation is a critical component of Prognostics and Health Management (PHM), enabling proactive maintenance scheduling and reducing unplanned failures in industrial equipment. This paper presents a comparative study of machine learning approaches for RUL estimation on the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan engine dataset: classical baselines (Ridge Regression, Polynomial Ridge, and XGBoost), a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. All models are evaluated on the FD001 and FD003 subsets under an identical preprocessing pipeline to ensure a fair comparison. Among raw-sequence models, the LSTM achieves RMSE of 14.93 and 14.20 on FD001 and FD003 respectively, outperforming the deep LSTM reported by Zheng et al.~\cite{paper} (RMSE 16.14 and 16.18) despite using a simpler single-layer architecture. The 1D CNN achieves RMSE of 16.97 on FD001 and 15.68 on FD003, demonstrating competitive performance on FD003 while producing more conservative RUL predictions on FD001. Ridge Regression is evaluated on raw and engineered features, while other classical models use only engineered inputs. XGBoost achieves an RMSE of 13.36 on FD003, highlighting the competitiveness of nonlinear modeling.

AI.tldr: Comparative benchmarking of classical and deep learning models for RUL estimation on NASA C-MAPSS FD001/FD003. LSTM excels among raw-sequence models; XGBoost performs best on FD003 using engineered features; 1D-CNN is competitive on FD003; Ridge on raw and engineered features; overall nonlinear models show strong performance under identical preprocessing.
AI.motivation: Assess the relative performance of traditional ML, feature-engineered baselines, and deep learning models for RUL prediction under a fair preprocessing pipeline to guide model selection for PHM applications.
AI.method: Train Ridge, Polynomial Ridge, and XGBoost (using engineered features or raw input per model), a 1D CNN, and an LSTM on the NASA C-MAPSS FD001 and FD003 subsets with identical preprocessing. Report RMSE and compare to a prior reference (Zheng et al.).
AI.result: LSTM RMSE: 14.93 (FD001) and 14.20 (FD003); outperforms Zheng et al.’s deeper LSTM (16.14/16.18); 1D-CNN RMSE: 16.97 (FD001) and 15.68 (FD003); CNN competitive on FD003 and more conservative on FD001; Ridge regression evaluated on raw and engineered features; XGBoost RMSE: 13.36 on FD003; nonlinear models show strong performance, with XGBoost achieving the best FD003 result among the models tested; Ridge and engineered features emphasize fair baseline comparisons across models.
AI.conclusion: Nonlinear modeling yields strong RUL estimates on the C-MAPSS data under fair preprocessing. LSTM is strong among sequence models, XGBoost attains the best FD003 performance with engineered features, and even simpler CNNs are competitive. For FD001, LSTM shapes uncertainty better; future work should ensure consistent feature usage across models and explore generalization across subsets and horizons.

title: Machine Collective Intelligence for Explainable Scientific Discovery
summary: Deriving governing equations from empirical observations is a longstanding challenge in science. Although artificial intelligence (AI) has demonstrated substantial capabilities in function approximation, the discovery of explainable and extrapolatable equations remains a fundamental limitation of modern AI, posing a central bottleneck for AI-driven scientific discovery. Here, we present machine collective intelligence, a unified paradigm that integrates two fundamental yet distinct traditions in computational intelligence--symbolism and metaheuristics--to enable autonomous and evolutionary discovery of governing equations. It orchestrates multiple reasoning agents to evolve their symbolic hypotheses through coordinated generation, evaluation, critique, and consolidation, enabling scientific discovery beyond single-agent inference. Across scientific systems governed by deterministic, stochastic, or previously uncharacterized dynamics, machine collective intelligence autonomously recovered the underlying governing equations without relying on hand-crafted domain knowledge. Furthermore, the resulting equations reduced extrapolation error by up to six orders of magnitude relative to deep neural networks, while condensing 0.5-1 million model parameters into just 5-40 interpretable parameters. This study marks an important shift in AI toward the autonomous discovery of principled scientific equations.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent symbolic-evolution framework called machine collective intelligence (MCI) combines symbolism and metaheuristics to autonomously discover governing equations, yielding interpretable models with strong extrapolation capabilities across deterministic, stochastic, and uncharacterized dynamics; achieves significant parameter reduction.
AI.motivation: Deriving governing equations from empirical data is a long-standing challenge: AI excels at function approximation but struggles to produce explainable, extrapolatable equations without domain-specific priors.
AI.method: Integrates symbolism and metaheuristics in a coordinated, multi-agent system. Multiple reasoning agents generate, evaluate, critique, and consolidate symbolic hypotheses, evolving governing equations without hand-crafted domain knowledge, applicable to deterministic, stochastic, or previously uncharacterized dynamics.
AI.result: The approach autonomously recovered underlying governing equations. Extrapolation error is reduced by up to six orders of magnitude compared with deep neural networks, and model size is condensed from ~0.5–1 million parameters to 5–40 interpretable parameters.
AI.conclusion: This work marks a shift toward autonomous discovery of principled scientific equations, advancing AI from function approximation to explainable, extrapolatable equation discovery across diverse scientific domains.

title: LaST-R1: Reinforcing Action via Adaptive Physical Latent Reasoning for VLA Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have increasingly incorporated reasoning mechanisms for complex robotic manipulation. However, existing approaches share a critical limitation: whether employing explicit linguistic reasoning that suffers from latency and discretization, or utilizing more expressive continuous latent reasoning, they are predominantly confined to static imitation learning that limits adaptability and generalization. While online reinforcement learning (RL) has been introduced to VLAs to enable trial-and-error exploration, current methods exclusively optimize the vanilla action space, bypassing the underlying physical reasoning process. In this paper, we present \textbf{LaST-R1}, a unified VLA framework that integrates latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning over physical dynamics prior to action execution, along with a tailored RL post-training paradigm. Specifically, we propose \textbf{Latent-to-Action Policy Optimization (LAPO)}, a novel RL algorithm that jointly optimizes the latent reasoning process and the action generation. By bridging reasoning and control, LAPO improves the representation of physical world modeling and enhances robustness in interactive environments. Furthermore, an \textbf{adaptive latent CoT mechanism} is introduced to allow the policy to dynamically adjust its reasoning horizon based on environment complexity. Extensive experiments show that LaST-R1 achieves a near-perfect 99.8\% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark with only one-shot supervised warm-up, significantly improving convergence speed and performance over prior state-of-the-art methods. In real-world deployments, LAPO post-training yields up to a 44\% improvement over the initial warm-up policy across four complex tasks, including both single-arm and dual-arm settings. Finally, LaST-R1 demonstrates strong generalization across simulated and real-world environments.

AI.tldr: LaST-R1 integrates latent Chain-of-Thought reasoning over physical dynamics with a new LATENT-to-Action Policy Optimization (LAPO) algorithm and an adaptive reasoning horizon, delivering rapid convergence and strong generalization in vision-language-action robotics, achieving state-of-the-art LIBERO performance and notable real-world gains.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA methods face a trade-off: explicit linguistic reasoning suffers latency/discretization, while expressive continuous latent reasoning often remains imitation-based. Online RL typically optimizes only the action space, ignoring the underlying physical reasoning. A unified framework that couples latent reasoning about dynamics with control is needed to improve robustness and generalization.
AI.method: LaST-R1 introduces latent CoT reasoning over dynamics prior to action, coupled with LAPO that jointly optimizes latent reasoning and action generation. An adaptive latent CoT mechanism adjusts the reasoning horizon based on environment complexity. The approach includes a one-shot supervised warm-up followed by RL post-training, and is evaluated across simulated LIBERO benchmarks and real-world tasks with single- and dual-arm setups.
AI.result: Achieves 99.8% average success on LIBERO with one-shot warm-up; LAPO accelerates convergence and improves performance over prior methods; post-training yields up to 44% real-world performance gains across four tasks; demonstrates strong sim-to-real generalization.
AI.conclusion: Bridging latent reasoning and control via adaptive latent CoT and joint optimization yields improved physical world modeling, robustness, and sample efficiency in VLA robotics, with strong results on benchmark and real-world tasks.

title: LA-Pose: Latent Action Pretraining Meets Pose Estimation
summary: This paper revisits camera pose estimation through the lens of self-supervised pretraining, focusing on inverse-dynamics pretraining as a scalable alternative to the current trend of fully supervised training with 3D annotations. Concretely, we employ inverse- and forward-dynamics models to learn latent action representations, similar to Genie from large-scale driving videos. Our idea is simple yet effective. Existing methods use latent actions in their original capacity, that is, as action conditioning of world-models or as proxies of robot action parameters in policy networks. Our method, dubbed LA-Pose, repurposes the latent action features as inputs to a camera pose estimator, finetuned on a limited set of high-quality 3D annotations. This formulation enables accurate and generalizable pose prediction while maintaining feed-forward efficiency. Extensive experiments on driving benchmarks show that LA-Pose achieves competitive and even superior performance to state-of-the-art methods while using orders of magnitude less labeled data. Concretely, on the Waymo and PandaSet benchmarks, LA-Pose achieves over 10% higher pose accuracy than recent feed-forward methods. To our knowledge, this work is the first to demonstrate the power of inverse-dynamics self-supervised learning for pose estimation.

AI.tldr: LA-Pose uses inverse-dynamics self-supervised pretraining to learn latent action features and repurposes them as inputs to a camera pose estimator, finetuned on limited 3D annotations, achieving strong pose accuracy with far less labeled data on driving benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To reduce label requirements for camera pose estimation by leveraging self-supervised latent action representations, addressing data-hungry supervised 3D pose learning.
AI.method: Train inverse- and forward-dynamics models to produce latent action representations; feed these features into a pose estimator; fine-tune with a small set of high-quality 3D pose annotations; evaluate on Waymo and PandaSet.
AI.result: LA-Pose matches or surpasses state-of-the-art pose estimators, with orders of magnitude less labeled data; >10% higher pose accuracy on Waymo and PandaSet compared to recent feed-forward methods; first demonstration of inverse-dynamics self-supervised learning for pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: Inverse-dynamics self-supervised pretraining is a viable, data-efficient approach for video-to-pose estimation in driving scenarios, maintaining feed-forward efficiency and generalization.

title: Analytical Correction for Subsampling Bias in Drifting Models
summary: Drifting models are capable one-step generative models trained to follow a drifting field. The field combines attractive and repulsive softmax-weighted centroids over the data and current-generator distributions. In practice, only a minibatch of $n$ samples from each distribution is available, and each centroid is approximated by an empirical estimate. In this paper, we begin by showing that the minibatch centroid is in general a biased estimator of the target centroid, with a pointwise $O(1/n)$ bias arising from softmax self-normalization. Correcting this bias requires the expectation over the full distribution, which is intractable. We instead approximate the leading bias term from in-batch statistics and propose Analytical Bias Correction (ABC), a closed-form plug-in adjustment. We prove that ABC reduces the bias from $O(1/n)$ to $O(1/n^2)$, introduces no first-order increase in total variance, and preserves convex-hull containment of the corrected centroid. In practice, ABC requires only two additional lines of code and has negligible wall-time overhead under compiled execution. Toy experiments confirm the theoretical $O(1/n)$ and $O(1/n^2)$ scaling. On CIFAR-10, ABC reduces FID and trains faster, with the largest gains at small $n$, where the bias is most significant.

AI.tldr: We introduce Analytical Bias Correction (ABC) for minibatch-driven centroids in drifting models, reducing the bias from O(1/n) to O(1/n^2) with negligible overhead, improving training efficiency and FID on CIFAR-10, especially at small batch sizes.
AI.motivation: Minibatch estimates of softmax-weighted centroids are biased due to softmax self-normalization. The resulting O(1/n) bias is intractable to correct exactly (requires full-distribution expectations), which harms performance, particularly for small minibatches.
AI.method: Derive the leading bias term from in-batch statistics and apply a closed-form plug-in adjustment (Analytical Bias Correction, ABC). The correction reduces bias to O(1/n^2), does not raise first-order variance, and preserves convex-hull containment; implementation adds only two lines of code.
AI.result: Toy experiments verify the theoretical O(1/n) and O(1/n^2) bias scaling. In CIFAR-10 experiments, ABC reduces FID and speeds up training, with the largest gains at small n where bias is most pronounced.
AI.conclusion: ABC is a lightweight, effective bias correction for drifting models that enables better performance with smaller minibatches and minimal computational overhead.

title: METASYMBO: Multi-Agent Language-Guided Metamaterial Discovery via Symbolic Latent Evolution
summary: Metamaterial discovery seeks microstructured materials whose geometry induces targeted mechanical behavior. Existing inverse-design methods can efficiently generate candidates, but they typically require explicit numerical property targets and are less suitable for early-stage exploration, where researchers often begin with incomplete constraints and qualitative intents expressed in natural language. Large language models can interpret such intents, but they lack geometric awareness and physical property validity. To address this gap, we propose MetaSymbO, a multi-agent framework for language-guided Metamaterial discovery via Symbolic-driven latent evOlution. Specifically, MetaSymbO contains three agents: a Designer that interprets free-form design intents and retrieves a semantically consistent scaffold, a Generator that synthesizes candidate microstructures in a disentangled latent space, and a Supervisor that provides fast property-aware feedback for iterative refinement. To move beyond the limitations of reproducing known samples from literature and training data, we further introduce symbolic-driven latent evolution, which applies programmable operators over disentangled latent factors to compose, modify, and refine structures at inference time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that (i) MetaSymbO improves structural validity by up to 34% in symmetry and nearly 98% in periodicity compared to state-of-the-art baselines; (ii) MetaSymbO achieves about 6-7% higher language-guidance scores while maintaining superior structure novelty compared to advanced reasoning LLMs; (iii) qualitative analyses confirm the effectiveness of symbolic logic operators in enabling programmable semantic alignment; and (iv) realworld case studies on auxetic, high-stiffness metamaterial design further validate its practical capability.

AI.tldr: A language-guided metamaterial discovery framework (MetaSymbO) using three agents and symbolic-driven latent evolution to align natural-language intents with geometry; shows improved symmetry/periodicity validity, language-guidance scores, and real-world case studies.
AI.motivation: Existing inverse-design methods require explicit targets and struggle with early-stage exploratory intents expressed in natural language; LLMs lack geometric and physical validity; need a framework combining language understanding with geometry-aware search.
AI.method: Three-agent architecture (Designer, Generator, Supervisor) that integrates semantic scaffolding with a disentangled latent space, plus symbolic-driven latent evolution to apply programmable operators over latent factors at inference time, enabling composition/modification/refinement of microstructures; fast property-aware feedback.
AI.result: Improved symmetry by up to 34% and periodicity by ~98% over baselines; language-guidance scores 6-7% higher with better novelty than advanced LLMs; symbolic operators enable semantic alignment; validates on auxetic and high-stiffness metamaterial design via real-world case studies.
AI.conclusion: MetaSymbO advances language-guided metamaterial discovery by integrating symbolic latent manipulation and fast feedback, enabling explorations beyond existing data and improving structural validity and alignment with intents.

title: OmniRobotHome: A Multi-Camera Platform for Real-Time Multiadic Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Human-robot collaboration has been studied primarily in dyadic or sequential settings. However, real homes require multiadic collaboration, where multiple humans and robots share a workspace, acting concurrently on interleaved subtasks with tight spatial and temporal coupling. This regime remains underexplored because close-proximity interaction between humans, robots, and objects creates persistent occlusion and rapid state changes, making reliable real-time 3D tracking the central bottleneck. No existing platform provides the real-time, occlusion-robust, room-scale perception needed to make this regime experimentally tractable. We present OmniRobotHome, the first room-scale residential platform that unifies wide-area real-time 3D human and object perception with coordinated multi-robot actuation in a shared world frame. The system instruments a natural home environment with 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras for markerless, occlusion-robust tracking of multiple humans and objects, temporally aligned with two Franka arms that act on live scene state. Continuous capture within this consistent frame further supports long-horizon human behavior modeling from accumulated trajectories. The platform makes the multiadic collaboration regime experimentally tractable. We focus on two central problems: safety in shared human-robot environments and human-anticipatory robotic assistance, and show that real-time perception and accumulated behavior memory each yield measurable gains in both.

AI.tldr: OmniRobotHome presents the first room-scale residential platform for real-time, occlusion-robust, multi-robot perception and actuation, enabling the study and advancement of multiadic human-robot collaboration in homes.
AI.motivation: To enable experimental study of multiadic, in-home collaboration where multiple humans and robots act concurrently under tight spatial/temporal coupling, overcoming occlusion and real-time 3D tracking bottlenecks without a suitable platform.
AI.method: A room-scale setup instrumented with 48 synchronized RGB cameras for markerless, occlusion-robust 3D tracking of multiple humans/objects, temporally aligned with two Franka arms acting on an in-scene shared state, enabling continuous capture in a consistent frame for long-horizon behavior modeling.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time perception and accumulated behavior memory yield measurable gains in safety and human-anticipatory robotic assistance; the platform makes multiadic collaboration experimentally tractable and supports room-scale perception with coordinated multi-robot actuation.
AI.conclusion: OmniRobotHome constitutes a first-of-its-kind, room-scale platform unifying real-time 3D perception with coordinated actuation in a shared world frame, enabling rigorous study and development of multiadic human-robot collaboration in residential environments.

title: EdgeFM: Efficient Edge Inference for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong applicability in edge industrial applications, yet their deployment remains severely constrained by requirements for deterministic low latency and stable execution under resource limitations. Existing frameworks either rely on bloated general-purpose designs or force developers into opaque, hardware-specific closed-source ecosystems, leading to hardware lock-in limitation and poor cross-platform adaptability. Observing that modern AI agents can efficiently search and tune configurations to generate highly optimized low-level kernels for standard LLM operators, we propose EdgeFM, a lightweight, agent-driven VLM/LLM inference framework tailored for cross-platform industrial edge deployment. EdgeFM removes non-essential features to reduce single-request latency, and encapsulates agent-tuned kernel optimizations as a modular library of reusable skills. By allowing direct invocation of these skills rather than waiting for closed-source implementations, it effectively closes the performance gap long dominated by proprietary toolchains. The framework natively supports mainstream platforms including x86 and NVIDIA Orin SoCs, and represents the first end-to-end VLA deployment on the domestic Horizon Journey platform, enhancing cross-platform portability. In most cases, it yields clearly better inference performance than conventional vendor-specific toolchains, achieving up to 1.49 times speedup over TensorRT-Edge-LLM on the NVIDIA Orin platform. Experimental results show that EdgeFM delivers favorable end-to-end inference performance, providing an open-source, production-grade solution for diverse edge industrial scenarios.

AI.tldr: EdgeFM is a lightweight, agent-driven VLM/LLM inference framework for cross-platform edge deployment, achieving portability and improved end-to-end performance by modular, reusable kernel-optimization skills, with up to 1.49x speedup over TensorRT-Edge-LLM on NVIDIA Orin.
AI.motivation: There is a need for deterministic low latency, resource-efficient edge inference and avoidance of hardware lock-in, enabling cross-platform deployment and open-source tooling.
AI.method: EdgeFM trims non-essential features, uses agent-driven configuration search to generate optimized low-level kernels for standard LLM operators, and encapsulates kernel optimizations as modular reusable skills that can be invoked directly. It supports x86, NVIDIA Orin, and Horizon Journey (first end-to-end VLA deployment on this platform).
AI.result: The framework delivers favorable end-to-end inference performance across edge scenarios and outperforms vendor-specific toolchains in many cases, exemplified by up to 1.49x speedup over TensorRT-Edge-LLM on NVIDIA Orin.
AI.conclusion: EdgeFM is a portable, open-source production-grade VLM/LLM inference framework for industrial edge settings, offering cross-platform compatibility and improved performance through agent-driven kernel optimization and modular skills.

title: AutoREC: A software platform for developing reinforcement learning agents for equivalent circuit model generation from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data
summary: This paper introduces AutoREC, an open-source Python package for developing reinforcement learning (RL) agents to automatically generate equivalent circuit models (ECMs) from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data. While ECMs are a standard framework for interpreting EIS data, traditional identification is typically based on manual trial-and-error, which requires domain experts and limits scalability, particularly in autonomous experimental pipelines such as self-driving laboratories. AutoREC addresses this challenge by formulating ECM construction as a sequential decision-making problem within a Markov Decision Process framework. It implements a Double Deep Q-Network with prioritized experience replay, along with a dedicated dead-loop mitigation strategy, to efficiently explore a complex action space for circuit generation. To demonstrate the capabilities of the platform, we trained an RL agent using AutoREC and evaluated its strengths and limitations across diverse datasets, while also discussing possible strategies to mitigate these limitations in future agent designs. The trained agent achieved a success rate exceeding $99.6\%$ on synthetic datasets and demonstrated strong generalization to unseen experimental EIS data from batteries, corrosion, oxygen evolution reaction, and CO$_2$ reduction systems. These results position AutoREC as a promising platform for adaptive and data-driven ECM generation, with potential for integration into automated electrochemical workflows.

AI.tldr: AutoREC is an RL-based framework to automatically construct equivalent circuit models from EIS data, using Double DQN with prioritized replay and a dead-loop mitigation strategy; achieves high synthetic success and generalizes to unseen experimental data, enabling adaptive ECM generation in automated electrochemical workflows.
AI.motivation: ECMs are essential for interpreting EIS data, but traditional identification is manual, domain-expert–driven, and scales poorly. An autonomous, data-driven approach is needed for scalable ECM generation in autonomous electrochemical setups.
AI.method: Formulates ECM construction as a sequential decision-making problem in an MDP. Implements a Double Deep Q-Network with prioritized experience replay and a dedicated dead-loop mitigation strategy. Trains an RL agent with AutoREC and evaluates on diverse synthetic and experimental EIS datasets across multiple systems.
AI.result: The RL agent achieved a success rate exceeding 99.6% on synthetic datasets and demonstrated strong generalization to unseen experimental EIS data from batteries, corrosion, oxygen evolution reaction, and CO2 reduction systems. The study also analyzes strengths/limitations and discusses mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: AutoREC is a promising platform for adaptive, data-driven ECM generation with potential for integration into automated electrochemical workflows and autonomous laboratory pipelines.

title: End-to-End Evaluation and Governance of an EHR-Embedded AI Agent for Clinicians
comment: 19 pages, 6 figures, 6 tables, submitted to npj Digital Medicine
summary: Clinical AI systems require not just point-in-time evaluation but continuous governance: the ongoing practice of monitoring, evaluating, iterating, and re-evaluating performance throughout deployment. We present an end-to-end framework of governance that integrates rubric validation, live deployment feedback, technical performance monitoring, and cost tracking, with controlled experimentation gating system changes before deployment. Applied to Hyperscribe, an EHR-embedded agent that converts ambient audio into structured chart updates, twenty clinicians authored 1,646 validated rubrics across 823 cases. Seven Hyperscribe versions were evaluated through controlled experiments, with median scores improving from 84% to 95%. Analysis of 107 live feedback entries over three months showed feedback composition shifting from 79% error reports and 14% positive observations to 30% errors and 45% positive observations as engineering interventions resolved failures. Median processing time per audio segment was 8.1 seconds with a 99.6% effective completion rate after retry mechanisms absorbed transient model errors. These results demonstrate that continuous, multi-channel governance of deployed clinical AI is both achievable and effective.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive end-to-end governance framework for clinical AI deployment, incorporating rubric validation, live feedback, performance monitoring, cost tracking, and gated controlled experimentation, demonstrated on Hyperscribe with improved rubric validity, live feedback quality, and processing efficiency.
AI.motivation: Clinical AI systems require continuous governance beyond one-off evaluations to ensure ongoing safety, reliability, and alignment with clinical workflows as they evolve.
AI.method: Develop an end-to-end governance framework integrating rubric validation, live deployment feedback, technical performance monitoring, cost tracking, and controlled experimentation gating. Applied to Hyperscribe (an EHR-embedded agent converting ambient audio to structured chart updates): 20 clinicians authored 1,646 rubrics across 823 cases; seven versions evaluated via controlled experiments; live feedback collected over 3 months; processing metrics recorded; retry mechanisms absorbed transient model errors.
AI.result: Median rubric scores improved from 84% to 95% across versions; live feedback shifted from 79% error reports and 14% positive observations to 30% error reports and 45% positive observations as interventions fixed failures; median processing time per audio segment 8.1 seconds; 99.6% effective completion rate after retries; multi-channel governance is feasible and effective in deployed clinical AI.
AI.conclusion: Continuous, multi-channel governance of deployed clinical AI is achievable and effective, enabling ongoing improvement, reliability, and alignment with clinical practice.

title: Uni-HOI:A Unified framework for Learning the Joint distribution of Text and Human-Object Interaction
summary: Modeling 4D human-object interaction (HOI) is a compelling challenge in computer vision and an essential technology powering virtual and mixed-reality applications. While existing works have achieved promising results on specific HOI tasks-such as text-conditioned HOI generation and human motion generation from object motion, they typically rely on task-specific architectures and lack a unified framework capable of handling diverse conditional inputs. Building on this, we propose Uni-HOI, a unified framework that learns the joint distribution among text, human motion, and object motion. By leveraging large language models (LLMs) and two motion-specific vector quantized variational autoencoders (VQ-VAEs), we convert heterogeneous motion data into token sequences compatible with LLM inputs, enabling seamless integration and joint modeling of all three modalities. We introduce a two-stage training strategy: the first stage performs multi-task learning on a large-scale HOI dataset to capture the underlying correlations among the three modalities, while the second stage fine-tunes the model on specific tasks to further enhance performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Uni-HOI achieves remarkable performances on multiple HOI-related tasks including text-driven HOI generation, object motion-driven human motion generation (optionally with text) and human motion-driven object motion prediction within a unified framework.

AI.tldr: Uni-HOI proposes a unified framework to model joint distributions among text, human motion, and object motion using LLMs and motion-specific VQ-VAEs, with a two-stage training regime, achieving multi-task HOI capabilities within a single system.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a unified framework for diverse conditional HOI inputs, enabling joint modeling of text, human motion, and object motion rather than task-specific architectures.
AI.method: Convert motion data into token sequences via two motion-specific VQ-VAEs and feed into a large language model (LLM). Train in two stages: (1) multi-task learning on a large HOI dataset to capture cross-modal correlations; (2) task-specific fine-tuning for better performance across tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art or strong performance on multiple HOI tasks, including text-driven HOI generation, object-motion-driven human motion generation (with optional text), and human-motion-driven object motion prediction within a single framework.
AI.conclusion: A promising step toward a versatile HOI framework capable of handling diverse conditional inputs and tasks by unifying modalities via tokenized representations and staged training.

title: BrainDINO: A Brain MRI Foundation Model for Generalizable Clinical Representation Learning
summary: Brain MRI underpins a wide range of neuroscientific and clinical applications, yet most learning-based methods remain task-specific and require substantial labeled data. Here we show that a single self-supervised representation can generalize across heterogeneous brain MRI endpoints. We trained BrainDINO, a self-distilled foundation model, on approximately 6.6 million unlabeled axial slices from 20 datasets encompassing broad variation in population, disease, and acquisition setting. Using a frozen encoder with lightweight task heads, BrainDINO supported transfer across tumor segmentation, neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental conditions classification, brain age estimation, post-stroke temporal prediction, molecular status prediction, MRI sequence classification, and survival modeling. Across tasks and supervision regimes, BrainDINO consistently equaled or exceeded natural-image and MRI-specific self-supervised baselines, with particularly strong advantages under label scarcity. Representation analyses further showed anatomically organized and pathology-sensitive feature structure in the absence of task-specific supervision. Our findings indicate that large-scale slice-wise self-supervised learning can yield a unified brain MRI representation that supports diverse neuroimaging tasks without volumetric pretraining or full-network fine-tuning, establishing a scalable foundation for robust and data-efficient brain imaging analysis.

AI.tldr: A single self-supervised slice-wise foundation model (BrainDINO) trained on millions of unlabeled brain MRI slices generalizes to diverse downstream tasks without full-network fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Reduce labeled-data requirements and create a single, scalable representation that generalizes across heterogeneous brain MRI endpoints; existing methods are typically task-specific and data-hungry.
AI.method: Train BrainDINO, a self-distilled foundation model, on ~6.6 million unlabeled axial MRI slices from 20 datasets with broad variation in population, disease, and acquisition. Use a frozen encoder with lightweight task heads. Evaluate transfer across tumor segmentation, neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental classification, brain age estimation, post-stroke temporal prediction, molecular status prediction, MRI sequence classification, and survival modeling. Compare to natural-image and MRI-specific self-supervised baselines. Perform representation analyses to examine anatomy- and pathology-sensitive structure.
AI.result: BrainDINO equaled or exceeded baselines across tasks and supervision regimes, with especially strong advantages under label scarcity. A frozen encoder with lightweight task heads achieved competitive or superior performance to full fine-tuning approaches. Representation analyses revealed anatomically organized and pathology-sensitive features in the absence of task-specific supervision.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale slice-wise self-supervised learning can yield a unified brain MRI representation that supports diverse neuroimaging tasks without volumetric pretraining or full-network fine-tuning, enabling robust and data-efficient brain imaging analysis.

title: Investigating More Explainable and Partition-Free Compositionality Estimation for LLMs: A Rule-Generation Perspective
summary: Compositional generalization tests are often used to estimate the compositionality of LLMs. However, such tests have the following limitations: (1) they only focus on the output results without considering LLMs' understanding of sample compositionality, resulting in explainability defects; (2) they rely on dataset partition to form the test set with combinations unseen in the training set, suffering from combination leakage issues. In this work, we propose a novel rule-generation perspective for compositionality estimation for LLMs. It requires LLMs to generate a program as rules for dataset mapping and provides estimates of the compositionality of LLMs using complexity-based theory. The perspective addresses the limitations of compositional generalization tests and provides a new way to analyze the compositionality characterization of LLMs. We conduct experiments and analysis of existing advanced LLMs based on this perspective on a string-to-grid task, and find various compositionality characterizations and compositionality deficiencies exhibited by LLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces a rule-generation approach to estimate LLM compositionality via generated mapping rules, addressing explainability gaps and combination leakage in existing tests, validated on a string-to-grid task.
AI.motivation: Current compositional generalization tests focus on output accuracy and rely on dataset partitions, leading to explainability gaps and leakage. A rule-based, complexity-theory–driven framework can provide deeper insight into LLMs' compositional understanding.
AI.method: LLMs are asked to generate a program that acts as rules for mapping datasets. The approach uses a complexity-based theory to quantify compositionality. Evaluation is conducted on a string-to-grid task to analyze compositionality characterizations and deficiencies.
AI.result: The study identifies varying compositionality characterizations and deficiencies across advanced LLMs, demonstrating that the rule-generation perspective captures aspects of compositionality not revealed by traditional tests.
AI.conclusion: A rule-generation, complexity-theory–driven framework offers explainable, leakage-resistant estimation of LLMs' compositionality and provides a new lens for analyzing how LLMs learn and generalize compositional mappings.

title: PRTS: A Primitive Reasoning and Tasking System via Contrastive Representations
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models advance robotic control via strong visual-linguistic priors. However, existing VLAs predominantly frame pretraining as supervised behavior cloning, overlooking the fundamental nature of robot learning as a goal-reaching process that requires understanding temporal task progress. We present \textbf{PRTS} (\textbf{P}rimitive \textbf{R}easoning and \textbf{T}asking \textbf{S}ystem), a VLA foundation model that reformulates pretraining through Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning. By treating language instructions as goals and employing contrastive reinforcement learning, PRTS learns a unified embedding space where the inner product of state-action and goal embeddings approximates the log-discounted goal occupancy, the probability of reaching the language-specified goal from the current state-action, quantitatively assessing physical feasibility beyond static semantic matching. PRTS draws this dense goal-reachability supervision directly from offline trajectories without reward annotations, and folds it into the VLM backbone via a role-aware causal mask, incurring negligible overhead over vanilla behavior cloning. This paradigm endows the high-level reasoning system with intrinsic goal reachability awareness, bridging semantic reasoning and temporal task progress, and further benefits goal-conditioned action prediction. Pretrained on 167B tokens of diverse manipulation and embodied-reasoning data, PRTS reaches state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO, LIBERO-Pro, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and a real-world suite of 14 complex tasks, with particularly substantial gains on long-horizon, contact-rich, and zero-shot novel-instruction settings, confirming that injecting goal-reachability awareness significantly improves both execution success and long-horizon planning of general-purpose robotic foundation policies.

AI.tldr: PRTS reframes Vision-Language-Action pretraining as goal-conditioned reinforcement learning, introducing a dense, offline-goal reachability signal and a role-aware mask to integrate into the VLM backbone, achieving state-of-the-art on manipulation benchmarks and strong zero-shot and long-horizon performance.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of supervised behavior cloning in VLA pretraining by encoding temporal task progress and goal reachability, enabling better planning and feasibility assessment in robotics.
AI.method: Treat language instructions as goals and train with contrastive reinforcement learning to learn a unified embedding space where the inner product of state-action and goal embeddings approximates the log-discounted goal occupancy (probability of reaching the goal). Leverage offline trajectories for dense supervision without reward labels, and embed into the VLM backbone via a role-aware causal mask with negligible overhead.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on LIBERO, LIBERO-Pro, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and a real-world suite of 14 tasks; exhibits substantial gains on long-horizon, contact-rich, and zero-shot novel-instruction settings.
AI.conclusion: Integrating goal-reachability awareness into VLA foundation models enhances temporal reasoning and planning, leading to better execution and long-horizon strategy while maintaining compatibility with existing pretrained visual-language models.

title: Towards All-Day Perception for Off-Road Driving: A Large-Scale Multispectral Dataset and Comprehensive Benchmark
summary: Off-road nighttime autonomous driving suffers from unreliable visible-light perception, making infrared modality crucial for accurate freespace detection. However, progress remains limited due to the scarcity of annotated infrared off-road datasets and the inter-frame inconsistencies inherent to current single-frame methods. To address these gaps, we present the IRON dataset, which, to our knowledge, is the first large-scale infrared dataset for off-road temporal freespace detection under all-day conditions, with strong support for nighttime perception. The dataset comprises 24,314 densely annotated infrared images with synchronized RGB images in diverse scenes and different light conditions. Building upon this dataset, we propose IRONet, a novel flow-free framework for temporal freespace detection that addresses inter-frame inconsistencies by aggregating historical context via a memory-attention mechanism and a carefully designed mask decoder. On our IRON dataset, IRONet achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 82.93%(+1.19%) IoU and 90.66%(+0.71%) F1 score at real-time inference. Remarkably, IRONet also exhibits robust generalization to RGB modalities on ORFD and Rellis datasets. Overall, our work establishes a foundation for reliable all-day off-road autonomous driving and future research in infrared temporal perception. The code and IRON dataset are available at https://github.com/wsnbws/IRON.

AI.tldr: IRON introduces a large infrared off-road temporal freespace dataset (IRON) and a memory-attention-based framework (IRONet) for temporal freespace detection, achieving state-of-the-art IoU/F1 with real-time inference and cross-modality generalization to RGB.
AI.motivation: Address unreliable visible-light perception in off-road night driving, the lack of annotated infrared off-road datasets, and inter-frame inconsistencies in single-frame freespace methods.
AI.method: IRONet is a flow-free, memory-attention-based framework that aggregates historical context for temporal freespace detection and uses a specially designed mask decoder to handle inter-frame inconsistencies.
AI.result: On the IRON dataset, IRONet achieves 82.93% IoU (+1.19) and 90.66% F1 (+0.71) at real-time inference; demonstrates robust generalization to RGB on ORFD and Rellis datasets; code and IRON dataset released.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a foundation for reliable all-day off-road autonomous driving in infrared temporal perception and provides resources to advance future research in this area.

title: Predicting Covariate-Driven Spatial Deformation for Nonstationary Gaussian Processes
summary:
Nonstationary Gaussian processes (GPs) are essential for modeling complex, locally heterogeneous spatial data. A common modeling approach is the spatial deformation method that warps the domain to recover isotropy. However, this static method does not account for changes in spatial correlation induced by covariates, limiting its ability to predict nonstationary GPs under new covariate conditions.
  To enable predictive modeling of the deformation method, we propose to model the spatial deformation as a function of covariates. The spaces of diffeomorphic deformations and Euclidean covariate vectors are connected by characterizing deformations as generated by velocity fields living in a Lie algebra. To overcome the estimation instability caused by high-order interactions between multiple covariates in a general Lie algebra, we prove that those interactions can be truncated with a moderate physical assumption. Based on the theoretical results, a concise functional form of deformations driven by multiple covariates can be established, and an efficient estimation-inference algorithm is developed for out-of-sample nonstationary GP prediction with limited covariate-deformation sample pairs. The effectiveness and generalizability of the method are demonstrated on a simulation study and two case studies, in the fields of manufacturing and geostatistics, respectively.

AI.tldr: Covariate-driven spatial deformation for nonstationary GPs using velocity-field deformations from Lie algebra; truncates high-order covariate interactions for stability; yields efficient out-of-sample prediction validated with simulations and two case studies.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary GPs model complex, locally varying spatial data; static spatial deformation fails to account for covariate-induced changes in spatial correlation, hindering predictive performance under new covariate conditions.
