AI.method: Model spatial deformation as a covariate-dependent function, linking diffeomorphic deformations to Euclidean covariates via velocity fields in a Lie algebra; apply a moderate-physical-assumption-based truncation of high-order covariate interactions; derive a concise deformation form; develop an efficient estimation/inference algorithm for out-of-sample prediction with limited covariate–deformation samples.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness and generalizability on simulations and two case studies in manufacturing and geostatistics; the method enables reliable predictions under unseen covariate configurations with limited data.
AI.conclusion: Covariate-driven deformation extends the spatial deformation approach to nonstationary GPs under changing covariate conditions, providing a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient framework for predictive inference.

title: Heterogeneous Scientific Foundation Model Collaboration
summary: Agentic large language model systems have demonstrated strong capabilities. However, their reliance on language as the universal interface fundamentally limits their applicability to many real-world problems, especially in scientific domains where domain-specific foundation models have been developed to address specialized tasks beyond natural language. In this work, we introduce Eywa, a heterogeneous agentic framework designed to extend language-centric systems to a broader class of scientific foundation models. The key idea of Eywa is to augment domain-specific foundation models with a language-model-based reasoning interface, enabling language models to guide inference over non-linguistic data modalities. This design allows predictive foundation models, which are typically optimized for specialized data and tasks, to participate in higher-level reasoning and decision-making processes within agentic systems. Eywa can serve as a drop-in replacement for a single-agent pipeline (EywaAgent) or be integrated into existing multi-agent systems by replacing traditional agents with specialized agents (EywaMAS). We further investigate a planning-based orchestration framework in which a planner dynamically coordinates traditional agents and Eywa agents to solve complex tasks across heterogeneous data modalities (EywaOrchestra). We evaluate Eywa across a diverse set of scientific domains spanning physical, life, and social sciences. Experimental results demonstrate that Eywa improves performance on tasks involving structured and domain-specific data, while reducing reliance on language-based reasoning through effective collaboration with specialized foundation models.

AI.tldr: Eywa extends language-centric agent systems by integrating domain-specific foundation models with a language-model reasoning interface, enabling cross-modal inference and planning across heterogeneous data. It offers EywaAgent (single-agent), EywaMAS (multi-agent integration), and EywaOrchestra (planner-based coordination across modalities), and shows improvements across physical, life, and social sciences by leveraging specialized models to complement language reasoning.
AI.motivation: Language-only interfaces limit applicability in scientific domains where domain-specific data modalities and models exist. There is a need for a framework that lets language models reason over non-linguistic data through integration with specialized foundation models to broaden the scope of agentic systems.
AI.method: Introduce Eywa and its components: EywaAgent (drop-in replacement for a single-agent pipeline), EywaMAS (integration into multi-agent systems with specialized agents), and EywaOrchestra (planner-based orchestration coordinating traditional and Eywa agents). A language-model-based reasoning interface guides inference over non-linguistic data, enabling domain models to participate in higher-level reasoning and decision-making. Evaluate across physical, life, and social sciences.
AI.result: Eywa improves performance on tasks involving structured and domain-specific data and reduces reliance on language-based reasoning by enabling effective collaboration between language models and specialized foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Eywa broadens the applicability of agentic systems in science by enabling heterogeneous data modalities to participate in high-level reasoning and planning, with planner-based orchestration validating cross-domain coordination between traditional and Eywa agents.

title: REVIVE 3D: Refinement via Encoded Voluminous Inflated prior for Volume Enhancement
summary: Recent generative models have shown strong performance in generating diverse 3D assets from 2D images, a fundamental research topic in computer vision and graphics. However, these models still struggle to generate voluminous 3D assets when the input is a flat image that provides limited 3D cues. We introduce REVIVE 3D, a two-stage, plug-and-play pipeline for generating voluminous 3D assets from flat images. In Stage 1, we construct an Inflated Prior by inflating the foreground silhouette to recover global volume and superimposing part-aware details to capture local structure. In Stage 2, 3D Latent Refinement injects Gaussian noise into the Inflated Prior's latent and then denoises it, using the prior's geometric cues to leverage the backbone's pretrained 3D knowledge. Furthermore, our framework supports image-conditioned 3D editing. To quantify volume and surface flatness, we propose Compactness and Normal Anisotropy. We validate Compactness and Normal Anisotropy through a user study, showing that these metrics align with human perception of volume and quality. We show that REVIVE 3D achieves state-of-the-art performance on a challenging flat image dataset, based on extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

AI.tldr: REVIVE 3D is a two-stage pipeline that generates voluminous 3D assets from flat images via an Inflated Prior stage (global volume + part-aware details) and a 3D Latent Refinement stage (noise injection and denoising), with new metrics Compactness and Normal Anisotropy, achieving state-of-the-art results and enabling image-conditioned 3D editing.
AI.motivation: Strong performance on expanding flat 2D cues into volumetric 3D representations is challenging due to limited 3D cues; prior work struggles with volume, fidelity, and editing; there is a need for robust, plug-and-play pipelines and perceptually aligned metrics.
AI.method: Stage 1: Inflated Prior — inflate foreground silhouette to recover global volume; add part-aware details for local structure. Stage 2: 3D Latent Refinement — inject Gaussian noise into latent of Inflated Prior, then denoise using prior's geometric cues and pretrained 3D knowledge. Supports image-conditioned 3D editing. Quantitative metrics: Compactness and Normal Anisotropy.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on a challenging flat image dataset via extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations. User study validates that Compactness and Normal Anisotropy align with human perception of volume and surface quality.
AI.conclusion: REVIVE 3D delivers a plug-and-play pipeline to convert flat images into voluminous 3D assets, introduces perceptually aligned volume metrics, and supports image-conditioned editing with strong empirical validation.

title: CoAX: Cognitive-Oriented Attribution eXplanation User Model of Human Understanding of AI Explanations
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) aims to improve user understanding and decisions when using AI models. However, despite innovations in XAI, recent user evaluations reveal that this goal remains elusive. Understanding human cognition can help explain why users struggle to effectively use AI explanations. Focusing on reasoning on structured (tabular) data, we examined various reasoning strategies for different XAI methods (none, feature importance, feature attribution) in the decision task of anticipating AI decisions (i.e., forward simulation). We i) elicited reasoning strategies from a formative user study, and ii) collected decisions from a summative user study. Using cognitive modeling, we implemented the processes underlying each reasoning strategy and evaluated their alignment with human decision-making. We found that our models better fit human decisions than baseline machine learning proxies, providing insights into which reasoning strategies are (in)effective. We then demonstrate how the fitted model can be used to form hypotheses and investigate research questions that are costly to study with real human participants. This work contributes to debugging human understanding of XAI, informing the future development of more usable and interpretable AI explanations.

AI.tldr: Uses cognitive modeling to map and evaluate human reasoning strategies in explainable AI for tabular data, combining formative and summative studies to assess how different XAI modalities (none, feature importance, feature attribution) align with decisions, and demonstrating that the models can generate testable hypotheses for future work.
AI.motivation: Despite advances in XAI, user understanding and effective use of explanations remain limited. Understanding which cognitive reasoning strategies users employ can explain why explanations fail to improve decisions and guide the design of more usable XAI methods, particularly for forward simulation on structured data.
AI.method: Formative study to elicit human reasoning strategies; summative study to collect decisions in a forward-simulation task; implement process-level cognitive models for each strategy and XAI condition; compare model predictions to human decisions against baselines (ML proxies); use the fitted model to generate hypotheses and to enable cost-effective exploration of research questions.
AI.result: Cognitive models provided better fits to human decisions than baseline ML proxies, revealing which reasoning strategies are effective or ineffective for different XAI conditions; the fitted model serves as a tool to test hypotheses that would be costly with real participants.
AI.conclusion: The work contributes to debugging human understanding of XAI and offers a framework to inform the development of more usable and interpretable explanations by diagnosing which reasoning strategies actually support decision-making and enabling hypothesis-driven, cost-efficient experimentation.

title: Leveraging Verifier-Based Reinforcement Learning in Image Editing
summary: While Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become a pivotal paradigm for text-to-image generation, its application to image editing remains largely unexplored. A key bottleneck is the lack of a robust general reward model for all editing tasks. Existing edit reward models usually give overall scores without detailed checks, ignoring different instruction requirements and causing biased rewards. To address this, we argue that the key is to move from a simple scorer to a reasoning verifier. We introduce Edit-R1, a framework that builds a chain-of-thought (CoT) verifier-based reasoning reward model (RRM) and then leverages it for downstream image editing. The Edit-RRM breaks instructions into distinct principles, evaluates the edited image against each principle, and aggregates these checks into an interpretable, fine-grained reward. To build such an RRM, we first apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a ``cold-start'' to generate CoT reward trajectories. Then, we introduce Group Contrastive Preference Optimization (GCPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm that leverages human pairwise preference data to reinforce our pointwise RRM. After building the RRM, we use GRPO to train editing models with this non-differentiable yet powerful reward model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our Edit-RRM surpasses powerful VLMs such as Seed-1.5-VL and Seed-1.6-VL as an editing-specific reward model, and we observe a clear scaling trend, with performance consistently improving from 3B to 7B parameters. Moreover, Edit-R1 delivers gains to editing models like FLUX.1-kontext, highlighting its effectiveness in enhancing image editing.

AI.tldr: Proposes Edit-R1: a chain-of-thought verifier-based reward model (RRM) for image editing, enabling fine-grained, interpretable rewards via decomposition into principles; introduces GCPO and GRPO; shows scaling benefits and improved editing models.
AI.motivation: RLHF for image editing suffers from weak, non-fine-grained rewards that ignore differing instruction requirements and can bias outcomes. A reasoning verifier-based reward model aims to provide interpretable, principle-based evaluation to guide editing tasks.
AI.method: Build Edit-R1 with a chain-of-thought (CoT) verifier-based RRM. Decompose instructions into distinct principles, evaluate edited images per principle, and aggregate into a fine-grained reward. Generate CoT reward trajectories via supervised fine-tuning (cold-start). Introduce Group Contrastive Preference Optimization (GCPO) to learn from human pairwise preferences and reinforce the RRM. Use GRPO to train editing models using the non-differentiable RRM.
AI.result: Edit-RRM outperforms strong vision-language models like Seed-1.5-VL and Seed-1.6-VL as an editing-specific reward model. Demonstrates a scaling trend: performance improves from 3B to 7B parameters. Edit-R1 also improves editing models such as FLUX.1-kontext.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides an interpretable, fine-grained reward framework for image editing that enhances RLHF-based editing systems, with observed scaling benefits and transfer to existing editing models.

title: PINN-Cast: Exploring the Role of Continuous-Depth NODE in Transformers and Physics Informed Loss as Soft Physical Constraints in Short-term Weather Forecasting
comment: 14 pages, 4 Figures, Accepted in 26th International Conference on Computational Science (ICCS 2026)
summary: Operational weather prediction has long relied on physics-based numerical weather prediction (NWP), whose accuracy comes at the cost of substantial compute and complex simulation workflows. Recent transformer-based forecasters offer efficient data-driven alternatives, however transformers are physics-agnostic models. Additionally, standard transformer encoders evolve representations through discrete layer updates that may be less suited to modeling smooth latent dynamics. In this work, we propose a continuous-depth transformer encoder for weather forecasting that integrates Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) dynamics within each encoder block. Specifically, we replace discrete residual updates with ODE-based updates solved using adaptive numerical integration. We also introduce a two-branch attention module that combines conventional patch-wise self-attention with an auxiliary branch that applies a derivative operator to attention logits, providing an additional change-sensitive interaction signal. To further align forecasts with governing principles, we propose a customized physics-informed training objective that enforces physical consistency as a soft constraint. We evaluate the proposed method against a standard discrete transformer baseline and an existing continuous-time Neural ODE forecasting variant, demonstrating the importance of PINN-Cast in short term weather forecasting.

AI.tldr: A continuous-depth transformer encoder using Neural ODE dynamics with a two-branch attention module and physics-informed training (PINN-Cast) to improve short-term weather forecasting, outperforming standard discrete transformers and prior continuous-time Neural ODE forecasts.
AI.motivation: Address the computational and accuracy trade-offs of physics-based NWP and the lack of physics in purely data-driven transformers; leverage smooth latent dynamics and physical constraints to improve short-term forecasts.
AI.method: Integrate Neural ODE-based updates in each encoder block; replace discrete residuals with adaptive ODE integration; add a two-branch attention: patch-wise self-attention plus derivative-augmented attention logits; train with physics-informed objective as soft constraint.
AI.result: Compared to discrete transformer baseline and existing continuous-time Neural ODE forecasts, PINN-Cast improves short-term forecast performance and promotes physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: PINN-Cast provides a viable framework combining continuous-depth modeling, derivative-based attention signals, and physics-informed learning to enhance weather forecasting efficiency and reliability; merits further exploration.

title: Safe Bilevel Delegation (SBD): A Formal Framework for Runtime Delegation Safety in Multi-Agent Systems
summary:
As large language model (LLM) agents are deployed in high-stakes environments, the question of how safely to delegate subtasks to specialized sub-agents becomes critical. Existing work addresses multi-agent architecture selection at design time or provides broad empirical guidelines, but neither provides a runtime mechanism that dynamically adjusts the safety-efficiency trade-off as task context changes during execution.
  We propose Safe Bilevel Delegation (SBD), a formal framework for runtime delegation safety in hierarchical multi-agent systems. SBD formulates task delegation as a bilevel optimization problem: an outer meta-weight network phi learns context-dependent safety-efficiency weights lambda(s) in [0,1]; an inner loop optimizes the delegation policy pi subject to a probabilistic safety constraint P(safe) >= 1-delta. The continuous delegation degree alpha in [0, 1] controls how much decision authority is transferred to each sub-agent, interpolating smoothly between full human override (alpha=0) and fully autonomous execution (alpha=1).
  We establish three theoretical results: (1) Safety Monotonicity--higher outer safety weight produces a weakly safer inner policy; (2) Inner Policy Convergence--projected gradient descent on the inner problem converges linearly under standard smoothness assumptions; (3) an Accountability Propagation bound that distributes responsibility across multi-hop delegation chains with a provable per-agent ceiling. We instantiate SBD in three high-stakes domains--medical AI (MIMIC-III), financial risk control (S and P 500), and educational agent supervision (ASSISTments)--specifying datasets, safety constraint sets, baselines, and evaluation protocols. This manuscript presents the formal framework and theoretical results in full; empirical validation following the protocols described herein is planned and will be reported in a forthcoming revision.

AI.tldr: Bilevel framework for runtime safe delegation in hierarchical LLM-agent systems (SBD) with outer context-sensitive safety weights and inner constrained delegation, plus three theoretical guarantees; empirical validation planned across medical, financial, and educational domains.
AI.motivation: Address the need for dynamic, runtime adaptation of safety-efficiency trade-offs when delegating subtasks to sub-agents in high-stakes, context-varying environments, beyond design-time guidelines.
AI.method: Formulate delegation as a bilevel optimization: an outer meta-weight network phi learns context-dependent safety-efficiency weights lambda(s) in [0,1]; an inner loop optimizes the delegation policy pi subject to a probabilistic safety constraint P(safe) >= 1-delta. The delegation degree alpha in [0,1] interpolates between human override (alpha=0) and autonomous execution (alpha=1).
AI.result: Three theoretical results are established: (1) Safety Monotonicity: increasing outer safety weight yields a weakly safer inner policy; (2) Inner Policy Convergence: projected gradient descent on the inner problem converges linearly under standard smoothness assumptions; (3) Accountability Propagation bound: distributes responsibility across multi-hop delegation chains with a per-agent ceiling. Datasets and evaluation protocols are specified for three domains (medical AI, financial risk control, educational supervision); empirical validation is planned and will be reported in a forthcoming revision.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a formal runtime framework for safe delegation in hierarchical multi-agent systems, with theoretical guarantees and domain-instantiated protocols; empirical validation is forthcoming in subsequent revisions.

title: Adjoint Inversion Reveals Holographic Superposition and Destructive Interference in CNN Classifiers
summary:
A foundational assumption in CNN interpretability -- that deep encoders suppress background pixels while classifiers merely select from a cleaned feature pool (the Spatial Funnel Hypothesis) -- remains untested due to spatial hallucinations in existing visualization tools. We address this by introducing a hallucination-free inversion framework built on magnitude-phase decoupling and Local Adjoint Correctors. Our method mathematically guarantees that the spatial gradient support of every reconstruction stems strictly from genuinely active channels.
  Using this framework as a geometric probe, we uncover the first pixel-level evidence of strong superposition in vision encoders. We show that per-channel inversions are uniformly holographic: positive and negative weight reconstructions are visually and energetically indistinguishable. However, their algebraic sum sharply concentrates on the foreground. This proves classification operates via destructive interference -- classifier weights cancel a shared background direction in pixel space and constructively assemble class-discriminative residuals, directly falsifying the Spatial Funnel Hypothesis.
  This interference model identifies the volume of the admissible interference subspace as the geometric quantity governing channel requirements. We prove this volume is dual to the GAP covariance determinant, yielding a covariance-volume channel selection algorithm with a $(1-1/e)$ approximation guarantee. This algorithm mathematically reveals out-of-distribution (OOD) failure as a measurable collapse of the covariance volume essential for interference-based classification. Our framework extends seamlessly to attention-based heads without retraining.

AI.tldr: Introduces a hallucination-free inversion framework (magnitude-phase decoupling + Local Adjoint Correctors) to test the Spatial Funnel Hypothesis; uncovers per-channel holography and destructive interference as the mechanism for classification; provides a covariance-volume based channel selection with a (1-1/e) guarantee and OOD diagnostics; extends to attention heads.
AI.motivation: To test a core assumption in CNN interpretability (Spatial Funnel Hypothesis) that encoders suppress background while classifiers pick from a cleaned feature pool. Existing visualizations suffer from spatial hallucinations, making the hypothesis untestable. The work seeks a mathematically rigorous, hallucination-free inversion to reveal true channel contributions and their spatial support.
AI.method: Develop a Hallucination-free Inversion framework using magnitude-phase decoupling and Local Adjoint Correctors to guarantee that the spatial gradient support of reconstructions comes only from genuinely active channels. Use per-channel inversions as a geometric probe to reveal interference patterns: positive/negative reconstructions are holographic; their algebraic sum concentrates on foreground. Introduce the concept of an admissible interference subspace; show its volume is dual to the GAP (Global Average Pooling) covariance determinant. Propose a covariance-volume channel selection algorithm with a (1-1/e) approximation guarantee. Demonstrate the OOD failure mode as a collapse of the covariance volume. Extend framework to attention heads without retraining.
AI.result: Pixel-level evidence of strong superposition in vision encoders: per-channel inversions are uniformly holographic and their sums yield foreground emphasis. Classification operates via destructive interference that cancels a shared background direction and assembles discriminative residuals. The interference subspace volume governs channel requirements and is mathematically linked to the covariance determinant; the proposed selection algorithm achieves a (1-1/e) approximation. OOD failure correlates with covariance-volume collapse. Framework generalizes to attention heads.
AI.conclusion: The Spatial Funnel Hypothesis is falsified under the proposed framework. Classification relies on interference-based reconstruction rather than background suppression. The work provides a principled, geometry-driven approach to channel selection and OOD diagnosis, with broad applicability including attention mechanisms.

title: A Short Note on Batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm for EigenDecomposition
summary: EigenDecomposition (ED) is at the heart of many computer vision algorithms and applications. One crucial bottleneck limiting its usage is the expensive computation cost, particularly for a mini-batch of matrices in deep neural networks. Our previous work proposed a dedicated QR-based ED algorithm for batched small matrices (dim${<}32$). This short paper targets the limitation and proposes a batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer based ED algorithm for larger matrices. The numerical test shows that for a mini-batch of matrices whose dimensions are smaller than $64$, our method can be much faster than the Pytorch SVD function.

AI.tldr: Proposes a batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer eigen-decomposition (ED) algorithm for larger matrices (up to around 64×64), achieving significant speedups over PyTorch's SVD on batched inputs.
AI.motivation: ED is central to many computer vision algorithms; batched ED is a bottleneck due to high computational cost, especially for mid-sized matrices. Previous work addressed small matrices (<32) with a QR-based ED but scalability to larger matrices remained an open issue.
AI.method: Develops a batch-oriented Divide-and-Conquer ED algorithm that operates efficiently on batched matrices, enabling parallelism and improved performance for matrix sizes up to about 64.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the Divide-and-Conquer ED method is much faster than PyTorch SVD for mini-batches with dimensions under 64.
AI.conclusion: The batch DCED provides a practical, scalable alternative to SVD for ED in batched deep learning tasks, expanding the feasible size range for efficient eigen decomposition in computer-vision pipelines.

title: TIO-SHACL: Comprehensive SHACL validation for TMF Intent Ontologies
summary: Intent-based networking promises to revolutionize telecommunications network management by enabling operators to specify high-level goals rather than low-level configurations. The TM Forum Intent Ontology (tio) provides a standardized vocabulary for expressing network intents, yet lacks formal validation mechanisms to ensure intent correctness before its admission. We present tio-shacl, the first comprehensive SHACL (Shapes Constraint Language) validation framework for the TMF Intent Ontology. Our contribution includes 56 node shapes and 69 property shapes across all 15 tio v3.6.0 ontology modules, a reusable constraint library with 25 parameterized SPARQL-based constraint components, and novel validation patterns for recursive logical operators, quantity-based constraints, and cross-expectation relationships. We pursued 100% vocabulary coverage (87 classes, 109 properties, 72 functions), cross-implementation compatibility across three major SHACL engines, and validation accuracy on a corpus of 133 test cases. tio-shacl is publicly available under MIT license at https://github.com/EricssonResearch/tio-shacl and enables automated syntactic and semantic validation of network intents, addressing a critical gap in the field.

AI.tldr: tio-shacl provides a comprehensive SHACL validation framework for the TM Forum Intent Ontology, with extensive shapes, a reusable SPARQL constraint library, cross-engine compatibility, and validated coverage across 133 test cases, addressing the lack of formal intent validation.
AI.motivation: Intent-based networking requires formal validation to ensure high-level intents are correctly expressed and admitted; tio lacked a validation mechanism; this work fills that gap.
AI.method: Developed tio-shacl as a SHACL-based validation framework for tio v3.6.0, including 56 node shapes and 69 property shapes across 15 modules, plus a library of 25 parameterized SPARQL constraint components; introduced patterns for recursive operators, quantity constraints, and cross-expectation relations; achieved full vocabulary coverage (87 classes, 109 properties, 72 functions) and validated across three SHACL engines on 133 test cases; released under MIT.
AI.result: Comprehensive, reusable constraint library; recursive and cross-expectation validation; cross-engine compatibility; publicly available; enables automated syntactic and semantic validation of network intents.
AI.conclusion: First comprehensive SHACL validation framework for the TMF Intent Ontology; closes a critical validation gap for intent admission and interoperability; broad community utility with MIT licensing.

title: Self-Supervised Learning of Plant Image Representations
summary: Automated plant recognition plays a crucial role in biodiversity monitoring and conservation, yet current approaches rely heavily on supervised learning, which is limited by the availability of expert-labeled data. Self-supervised learning (SSL) offers a scalable alternative, but existing methods and training protocols are largely designed for coarse-grained visual tasks and may not transfer well to fine-grained domains such as plant species recognition. In this work, we investigate SSL for plant image representation learning. We show that commonly used augmentations in SSL pipelines - such as Gaussian blur, grayscale conversion, and solarization - are detrimental in the context of plant images, as they remove subtle discriminative cues essential for fine-grained recognition. We instead identify alternative transformations, including affine and posterization, that are better suited to this domain. We further demonstrate that training SimDINOv2 on the iNaturalist 2021 Plantae subset yields significantly stronger representations than training on ImageNet-1K, highlighting the importance of domain-specific data for SSL. Our findings are consistent across both ViT-Base and ViT-Large architectures. Moreover, our models achieve competitive performance and sometimes outperform strong supervised baselines Pl@ntCLEF and BioCLIP on downstream plant recognition tasks in few-shot settings. Overall, our results highlight the critical importance of domain-adapted augmentation strategies and dataset selection in self-supervised learning, and provide practical guidelines for building scalable models for biodiversity monitoring.

AI.tldr: Domain-adapted self-supervised learning (SSL) for plants improves fine-grained recognition: standard SSL augmentations harm plant cues, while affine transformations and posterization are better; pretraining on iNaturalist Plantae outperforms ImageNet-1K; ViT-Base/Large show consistent gains and competitive few-shot results against Pl@ntCLEF and BioCLIP.
AI.motivation: Plant biodiversity monitoring suffers from limited expert-labeled data. SSL offers scalable representation learning, but many SSL pipelines use coarse-grained augmentations that may erase subtle plant-discriminative cues, potentially limiting transfer to fine-grained plant species recognition. There is a need to study domain-specific SSL for plants and assess appropriate data and augmentations.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate SSL augmentations on plant images, contrasting standard augmentations (Gaussian blur, grayscale, solarization) with domain-suited transformations (affine, posterization). Train SimDINOv2 on two pretraining datasets: iNaturalist 2021 Plantae subset and ImageNet-1K. Evaluate representations on downstream plant recognition tasks using ViT-Base and ViT-Large, and compare against strong supervised baselines (Pl@ntCLEF, BioCLIP) in few-shot settings.
AI.result: Standard SSL augmentations degrade performance on fine-grained plant tasks; domain-adapted augmentations (affine transformations, posterization) yield stronger representations. Plantae-pretrained models outperform ImageNet-pretrained ones for plant recognition. Across ViT-Base and ViT-Large, SSL models achieve competitive or superior performance to supervised baselines in few-shot plant tasks (Pl@ntCLEF, BioCLIP).
AI.conclusion: Domain adaptation is crucial for SSL in biodiversity contexts. Tailored augmentations and dataset choices enable scalable, strong plant representations, providing practical guidelines for deploying SSL-based models in biodiversity monitoring.

title: TypeBandit: Type-Level Context Allocation and Reweighting for Effective Attribute Completion in Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Heterogeneous graphs are widely used to model multi-relational systems, but missing node attributes remain a major bottleneck for downstream learning. In this paper, we identify and formalize type-dependent information asymmetry: the phenomenon that different node types provide substantially different levels of useful signal for attribute completion. Motivated by this observation, we propose TypeBandit, a lightweight, model-agnostic methodology for heterogeneous attribute completion. TypeBandit combines topology-aware initialization, type-level bandit sampling, and joint representation learning. It allocates a finite global sampling budget across node types, samples representative nodes within each type, and uses the resulting sampled type summaries as shared contextual signals during representation construction. By operating at the type level rather than over each target node's local neighborhood, TypeBandit keeps the adaptive state compact and practical for large heterogeneous graphs.
  A key advantage of TypeBandit is architectural flexibility. Rather than requiring a new heterogeneous graph neural network architecture, TypeBandit acts as a type-aware front end for representative heterogeneous GNN backbones, including R-GCN, HetGNN, HGT, and SimpleHGN. We further introduce a hybrid pretraining scheme that combines structural degree priors with feature propagation, yielding a more reliable initializer than degree-only pretraining. Under a fixed-split protocol on DBLP, IMDB, and ACM, TypeBandit provides dataset-dependent but practically meaningful gains. Additional ablation, stability, efficiency, semantic-propagation, and sampled OGBN-MAG experiments support TypeBandit as a practical strategy for heterogeneous attribute completion when type-specific information is unevenly distributed and sampling resources are limited.

AI.tldr: TypeBandit is a lightweight, model-agnostic approach for completing missing node attributes in heterogeneous graphs by exploiting type-dependent information asymmetry through type-level bandit sampling, topology-aware initialisation, and joint representation learning.
AI.motivation: Different node types contribute varying amounts of useful signal for attribute completion; missing attributes in heterogeneous graphs hinder downstream learning.
AI.method: Topology-aware initialisation; type-level bandit sampling to allocate a global budget across node types; use sampled type summaries as contextual signals; representation learning with existing heterogeneous GNN backbones; a hybrid pretraining scheme combining degree priors with feature propagation.
AI.result: Empirically yields dataset-dependent but meaningful gains on DBLP, IMDB, ACM under a fixed-split protocol; supported by ablation, stability, efficiency, semantic-propagation, and sampled OGBN-MAG experiments; demonstrates practicality when type-specific information is uneven and sampling resources are limited.
AI.conclusion: TypeBandit provides a flexible front-end for heterogeneous GNNs that leverages type-level information to improve attribute completion with limited sampling budgets; effective across multiple backbones and datasets.

title: Measurement Risk in Supervised Financial NLP: Rubric and Metric Sensitivity on JF-ICR
comment: 16 Pages, Submitted to IEEE Computational Intelligence in Financial Engineering and Economics (CIFEr) 2026, Tokyo, JP
summary: As LLMs become credible readers of earnings calls, investor-relations Q\&A, guidance, and disclosure language, supervised financial NLP benchmarks increasingly function as decision evidence for model selection and deployment. A hidden assumption is that gold labels make such evidence objective. This assumption breaks down when the benchmark ruler itself is sensitive to rubric wording, metric choice, or aggregation policy. We study this measurement risk on Japanese Financial Implicit-Commitment Recognition (JF-ICR; a pinned 253-item test split x 4 frontier LLMs x 5 rubrics x 3 temperatures x 5 ordinal metrics). Three findings follow. First, rubric wording materially changes model-assigned labels: R2--R3 agreement ranges from 70.0% to 83.4%, with the dominant movement near the +1 / 0 implicit-commitment boundary. This pattern is consistent with a pragmatic-boundary interpretation, but is not a validated linguistic-causality claim because the present rubric variants confound semantics, examples, and verbosity. Second, not every metric remains informative under the JF-ICR class distribution. Within-one accuracy is too easy because near misses receive credit and the majority class dominates; worst-class accuracy is too noisy because the rarest class has only two examples. Exact accuracy, macro-F1, and weighted \k{appa} are therefore the identifiable metrics under our operational rule. Third, ranking claims become more defensible only after this metric-identifiability audit: Bradley--Terry, Borda, and Ranked Pairs agree on the identifiable metric subset, while the full five-metric sweep produces disagreement on the closest pair. The contribution is not a new leaderboard, but a reporting discipline for supervised financial benchmarks whose gold labels exist and whose evaluation ruler still requires governance.

AI.tldr: Rubric wording and metric choices shape model labels and rankings in financial NLP benchmarks, revealing measurement risk; only a subset of metrics are reliable given the data distribution; calls for governance in reporting benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Gold labels in financial benchmarks may be artifacts of the evaluation rubric. Rubric semantics, exemplars, and verbosity can alter outcomes, risking objective decision evidence for model selection in finance.
AI.method: Assess JF-ICR (253 items) across 4 frontier LLMs, 5 rubrics, 3 temperatures, and 5 ordinal metrics. Analyze label agreement (R2–R3), metric informativeness, distribution effects, and ranking stability using Bradley–Terry, Borda, and Ranked Pairs.
AI.result: 1) Rubric wording materially shifts labels; agreement between R2 and R3 ranges 70.0–83.4%, with shifts near the implicit boundary. 2) Not all metrics are informative given class distribution; within-one accuracy is too easy; worst-class accuracy too noisy; exact accuracy, macro-F1, and weighted kappa are identifiable. 3) Ranking claims stabilize only after metric-identifiability audit; partial agreement across some methods, but full 5-metric sweep yields disagreements.
AI.conclusion: This work advocates reporting discipline and governance for supervised financial benchmarks, focusing on metric-identifiability rather than chasing a new leaderboard.

title: Residual Gaussian Splatting for Ultra Sparse-View CBCT Reconstruction
summary: While 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) offers explicit and efficient scene representations for cone-beam computed tomography reconstruction, conventional photometric optimization inherently suffers from spectral bias under ultra sparse-view conditions, leading to over-smoothing and a loss of high-frequency anatomical details. Since wavelet transforms provide rich high-frequency information and have been widely utilized to enhance sparse reconstruction, this work integrates wavelet multi-resolution analysis with 3DGS. To circumvent the mathematical mismatch between the strict non-negativity of physical X-ray attenuation and the bipolar nature of high-frequency wavelet coefficients, we propose Residual Gaussian Splatting (RGS). Methodologically, we introduce a spectrally-decoupled Gaussian representation that stratifies the volumetric field into a geometric base component and a residual detail component. This decomposition systematically transforms explicit high-frequency fitting into a physically consistent, implicit residual compensation task. Furthermore, we devise a spectral-spatial collaborative optimization strategy to coordinate the interplay between geometric anchoring and texture refinement, effectively preventing spectral crosstalk. Extensive experiments on clinical datasets demonstrate that RGS enables the reconstructed images to capture highly refined geometric textures. It successfully resolves the trade-off between artifact suppression and detail preservation, yielding superior visual fidelity in complex trabecular and vascular structures compared to existing neural rendering baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes Residual Gaussian Splatting (RGS) by coupling wavelet multi-resolution analysis with 3D Gaussian splatting for ultra-sparse-view CT, addressing spectral bias and non-negativity constraints through a spectrally-decoupled base+residual representation and a spectral-spatial optimization to avoid crosstalk; yields improved recovery of high-frequency textures in trabecular/vascular structures.
AI.motivation: Photometric optimization under ultra sparse-view CT suffers from spectral bias, causing over-smoothing and loss of high-frequency anatomical details. Wavelet analysis offers high-frequency information but must be reconciled with non-negative X-ray attenuation.
AI.method: Introduce a spectrally-decoupled Gaussian representation that separates the volumetric field into a geometric base component and a residual detail component; transform explicit high-frequency fitting into an implicit residual compensation task; employ a spectral-spatial collaborative optimization to coordinate geometry anchoring and texture refinement while preventing spectral crosstalk.
AI.result: Clinical datasets show that RGS captures finer geometric textures, improves artifact suppression and detail preservation, and achieves superior visual fidelity for complex trabecular and vascular structures compared with existing neural rendering baselines.
AI.conclusion: RGS effectively integrates wavelet-based high-frequency information with non-negative Gaussian splatting, enabling high-fidelity sparse CT reconstructions by disentangling geometry from residual texture and ensuring spectral-spatial coherence.

title: AG-TAL: Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss for Multiclass Segmentation of the Circle of Willis Using Large-Scale Multi-Center Datasets
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE JBHI
summary: Accurate multiclass segmentation of the Circle of Willis (CoW) is essential for neurovascular disease management but remains challenging due to complex vascular topology and variable morphology. Existing deep learning methods often suffer from vascular discontinuities and inter-class misclassification, while current topological loss functions incur prohibitive computational costs in 3D multiclass settings. To address these limitations, we propose an Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss (AG-TAL) and introduce a large-scale, multi-center CoW dataset with unified annotations to facilitate robust model training. AG-TAL specifically integrates a radius-aware Dice loss to address class imbalance in small vessels, a breakage-aware clDice loss that utilizes group convolutions to efficiently preserve local connectivity, and an adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss that leverages anatomical priors to enforce distinct boundaries between neighboring arteries. Evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation, AG-TAL achieved an average Dice score of 80.85% for all CoW arteries, with small arteries notably higher by 1.05-3.09% compared to state-of-the-art methods. Across six independent datasets, the performance of AG-TAL achieved Dice scores ranging from 74.46% to 81.17% for all CoW arteries, with improvements of 2.20% to 9.98% for small arteries compared to other methods. This study demonstrates the superiority of AG-TAL in identifying multiclass CoW arteries and its ability to generalize well to multiple independent datasets. Furthermore, reliability analyses and clinical applications in an Alzheimer's disease cohort validate the AG-TAL's robustness and its potential for discovering imaging-based morphological biomarkers.

AI.tldr: AG-TAL (Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss) is a multiclass Circle of Willis segmentation method that combines a radius-aware Dice loss for class imbalance in small vessels, a breakage-aware clDice loss using group convolutions to preserve connectivity, and an adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss leveraging anatomical priors to enforce distinct neighboring arteries. Trained on a large multi-center CoW dataset, it achieves ~80.85% Dice on all CoW arteries (with small arteries gaining 1.05–3.09%), and maintains 74.46–81.17% Dice across six independent datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods especially for small vessels; reliability analyses and Alzheimer's disease cohort validations support robustness and potential biomarker discovery.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in 3D multiclass CoW segmentation due to complex topology, variable morphology, vascular discontinuities, and class imbalance, while avoiding prohibitive computational costs of existing topology losses.
AI.method: Introduce AG-TAL, integrating three components: (1) radius-aware Dice loss for small-vessel imbalance, (2) breakage-aware clDice loss with group convolutions to efficiently preserve local connectivity, (3) adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss using anatomical priors to enforce distinct boundaries between neighboring arteries. Trained on a large-scale, multi-center CoW dataset with unified annotations; evaluated with 5-fold cross-validation and across six independent datasets.
AI.result: Average Dice score of 80.85% for all CoW arteries; small arteries improved by 1.05–3.09% over SOTA; six datasets yield 74.46–81.17% Dice with small-vessel gains of 2.20–9.98%; reliability analyses and Alzheimer's disease cohort validation show robustness and potential imaging-based biomarkers.
AI.conclusion: AG-TAL demonstrates superior multiclass CoW artery segmentation and generalizes well across datasets, with potential clinical utility in biomarker discovery for neurovascular diseases such as Alzheimer's.

title: Robust Learning on Heterogeneous Graphs with Heterophily: A Graph Structure Learning Approach
summary: Heterogeneous graphs with heterophily have emerged as a powerful abstraction for modeling complex real-world systems, where nodes of different types and labels interact in diverse and often non-homophilous ways. Despite recent advances, robust representation learning for such graphs remains largely unexplored, particularly in the presence of noisy or misleading connectivity. In this work, we investigate this problem and identify structural noise as a critical challenge that significantly degrades model performance. To address this issue, we propose a unified framework, Heterogeneous Graph Unified Learning (HGUL), which jointly handles heterophily and noisy graph structures. The framework consists of three complementary modules: a kNN-based graph construction module that recovers reliable local neighborhoods, a graph structure learning module that adaptively refines the adjacency by filtering noisy edges, and a heterogeneous affinity learning module that captures class-level relationships via an extended affinity matrix derived from a polynomial graph kernel. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that HGUL consistently outperforms existing methods on clean graphs and maintains strong robustness under varying levels of structural noise. The results further underscore the importance of jointly modeling heterophily and noise in heterogeneous graph learning.

AI.tldr: HGUL is a unified framework for robust representation learning on heterogeneous graphs with heterophily and structural noise, combining kNN-based local neighborhoods, adaptive graph structure learning, and heterogenous affinity learning via a polynomial graph kernel.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in robust representation learning for heterogeneous graphs under heterophily and noisy/ misleading connectivity.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) a kNN-based graph construction to recover reliable local neighborhoods; (2) a graph structure learning module to adaptively refine the adjacency by filtering noisy edges; (3) a heterogeneous affinity learning module that captures class-level relationships via an extended affinity matrix derived from a polynomial graph kernel.
AI.result: HGUL consistently outperforms existing methods on clean graphs and maintains robustness under varying levels of structural noise across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling heterophily and noise is crucial; HGUL provides a robust, unified approach for heterogeneous graph learning.

title: Revealing the Impact of Visual Text Style on Attribute-based Descriptions Produced by Large Visual Language Models
comment: Accepted by ICMR 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/XiaomengWang-AI/The-Impact-of-Visual-Text-style-on-Attribute-based-Descriptions-Produced-by-LVLMs
summary: When the visual style of text is considered, a wide variety can be observed in font, color, and size. However, when a word is read, its meaning is independent of the style in which it has been written or rendered. In this paper, we investigate whether, and how, the style in which a word is visualized in an image impacts the description that a Large Visual Language Model (LVLM) provides for the concept to which that word refers. Specifically, we investigate how functional text styles (readability-oriented, e.g., black sans-serif) versus decorative styles (display-oriented, e.g., colored cursive/script) affect LVLMs' descriptions of a concept in terms of the attributes of that concept. Our experiments study the situation in which the LVLM is able to correctly identify the concept referred to by a visual text, i.e., by a word or words rendered as an image, and in which the visual text style should not influence the attribute-based description that the LVLM produces. Our experimental results reveal that even when the concept is correctly identified, text style influences the model's attribute-based descriptions of the concept. Our findings demonstrate non-trivial style leakage from text style into semantic inference and motivate style-aware evaluation and mitigation for LVLM-based multimedia systems.

AI.tldr: Visual text style leaks into LVLMs' semantic descriptions: even when a word is correctly recognized, its attribute-based description is biased by font/style; this motivates style-aware evaluation and mitigation in LVLM-based systems.
AI.motivation: To determine whether and how visual text style (functional vs decorative) biases meaning extraction in LVLMs, assessing robustness of attribute-based descriptions.
AI.method: Render words as images in distinct text styles; prompt LVLMs to describe the concept; control for correct concept identification; compare attribute descriptions across styles; analyze style leakage.
AI.result: Attribute-based descriptions vary with text style despite correct concept identification, indicating non-trivial style leakage from visual style to semantic inferences.
AI.conclusion: Style-aware evaluation and mitigation are needed for LVLM-based multimedia systems to reduce bias introduced by visual text style.

title: Stable but Wrong: An Inference Limit in Galactic Archaeology
summary: Statistical inference in observational science typically relies on a fundamental assumption: as sample size increases and uncertainties decrease, the inferred results should converge to the true physical quantities. This assumption underpins the notion that big data lead to more reliable conclusions. In Galactic archaeology, stellar ages inferred from spectroscopic surveys are widely used to reconstruct the formation history of the Milky Way disk. The age metallicity relation (AMR) and its derived formation timescale are often regarded as key physical diagnostics of early disk evolution. This interpretation carries an implicit premise: that observational quality does not introduce systematic bias into age inference. Here we show that this premise may fail. Using a large sample of subgiant stars, we identify a region in the observational quality parameter space (signal-to-noise ratio and parallax precision) where the inferred formation timescale exhibits a systematic offset of 0.5-1 Gyr relative to an independent asteroseismic reference, while the statistical uncertainties remain small, thus producing a stable-but-wrong inference state.

AI.tldr: Observation quality can induce a systematic bias in inferred stellar ages and formation timescales in Galactic archaeology, even when uncertainties appear small; analysis of subgiants reveals a 0.5–1 Gyr offset in formation timescale compared with asteroseismic ages within a region of specific SNR and parallax precision.
AI.motivation: Testing the common assumption that increasing data quality and quantity generically reduce bias in age inferences; specifically whether observational quality biases AMR-derived formation timescales.
AI.method: Analyze a large sample of subgiant stars with spectroscopic ages; compare age inferences to independent asteroseismic ages across a grid of observational quality (SNR and parallax precision); identify regions where systematic offsets occur and quantify the magnitude relative to statistical uncertainties.
AI.result: Identification of a region in SNR–parallax quality space where the inferred formation timescale is biased by 0.5–1 Gyr relative to the asteroseismic reference, while formal uncertainties remain small.
AI.conclusion: Observational quality can introduce a measurable systematic bias in age-based diagnostics (AMR and formation timescales) in Galactic archaeology; robust interpretation requires accounting for quality-driven systematics and cross-calibration with independent age indicators.

title: Leading Across the Spectrum of Human-AI Relationships: A Conceptual Framework for Increasingly Heterogeneous Teams
summary:
What shapes a consequential decision when human and artificial intelligence work on it together? The answer is becoming harder to see. A decision may look human-led after AI has set the frame, or appear automated while human judgment still carries decisive force. This paper offers a leadership-facing spectrum to see those relationships within a bounded mandate: Pure Human, Centaur (human-dominant, with AI in the loop), Co-equal, Minotaur (AI-dominant, with humans in the loop), and Pure AI.
  The spectrum asks where leadership work occurs: who frames the problem, who redirects the work, and who can answer for what follows. The five positions are landmarks that help leaders recognize configurations as they layer, drift, or change in a single decision. The central risk is misrecognition: leaders may keep a human-centered story in place after decision-shaping authority has shifted elsewhere. They may believe oversight remains meaningful when it has become ceremonial, or keep humans in the loop when their involvement could make the decision worse. The framework introduces co-adaptability, the capacity of a configuration to improve as human and non-human participants adjust together, and places it within heterogeneous teaming, where participants may vary by number, substrate, model architecture, capability, speed, memory, and form of participation.
  The aim is practical: to help strategic leaders and those designing or deploying AI systems recognize the configuration at work, notice when it shifts, and judge whether it fits the decision before them. These configurations will shape how power, responsibility, and trust are distributed in organizational life. Whether the futures they help create remain governable and worth inhabiting will depend on leaders who can see, early enough, where and how consequential decisions are actually being shaped.

AI.tldr: A leadership-facing spectrum for shaping and governing consequential decisions as humans and AI collaborate, with five configurations (Pure Human, Centaur, Co-equal, Minotaur, Pure AI), emphasizing framing, control, and responsibility, and introducing co-adaptability within heterogeneous teaming to guide leaders in recognizing and managing shifts in decision-making authority.
AI.motivation: Address the ambiguous and evolving locus of control in human-AI decision making, where authority can shift between humans and algorithms, potentially undermining governance, accountability, and trust.
AI.method: Conceptual framework development: defines a spectrum of five configurations, identifies leadership tasks (frame problem, redirect work, accountable outcomes), introduces co-adaptability, and discusses misrecognition risks within heterogeneous teams.
AI.result: A practical diagnostic for leaders to map the current decision-configuration, detect shifts, and assess governance implications for power, responsibility, and trust in organizational settings.
AI.conclusion: Effective governance of consequential decisions requires leaders to recognize configurations early, monitor shifts, and ensure that the chosen configuration remains governable and aligned with desired futures.

title: RIHA: Report-Image Hierarchical Alignment for Radiology Report Generation
comment: Accepted by Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics (JBHI)
summary: Radiology report generation (RRG) has emerged as a promising approach to alleviate radiologists' workload and reduce human errors by automatically generating diagnostic reports from medical images. A key challenge in RRG is achieving fine-grained alignment between complex visual features and the hierarchical structure of long-form radiology reports. Although recent methods have improved image-text representation learning, they often treat reports as flat sequences, overlooking their structured sections and semantic hierarchies. This simplification hinders precise cross-modal alignment and weakens RRG accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose RIHA (Report-Image Hierarchical Alignment Transformer), a novel end-to-end framework that performs multi-level alignment between radiological images and their corresponding reports across paragraph, sentence, and word levels. This hierarchical alignment enables more precise cross-modal mapping, essential for capturing the nuanced semantics embedded in clinical narratives. Specifically, RIHA introduces a Visual Feature Pyramid (VFP) to extract multi-scale visual features and a Text Feature Pyramid (TFP) to represent multi-granularity textual structures. These components are integrated through a Cross-modal Hierarchical Alignment (CHA) module, leveraging optimal transport to effectively align visual and textual features across various levels. Furthermore, we incorporate Relative Positional Encoding (RPE) into the decoder to model spatial and semantic relationships among tokens, enhancing the token-level alignment between visual features and generated text. Extensive experiments on two benchmark chest X-ray datasets, IU-Xray and MIMIC-CXR, demonstrate that RIHA outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in both natural language generation and clinical efficacy metrics.

AI.tldr: RIHA introduces hierarchical cross-modal alignment for radiology report generation using multi-scale visual/text features and optimal transport, achieving state-of-the-art results on IU-Xray and MIMIC-CXR.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap that radiology reports have rich hierarchical structure (sections, sentences, tokens) but current RRG models treat reports as flat sequences, weakening cross-modal alignment and accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Visual Feature Pyramid (VFP) for multi-scale image features, Text Feature Pyramid (TFP) for multi-granularity text structures, and Cross-modal Hierarchical Alignment (CHA) using optimal transport to align features across paragraph, sentence, and word levels. Adds Relative Positional Encoding (RPE) in the decoder to improve token-level alignment.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on IU-Xray and MIMIC-CXR shows RIHA outperforms state-of-the-art models on natural language generation metrics and clinical efficacy metrics.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical, multi-level alignment with multi-scale features and principled cross-modal matching yields more accurate and clinically relevant radiology reports, suggesting promising directions for structured multimodal generation.

title: Detecting is Easy, Adapting is Hard: Local Expert Growth for Visual Model-Based Reinforcement Learning under Distribution Shift
summary: Visual model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents can perform well on the training distribution, but often break down once the test environment shifts. In visual MBRL, recognizing that a shift has occurred is often the easier part; the harder part is turning that recognition into useful action-level correction. We study several ways of responding to shift, including planning penalties, direct fine-tuning, global residual correction, and coarse gating. In our experiments, these approaches either do not improve closed-loop control or hurt in-distribution (ID) performance. Based on these negative results, we propose JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth. The method uses a frozen JEPA representation only for problem indexing, while cluster-specific residual experts add local action corrections on top of the original controller. The baseline controller itself is not modified. Using paired-bootstrap evaluation, we find that the original naive-preference variant is not stable under stricter testing. In contrast, the harder-pair variant produces statistically significant OOD improvements on all four evaluated shift conditions while preserving ID performance. The learned experts also remain useful when the same shift is encountered again, which supports the view of adaptation as incremental knowledge growth rather than repeated full retraining. We further show that automatic ID rejection can be achieved with simple density models, whereas fine-grained discrimination among OOD sub-families is limited by the representation. Overall, the results indicate that, for visual MBRL under distribution shift, the main challenge is not simply noticing that the environment has changed, but applying the right local action correction after the change has been recognized.

AI.tldr: JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth enables local residual experts to correct actions in visual MBRL under distribution shift by indexing a frozen JEPA representation, delivering OOD improvements with a harder-pair variant while preserving in-distribution (ID) performance; several baseline shift-handling methods fail, suggesting the key is local action correction after recognizing shift, and adaptation grows knowledge incrementally rather than retraining.
AI.motivation: Visual model-based RL often performs well on training data but degrades under distribution shift. Recognizing a shift is easier than producing useful action-level corrections, and naive or global adjustment methods can harm ID performance. A method that adds targeted, local corrections on top of a fixed controller could better adapt to shifts.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of several strategies for handling shift in visual MBRL: planning penalties, direct fine-tuning, global residual correction, and coarse gating. Propose JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth (JEPA-ILeg): freeze the JEPA representation for problem indexing and learn cluster-specific residual experts that provide local action corrections on top of the baseline controller with no modification to the baseline. Use paired-bootstrap evaluation; compare naive-preference vs harder-pair variants; assess generalization to repeated shifts and ID rejection via density models.
AI.result: Across four shift conditions, many baseline approaches do not improve or even hurt ID performance. The harder-pair JEPA-ILeg variant yields statistically significant OOD improvements on all four shifts while preserving ID performance. The learned local experts remain useful when the same shift recurs, supporting incremental knowledge growth rather than full retraining. Simple density models enable automatic ID rejection; fine-grained discrimination among OOD sub-families is limited by the representation.
AI.conclusion: The main challenge in visual MBRL under distribution shift is not merely detecting the shift but applying the appropriate local action correction after recognition. JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth demonstrates that localized, incremental knowledge growth can improve OOD performance without sacrificing ID performance, while general-purpose shift-handling methods may be ineffective.

title: InteractWeb-Bench: Can Multimodal Agent Escape Blind Execution in Interactive Website Generation?
summary: With the advancement of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and coding agents, the website development has shifted from manual programming to agent-based project-level code synthesis. Existing benchmarks rely on idealized assumptions, especially for well-structured, information-rich inputs and static execution settings. In contrast, real-world development is constrained by a critical bottleneck: the semantic misalignment between ambiguous, low-quality instructions from non-expert users and model understanding, which results in a failure mode that we term blind execution. To address this gap, we introduce InteractWeb-Bench, the first multimodal interactive benchmark for website generation under non-expert low-code user conditions. InteractWeb-Bench introduces four types of user agents and persona-driven instruction perturbations to systematically simulate diverse user behaviors, including ambiguity, redundancy, and contradiction, grounded in requirement engineering defect taxonomies. We develop an interactive execution environment for agents, featuring a unified action space comprising Clarify, Implement, Verify, and Submit, enabling iterative intent refinement, code synthesis, and visual feedback-based validation. Extensive experiments and analysis reveal that frontier MLLM-based agents remain trapped in blind execution, exposing limitations in intent recognition and adaptive interaction.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark, InteractWeb-Bench, evaluates multimodal LLMs in interactive, non-expert, low-code website generation, using four user agent types and an Clarify/Implement/Verify/Submit action space; findings show current frontier models suffer from blind execution due to intent misrecognition and insufficient adaptive interaction.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between idealized input assumptions and real-world non-expert user instructions; semantic misalignment causes failure modes in agent-based code synthesis; need interactive, requirement-engineering grounded evaluation.
AI.method: Introduce InteractWeb-Bench with four user-agent types and instruction perturbations (ambiguity, redundancy, contradiction) based on defect taxonomies; build an interactive execution environment with a unified action space (Clarify, Implement, Verify, Submit); enable iterative intent refinement, code synthesis, visual validation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments reveal frontier MLLMs remain trapped in blind execution; highlight limitations in intent recognition and adaptive interaction.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarks with interactive environment reveal critical gaps; provide framework to study and improve MLLMs for non-expert low-code website generation; suggest directions for improving intent understanding and interactive clarification.

title: World2Minecraft: Occupancy-Driven Simulated Scenes Construction
summary: Embodied intelligence requires high-fidelity simulation environments to support perception and decision-making, yet existing platforms often suffer from data contamination and limited flexibility. To mitigate this, we propose World2Minecraft to convert real-world scenes into structured Minecraft environments based on 3D semantic occupancy prediction. In the reconstructed scenes, we can effortlessly perform downstream tasks such as Vision-Language Navigation(VLN). However, we observe that reconstruction quality heavily depends on accurate occupancy prediction, which remains limited by data scarcity and poor generalization in existing models. We introduce a low-cost, automated, and scalable data acquisition pipeline for creating customized occupancy datasets, and demonstrate its effectiveness through MinecraftOcc, a large-scale dataset featuring 100,165 images from 156 richly detailed indoor scenes. Extensive experiments show that our dataset provides a critical complement to existing datasets and poses a significant challenge to current SOTA methods. These findings contribute to improving occupancy prediction and highlight the value of World2Minecraft in providing a customizable and editable platform for personalized embodied AI research. Project page:https://world2minecraft.github.io/.

AI.tldr: World2Minecraft converts real-world scenes into structured Minecraft environments via 3D semantic occupancy prediction, enabling scalable embodied AI research and downstream tasks like Vision-Language Navigation; introduces MinecraftOcc, a large-scale occupancy dataset (100,165 images from 156 indoor scenes) that complements existing data and challenges SOTA methods.
AI.motivation: Current simulators for embodied AI suffer from data contamination, limited flexibility, and reliance on scarce occupancy data with poor generalization. A scalable pipeline is needed to create diverse, customizable occupancy datasets and realistic environments for perception and decision-making research.
AI.method: 1) World2Minecraft converts real-world scenes into Minecraft-based environments using 3D semantic occupancy prediction. 2) A low-cost, automated pipeline to acquire large-scale occupancy datasets is proposed. 3) MinecraftOcc dataset: 100,165 images from 156 richly detailed indoor scenes, used to evaluate occupancy prediction and complement existing datasets. 4) Demonstrations on downstream tasks (Vision-Language Navigation) within reconstructed scenes.
AI.result: The dataset complements existing datasets and presents a significant challenge to current SOTA methods. Extensive experiments show improved occupancy prediction and demonstrate the utility of World2Minecraft as a customizable, editable platform for embodied AI research.
AI.conclusion: World2Minecraft provides a customizable, scalable platform for personalized embodied AI research and highlights the value of occupancy-driven reconstruction (MinecraftOcc) to improve and evaluate occupancy prediction in realistic, editable environments.

title: ChipLingo: A Systematic Training Framework for Large Language Models in EDA
summary:
With the rapid advancement of semiconductor technology, Electronic Design Automation (EDA) has become an increasingly knowledge-intensive and document-driven engineering domain. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong general capabilities, applying them directly to EDA remains challenging due to limited domain expertise, cross-tool knowledge confusion, and degraded retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) performance after domain training. To address these issues, this paper presents ChipLingo, a systematic training pipeline for domain-adapted LLMs tailored to EDA scenarios.
  ChipLingo consists of three stages: domain corpus construction with multi-source data curation and QA augmentation, domain-adaptive pretraining with comparisons of different parameter training strategies, and instruction alignment with RAG scenario training under diverse retrieval conditions. We also curate an internal benchmark, EDA-Bench, covering representative EDA tool scenarios, with plans for public release.
  Experiments show that ChipLingo-8B achieves 59.7% accuracy on EDA-Bench, outperforming the same-scale base model and some larger general-purpose models. ChipLingo-32B reaches 70.02%, approaching leading closed-source commercial models. Further analysis shows that QA augmentation improves domain performance, Partial FT offers a better balance between adaptation and general capability retention than LoRA, and explicit RAG scenario training mitigates the decline in retrieval utilization after domain training. These results demonstrate the practical value of systematic domain training for knowledge-intensive EDA tasks and provide a foundation for future EDA agents and external-knowledge-driven systems.

AI.tldr: ChipLingo introduces a three-stage domain-adaptive training pipeline for LLMs in EDA: domain data curation with QA augmentation, domain-adaptive pretraining comparing parameter strategies, and instruction alignment with RAG under varied retrieval. It also curates EDA-Bench. Results show strong gains: 8B model at 59.7% and 32B at 70.02% on EDA-Bench, with analysis highlighting QA augmentation, Partial FT over LoRA, and explicit RAG training to preserve retrieval usefulness.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between general LLM capabilities and EDA-specific requirements, caused by limited domain knowledge, cross-tool confusion, and degraded retrieval-augmented generation after domain specialization.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) domain corpus construction from multi-source data with QA augmentation; (2) domain-adaptive pretraining with experiments comparing parameter-efficient and full fine-tuning strategies; (3) instruction alignment including RAG-scenario training under varied retrieval settings; development of an internal EDA benchmark (EDA-Bench).
AI.result: Empirical gains on EDA-Bench: ChipLingo-8B = 59.7% accuracy; ChipLingo-32B = 70.02%, approaching leading closed-source models. QA augmentation improves domain performance. Partial FT yields better balance between adaptation and retention than LoRA. Explicit RAG-scenario training mitigates retrieval utilization decline after domain training.
AI.conclusion: Systematic domain training substantially benefits knowledge-intensive EDA tasks and can underpin future EDA agents and knowledge-driven systems; the internal EDA-Bench will enable reproducibility and public benchmarking.

title: Assessing Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Vascular Invasion: the PDACVI Benchmark
summary: Surgical resection remains the only potentially curative treatment for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), and eligibility depends on accurate assessment of vascular invasion (VI), i.e., tumor extension into adjacent critical vessels. Despite its importance for preoperative staging and surgical planning, computational VI assessment remains underexplored. Two major challenges are the lack of public datasets and the diagnostic ambiguity at the tumor-vessel interface, which leads to substantial inter-rater variability even among expert radiologists. To address these limitations, we introduce the CURVAS-PDACVI Dataset and Challenge, an open benchmark for uncertainty-aware AI in PDAC staging based on a densely annotated dataset with five independent expert annotations per scan. We also propose a multi-metric evaluation framework that extends beyond spatial overlap to include probabilistic calibration and VI assessment. Evaluation of six state-of-the-art methods shows that strong global volumetric overlap does not necessarily translate into reliable performance at clinically critical tumor-vessel interfaces. In particular, methods optimized for binary segmentation perform competitively on average overlap metrics, but often degrade in high-complexity cases with low expert consensus, either collapsing in volume or overextending at uncertain boundaries. In contrast, methods that model inter-rater disagreement produce better calibrated probabilistic maps and show greater robustness in these ambiguous cases. The benchmark highlights the limitations of volumetric accuracy as a proxy for localized surgical utility, motivating uncertainty-aware probabilistic models for preoperative decision-making.

AI.tldr: Public dataset and benchmark for uncertainty-aware tumor-vessel invasion (VI) assessment in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC); shows global segmentation overlap fails at critical interfaces; inter-rater disagreement modeling yields better-calibrated probabilistic maps and robustness in ambiguous cases.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of public VI datasets and high inter-rater variability at the tumor-vessel interface; move beyond voxel-wise accuracy to clinically meaningful, uncertainty-aware evaluation for preoperative staging.
AI.method: Introduce CURVAS-PDACVI dataset with five expert annotations per scan; propose a multi-metric evaluation framework including probabilistic calibration and VI assessment; evaluate six state-of-the-art methods, contrasting binary segmentation with uncertainty-aware approaches.
AI.result: High-level overlap metrics do not guarantee reliable performance at tumor-vessel interfaces; segmentation-optimized methods can perform well on average but fail in complex cases with low expert consensus; models that capture inter-rater disagreement provide better calibration and robustness in ambiguous regions.
AI.conclusion: Volumetric accuracy alone is insufficient as a proxy for surgical utility; uncertainty-aware probabilistic models are needed to inform preoperative decision-making and PDAC staging.

title: AdaBFL: Multi-Layer Defensive Adaptive Aggregation for Bzantine-Robust Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm in machine learning, which enables multiple clients to collaboratively train models under the guidance of a server without exposing private client data. However, FL's decentralized nature makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients can submit corrupted models to manipulate the system. To counter such attacks, although various Byzantine-robust methods have been proposed, these methods struggle to provide balanced defense against multiple types of attacks or rely on possessing the dataset in the server. To deal with these drawbacks, thus, we propose an effective multi-layer defensive adaptive aggregation for Bzantine-robust federated learning (AdaBFL) based on a novel three-layer defensive mechanism, which can adaptively adjust the weights of defense algorithms to counter complex attacks. Moreover, we provide convergence properties of our AdaBFL method under the non-convex setting on non-iid data. Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets validate the superiority of our AdaBFL over the comparable algorithms.

AI.tldr: AdaBFL: adaptive multi-layer defense for Byzantine-robust federated learning that reweights defense modules to counter complex attacks; provides convergence guarantees on non-convex, non-IID data with empirical superiority over baselines.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of existing Byzantine-robust FL methods that struggle against diverse attacks or rely on server-held datasets; introduce adaptive, layered defense without assuming centralized data access.
