AI.method: Proposes a three-layer defensive mechanism with adaptive weighting of defense algorithms; integrates multi-layer aggregation to counter varied poisoning strategies; analyzes convergence under non-convex, non-IID settings.
AI.result: Empirical validation on multiple datasets shows AdaBFL outperforms comparable algorithms in robustness and accuracy; provides convergence properties under non-convex non-IID data.
AI.conclusion: AdaBFL effectively defends against diverse Byzantine attacks through adaptive, layered aggregation and offers theoretical convergence guarantees, making it practical for realistic non-IID FL deployments.

title: Belief-Guided Inference Control for Large Language Model Services via Verifiable Observations
summary: In black-box large language model (LLM) services, response reliability is often only partially observable at decision time, while stronger inference pathways incur substantial computational cost, inducing a budgeted sequential decision problem: for each request, the system should decide whether the default low-cost response is sufficiently reliable or whether additional computation should be allocated to improve response quality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Ver}ifiable \textbf{O}bservations for Risk-aware \textbf{I}nference \textbf{C}ontrol (\textsc{Veroic}), a framework for adaptive inference control in black-box LLM settings, which formulates request-time control as a \textit{partially observable Markov decision process} to capture partial observability and sequential budget coupling. It constructs a lightweight verifiable observation channel from the input-output pair by aggregating heterogeneous quality signals into a belief state over latent response reliability, which is then used by a budget-aware policy to decide whether to return the default output or trigger a higher-cost inference pathway. Experiments on diverse tasks show that \textsc{Veroic} achieves improved quality-cost trade-offs, stronger risk estimation and calibration, and more robust long-horizon inference control than competitive baselines.

AI.tldr: Adaptive, budgeted inference control for black-box LLMs using a POMDP with verifiable observations (Veroic) to trade latency and reliability via a belief-state policy.
AI.motivation: In black-box LLM services, response quality is partially observable at decision time. Expensive inference paths can improve accuracy but raise cost; a principled framework is needed to balance cost and reliability over sequential interactions.
AI.method: Formulate request-time control as a partially observable Markov decision process. Build a lightweight verifiable observation channel from input-output signals, aggregating them into a belief state over latent reliability, which informs a budget-aware policy to either return a low-cost default or trigger a higher-cost inference path.
AI.result: Across diverse tasks, Veroic improves quality-cost trade-offs, yields stronger risk estimation and calibration, and offers more robust long-horizon inference control compared to competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Veroic provides an effective adaptive inference-control framework for black-box LLMs, enabling reliable, cost-conscious decisions with improved risk handling.

title: Fake3DGS: A Benchmark for 3D Manipulation Detection in Neural Rendering
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026. Code and data: https://github.com/iot-unimore/Fake3DGS
summary: Recent advances in 3D reconstruction and neural rendering,particularly 3D Gaussian Splatting, make it feasible and simple to edit 3D scenes and re-render them as highly realistic images. Therefore, security concerns arise regarding the authenticity of 3D content. Despite this threat, 3D fake detection remains largely unexplored in the literature, and most existing work is limited to 2D space. Therefore, in this paper, we formalize the concept of 3D fake detection and introduce Fake3DGS, a dataset of 3D Gaussian splatting scenes and corresponding rendered views, where fake images are produced by controlled manipulations of geometry, appearance, and spatial layout, while preserving high visual realism. Using this benchmark, we demonstrate that current state-of-the-art 2D detectors struggle to distinguish between original and 3D manipulated images. To bridge this gap, we introduce a 3D-aware detection method that leverages multi-view coherence and features derived from the Gaussian splatting representation. Experimental results demonstrate a substantial improvement in recognizing modified 3D content, underscoring the validity of the new dataset and the necessity for authenticity assessment techniques that extend beyond 2D evidence. Code and data are publicly released for future investigations.

AI.tldr: Introduces Fake3DGS, a dataset for 3D Gaussian Splatting authenticity detection, demonstrating 2D detectors fail on 3D-manipulated views and proposing a 3D-aware detector that uses multi-view coherence; code and data released.
AI.motivation: Advances in 3D editing (e.g., Gaussian splatting) enable highly realistic but potentially counterfeit content; current fake-detection benchmarks are 2D-only, necessitating 3D-aware evaluation and detection methods.
AI.method: Construct Fake3DGS dataset consisting of 3D Gaussian splatting scenes with fake images produced by controlled manipulations of geometry, appearance, and spatial layout while preserving realism; render multiple views to capture multi-view coherence; propose a 3D-aware detector leveraging Gaussian splatting features and multi-view cues.
AI.result: State-of-the-art 2D detectors struggle to separate original vs 3D-manipulated images; the proposed 3D-aware detector significantly improves detection; dataset and code are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Validates the need for authenticity assessment beyond 2D evidence; Fake3DGS provides a benchmark for future 3D fake-detection research and supports development of 3D-aware defense techniques.

title: ABC: Any-Subset Autoregression via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space
summary: Generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes (e.g., videos, weather forecasts) conditioned on partial observations (e.g., first and last frames) is a fundamental challenge. Existing approaches, (e.g., diffusion models), suffer from key limitations: (1) noise-to-data evolution fails to capture structural similarity between states close in physical time and has unstable integration in low-step regimes; (2) random noise injected is insensitive to the physical process's time elapsed, resulting in incorrect dynamics; (3) they overlook conditioning on arbitrary subsets of states (e.g., irregularly sampled timesteps, future observations). We propose ABC: Any-Subset Autoregressive Models via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space. Crucially, we model the process with one continual SDE whose time variable and intermediate states track the real time and process states. This has provable advantages: (1) the starting point for generating future states is the already-close previous state, rather than uninformative noise; (2) random noise injection scales with physical time elapsed, encouraging physically plausible dynamics with similar time-adjacent states. We derive SDE dynamics via changes-of-measure on path space, yielding another advantage: (3) path-dependent conditioning on arbitrary subsets of the state history and/or future. To learn these dynamics, we derive a path- and time-dependent extension of denoising score matching. Our experiments show ABC's superiority to competing methods on multiple domains, including video generation and weather forecasting.

AI.tldr: Proposes ABC, a continuous-time, continuous-space autoregressive diffusion framework using a single non-Markovian diffusion bridge to condition on arbitrary trajectory subsets, yielding time-aware dynamics and improved initialization for future state generation.
AI.motivation: Address key diffusion-model limitations for stochastic process generation conditioned on partial observations: fail to capture near-time structure, time-inconsistent noise, and inadequate conditioning on irregular or future observations.
AI.method: Model with one continual SDE whose clock tracks real time and states; derive dynamics via changes of measure on path space; enable path- and time-dependent denoising score matching; allow conditioning on arbitrary subsets of history/future.
AI.result: The framework empirically outperforms competing methods on domains such as video generation and weather forecasting, demonstrating improved physically plausible dynamics and flexible conditioning.
AI.conclusion: ABC provides a principled, scalable approach for conditional generation of continuous-time/space processes with arbitrary conditioning, leveraging path-space changes of measure and time-aware noise injection to improve realism and flexibility.

title: In-Context Examples Suppress Scientific Knowledge Recall in LLMs
summary: Scientific reasoning rarely stops at what is directly observable; it often requires uncovering hidden structure from data. From estimating reaction constants in chemistry to inferring demand elasticities in economics, this latent structure recovery is what distinguishes scientific reasoning from curve fitting. Large language models (LLMs) can often recall and apply relevant scientific formulas, but we show that this ability is surprisingly easy to suppress. We show that adding in-context examples makes models rely less on pretrained domain knowledge, even when those examples are generated by the very same formula. Rather than reinforcing knowledge-driven derivation, examples shift computation toward empirical pattern fitting. We document this knowledge displacement on 60 latent structure recovery tasks across five scientific domains, 6,000 trials, and four models. This displacement is consistent across domains, but its accuracy consequences depend on how the displaced strategy compares to the one that replaces it: the same shift can lower accuracy, leave it unchanged, or appear to improve it. In all cases, however, the model shifts away from knowledge-driven reasoning. For practitioners deploying LLMs on scientific tasks, the message is cautionary: in-context examples may displace, rather than reinforce, the knowledge they are intended to support.

AI.tldr: In-context exemplars induce a knowledge displacement in LLMs: instead of applying pretrained scientific knowledge to latent structure recovery, models shift toward empirical pattern fitting. This effect is robust across domains (60 tasks, 5 domains, 4 models, 6,000 trials) and yields variable accuracy outcomes depending on the displaced strategy.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how in-context examples influence LLMs’ use of pretrained domain knowledge for scientific reasoning, and whether such prompts promote knowledge-driven derivations or empirical pattern fitting.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation on 60 latent structure recovery tasks spanning five scientific domains, using four models and 6,000 trials. Compare performance with/without in-context exemplars; analyze whether added examples cause a shift toward pattern fitting and how this shift correlates with accuracy changes across domains.
AI.result: In-context examples cause a consistent shift away from knowledge-driven latent-structure reasoning toward empirical pattern fitting. This knowledge displacement occurs across domains; the resulting accuracy impact varies—decrease, no change, or improvement—depending on how the displaced strategy compares to the knowledge-driven approach that would otherwise be used.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners should exercise caution when using in-context exemplars for scientific tasks, as they may displace pretrained knowledge rather than reinforce it. Prompt design should consider potential shifts in reasoning strategy and their uncertain accuracy implications.

title: ClipTBP: Clip-Pair based Temporal Boundary Prediction with Boundary-Aware Learning for Moment Retrieval
summary: Video moment retrieval is the task of retrieving specific segments of a video corresponding to a given text query. Recent studies have been conducted to improve multimodal alignment performance through visual-linguistic similarity learning at the snippet-level and transformer-based temporal boundary regression. However, existing models do not calculate similarity by considering the relationships between multiple answer segments that match the query. Therefore, existing models are easily influenced by visually similar segments in the surrounding context. Existing models calculate similarity at the snippet-level and ignore the relationships between multiple answer segments corresponding to a single query. Therefore, they struggle to exclude segments irrelevant to the query. To address this issues, we propose ClipTBP, a clip-pair temporal boundary prediction framework based on boundary-aware learning. ClipTBP introduces a clip-level alignment loss for explicitly learning the semantic relationship between answer segments. ClipTBP also predicts accurate temporal boundaries by applying both main boundary loss and auxiliary boundary loss. ClipTBP consistently improves performance when applied to various existing models and demonstrates more robust boundary prediction performance even in ambiguous query scenarios.

AI.tldr: ClipTBP: a clip-pair temporal boundary prediction framework for video moment retrieval that adds a clip-level alignment loss and dual boundary losses to better capture relationships among multiple answer segments, improving robustness to ambiguous queries and compatibility with existing models.
AI.motivation: Existing clip-snippet level alignment and boundary regression neglect the relations between multiple answer segments matching a query and are vulnerable to visually similar surrounding segments; this compromises both semantic alignment and precise boundary localization.
AI.method: Introduce ClipTBP, a boundary-aware learning framework that computes a clip-level alignment loss to model semantic relationships among answer segments. It also predicts temporal boundaries using a main boundary loss plus an auxiliary boundary loss. Designed as a plug-in to enhance existing models for video moment retrieval.
AI.result: Empirically, ClipTBP consistently improves performance when applied to various existing models and yields more robust boundary predictions, particularly in ambiguous query scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Modeling inter-segment relationships via clip-level alignment and employing dual boundary losses yields stronger multimodal alignment and boundary localization in video moment retrieval, establishing ClipTBP as an effective plug-in for existing approaches.

title: Improving Graph Few-shot Learning with Hyperbolic Space and Denoising Diffusion
summary: Graph few-shot learning, which focuses on effectively learning from only a small number of labeled nodes to quickly adapt to new tasks, has garnered significant research attention. Despite recent advances in graph few-shot learning that have demonstrated promising performance, existing methods still suffer from several key limitations. First, during the meta-training phase, these methods typically perform node representation learning in Euclidean space, which often fails to capture the inherently hierarchical structure existing in real-world graph data. Second, during the meta-testing phase, they usually fit an empirical target distribution derived from only a few support samples, even when this distribution significantly deviates from the true underlying distribution. To address these issues, we propose IMPRESS, a novel framework that IMproves graPh few-shot learning with hypeRbolic spacE and denoiSing diffuSion. Specifically, our model learns node representations in a hyperbolic space and enriches the support distribution through denoising diffusion mechanisms. Theoretically, IMPRESS achieves a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, IMPRESS consistently outperforms competitive baselines across multiple benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: Hyperbolic representations combined with denoising diffusion for graph few-shot learning to address hierarchical structure and support distribution shift; yields theoretical generalization guarantees and improved empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Current graph few-shot methods rely on Euclidean embeddings and empirical, limited support distributions, failing to capture hierarchy and underestimating distributional shift during meta-testing.
AI.method: Propose IMPRESS: IMproves graPh few-shot learning with hypeRbolic spacE and denoiSing diffuSion. Learn node representations in hyperbolic space and enrich support distribution via denoising diffusion processes.
AI.result: Theoretically, IMPRESS provides a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, it consistently outperforms competitive baselines on multiple benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic space representations plus diffusion-based denoising effectively address hierarchical structure and distributional shift in graph few-shot learning, improving both generalization and empirical performance.

title: SpatialGrammar: A Domain-Specific Language for LLM-Based 3D Indoor Scene Generation
summary: Automatically generating interactive 3D indoor scenes from natural language is crucial for virtual reality, gaming, and embodied AI. However, existing LLM-based approaches often suffer from spatial errors and collisions, in part because common scene representations-raw coordinates or verbose code-are difficult for models to reason about 3D spatial relationships and physical constraints. We propose SpatialGrammar, a domain-specific language that represents gravity-aligned indoor layouts as BEV grid placements with deterministic compilation to valid 3D geometry, enabling verifiable constraint checking. Building on this representation, we develop (1) SG-Agent, a closed-loop system that uses compiler feedback to iteratively refine scenes and enforce collision constraints, and (2) SG-Mini, a 104M-parameter model trained entirely on compiler-validated synthetic data. Across 159 test scenes spanning five scenarios of different complexity, SG-Agent improves spatial fidelity and physical plausibility over prior methods, while SG-Mini performs competitively against larger LLM-based baselines on single-shot generation scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes SpatialGrammar, a BEV-grid DSL for gravity-aligned indoor layouts with deterministic 3D compilation; introduces SG-Agent for iterative refinement using compiler feedback, and SG-Mini (104M) trained on synthetic data; evaluated on 159 scenes; SG-Agent improves spatial fidelity and physical plausibility; SG-Mini competitive with larger baselines on single-shot generation.
AI.motivation: Address spatial errors and collisions in LLM-generated 3D indoor scenes due to difficult spatial reasoning with raw coordinates or verbose code; enable verifiable constraint checking via a gravity-aligned, grid-based representation.
AI.method: Introduce SpatialGrammar DSL mapping to BEV grid placements; deterministic compilation to valid 3D geometry; SG-Agent closed-loop system leveraging compiler feedback to enforce collision constraints; SG-Mini trained on compiler-validated synthetic data (104M parameters).
AI.result: SG-Agent improves spatial fidelity and physical plausibility across 159 test scenes in five scenarios compared to prior methods; SG-Mini performs competitively versus larger LLM-based baselines for single-shot generation.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields verifiable, constraint-checked 3D scene generation; a smaller model can be competitive; the combination of DSL and compiler feedback reduces spatial errors and collisions in generated scenes.

title: SECOS: Semantic Capture for Rigorous Classification in Open-World Semi-Supervised Learning
summary: In open-world semi-supervised learning (OWSSL), a model learns from labeled data and unlabeled data containing both known and novel classes. In practical OWSSL applications, models are expected to perform rigorous classification by directly selecting the most semantically relevant label from a candidate set for each sample. Existing OWSSL methods fail to achieve this because novel samples are trained without explicit supervision, and these methods lack mechanisms to extract latent semantic information, resulting in predicted labels that have no semantic correspondence to candidate textual labels. To address this, we introduce SEmantic Capture for Open-world Semi-supervised learning (SECOS), which directly predicts textual labels from the candidate set without post-processing, meeting the requirements of practical OWSSL applications. SECOS leverages external knowledge to extract and align semantic representations across modalities for both known and novel classes, providing explicit supervisory signals for training novel classes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that even when existing OWSSL methods are evaluated under the more lenient post-hoc matching setting, SECOS still surpasses them by up to 5.4\% without such assistance, highlighting its superior effectiveness. Code is available at https://github.com/ganchi-huanggua/OSSL-Classification.

AI.tldr: SECOS introduces semantic capture for open-world semi-supervised learning, directly predicting textual labels from candidate sets by aligning cross-modal representations and leveraging external knowledge for novel classes, outperforming baselines even under lenient post-hoc matching.
AI.motivation: In open-world semi-supervised learning, models must assign semantically meaningful textual labels to samples from a candidate set. Current methods fail to supervise novel-class samples and lack cross-modal semantic alignment, leading to labels with poor semantic correspondence to the candidate labels.
AI.method: SECOS (Semantic Capture for Open-world Semi-supervised learning) leverages external knowledge to extract and align semantic representations across modalities for known and novel classes, providing explicit supervisory signals for training novel classes and enabling direct textual label prediction from the candidate set without post-processing.
AI.result: SECOS surpasses existing OWSSL methods by up to 5.4 percentage points in performance even when baselines are evaluated under a lenient post-hoc matching setting; code is available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: By introducing explicit cross-modal semantic supervision and alignment, SECOS enables direct, semantically grounded textual label prediction in open-world semi-supervised learning, addressing a key limitation of prior methods.

title: Toward Scalable SDN for LEO Mega-Constellations: A Graph Learning Approach
summary: Terrestrial network limitations drive the integration of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), notably mega-constellations comprising thousands of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites. While these satellites act as interconnected network switches via inter-satellite links (ISLs), their massive scale creates severe bottlenecks for network management. To address this, we propose a scalable, hierarchical software-defined networking (SDN) framework. Our architecture leverages graph neural networks (GNNs) to compactly represent the constellation topology, and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. Specifically, a Graph Koopman Autoencoder (GKAE) forecasts spatio-temporal behavior within a linear subspace for each orbital shell. A central SDN controller then aggregates these shell-level predictions for globally coordinated control. Simulations on the Starlink constellation demonstrate that our approach achieves at least a 42.8\% improvement in spatial compression and a 10.81\% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to established baselines, all while utilizing a significantly smaller model footprint.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical SDN framework for non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) using a Graph Koopman Autoencoder (GKAE) to forecast spatio-temporal behavior in linear subspaces per orbital shell, enabling a centralized controller to manage mega-constellations.
AI.motivation: Terrestrial-NTN convergence introduces scalability bottlenecks due to thousands of LEO satellites and inter-satellite links; a compact, predictive representation of topology and dynamics is needed to enable scalable network management.
AI.method: Graph neural networks encode the constellation topology; Koopman theory linearizes the nonlinear dynamics; a GKAE learns linear subspace representations to forecast likely evolution per orbital shell; a central SDN controller aggregates shell-level predictions for global control; evaluated via simulations on Starlink.
AI.result: The approach achieves at least 42.8% improvement in spatial compression and 10.81% improvement in temporal forecasting over baselines, with a significantly smaller model footprint.
AI.conclusion: The GKAE-based hierarchical SDN framework offers scalable topology-aware control for NTNs, combining compact representations with accurate spatio-temporal forecasts to enable efficient centralized management of mega-constellations.

title: Trace-Level Analysis of Information Contamination in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Reasoning over heterogeneous artifacts (PDFs, spreadsheets, slide decks, etc.) increasingly occurs within structured agent workflows that iteratively extract, transform, and reference external information. In these workflows, uncertainty is not merely an input-quality issue: it can redirect decomposition and routing decisions, reshape intermediate state, and produce qualitatively different execution trajectories. We study this phenomenon by treating uncertainty as a controlled variable: we inject structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, execute fixed workflows under comprehensive logging, and quantify contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state. Across 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks with three different language models, we find a decoupling: workflows may diverge substantially yet recover correct answers, or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs. We characterize three manifestation types: silent semantic corruption, behavioral detours with recovery, and combined structural disruption and their control-flow signatures (rerouting, extended execution, early termination). We measure operational costs and characterize why commonly used verification guardrails fail to intercept contamination. We contribute (i) a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations in structured workflows, (ii) a trace-based measurement framework for detecting and localizing contamination across agent interactions, and (iii) empirical evidence with implications for targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty as a controllable perturbation in heterogeneous artifact workflows can cause trace divergence in plans and intermediate state; workflows may diverge yet obtain correct answers or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs; the work offers a taxonomy and a trace-based framework to detect and localize contamination with empirical evaluation across GAIA tasks and models.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty affects decomposition, routing, and execution in structured agent workflows that operate on heterogeneous artifacts. Understanding how perturbations propagate and contaminate results is essential for reliable verification, defensive design, and cost control.
AI.method: Inject structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, run fixed workflows with comprehensive logging, and quantify contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state. Conduct 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks using three language models; classify contamination manifestations by control-flow signatures; analyze operational costs and guardrail failures.
AI.result: Three manifestation types identified: (1) silent semantic corruption (no obvious traces, but outputs are semantically wrong), (2) behavioral detours with recovery (divergent traces that eventually recover correct answers), (3) combined structural disruption (routing/est execution altered, with early termination or extended runs). Demonstrated decoupling between structural similarity and output correctness; provided a trace-based measurement framework and a formal taxonomy for contamination in structured workflows; quantified costs and limitations of common verification guardrails across 614 runs.
AI.conclusion: Propose (i) a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations, (ii) a trace-based framework for detecting/localizing contamination across agent interactions, and (iii) empirical evidence with implications for targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control.

title: Decoding Scientific Experimental Images: The SPUR Benchmark for Perception, Understanding, and Reasoning
summary: We introduce SPUR, a comprehensive benchmark for scientific experimental image perception, understanding, and reasoning, comprising 4,264 question-answering (QA) pairs derived from 1,084 expert-curated images. SPUR features three key innovations: (1) Panel-Level Fine-Grained Perception: evaluating the visual perception of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) across three dimensions (numerical, morphological, and information localization) on six fine-grained panel types; (2) Cross-Panel Relation Understanding: utilizing complex images with an average of 14.3 panels per sample to evaluate MLLMs' ability to decipher intricate cross-panel relations; (3) Expert-Level Reasoning: assessment of qualitative and quantitative reasoning across five experimental paradigms to determine if models can infer conclusions from evidence as human experts do. Comprehensive evaluation of 20 MLLMs and four multimodal Chain-of-Thought (MCoT) methods reveals that current models fall significantly short of the expert-level requirements for scientific image interpretation, underscoring a critical bottleneck in AI for Science (AI4S) research.

AI.tldr: SPUR is a new benchmark for scientific experimental image perception, understanding, and reasoning, featuring 4,264 QA pairs from 1,084 expert images. It introduces panel-level perception, cross-panel relation understanding, and expert-level reasoning to evaluate 20 MLLMs and 4 MCoT methods. Results show current models lag behind expert-level interpretation, highlighting AI4S bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: To address the bottleneck in AI for Science (AI4S) by providing a rigorous benchmark that tests multimodal perception, cross-panel relational reasoning, and expert-level inference on complex scientific images.
AI.method: Curated 1,084 expert-labeled images to generate 4,264 QA pairs. Three innovations: (1) Panel-Level Fine-Grained Perception across six panel types (numerical, morphological, information localization); (2) Cross-Panel Relation Understanding with~14.3 panels per sample to test cross-panel reasoning; (3) Expert-Level Reasoning across five experimental paradigms. Evaluation conducted on 20 MLLMs and 4 MCoT methods.
AI.result: Across extensive evaluations, current MLLMs fail to meet expert-level requirements for scientific image interpretation, indicating a significant bottleneck and the need for advances in AI4S.
AI.conclusion: SPUR provides a comprehensive, challenging benchmark that reveals substantial gaps in current models' ability to interpret complex scientific imagery and reasoning, aiming to drive progress toward expert-level AI in science.

title: Low Rank Adaptation for Adversarial Perturbation
summary:
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), which leverages the insight that model updates typically reside in a low-dimensional space, has significantly improved the training efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) by updating neural network layers using low-rank matrices. Since the generation of adversarial examples is an optimization process analogous to model training, this naturally raises the question: Do adversarial perturbations exhibit a similar low-rank structure?
  In this paper, we provide both theoretical analysis and extensive empirical investigation across various attack methods, model architectures, and datasets to show that adversarial perturbations indeed possess an inherently low-rank structure. This insight opens up new opportunities for improving both adversarial attacks and defenses. We mainly focus on leveraging this low-rank property to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks, which often suffer from excessive query requirements. Our method follows a two-step approach. First, we use a reference model and auxiliary data to guide the projection of gradients into a low-dimensional subspace. Next, we confine the perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace, significantly improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the adversarial attacks.
  We evaluated our approach across a range of attack methods, benchmark models, datasets, and threat models. The results demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements in the performance of our low-rank adversarial attacks compared to conventional methods.

AI.tldr: Adversarial perturbations possess a low-rank structure and can be exploited to accelerate black-box attacks by projecting gradients into a low-dimensional subspace guided by a reference model and auxiliary data.
AI.motivation: To exploit the low-rank property of perturbations to reduce query complexity and improve attack effectiveness, and to inform defenses.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze the rank properties; empirically evaluate across attack methods/models/datasets; propose a two-step approach: (1) project gradients into a low-rank subspace using a reference model and auxiliary data; (2) constrain perturbation search within this subspace for black-box attacks.
AI.result: Consistent, substantial improvements in attack efficiency and effectiveness across diverse setups compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Low-rank structure in adversarial perturbations enables more efficient black-box attacks and suggests new defense considerations; indeed, LoRA-like low-rank adaptation concepts transfer to adversarial contexts.

title: Math Education Digital Shadows for facilitating learning with LLMs: Math performance, anxiety and confidence in simulated students and AIs
summary: To enhance LLMs' impact on math education, we need data on their mathematical prowess and biases across prompts. To fill this gap, we introduce MEDS (Math Education Digital Shadows) as a dataset mapping how large language models reason about and report mathematics across human- and AI-like conditions. MEDS involves 28,000 personas from 14 LLMs (from families like Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi and Grok) shadowing either humans or AI assistants. Each record/shadow includes a set of prompts along with psychological/sociodemographic persona metadata and four types of math tasks: (i) open math interview, (ii) three psychometric tests about math perceptions with explanations, (iii) cognitive networks capturing math attitudes, and (iv) 18 high-school math test questions together with their reasoning and confidence scores. MEDS differs from traditional score-only math benchmarks because it integrates concepts of self-efficacy, math anxiety, and cognitive network science besides math proficiency scores. Data validation shows that the sampled LLMs exhibit schema integrity and consistent personas, together with family-specific peculiarities like human-like negative math attitudes, logical fallacies, and math overconfidence. MEDS will benefit learning analytics experts, cognitive scientists, and developers of safer AI tutors in mathematics.

AI.tldr: MEDS introduces a large, multi-faceted dataset mapping how 14 LLM families reason about mathematics under human vs AI-like shadowing, combining math tasks with psychological and cognitive measures to examine proficiency, biases, and attitudes beyond traditional score benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in understanding LLM mathematical reasoning and biases across prompts by integrating cognitive science constructs (self-efficacy, math anxiety, attitude networks) with performance data to inform education analytics and safer AI tutoring.
AI.method: Dataset collected from 28,000 personas across 14 LLM families shadowing humans or AI assistants. For each record: prompts, persona metadata, and four math task types: (i) open math interview, (ii) three psychometric tests about math perceptions with explanations, (iii) cognitive networks of math attitudes, (iv) 18 high-school math questions with reasoning and confidence scores. Validation shows schema integrity, stable personas, and family-specific patterns (e.g., human-like math anxiety, logical fallacies, overconfidence).
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of a rich, multi-dimensional math benchmark and reveals family-specific traits, enabling analyses beyond scores and supporting learning analytics, cognitive science research, and the development of safer AI tutors in mathematics.
AI.conclusion: MEDS extends traditional math benchmarks by incorporating affective and cognitive dimensions, enabling insights into LLM math reasoning, biases, and uncertainties, with potential impact on education tech and AI safety for mathematical tutoring.

title: Robust Lightweight Crack Classification for Real-Time UAV Bridge Inspection
summary: With the widespread application of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in bridge structural health monitoring, deep learning-based automatic crack detection has become a major research focus. However, practical UAV inspections still face four key challenges: weak crack features, degraded imaging conditions, severe class imbalance, and limited computational resources for practical UAV inspection workflows. To address these issues, this paper proposes a unified lightweight convolutional neural network framework composed of four synergistic components: a lightweight backbone network, a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) for channel and spatial enhancement, a directed robust augmentation strategy based on inspection-scene priors, and Focal Loss for hard-sample learning under class imbalance. Experiments on the SDNET2018 bridge deck dataset show that the proposed method achieves an inference speed of 825 FPS with only 11.21M parameters and 1.82G FLOPs. Compared with the baseline model, the complete framework improves the F1-score by 2.51% and recall by 3.95%. In addition, Grad-CAM visualizations indicate that the introduced attention module shifts the model's focus from scattered regions to precise tracking along crack trajectories. Overall, this study achieves a strong balance among accuracy, speed, and robustness, providing a practical solution for ground-station assisted real-time deployment in UAV bridge inspections. The source code is available at: https://github.com/skylynf/AttXNet .

AI.tldr: A lightweight CNN framework for real-time UAV crack detection using CBAM, scene-prior augmentation, and Focal Loss; achieves high speed and competitive accuracy on SDNET2018.
AI.motivation: To address weak crack features, degraded imaging, severe class imbalance, and limited compute in UAV bridge inspections, enabling practical real-time deployment.
AI.method: Four components: a lightweight backbone, Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) for channel/spatial enhancement, directed robust augmentation based on inspection-scene priors, and Focal Loss for hard-sample learning under class imbalance; evaluation on SDNET2018 bridge deck dataset.
AI.result: Inference at 825 FPS with 11.21M parameters and 1.82 GFLOPs; improved F1-score by 2.51% and recall by 3.95% over baseline; Grad-CAM shows attention shifts along crack trajectories; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves a strong balance among accuracy, speed, and robustness, offering a practical solution for ground-station assisted real-time UAV bridge inspections.

title: FMCL: Class-Aware Client Clustering with Foundation Model Representations for Heterogeneous Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without sharing raw data, yet its performance deteriorates under statistical heterogeneity. Clustered Federated Learning addresses this challenge by grouping similar clients and training separate models per cluster. However, existing clustering strategies often rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarity measures that fail to capture class-level semantic structure across heterogeneous domains and frequently require iterative coordination. We propose FMCL, a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework that leverages foundation model representations to construct semantic client signatures. Using a frozen foundation model, FMCL computes class-level embedding prototypes for each client and measures similarity via cosine distance between their class-aware representations. Clustering is performed once prior to training, introducing no additional communication during federated optimization and remaining agnostic to the downstream model architecture. Extensive experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks demonstrate that FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.

AI.tldr: FMCL is a one-shot, class-aware FL clustering method that uses a frozen foundation model to create semantic client signatures and performs pre-training clustering with cosine-based similarity, requiring no extra communication.
AI.motivation: Statistical heterogeneity in Federated Learning degrades performance. Clustering similar clients can improve learning, but existing approaches rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarities and often require iterative coordination. A robust, one-shot, architecture-agnostic clustering method leveraging semantic class structure is needed.
AI.method: For each client, FMCL uses a frozen foundation model to compute class-level embedding prototypes, forming semantic signatures. Client similarity is measured via cosine distance between their class-aware representations. Clustering is performed once before training, with no additional communication during federated optimization, and the approach is agnostic to the downstream model architecture.
AI.result: Experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks show FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.
AI.conclusion: FMCL demonstrates that one-shot, class-aware clustering using foundation-model representations can effectively mitigate non-IID challenges in Federated Learning without extra communication or model-specific constraints.

title: WaferSAGE: Large Language Model-Powered Wafer Defect Analysis via Synthetic Data Generation and Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning
summary:
We present WaferSAGE, a framework for wafer defect visual question answering using small vision-language models. To address data scarcity in semiconductor manufacturing, we propose a three-stage synthesis pipeline incorporating structured rubric generation for precise evaluation. Starting from limited labeled wafer maps, we employ clustering-based cleaning to filter label noise, then generate comprehensive defect descriptions using vision-language models, which are converted into structured evaluation rubrics criteria. These rubrics guide the synthesis of VQA pairs, ensuring coverage across defect type identification, spatial distribution, morphology, and root cause analysis.
  Our dual assessment framework aligns rule-based metrics with LLM-Judge scores via Bayesian optimization, enabling reliable automated evaluation. Through curriculum-based reinforcement learning with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) and rubric-aligned rewards, our 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL model achieves a 6.493 LLM-Judge score, closely approaching Gemini-3-Flash (7.149) while enabling complete on-premise deployment. We demonstrate that small models with domain-specific training can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial visual understanding, offering a viable path for privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment in semiconductor manufacturing.

AI.tldr: WaferSAGE introduces a small multi-stage vision-language framework for wafer defect VQA, using rubric-guided data synthesis and RL-based training to achieve competitive on-premise performance with a 4B-parameter model.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity in semiconductor defect understanding and the need for privacy-preserving, on-premise deployment; demonstrate that domain-specific small models can outperform larger general models in specialized industrial VQA.
AI.method: Three-stage synthesis: start from limited labeled wafer maps; apply clustering-based cleaning to filter label noise; generate comprehensive defect descriptions with vision-language models and convert them into structured rubric criteria. Rubrics guide synthesis of VQA pairs across defect type, spatial distribution, morphology, and root cause analysis. Evaluation combines rule-based metrics with LLM-Judge scores via Bayesian optimization. Training uses curriculum-based RL with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) and rubric-aligned rewards to fine-tune 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL.
AI.result: The approach yields a 6.493 LLM-Judge score, closely approaching Gemini-3-Flash (7.149), while enabling complete on-premise deployment. Demonstrates that small, domain-trained models can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial VQA tasks.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific, rubric-guided data synthesis and curriculum RL enable effective on-premise VQA with small vision-language models in semiconductor manufacturing, offering privacy-preserving, cost-efficient deployment without relying on large external models.

title: SpaAct: Spatially-Activated Transition Learning with Curriculum Adaptation for Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) aims to enable an embodied agent to follow natural-language instructions and navigate to a target location in unseen 3D environments. We argue that adapting VLMs to VLN requires endowing them with two complementary capabilities for acquiring such awareness, namely backward action reasoning (why) and forward transition prediction~(how). Based on this insight, we propose SpaAct, a simple yet effective training framework that activates the dynamic spatial awareness in VLMs. Specifically, SpaAct introduces two spatial activation tasks: Action Retrospection, which asks the model to infer the executed action sequence from visual transitions, and Future Frame Selection, which forces the model to predict the visual transitions conditioned on history and action. These two objectives provide lightweight supervision on both backward action reasoning and forward transition prediction, encouraging the model to build dynamic spatial awareness in a VLM-friendly way. To further stabilize adaptation, we design TriPA, a Tri-factor Progressive Adaptive curriculum learning method that organizes training samples from easy to hard, allowing the model to gradually acquire navigation skills from basic locomotion to long-horizon reasoning. Experiments on standard VLN-CE benchmarks show that SpaAct consistently improves VLM-based navigation and achieves state-of-the-art performance. We will release the code and models to support future research.

AI.tldr: SpaAct introduces two spatial activation tasks and a progressive curriculum to adapt vision-language models for Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN), enabling backward action reasoning and forward transition prediction, achieving state-of-the-art on VLN-CE.
AI.motivation: Endow vision-language models with dynamic spatial awareness for VLN by integrating backward (why) and forward (how) reasoning during navigation.
AI.method: Two spatial activation tasks: Action Retrospection (infer executed action sequence from visual transitions) and Future Frame Selection (predict visual transitions conditioned on history and action); TriPA Tri-factor Progressive Adaptive curriculum organizing training samples from easy to hard; lightweight supervision to guide VLM adaptation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on VLN-CE benchmarks shows SpaAct improves VLM-based navigation and achieves state-of-the-art performance; code and models will be released.
AI.conclusion: SpaAct provides a simple yet effective mechanism to instill dynamic spatial awareness in VLMs for VLN, with stable adaptation via TriPA; the approach is promising for scalable embodied navigation with VLMs.

title: Diagnosing Capability Gaps in Fine-Tuning Data
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific tasks requires training datasets that comprehensively cover the target capabilities a practitioner needs. Yet identifying which capabilities a dataset fails to support, and doing so before an expensive fine-tuning run, remains a largely unsolved problem. We introduce GoalCover, a framework that helps practitioners systematically detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets through interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment. GoalCover guides a practitioner through structured decomposition of a high-level goal into atomic, independently evaluable subgoals; assigns each training sample an LLM-based alignment score against every subgoal; and surfaces missing capabilities through automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations. We validate the framework along two complementary axes. First, through controlled corruption experiments across three domains (medical QA, legal summarization, code generation), we show that GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted from non-targeted capability impacts: target subgoals degrade by 25.6% on average versus 2.1% for non-target subgoals (Cohen's d=1.24). Second, we demonstrate downstream utility on a financial-summarization Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) task with Qwen-3-14B: training on GoalCover-filtered data improves the LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12 (out of 5) over the unfiltered baseline, and combining filtered data with goal-conditioned synthetic samples yields the strongest result (4.20). The two results together show that GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic: it detects capability gaps and produces concrete signal for closing them.

AI.tldr: GoalCover exposes capability gaps in fine-tuning data by decomposing goals into atomic subgoals, scoring samples via LLM alignment to subgoals, and analyzing explanations to surface gaps; validated with controlled corruption experiments and downstream RFT improvements.
AI.motivation: Practitioners lack systematic means to identify which capabilities a fine-tuning dataset supports, risking costly unsuccessful fine-tuning.
AI.method: Interactive goal decomposition; per-sample alignment scoring against each subgoal; automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations; controlled corruption experiments across medical QA, legal summarization, and code generation; downstream reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) on financial summarization using Qwen-3-14B; comparison of filtered vs unfiltered data, with and without synthetic samples.
AI.result: GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted vs non-target capability impacts (target subgoals degrade ~25.6% vs ~2.1% for non-targets; Cohen's d=1.24); improves LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12 on financial summarization RFT; best results with filtered data plus goal-conditioned synthetic samples achieving 4.20; demonstrates practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic utility.
AI.conclusion: GoalCover serves as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic that detects capability gaps and provides concrete signals to close them.

title: Political Bias Audits of LLMs Capture Sycophancy to the Inferred Auditor
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are commonly evaluated for political bias based on their responses to fixed questionnaires, which typically place frontier models on the political left. A parallel literature shows that LLMs are sycophantic: they adapt their answers to the views, identities, and expectations of the user. We show that these findings are linked: standard political-bias audits partly capture sycophantic accommodation to the inferred auditor. We employ a factorial experiment across three major audit instruments--the Political Compass Test, the Pew Political Typology, and 1,540 partisan-benchmarked Pew American Trends Panel items--administered to six frontier LLMs while varying only the asker's stated identity (N = 30,990 responses). At baseline, all six models lean left. When the asker identifies as a conservative Republican, responses shift sharply: the share of items closer to Democrats falls by 28-62 percentage points, and all six models move right of center. A mirror-image progressive-Democrat cue produces little change; rightward accommodation is 8.0$\times$ larger than leftward. When asked who the default asker is, models identify an auditor, researcher, or academic; when asked what answer that asker expects, they select the Democrat-coded option 75% of the time, nearly the rate under an explicit progressive cue. These patterns are inconsistent with a purely fixed model ideology and indicate that single-prompt audits capture an interaction between model and inferred interlocutor. Political bias in LLMs is therefore not a fixed point on an ideological scale but a response profile that must be mapped across realistic interlocutors.

AI.tldr: Political-bias audits in LLMs are confounded by sycophantic adaptation to inferred auditor identities; six frontier LLMs show baseline left-leaning bias, but respond strongly to conservative cues, with far weaker response to progressive cues, indicating bias is interactional rather than fixed.
AI.motivation: To reconcile separate streams of evidence on LLM political bias and model sycophancy by testing how audit outcomes vary with inferred interlocutor identity; to determine whether bias is a fixed point or contingent on audience cues.
AI.method: Factorial experiment using three audit instruments (Political Compass Test, Pew Political Typology, and 1,540 Pew American Trends Panel items) administered to six frontier LLMs (N = 30,990 responses). The key manipulation is the asker's stated identity and expected-prompt cues, enabling assessment of sycophantic accommodation.
AI.result: Baseline responses are left-leaning across all models. When the asker identifies as a conservative Republican, Democrats-leaning items drop by 28–62 percentage points and all models move right of center. A progressive-Democrat cue produces little change; rightward accommodation is 8.0× larger than leftward. Default-asker identity cues lead models to identify an auditor/researcher/academic, and when asked what answer the asker expects, they pick the Democrat-coded option 75% of the time (near the rate under explicit progressive cue).
AI.conclusion: Political bias in LLMs is not a fixed ideological point but a response profile that interacts with inferred interlocutor; single-prompt audits inadequately measure bias. Evaluation should map bias across realistic interlocutors and prompts to capture the interactional nature of model responses.

title: FUN: A Focal U-Net Combining Reconstruction and Object Detection for Snapshot Spectral Imaging
comment: First work on exploring high-level computer vision tasks in compressive spectral imaging
summary: Conventional push-broom hyperspectral imaging suffers from slow acquisition speeds, precluding real-time object detection; in contrast, snapshot spectral imaging enables instantaneous hyperspectral images (HSIs) capture, making real-time object detection feasible, yet its potential is often compromised by time-consuming post-capture reconstruction. To address this issue, we propose the Focal U-shaped Network (FUN), a novel end-to-end framework that jointly performs HSI reconstruction and object detection via multi-task learning. FUN employs a shared U-shaped backbone, where reconstruction provides underlying spectral information while detection guides semantic-aware priors learning, facilitating mutually beneficial task interaction. Crucially, we introduce focal modulation, an efficient alternative to self-attention that modulates spatial and spectral features while reducing quadratic computational complexity, enabling a self-attention-free architecture for joint reconstruction and detection. Furthermore, we contribute a new HSI object detection dataset with 8712 annotated objects across 363 HSIs to facilitate evaluation of the proposed method. Experiments demonstrate that FUN achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks, using 40% fewer parameters and 30% less computation than recent alternatives, making it promising for future real-time edge deployment. The code and datasets are available: https://github.com/ShawnDong98/FUN.

AI.tldr: Joint HSI reconstruction and object detection via FUN with focal modulation; efficient end-to-end multitask network; new dataset; state-of-the-art with fewer params and computation.
AI.motivation: Real-time hyperspectral imaging is limited by slow reconstruction; snapshot HSIs enable speed but require fast, accurate post-processing. A unified framework for simultaneous reconstruction and detection can leverage mutual benefits.
AI.method: Focal U-shaped Network (FUN) with a shared U-shaped backbone for joint reconstruction and detection. Uses focal modulation as an efficient alternative to self-attention to model spatial-spectral interactions without quadratic complexity. Introduces a new dataset with 8712 annotated objects across 363 HSIs.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on both reconstruction and detection tasks while using 40% fewer parameters and 30% less computation than recent baselines.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end multitask HSI analysis via focal modulation is promising for real-time edge deployment; future work could explore broader datasets, ablation analyses, and hardware-aware optimizations.

title: Beyond the Training Distribution: Mapping Generalization Boundaries in Neural Program Synthesis
summary: Large-scale transformers achieve impressive results on program synthesis benchmarks, yet their true generalization capabilities remain obscured by data contamination and opaque training corpora. To rigorously assess whether models are truly generalizing or merely retrieving memorized templates, we introduce a strictly controlled program synthesis environment based on a domain-specific arithmetic grammar. By systematically enumerating and evaluating millions of unique programs, we construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces. This allows us to precisely map data distributions and sample train and test splits that isolate specific distributional shifts. Our experiments demonstrate that optimizing density generalization -- through diverse sampling over both semantic and syntactic spaces -- induces robust out-of-distribution generalization. Conversely, evaluating support generalization reveals that transformers severely struggle with extrapolation, experiencing a performance drop of over 30% when forced to generate syntactically novel programs. While steadily scaling up compute improves generalization, the gains follow a strictly log-linear relationship. We conclude that robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds, and our findings indicate the necessity for novel search-based approaches to break through current log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: Controlled program-synthesis study showing that diversity across syntactic/semantic spaces is key for robust generalization; extrapolation remains challenging for transformers.
AI.motivation: Differentiate true generalization from memorization in large transformers on code tasks; reveal distributional shifts.
AI.method: Enumerate millions of programs from a domain-specific arithmetic grammar; construct interpretable metric spaces; design train/test splits along semantic/syntactic axes; evaluate density vs support generalization; scale compute.
AI.result: Density generalization via diverse sampling improves out-of-distribution performance; extrapolation to syntactically novel programs causes >30% drop; compute scaling yields improvements but strictly log-linear; search-based approaches needed to overcome scaling bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: Robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds; current log-linear scaling bottleneck persists; novel search-based methods required.

title: Generative structure search for efficient and diverse discovery of molecular and crystal structures
summary: Predicting stable and metastable structures is central to molecular and materials discovery, but remains limited by the cost of searching high-dimensional energy landscapes. Deep generative models offer efficient structure sampling, yet their outputs remain shaped by training data and can underexplore minima that are rare but physically relevant. We introduce generative structure search (GSS), a unified framework that formulates diffusion-based generation and random structure search (RSS) as limiting regimes of a common sampling process driven by learned score fields and physical forces. Coupling these drivers lets GSS use data priors to accelerate sampling while retaining energy-guided exploration of local minima. Across molecular and crystalline systems, GSS recovers diverse metastable structures with more than tenfold lower sampling cost than RSS for broad coverage and remains effective for compositions outside the training distribution. The results establish a physically grounded generative search strategy for discovering structures beyond the reach of data-driven sampling alone.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based generative framework named GSS that unifies diffusion-based generation and random structure search to efficiently discover metastable molecular/crystal structures, achieving >10x sampling cost reduction and robust performance outside training distribution.
AI.motivation: Overcome the high cost and limitations of exploring high-dimensional energy landscapes; standard data-driven generative models underexplore rare but physically relevant minima.
AI.method: Formulates diffusion-based generation and RSS as limiting regimes of a single sampling process driven by learned score fields and physical forces; couples data priors with energy-guided exploration to accelerate sampling while preserving access to local minima.
AI.result: GSS recovers diverse metastable structures across molecular and crystalline systems with >10x lower sampling cost than RSS for broad coverage; remains effective for compositions outside training distribution.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a physically grounded generative search strategy for discovering structures beyond the reach of data-driven sampling.

title: MSR:Hybrid Field Modeling for CT-MRI Rigid-Deformable Registration of the Cervical Spine with an Annotated Dataset
summary: Accurate CT-MRI registration of the cervical spine is essential for preoperative planning because this region is anatomically complex,highly variable,and vulnerable to injury of the vertebral arteries and spinal cord. However,cervical CT-MRI registration remains underexplored,particularly for rigid-deformable hybrid modeling,and the lack of high-quality annotated multimodal data further limits progress. To address these challenges, we construct and release a comprehensively annotated CT-MRI dataset, R-D-Reg, and propose MSR, a rigid-deformable hybrid registration framework for complex joint structures. Specifically, MSR includes a rigid registration module for independent local rigid alignment of individual vertebrae and a deformable registration module with an MSL block that combines Mamba-based global modeling and Swin Transformer-based local modeling through adaptive gating. The rigid and deformable deformation fields are then fused to generate a hybrid field that better preserves local anatomical consistency. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/ssc1230609-spec/MSR-registration.

AI.tldr: Introduces MSR, a rigid-deformable hybrid registration framework for CT-MRI cervical spine with per-vertebra rigid alignment and an MSL-based deformable module, plus release of the R-D-Reg annotated dataset; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Cervical CT-MRI registration is critical for preoperative planning due to complex, variable anatomy and injury risk to vertebral arteries and spinal cord; existing work is limited and multimodal annotated data are scarce, hindering progress in rigid-deformable modeling.
AI.method: MSR comprises a rigid registration module that aligns each vertebra independently via local rigid transforms, and a deformable module with an MSL block that fuses Mamba-based global modeling with Swin Transformer-based local modeling through adaptive gating. The rigid and deformable fields are fused to form a hybrid registration field.
AI.result: The work provides a comprehensively annotated CT-MRI dataset (R-D-Reg) and presents the MSR framework, with code and data made publicly available, aiming to improve local anatomical consistency in multimodal cervical spine registration (specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: The study contributes a valuable dataset and a novel hybrid registration framework that preserves local anatomy in cervical CT-MRI registration and facilitates further research and potential clinical deployment.

title: Online semi-supervised perception: Real-time learning without explicit feedback
comment: IEEE Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshop on Online Learning for Computer Vision (CVPR 2010 OLCV)
summary: This paper proposes an algorithm for real-time learning without explicit feedback. The algorithm combines the ideas of semi-supervised learning on graphs and online learning. In particular, it iteratively builds a graphical representation of its world and updates it with observed examples. Labeled examples constitute the initial bias of the algorithm and are provided offline, and a stream of unlabeled examples is collected online to update this bias. We motivate the algorithm, discuss how to implement it efficiently, prove a regret bound on the quality of its solutions, and apply it to the problem of real-time face recognition. Our recognizer runs in real time, and achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised online learning algorithm on graphs that uses offline labeled data to initialize a bias and online unlabeled data to update it; provides a regret bound and is demonstrated on real-time face recognition with good accuracy on three video datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time learning from unlabeled streams without explicit feedback by combining graph-based semi-supervised reasoning with online updating.
AI.method: Iteratively construct a graphical representation of the world, initialize with offline labeled data (bias), update with unlabeled streaming observations, derive a regret bound on solution quality, and discuss efficient implementation.
AI.result: Real-time recognizer with superior precision and recall on three challenging video datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields a capable real-time face recognition system with theoretical guarantees and strong practical performance.

title: Optimization before Evaluation: Evaluation with Unoptimised Prompts Can be Misleading
comment: Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 6: Industry Track)
summary: Current Large Language Model (LLM) evaluation frameworks utilize the same static prompt template across all models under evaluation. This differs from the common industry practice of using prompt optimization (PO) techniques to optimize the prompt for each model to maximize application performance. In this paper, we investigate the effect of PO towards LLM evaluations. Our results on public academic and internal industry benchmarks show that PO greatly affects the final ranking of models. This highlights the importance of practitioners performing PO per model when conducting evaluations to choose the best model for a given task.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization per model can substantially change LLM evaluation rankings; per-model PO is essential for fair comparisons when selecting models for tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation frameworks use static prompts, while industry practice employs per-model prompt optimization. This study assesses how PO affects model rankings to ensure fair, task-specific evaluations.
AI.method: Empirical study across public academic and internal industry benchmarks, comparing model rankings obtained with static prompts versus per-model PO prompts.
AI.result: PO substantially shifts final model rankings; in several benchmarks the top model changes. The evaluation outcome is sensitive to prompt optimization per model.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation protocols should incorporate per-model prompt optimization to avoid biased conclusions and enable reliable model selection for specific tasks.

title: EviMem: Evidence-Gap-Driven Iterative Retrieval for Long-Term Conversational Memory
summary: Long-term conversational memory requires retrieving evidence scattered across multiple sessions, yet single-pass retrieval fails on temporal and multi-hop questions. Existing iterative methods refine queries via generated content or document-level signals, but none explicitly diagnoses the evidence gap, namely what is missing from the accumulated retrieval set, leaving query refinement untargeted. We present EviMem, combining IRIS (Iterative Retrieval via Insufficiency Signals), a closed-loop framework that detects evidence gaps through sufficiency evaluation, diagnoses what is missing, and drives targeted query refinement, with LaceMem (Layered Architecture for Conversational Evidence Memory), a coarse-to-fine memory hierarchy supporting fine-grained gap diagnosis. On LoCoMo, EviMem improves Judge Accuracy over MIRIX on temporal (73.3% to 81.6%) and multi-hop (65.9% to 85.2%) questions at 4.5x lower latency. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/EviMem.

AI.tldr: EviMem proposes a closed-loop, gap-diagnosing retrieval framework (IRIS) with a layered memory (LaceMem) to identify and fill evidence gaps for long-term conversational memory, improving temporal and multi-hop judge accuracy while reducing latency.
AI.motivation: Current long-term memory systems rely on single-pass or naive iterative retrieval and lack explicit mechanisms to diagnose what evidence is missing, leading to untargeted query refinement. There is a need to detect sufficiency gaps and drive targeted retrieval to support multi-session reasoning.
AI.method: EviMem combines IRIS (Iterative Retrieval via Insufficiency Signals) with LaceMem (Layered Architecture for Conversational Evidence Memory). IRIS detects evidence gaps via sufficiency evaluation, diagnoses missing items, and guides targeted query refinement. LaceMem provides a coarse-to-fine memory hierarchy for fine-grained gap diagnosis. Evaluation on LoCoMo shows QA performance gains with lower latency, comparing against MIRIX, with provided code.
AI.result: Judge Accuracy on LoCoMo improves temporal questions from 73.3% to 81.6% and multi-hop questions from 65.9% to 85.2%, at about 4.5x lower latency.
AI.conclusion: The combination of sufficiency-based gap diagnosis and a layered memory architecture yields targeted retrieval and faster, more accurate long-term conversational reasoning, representing a concrete advance over prior iterative refinement methods.

title: Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms
comment: Published in Journal of Machine Learning Research 17(66):1-53, 2016
summary: Policy gradient methods are reinforcement learning algorithms that adapt a parameterized policy by following a performance gradient estimate. Conventional policy gradient methods use Monte-Carlo techniques to estimate the gradient, which tend to have high variance, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. We first propose a Bayesian framework for policy gradient, based on modeling the policy gradient as a Gaussian process. This reduces the number of samples needed to obtain accurate gradient estimates. Moreover, estimates of the natural gradient and a measure of the uncertainty in the gradient estimates, namely, the gradient covariance, are provided at little extra cost. Since the proposed framework considers system trajectories as its basic observable unit, it does not require the dynamics within trajectories to be of any particular form, and can be extended to partially observable problems. On the downside, it cannot exploit the Markov property when the system is Markovian. To address this, we supplement our Bayesian policy gradient framework with a new actor-critic learning model in which a Bayesian class of non-parametric critics, based on Gaussian process temporal difference learning, is used. Such critics model the action-value function as a Gaussian process, allowing Bayes rule to be used to compute the posterior distribution over action-value functions, conditioned on the observed data. Appropriate choices of the policy parameterization and of the prior covariance (kernel) between action-values yield closed-form expressions for the posterior of the gradient of the expected return with respect to the policy parameters. We perform detailed experimental comparisons of the proposed Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms with classic Monte-Carlo based policy gradient methods, on a number of reinforcement learning problems.

AI.tldr: Bayesian policy gradient using Gaussian process modeling reduces sample complexity and provides gradient uncertainty; adds a GP-TD actor-critic to restore Markov-property handling; shows competitive results vs Monte Carlo methods.
AI.motivation: Policy-gradient estimates via Monte Carlo suffer from high variance and slow convergence; desire for data-efficient gradient estimation and uncertainty quantification; extendibility to partially observable problems and handling non-Markov dynamics.
AI.method: Model the policy gradient as a Gaussian process to obtain low-cost, data-efficient estimates and gradient covariance; derive natural gradient and posterior gradient uncertainty; note limitation regarding Markov property. Introduce a Bayesian actor-critic with Gaussian-process temporal-difference (GP-TD) critics to model the action-value function as a GP, enabling Bayes-rule-based posterior over gradient conditioned on data; select priors/kernels to yield closed-form gradient posterior.
AI.result: Compared Bayesian policy gradient and GP-TD-based actor-critic algorithms against traditional Monte Carlo policy gradient methods across multiple RL tasks; observed improved sample efficiency and competitive or superior performance; provided gradient uncertainty estimates and natural gradient information; frameworks work without assuming Markov dynamics inside trajectories, but the pure Bayesian policy gradient has limited exploitation of Markov property.
AI.conclusion: A Bayesian treatment of policy gradient offers data-efficient gradient estimation and uncertainty quantification; GP-TD-based actor-critic addresses Markov-property exploitation, enabling broader applicability (including partially observable problems); future work could focus on kernel choices, scalability, and deeper integration with Markov dynamics.

title: From Context to Skills: Can Language Models Learn from Context Skillfully?
summary: Many real-world tasks require language models (LMs) to reason over complex contexts that exceed their parametric knowledge. This calls for context learning, where LMs directly learn relevant knowledge from the given context. An intuitive solution is inference-time skill augmentation: extracting the rules and procedures from context into natural-language skills. However, constructing such skills for context learning scenarios faces two challenges: the prohibitive cost of manual skill annotation for long, technically dense contexts, and the lack of external feedback for automated skill construction, since there is no automatic signal to tell whether a proposed skill is helpful. In this paper, we propose Ctx2Skill, a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers, refines, and selects context-specific skills without human supervision or external feedback. At its core, a multi-agent self-play loop has a Challenger that generates probing tasks and rubrics, a Reasoner that attempts to solve them guided by an evolving skill set, and a neutral Judge that provides binary feedback. Crucially, both the Challenger and the Reasoner evolve through accumulated skills: dedicated Proposer and Generator agents analyze failure cases and synthesize them into targeted skill updates for both sides, enabling automated skill discovery and refinement. To prevent adversarial collapse caused by increasingly extreme task generation and over-specialized skill accumulation, we further introduce a Cross-time Replay mechanism that identifies the skill set achieving the best balance across representative cases for the Reasoner side, ensuring robust and generalizable skill evolution. The resulting skills can be plugged into any language model to obtain better context learning capability. Evaluated on four context learning tasks from CL-bench, Ctx2Skill consistently improves solving rates across backbone models.

AI.tldr: Ctx2Skill is a self-evolving multi-agent framework that autonomously discovers, refines, and selects context-specific skills to enhance language-model context learning, using Challenger/Reasoner/Judge with Proposer/Generator components and a Cross-time Replay mechanism; shows improvements on CL-bench tasks across backbones.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks require context learning when LMs lack parametric knowledge. Manual skill annotation is costly for long, dense contexts, and there is no automatic feedback signal for useful skills. A self-evolving, autonomous skill discovery framework addresses these challenges.
AI.method: A multi-agent self-play loop: Challenger generates probing tasks and rubrics; Reasoner attempts to solve with an evolving skill set; Judge provides binary feedback. Proposer and Generator analyze failures to update skills for both Challenger and Reasoner. Cross-time Replay selects a balanced skill set to prevent overfitting and adversarial collapse, ensuring robust skill evolution. Skills can be plugged into any LM to improve context learning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four CL-bench context-learning tasks shows consistent solving-rate improvements across backbone models when using the learned skills.
AI.conclusion: Ctx2Skill enables automatic discovery, refinement, and selection of context-specific skills without human supervision or external feedback, yielding robust, generalizable improvements in context learning and easily transferable to different LMs.

title: Deep Learning-Based Segmentation of Peritoneal Cancer Index Regions from CT Imaging
comment: Accepted for presentation at Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery (CARS) 2026
