summary: Peritoneal metastases are currently assessed using diagnostic laparoscopy to determine Sugarbaker's Peritoneal Cancer Index (sPCI), which works by dividing the abdomen into 13 regions and scoring each region based on tumor size. A recent consensus study defined 3D regions to facilitate a radiological PCI (rPCI), providing standardized anatomical regions for imaging-based assessment. Despite its clinical value, sPCI is invasive and lacks a standardized imaging counterpart. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based approach to automatically segment the rPCI regions on CT. We evaluate nnU-Net and Swin UNETR on 62 CT scans with rPCI regions manually annotated by three clinical researchers and validated by two expert radiologists. Performance was assessed using five-fold cross-validation with the Dice Similarity Coefficient (Dice), 95th percentile Hausdorff distance and Average Surface Distance. nnU-Net achieved an overall Dice of 0.82, approaching interobserver agreement (0.88) and outperforming Swin UNETR (0.76), with remaining challenges primarily in right flank and small-bowel regions. These results demonstrate feasibility of automated rPCI segmentation, laying the foundation for non-invasive, imaging-based assessment.

AI.tldr: Feasible deep-learning segmentation of radiological PCI (rPCI) regions on CT; nnU-Net achieves Dice 0.82, near interobserver agreement (0.88), outperforming Swin UNETR (0.76); dataset of 62 CTs; five-fold CV; limitations include right flank and small-bowel regions.
AI.motivation: Replace invasive diagnostic laparoscopy-based sPCI with standardized, imaging-based rPCI using automated segmentation to enable non-invasive assessment and comparability across clinics.
AI.method: Data: 62 CT scans with rPCI regions manually annotated by three clinical researchers and validated by two expert radiologists. Models compared: nnU-Net and Swin UNETR. Evaluation: five-fold cross-validation using Dice, 95th percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95), and Average Surface Distance (ASD).
AI.result: nnU-Net: Dice 0.82 overall; closer to interobserver (0.88) and better than Swin UNETR (0.76). Remaining challenges in right flank and small-bowel regions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of automated rPCI segmentation on CT and lays groundwork for non-invasive imaging-based PCI assessment; performance is approaching human agreement, but regional difficulties and generalizability require further work.

title: Learning from a single labeled face and a stream of unlabeled data
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG 2013). doi:10.1109/FG.2013.6553720
summary: Face recognition from a single image per person is a challenging problem because the training sample is extremely small. We consider a variation of this problem. In our problem, we recognize only one person, and there are no labeled data for any other person. This setting naturally arises in authentication on personal computers and mobile devices, and poses additional challenges because it lacks negative examples. We formalize our problem as one-class classification, and propose and analyze an algorithm that learns a non-parametric model of the face from a single labeled image and a stream of unlabeled data. In many domains, for instance when a person interacts with a computer with a camera, unlabeled data are abundant and easy to utilize. This is the first paper that investigates how these data can help in learning better models in the single-image-per-person setting. Our method is evaluated on a dataset of 43 people and we show that these people can be recognized 90% of time at nearly zero false positives. This recall is 25+% higher than the recall of our best performing baseline. Finally, we conduct a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of our algorithm and provide a guideline for setting its parameters in practice.

AI.tldr: One-class face recognition from a single labeled image per person using a stream of unlabeled data to learn a non-parametric model; evaluated on 43 people with 90% recall at near-zero false positives, outperforming a strong baseline; includes sensitivity analysis and practical parameter guidelines.
AI.motivation: Address the realistic setting of authentication on personal devices where only one labeled example per person exists and negative samples are absent, making standard supervised learning and negative-class formulations ineffective.
AI.method: A non-parametric one-class classifier that learns from a single labeled image and leverages a stream of unlabeled data to refine the model. This is the first study to exploit unlabeled data in the single-image-per-person scenario for face recognition.
AI.result: On a dataset of 43 individuals, the method achieves about 90% recall while maintaining nearly zero false positives. The recall surpasses the best-performing baseline by over 25 percentage points. A comprehensive sensitivity analysis provides practical parameter settings.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled data in the deployment environment can significantly improve one-shot face recognition performance for a single subject per model, enabling practical authentication with robust precision-recall characteristics; the study also offers guidance for parameter tuning.

title: Fairness for distribution network operations and planning
comment: 16 pages, 0 figures, 2 tables, CIRED Conference Workshop Brussels 2026
summary: The incorporation of fairness into the distribution network (DN) planning and operation has become a key goal of recent studies. The cost of implementing fairness, denominated the price of fairness (PoF), covers the efficiency that is renounced for attaining social cohesion through fair outcomes. Locational disparity makes fairness schemes emerge to level the consumers playing field. However, fairness encompasses a range of notions. From egalitarian to merit-based criteria, various metrics are implemented as a tool for measuring equitable utility distribution. These have different mathematical complexities, from linear to non-linear programming cases, which affect their overall applicability. Hence, this study compiles the overarching fairness notions and metrics, reviewing how these affect stakeholders and the inherent mathematical optimisation in resource allocation problems. The aim is to support consistent and transparent planning and decision-making within DN operations.

AI.tldr: Systematic review of fairness notions and metrics in distribution network planning/operation, examining PoF, locational disparities, and optimization complexity to aid transparent, consistent decision-making.
AI.motivation: To address how fairness concepts can be incorporated into DN planning/operation and understand the trade-offs and computational challenges involved.
AI.method: Literature review and synthesis that catalogs and contrasts fairness notions (e.g., egalitarian to merit-based) and associated mathematical formulations across linear and non-linear optimization contexts.
AI.result: A comprehensive overview and taxonomy of fairness notions/metrics; analysis of their stakeholders' impacts and optimization implications; guidance for consistent planning.
AI.conclusion: Fairness-aware DN planning requires clear taxonomies and transparency; the assembled framework supports informed, equitable decision-making in DN operations.

title: RayFormer: Modeling Inter- and Intra-Ray Similarity for NeRF-Based Video Snapshot Compressive Imaging
summary: Video snapshot compressive imaging (SCI) enables the reconstruction of dynamic scenes from a single snapshot measurement. Recently, NeRF-based methods have shown promising reconstruction performance. However, such methods typically adopt random ray sampling strategies and fail to capture content structural similarities, resulting in limited reconstruction quality. To address these issues, we first propose a patch-level ray sampling strategy to enable the modeling of content structure. Then, we propose an Inter- and Intra-Ray Transformer (RayFormer) to capture the structural similarities, modeling both inter-ray similarities among spatially neighboring points at the same depth and intra-ray correlations between adjacent points along the viewing ray. Finally, benefiting from the patch-level sampling strategy, the total variation prior is incorporated into the objective function to enhance spatial smoothness and suppress artifacts. Experiments in both simulated and real-world scenes demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) reconstruction performance.

AI.tldr: Patch-level ray sampling paired with an Inter- and Intra-Ray Transformer (RayFormer) for video snapshot SCI NeRF reconstruction, plus TV prior, yielding state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: NeRF-based video SCI methods typically use random ray sampling, which neglects content structure and leads to limited reconstruction quality.
AI.method: 1) Introduce patch-level ray sampling to capture content structure. 2) Propose an Inter- and Intra-Ray Transformer (RayFormer) to model inter-ray similarities across neighboring points at the same depth and intra-ray correlations along a viewing ray. 3) Apply total variation prior in the loss, enabled by patch-level sampling, to promote spatial smoothness and reduce artifacts.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and real-world scenes demonstrate state-of-the-art reconstruction performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed patch-level sampling and RayFormer effectively model content structure and enforce spatial smoothness, achieving superior NeRF-based SCI reconstructions.

title: Statistical Channel Fingerprint Construction for Massive MIMO: A Unified Tensor Learning Framework
summary: Channel fingerprint (CF) is considered a key enabler for facilitating the acquisition of channel state information (CSI) in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication systems. In this work, we investigate a novel type of CF that stores statistical CSI (sCSI) at each potential location, referred to as statistical CF (sCF). Specifically, we reveal the relationship between sCSI, namely the channel spatial covariance matrix (CSCM), and the channel power angular spectrum (CPAS). Building on this foundation, we construct a unified tensor representation of the sCF and further reduce its dimension by exploiting the eigenvalue decomposition of the CSCM and its correlation with the PAS. Considering the practical constraints imposed by measurement cost, privacy, and security, we focus on three representative scenarios and uniformly formulate them as tensor restoration tasks. To this end, we propose a unified tensor-based learning architecture, termed LPWTNet. The architecture incorporates a closed-form Laplacian pyramid (LP) decomposition and reconstruction framework that replaces the traditional encoder-decoder structure, enabling efficient inference while capturing multi-scale frequency subband characteristics of the sCF. Additionally, a shared mask learning strategy is introduced to adaptively refine high-frequency sCF components through level-wise adjustments. To achieve a larger receptive field without over-parameterization, we further propose a small-kernel convolution mechanism based on the wavelet transform (WT), which decouples convolution across different frequency components of the sCF and enhances feature extraction efficiency. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach delivers competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various sCF construction scenarios when compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: A tensor-based framework LPWTNet reconstructs statistical channel fingerprints (sCF) for massive MIMO by linking CSCM to CPAS, using Laplacian pyramid decomposition, shared mask learning, and WT-based small kernels to enable scalable, efficient tensor restoration across three scenarios.
AI.motivation: To exploit sCF as a scalable fingerprint of the wireless channel, relate sCSI to CPAS/CSCM, and enable privacy-aware, cost-efficient CSI acquisition; address measurement cost and security concerns via tensor restoration.
AI.method: Form a unified tensor representation of the sCF; leverage CSCM eigen-decomposition and PAS correlation; design LPWTNet with LP decomposition/reconstruction (no encoder-decoder), shared mask learning, and wavelet-based small-kernel convolutions to capture multi-scale freq subbands and expand receptive field without heavy parameters.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency versus state-of-the-art baselines across three sCF construction scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an efficient, accurate method for reconstructing sCF, enabling practical CF-based CSI acquisition in massive MIMO, and demonstrates the effectiveness of LPWTNet for multi-scale tensor restoration in this domain.

title: The TEA Nets framework combines AI and cognitive network science to model targets, events and actors in text
summary: We introduce Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) as a computational framework to extract subjects (``Agents"), verbs (``Events"), and objects (``Targets") from texts. Grounded in cognitive network science and artificial intelligence, TEA Nets are implemented as an open-source Python library. We test TEA Nets in three case studies, demonstrating the framework's ability to perform interpretable emotion detection, semantic frame analyses, and linguistic inquiries across conspiracy texts and textual responses generated by LLMs. In the LOCO conspiracy corpus, TEA Nets revealed that highly conspiratorial narratives (4,227 texts) linked personal pronouns (``I", ``you", ``we") with the same actions twice as frequently as low-similarity conspiracy narratives. High-conspiracy narratives connected person-focused elements (``you", ``people") through actions eliciting anger above the random baseline ($z = 2.63, p < .05$), a trend absent in low-similarity conspiracy narratives, which emphasized scientific actors (``researcher", ``scientist"). In the HOPE and CounseLLMe datasets of 212 (human) and 200 (LLM-based) psychotherapy transcripts, respectively, TEA Nets highlighted emotional differences. When expressing feelings, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-3.5, and humans used sad words with higher frequency than random expectations but Haiku expressed sadness with lower emotional intensity than humans ($U = 1243.5, p = .036$). We discuss these differences in the context of psychotherapy training on LLM-simulated patients. Our results show that Target-Event-Agent Networks can extract relevant emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives, opening new avenues for text analysis with cognitive network science.

AI.tldr: TEA Nets extract Agents, Events, and Targets from text to enable interpretable emotion, semantic, and syntactic analysis; demonstrated on conspiracy narratives and psychotherapy transcripts; provided as an open-source Python library.
AI.motivation: To create a cognitively grounded, interpretable framework for narrative analysis by mapping text into Agent-Event-Target frames and examining their emotional and semantic patterns across domains and datasets.
AI.method: Define and implement Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) as an open-source Python library. Apply to three case studies: (1) LOCO conspiracy corpus (4,227 texts) for conspiratorial narrative analysis; (2) HOPE and CounseLLMe psychotherapy datasets (212 human, 200 LLM transcripts) for emotional analysis. Techniques include extraction of Agents, Events, and Targets, semantic/frame analyses, and linguistic inquiries; statistical assessments include z-scores, p-values, and nonparametric tests (e.g., U tests) comparing observed patterns to random baselines and across groups.
AI.result: In LOCO, highly conspiratorial narratives linked personal pronouns (I, you, we) with the same actions twice as often as low-similarity conspiracies; high-conspiracy narratives connected person-focused elements (you, people) through actions eliciting anger (z=2.63, p<.05) unlike low-similarity conspiracies (which emphasized researchers/scientists). In HOPE and CounseLLMe, TEA Nets detected emotional differences: when expressing feelings, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-3.5, and humans used sad words more than random, but Haiku showed lower emotional intensity than humans (U=1243.5, p=.036). These results suggest TEA Nets capture nuanced emotional, syntactic, and semantic patterns across diverse narratives and across human vs. LLM-generated text.
AI.conclusion: TEA Nets offer a cognitively grounded, interpretable framework for narrative analysis, demonstrated across conspiracy and psychotherapy datasets and implemented as an open-source Python library. The approach enables extraction of nuanced emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives and holds potential for broader applications in text analysis and LLM evaluation.

title: A generalised pre-training strategy for deep learning networks in semantic segmentation of remotely sensed images
summary: In the segmentation of remotely sensed images, deep learning models are typically pre-trained using large image databases like ImageNet before fine-tuned on domain-specific datasets. However, the performance of these fine-tuned models is often hindered by the large domain gaps (i.e., differences in scenes and modalities) between ImageNet's images and remotely sensed images being processed. Therefore, many researchers have undertaken efforts to establish large-scale domain-specific image datasets for pre-training, aiming to enhance model performance. However, establishing such datasets is often challenging, requiring significant effort, and these datasets often exhibit limited generaliza-bility to other application scenarios. To address these issues, this study introduces a novel yet simple pre-training strategy designed to guide a model away from learning domain-specific features in a pre-training dataset during pre-training, thereby improving the generalisation ability of the pre-trained model. To evaluate the strategy's effectiveness, deep learning models are pre-trained on ImageNet and subsequently fine-tuned on four semantic segmentation datasets with diverse scenes and modalities, including iSAID, MFNet, PST900 and Potsdam. Experimental results show that the proposed pre-training strategy led to state-of-the-art accuracies on all four datasets, namely 67.4% mIoU for iSAID, 56.9% mIoU for MFNet, 84.22% mIoU for PST900, 91.88% mF1 for Potsdam. This research lays the groundwork for developing a unified foundation model applicable to both computer vision and remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: A simple pre-training strategy reduces domain-specific feature learning to improve generalization for remote-sensing segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art results after ImageNet pretraining on four diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Large domain gaps between natural images (ImageNet) and remote-sensing imagery hinder transfer; creating large domain-specific pretraining datasets is costly and often lacks generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a pre-training strategy that discourages learning domain-specific features during pre-training; reuse ImageNet pretraining and fine-tune on four RS segmentation datasets (iSAID, MFNet, PST900, Potsdam).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art mIoU/mF1 on all four datasets: iSAID 67.4% mIoU, MFNet 56.9% mIoU, PST900 84.22% mIoU, Potsdam 91.88% mF1.
AI.conclusion: Supports the feasibility of a unified foundation model spanning CV and RS tasks; reduces reliance on domain-specific pretraining data and improves cross-domain generalization.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption for Cardiovascular Disease Risk Modeling
summary:
Protecting sensitive health data while enabling collaborative analysis is a central challenge in healthcare. Traditional machine learning (ML) requires institutions to pool anonymized patient records, centralizing analytical development and privacy risks at a single site. Privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), can mitigate these risks. However, they are mainly studied in conventional data-sharing settings and often introduce trade-offs, including reduced model utility, higher computational cost, and increased implementation complexity. Federated Learning (FL) reduces data centralization by enabling institutions to train models locally and share only model updates. Nevertheless, FL does not eliminate privacy risks, as shared parameters or gradients may still reveal sensitive information. Integrating DP or HE into FL can strengthen privacy guarantees, yet their comparative performance and deployment implications in real-world healthcare settings remain unclear.
  We systematically evaluated DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML (cML) to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in multi-institutional settings. Using nationwide Swedish healthcare data, we evaluated cardiovascular disease risk prediction using logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) learners. FL with HE achieved performance comparable to cML but introduced measurable cryptographic overhead, particularly in the NN implementation. FL with DP incurred lower computational cost; however, LR was more sensitive to calibrated noise than the NN, resulting in greater performance degradation. Our findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems.

AI.tldr: Evaluates DP and HE integration in federated learning for healthcare using Swedish data, comparing to standard FL and centralized ML across logistic regression and neural networks; HE-FL achieves near-centralized performance with cryptographic overhead, DP-FL is cheaper but model sensitivity to noise varies by model, providing practical deployment guidance.
AI.motivation: Privacy-preserving collaborative ML in healthcare with minimal data centralization; compare DP and HE within FL to understand trade-offs in real-world multi-institution settings.
AI.method: Systematic empirical evaluation on nationwide Swedish healthcare data for cardiovascular risk prediction using logistic regression and neural networks. Compare four settings: centralized ML, standard FL, FL with DP, and FL with HE. Assess model utility and cryptographic/ computational overhead.
AI.result: HE-enhanced FL attains performance comparable to centralized ML but incurs notable cryptographic overhead, especially for neural networks. DP-enhanced FL reduces computational costs; LR is more sensitive to DP noise than NN, leading to greater performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: For deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems, HE-FL can preserve utility at the cost of compute, whereas DP-FL offers cost benefits with varying impact by model type; results guide practical deployment decisions and highlight trade-offs between privacy guarantees, model type, and operational overhead.

title: When Agents Evolve, Institutions Follow
summary: Across millennia, complex societies have faced the same coordination problem of how to organize collective action among cognitively bounded and informationally incomplete individuals. Different civilizations developed different political institutions to answer the same basic questions of who proposes, who reviews, who executes, and how errors are corrected. We argue that multi-agent systems built on large language models face the same challenge. Their central problem is not only individual intelligence, but collective organization. Historical institutions therefore provide a structured design space for multi-agent architectures, making key trade-offs between efficiency and error correction, centralization and distribution, and specialization and redundancy empirically testable. We translate seven historical political institutions, spanning four canonical governance patterns, into executable multi-agent architectures and evaluate them under identical conditions across three large language models and two benchmarks. We find that governance topology strongly shapes collective performance. Within a single model, the gap between the best and worst institution exceeds 57 percentage points, while the optimal architecture shifts systematically with model capability and task characteristics. These results suggest that collective intelligence will not advance through a single optimal organizational form, but through governance mechanisms that can be reselected and reconfigured as tasks and capabilities evolve. More broadly, this points to a transition from \textbf{self-evolving agents} to the \textbf{self-evolving multi-agent system}. The code is available on \href{https://github.com/cf3i/SocialSystemArena}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: Historical governance patterns translated into executable MAS architectures reveal governance topology dictates performance; large gaps across architectures; no single best form; MAS should be reconfigurable; supports evolution of collective intelligence.
AI.motivation: Address coordination in cognitively bounded, information-incomplete agents; leverage historical institutional design as a design space for multi-agent LLM systems; test how governance affects performance.
AI.method: Translate seven historical political institutions across four governance patterns into executable MAS architectures; evaluate under identical conditions using three LLMs and two benchmarks; compare performance and robustness.
AI.result: Governance topology strongly shapes collective performance; within-model performance gap between best and worst architecture exceeds 57 percentage points; optimal architecture shifts with model capability and task; cross-model and cross-task consistency of topology effects.
AI.conclusion: Advancing collective intelligence requires reselectable and reconfigurable governance mechanisms; move from self-evolving agents to self-evolving multi-agent systems; historical institutions offer a structured design space; code available at GitHub.

title: Linguistically Informed Multimodal Fusion for Vietnamese Scene-Text Image Captioning: Dataset, Graph Framework, and Phonological Attention
summary: Scene-text image captioning requires fusing three information streams -- visual features, OCR-detected text, and linguistic knowledge -- to generate descriptions that faithfully integrate text visible in images. Existing fusion approaches treat text as language-agnostic, which fails for Vietnamese: a tonal language where diacritics alter word meaning, OCR errors are pervasive, and word boundaries are ambiguous. We argue that Vietnamese scene-text captioning demands \textit{linguistically informed multimodal fusion}, where language-specific structural knowledge is explicitly incorporated into the fusion mechanism. Motivated from these insights, we propose \textbf{HSTFG} (Heterogeneous Scene-Text Fusion Graph), a general-purpose graph fusion framework with learned spatial attention bias, and show through topology analysis that cross-modal graph edges are harmful for scene-text fusion. Building on this finding, we design \textbf{PhonoSTFG} (Phonological Scene-Text Fusion Graph) which specializes graph-level fusion for Vietnamese linguistic reasoning. To support evaluation, we introduce \textbf{ViTextCaps}, the first large-scale Vietnamese scene-text captioning dataset (\textbf{15{,}729} images with \textbf{74{,}970} captions), with comprehensive linguistic analysis showing that 52.8\% of the vocabulary is at risk of diacritic collision.

AI.tldr: Introduces HSTFG and PhonoSTFG graph-based fusion for Vietnamese scene-text captioning; finds cross-modal graph edges harm; releases ViTextCaps (15,729 images, 74,970 captions) with diacritic analysis.
AI.motivation: Vietnamese relies on diacritics and orthography; OCR errors and ambiguous word boundaries make language-agnostic fusion ineffective; a linguistically informed multimodal fusion is needed for accurate scene-text captioning in Vietnamese.
AI.method: Propose Heterogeneous Scene-Text Fusion Graph (HSTFG) with learned spatial attention bias and topology analysis showing cross-modal edges are detrimental; introduce PhonoSTFG, a graph fusion variant specialized for Vietnamese linguistic reasoning; release ViTextCaps, a large Vietnamese scene-text captioning dataset, with analyses on diacritic collisions.
AI.result: Topology analysis indicates cross-modal graph edges are harmful for scene-text fusion; ViTextCaps evaluation reveals 52.8% of vocabulary are at risk of diacritic collision; the dataset enables linguistic-aware assessment and benchmarking for Vietnamese S-T captioning.
AI.conclusion: Linguistically informed multimodal fusion is essential for Vietnamese scene-text captioning; a phonology-focused fusion (PhonoSTFG) augments general fusion (HSTFG); ViTextCaps provides a valuable resource and demonstrates the prevalence of diacritic-related ambiguities in Vietnamese captions.

title: ZAYAN: Disentangled Contrastive Transformer for Tabular Remote Sensing Data
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026) at Lyon, France. Code available at https://github.com/zadid6pretam/ZAYAN. PyPI package: pip install zayan
summary: Learning informative representations from tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science is challenging due to heterogeneity, scarce labels, and redundancy among features. We present ZAYAN (Zero-Anchor dYnamic feAture eNcoding), a self-supervised, feature-centric contrastive framework for tabular data. ZAYAN performs contrastive learning at the feature rather than sample level, removing the need for explicit anchor selection and any reliance on class labels, while encouraging a redundancy-minimized, disentangled embedding space. The framework has two modules: ZAYAN-CL, which pretrains feature embeddings via a zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, and ZAYAN-T, a Transformer that conditions on these embeddings for downstream classification. Across eight datasets, including six remote-sensing tabular benchmarks and two remote-sensing-driven flood-prediction tables from satellite and GIS products, ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift. These results indicate that feature-level contrastive learning and dynamic feature encoding provide an effective recipe for learning from tabular sensing data.

AI.tldr: Feature-centric, self-supervised tabular learning (ZAYAN) using zero-anchor contrastive learning to produce robust, disentangled feature embeddings for remote sensing data, with superior generalization under label scarcity.
AI.motivation: Tackle heterogeneity, label scarcity, and feature redundancy in tabular remote-sensing data by learning informative, low-redundancy representations that do not rely on class labels or anchor samples.
AI.method: A two-component framework: ZAYAN-CL (zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking to learn feature embeddings at the column level) and ZAYAN-T (a Transformer that consumes these embeddings for downstream classification). Contrastive learning is performed at the feature level rather than samples, avoiding explicit anchors and label dependence.
AI.result: Evaluated on eight datasets (six tabular remote-sensing benchmarks and two flood-prediction datasets from satellite and GIS sources). ZAYAN outperforms tabular deep learning baselines in accuracy, robustness, and generalization, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: Feature-level contrastive learning with dynamic feature encoding is an effective strategy for tabular sensing data, enabling robust, generalizable representations without reliance on labeled anchors.

title: Bridging Values and Behavior: A Hierarchical Framework for Proactive Embodied Agents
summary: Current embodied agents are often limited to passive instruction-following or reactive need-satisfaction, lacking a stable, high-order value framework essential for long-term, self-directed behavior and resolving motivational conflicts. We introduce \textit{ValuePlanner}, a hierarchical cognitive architecture that decouples high-level value scheduling from low-level action execution. \textit{ValuePlanner} employs an LLM-based cognitive module to generate symbolic subgoals by reasoning through abstract value trade-offs, which are then translated into executable action plans by a classical PDDL planner. This process is refined via a closed-loop feedback mechanism. Evaluating such autonomy requires methods beyond task-success rates, and we therefore propose a value-centric evaluation suite measuring cumulative value gain, preference alignment, and behavioral diversity. Experiments in the TongSim household environment demonstrate that \textit{ValuePlanner} arbitrates competing values to generate coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior absent from instruction-following and needs-driven baselines. Our work offers a structured approach to bridging intrinsic values and grounded behavior for autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: Decouples high-level value scheduling from action execution using a hierarchical architecture (ValuePlanner) that leverages an LLM to reason about abstract value trade-offs and generate subgoals, which are then translated into executable plans by a PDDL planner; evaluated with value-centric metrics showing improved long-horizon, self-directed behavior in TongSim.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of stable, high-order value frameworks in embodied agents, which impedes long-term self-directed behavior and coherent resolution of motivational conflicts beyond simple instruction-following or need-satisfaction.
AI.method: Use an LLM-based cognitive module to infer symbolic subgoals by reasoning over abstract value trade-offs; convert subgoals into executable plans via a classical PDDL planner; employ a closed-loop feedback mechanism to refine plans; assess with a value-centric evaluation suite (cumulative value gain, preference alignment, behavioral diversity).
AI.result: ValuePlanner effectively arbitrates competing values to produce coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior, outperforming instruction-following and needs-based baselines in TongSim household tasks.
AI.conclusion: Offers a structured approach to bridge intrinsic values with grounded behavior, enabling autonomous agents to operate with stable value-guided behavior across long horizons.

title: Improving Calibration in Test-Time Prompt Tuning for Vision-Language Models via Data-Free Flatness-Aware Prompt Pretraining
summary: Test-time prompt tuning (TPT) has emerged as a promising technique for enhancing the adaptability of vision-language models by optimizing textual prompts using unlabeled test data. However, prior studies have observed that TPT often produces poorly calibrated models, raising concerns about the reliability of their predictions. Recent works address this issue by incorporating additional regularization terms that constrain model outputs, which improve calibration but often degrade performance. In this work, we reveal that these regularization strategies implicitly encourage optimization toward flatter minima, and that the sharpness of the loss landscape around adapted prompts is a key factor governing calibration quality. Motivated by this observation, we introduce Flatness-aware Prompt Pretraining (FPP), a simple yet effective pretraining framework for TPT that initializes prompts within flatter regions of the loss landscape prior to adaptation. We show that simply replacing the initialization in existing TPT pipelines--without modifying any other components--is sufficient to improve both calibration and performance. Notably, FPP requires no labeled data and incurs no additional computational costs during test-time tuning, making it highly practical for real-world deployment. The code is available at: https://github.com/YonseiML/fpp.

AI.tldr: A simple, label-free pretraining approach—Flatness-aware Prompt Pretraining (FPP)—improves test-time prompt tuning (TPT) by initializing prompts in flatter regions of the loss landscape, boosting calibration and accuracy without extra costs.
AI.motivation: TPT can yield poorly calibrated predictions; existing regularization improves calibration but often harms performance. A more foundational approach is needed that enhances calibration without sacrificing accuracy or adding test-time burden.
AI.method: Pretrain prompts in flatter regions of the loss landscape (FPP) and replace the standard initialization in existing TPT pipelines; no labeled data required; no additional computation during test-time; can be integrated by simply changing initialization.
AI.result: Initialization in flatter regions yields improvements in both calibration and predictive performance for TPT, with no extra cost or labeling; simple substitution suffices to achieve gains.
AI.conclusion: Flatness-aware pretraining offers a practical, plug-in improvement for TPT, balancing calibration and accuracy; no labeled data and no extra test-time cost; code released.

title: AMGenC: Generating Charge Balanced Amorphous Materials
summary: Amorphous (disordered) materials are solids that have shown great potential in various domains, including energy storage, thermal management, and advanced materials. Unlike crystalline materials that can be described by unit cells containing a few to hundreds of atoms, amorphous materials require larger simulation cells with at least hundreds to thousands of atoms. To advance the design of amorphous materials with desired properties and facilitate the exploration of their vast design space, generative inverse design has emerged as a promising approach. It aims to directly output materials with properties closely aligned with the desired ones using probabilistic generative models conditioned on desired properties, which can be more resource efficient than the traditional trial-and-error approach. However, due to the inherent stochasticity of probabilistic generative models, when element assignments are unconstrained, a large portion of generated materials may be charge unbalanced, and no existing methods can effectively mitigate this limitation. In this work, we propose AMGenC, a new generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that can guarantee the generation of charge balanced samples, with minimal additional computational overhead and without sacrificing inverse design accuracy. AMGenC achieves this through an element noise that gives the generation process a starting point centered around charge balance, and the combination of a per-step soft projection and a final discrete projection for steering the elements toward exact charge balance throughout the generation. We perform extensive experiments on two amorphous materials datasets. Experimental results provide evidence that AMGenC achieves its design goal.

AI.tldr: AMGenC is a generative inverse-design method for amorphous materials that guarantees charge-balanced samples during generation using element noise, per-step soft projection, and a final discrete projection, with minimal overhead; validated on two datasets showing maintained inverse-design accuracy and improved charge balance.
AI.motivation: Amorphous materials require large simulation cells for accurate property prediction. Probabilistic generative models used for inverse design often produce charge-imbalanced materials when element assignments are unconstrained, limiting their practicality. A method to enforce charge balance without substantial computational cost is needed.
AI.method: AMGenC initializes generation with element noise biased toward charge balance, applies a per-step soft projection toward the charge-balanced subspace, and concludes with a discrete projection to ensure exact charge balance, all with minimal additional computational overhead to preserve inverse-design performance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two amorphous-material datasets show that AMGenC achieves guaranteed charge balance while maintaining inverse-design accuracy, outperforming unconstrained baselines in terms of balance compliance with similar computational cost.
AI.conclusion: AMGenC provides a practical, low-overhead solution to enforce charge balance in generative inverse design for amorphous materials, enabling more reliable design of compositionally constrained amorphous solids.

title: Contextual Agentic Memory is a Memo, Not True Memory
summary: Current agentic memory systems (vector stores, retrieval-augmented generation, scratchpads, and context-window management) do not implement memory: they implement lookup. We argue that treating lookup as memory is a category error with provable consequences for agent capability, long-term learning, and security. Retrieval generalizes by similarity to stored cases; weight-based memory generalizes by applying abstract rules to inputs never seen before. Conflating the two produces agents that accumulate notes indefinitely without developing expertise, face a provable generalization ceiling on compositionally novel tasks that no increase in context size or retrieval quality can overcome, and are structurally vulnerable to persistent memory poisoning as injected content propagates across all future sessions. Drawing on Complementary Learning Systems theory from neuroscience, we show that biological intelligence solved this problem by pairing fast hippocampal exemplar storage with slow neocortical weight consolidation, and that current AI agents implement only the first half. We formalize these limitations, address four alternative views, and close with a co-existence proposal and a call to action for system builders, benchmark designers, and the memory community.

AI.tldr: AI memory today is mostly retrieval-based lookup, not genuine memory; a dual-memory framework inspired by hippocampus-neocortex Complementary Learning Systems—fast exemplar storage plus slow consolidation—could enable long-term learning, better compositional generalization, and improved security; the paper argues for a shift and provides design guidance.
AI.motivation: Conflating retrieval with memory yields capabilities ceilings, unbounded metadata accumulation without real expertise, susceptibility to memory poisoning, and limited novel task generalization. A principled, neuroscience-grounded memory architecture is needed.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis grounded in Complementary Learning Systems (CLS) theory; critical examination of four alternative views of AI memory; formalization of memory vs lookup; proposal of a dual-memory coexistence model; implications for system design and benchmarks.
AI.result: Clarifies the category error between lookup and memory; identifies ceilings on generalization and security vulnerabilities; articulates a CLS-inspired dual-memory architecture (fast exemplar storage + slow consolidation); outlines action items for practitioners and benchmark designers.
AI.conclusion: Advocates adopting a dual-memory AI architecture and co-existence strategy; calls for concrete benchmarks, system-building practices, and community action to advance robust, long-horizon AI memory.

title: Learning to Reason: Targeted Knowledge Discovery and Fuzzy Logic Update for Robust Image Recognition
summary: Integrating domain knowledge into deep neural networks is a promising way to improve generalization. Existing methods either encode prior knowledge in the loss function or apply post-processing modules, but both depend on identifying useful symbolic knowledge to integrate. Since such rules are often unavailable in real-world vision tasks, we propose a method for targeted knowledge discovery. We propose a Differentiable Knowledge Unit (DKU) that enables modulating the classifier logits, yielding refined class probabilities. The DKU uses implication rules to represent relationships between task classes and implicit concepts learned entirely from the main task supervision, without requiring concept labels. Concepts are identified by dedicated classifiers, whose probabilities are passed to DKU alongside the primary class probabilities. DKU computes a logic-based adjustment vector via fuzzy inference, which modulates the primary class logits to yield refined class probabilities. When concept classifiers represent concepts that do not support the logical rule structure, the resulting adjustments to the class probabilities do not directly minimize the supervision loss. Consequently, optimizing the supervision loss on these adjusted class probabilities implicitly trains the concept classifiers. We construct the rule base so that bidirectional logical relations connect concepts and classes. We enforce the concepts to be distinct from each other and with respect to the classes. This design enforces a clean supervision signal for concept learning. We evaluate our methods on the PASCAL-VOC, COCO, and MedMNIST datasets. We demonstrate improvement through our knowledge integration across these datasets. We conduct domain generalization and hard-sample ablation studies and find that our implicit knowledge discovery and integration outperforms the baseline.

AI.tldr: A Differentiable Knowledge Unit (DKU) is proposed to automatically discover and integrate implicit domain concepts into deep classifiers by using implication-based, fuzzy logic rules to adjust class logits via concept-class relations learned from primary supervision alone.
AI.motivation: In many vision tasks, explicit symbolic domain knowledge or concept labels are unavailable. The work aims to discover useful relations between concepts and classes directly from data and to integrate them into the classifier to improve generalization.
AI.method: Introduce DKU that takes primary class probabilities and probabilities from dedicated concept classifiers. An implication-rule-based fuzzy inference system produces an adjustment vector that modulates the primary logits. Concepts and classes are connected bidirectionally via a designed rule base that enforces distinctness. Concept classifiers are trained through the supervision signal even when their outputs are used only to adjust logits. Evaluations on PASCAL-VOC, COCO, and MedMNIST include domain-generalization and hard-sample ablations.
AI.result: DKU-based integration yields improved performance over baselines across the three datasets, showing effective implicit knowledge discovery and improved generalization, including under domain shifts and challenging samples.
AI.conclusion: Targeted, implicit knowledge discovery via DKU enables effective integration of discovered concepts into classifier logits without requiring explicit concept labels, yielding robust generalization improvements.

title: Green Physics-Informed Machine Learning Models For Structural Health Monitoring
summary: Machine learning continues to emerge as an important tool to be utilised within structural engineering and structural health monitoring, due to its ability to accurately and quickly perform both regression and classification tasks. However, a purely data driven approach has its limitations, particularly where we lack data from relevant environmental and operational conditions, a situation that has led to the development of physics-informed machine learners for structural health monitoring. These "grey-box" models take into account the physical insight that an engineer would have about the structure they are modelling and have shown promising results in the structural engineering field among many others. This work compares black and grey-box models through a "green" lens, comparing them in terms of their environmental impact, and investigating how the high extrapolative performance of grey-box models can reduce their runtimes and therefore carbon emissions. The authors aim to develop physics-informed models with reduced computational costs, while maintaining high performance, illustrated through a structural health monitoring case study.

AI.tldr: This work contrasts black-box and grey-box physics-informed ML for structural health monitoring (SHM) through a green lens, arguing that grey-box models can reduce computational costs and carbon emissions while maintaining high performance, demonstrated via a SHM case study.
AI.motivation: Purely data-driven SHM models struggle under limited environmental/operational data; integrating physical insights (grey-box) can improve extrapolation, reliability, and efficiency while supporting sustainability goals.
AI.method: Compare black-box versus grey-box models with a focus on environmental impact; develop physics-informed models aiming for reduced computational cost without sacrificing accuracy; illustrate approach through a structural health monitoring case study.
AI.result: The abstract outlines a framework and expected outcomes rather than presenting concrete empirical results; it suggests grey-box, physics-informed models can achieve high performance with lower runtimes and environmental impact, demonstrated via a SHM case study.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed grey-box models show promise for sustainable SHM by delivering high performance at reduced computational cost, potentially lowering carbon emissions; further validation and detailed results are anticipated in the full work.

title: Knowledge Graph Representations for LLM-Based Policy Compliance Reasoning
summary: The risks posed by AI features are increasing as they are rapidly integrated into software applications. In response, regulations and standards for safe and secure AI have been proposed. In this paper, we present an agentic framework that constructs knowledge graphs (KGs) from AI policy documents and retrieves policy-relevant information to answer questions. We build KGs from three AI risk-related polices under two ontology schemas, and then evaluate five LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks spanning six reasoning types, from entity lookup to cross-policy inference, using both heuristic scoring and an LLM-as-judge. KG augmentation improves scores for all five models, and an open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the formal ontology.

AI.tldr: KG-based framework constructs knowledge graphs from AI policy documents to support policy-relevant QA across five LLMs; KG augmentation improves performance; an open, LLM-discovered schema can match or surpass formal ontologies.
AI.motivation: Rising AI risks in deployed software and the need for safe/secure AI regulatory guidance. Bridging regulatory texts to actionable QA enables compliance checks and policy understanding.
AI.method: Construct KGs from three AI risk policies using two ontology schemas; evaluate five LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks spanning six reasoning types (from entity lookup to cross-policy inference); employ heuristic scoring and LLM-as-judge for evaluation.
AI.result: KG augmentation yields improved scores for all five models; an open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the formal ontology.
AI.conclusion: The agentic KG-based framework effectively supports policy-relevant QA and demonstrates that automatic ontology discovery can approach or surpass predefined formal ontologies, enhancing LLM-assisted policy reasoning.

title: GourNet: A CNN-Based Model for Mango Leaf Disease Detection
summary: Mango cultivation is crucial in the agricultural sector, significantly contributing to economic development and food security. However, diseases affecting mango leaves can significantly reduce both the production and overall fruit grade. Detecting leaf diseases at an early stage with precision is key to effective disease prevention and sustaining crop productivity. In this paper, we introduce a "deep learning" model named "GourNet", which leverages "Convolutional Neural Networks" to identify infections in mango leaves. We utilize the "MangoLeafBD" (MBD) dataset to train and assess the effectiveness of the presented model. The MBD dataset contains seven disease classes and a Healthy class, making a total of eight classes. To enhance model performance, the images are preprocessed through steps like resizing, rescaling, and data augmentation prior to training. To properly evaluate the model, the dataset is separated into 80% for training, with the remaining 20% equally split between validation and testing. Our model uses only 683,656 total parameters and achieves a classification accuracy of 97%. This research's source code can be found at: https://github.com/ekramalam/GourNet-Repo.

AI.tldr: GourNet: a CNN-based mango leaf disease classifier using MangoLeafBD with 8 classes; 97% test accuracy; 683,656 parameters; 80/10/10 split; preprocessing; code available.
AI.motivation: Need accurate, efficient leaf-disease detection to safeguard mango yield and crop quality; small model suitable for deployment.
AI.method: Proposed GourNet CNN, trained on MangoLeafBD; preprocessing includes resizing, rescaling, and data augmentation; train/validation/test split 80/10/10; parameter count 683,656.
AI.result: Achieves 97% classification accuracy on the test set across eight classes (seven diseases + healthy).
AI.conclusion: GourNet demonstrates strong accuracy with a compact model, suitable for real-world deployment in agricultural monitoring; source code released.

title: ANCORA: Learning to Question via Manifold-Anchored Self-Play for Verifiable Reasoning
summary: We propose a paradigm shift from learning to answer to learning to question: can a language model generate verifiable problems, solve them, and turn the resulting feedback into self-improvement without human supervision? We introduce ANCORA, an anchored-curriculum framework in which a unified policy alternates between a Proposer that synthesizes novel specifications and a Solver that produces verified solutions. ANCORA rests on three load-bearing mechanisms: a two-level group-relative update that couples Proposer advantages across specifications with Solver advantages across solution attempts; iterative self-distilled SFT that projects the base model onto its valid-output manifold before RL; and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that grows only through strictly filtered, novel, Solver-verified specifications. These stabilizers are necessary because sparse verifier feedback otherwise drives Proposer collapse even under MLRL-aligned rewards. Instantiated in Verus, ANCORA lifts Dafny2Verus pass@1 from a 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in the test-time-training setting under 0-shot evaluation, outperforming the PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points despite PSV using 1-shot inference; in a separate transfer setting, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% and 17.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and HumanEval.

AI.tldr: ANCORA is a dual-policy, self-improving framework (Proposer and Solver) that generates verifiable problems and solutions, guided by a UCB-driven curriculum and self-distilled SFT. It achieves large gains on Dafny2Verus and transfer to MBPP/HumanEval, addressing sparse verifier signals with stabilizers.
AI.motivation: To move from answering questions to generating verifiable, solvable problems that self-improve without human supervision, mitigating sparse verifier feedback that destabilizes a Proposer.
AI.method: Two-level group-relative update coupling Proposer advantages across specifications with Solver across solution attempts; iterative self-distilled SFT projecting onto the valid-output manifold; and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that expands only with new, solver-verified specifications; instantiated in Verus with Dafny2Verus data; 0-shot and transfer evaluations.
AI.result: Test-time-training 0-shot pass@1 on Dafny2Verus improved from 26.6% to 81.5% (outperforming PSV by 15.8 points). In transfer, Dafny2Verus seeds yield 36.2% on MBPP and 17.2% on HumanEval.
AI.conclusion: ANCORA’s stabilizers enable a robust learning-to-question paradigm that leverages verifier feedback to drive self-improvement, achieving strong verifiable-code synthesis performance and transferable gains.

title: Auditing Frontier Vision-Language Models for Trustworthy Medical VQA: Grounding Failures, Format Collapse, and Domain Adaptation
summary: Deploying vision-language models (VLMs) in clinical settings demands auditable behavior under realistic failure conditions, yet the failure landscape of frontier VLMs on specialized medical inputs is poorly characterized. We audit five recent frontier and grounding-aware VLMs (Gemini~2.5~Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen~2.5~VL) on Medical VQA along two trust-relevant axes. Perception: all models localize anatomical and pathological targets poorly -- the best model reaches only 0.23 mean IoU and 19.1% Acc@0.5 -- and exhibit clinically dangerous laterality confusion. Pipeline integration: a self-grounding pipeline, where the same model localizes then answers, degrades VQA accuracy for every model -- driven by both inaccurate localization and format-compliance failures under the two-step prompt (parse failure rises to 70%--99% for Gemini and GPT-5 on VQA-RAD). Replacing predicted boxes with ground-truth annotations recovers and improves VQA accuracy, consistent with the failure residing in the perception module rather than in the decomposition itself. These observational findings identify grounding quality as a primary trustworthiness bottleneck in our SLAKE bounding-box setting. As a complementary fine-tuning follow-up, supervised fine-tuning of Qwen~2.5~VL on combined Med-VQA training data attains the highest reported SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%) among comparable methods, suggesting that the VQA-level gap is tractable with domain adaptation; whether this also closes the perception/trustworthiness bottleneck is left to future work.

AI.tldr: Auditing frontier vision-language models for medical VQA reveals severe perception (localization) failures and a negative impact of self-grounding pipelines on VQA performance; ground-truth localization improves accuracy, highlighting perception as the bottleneck. Domain adaptation via supervised fine-tuning on Med-VQA data yields the best open-ended SLAKE recall, but whether this resolves perception issues remains open.
AI.motivation: To quantify trustworthiness and failure modes of recent frontier VLMs when applied to specialized medical VQA, focusing on perception (localization accuracy) and integration with a grounding/decomposition pipeline.
AI.method: Evaluate five contemporary frontier VLMs (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen 2.5 VL) on Medical VQA tasks using two axes: perception (localization of anatomical/pathological targets; accuracy metrics such as IoU, Acc@0.5) and pipeline integration (self-grounding: localize then answer; analyze prompt parsing failures; compare against ground-truth boxes). Also perform a domain-adaptation/fine-tuning study by supervised fine-tuning Qwen 2.5 VL on combined Med-VQA data and evaluate SLAKE open-ended recall.
AI.result: Perception is poor across models (best mean IoU 0.23; Acc@0.5 19.1%), with dangerous laterality confusions. Self-grounding degrades VQA accuracy for all models, driven by localization errors and high parse failures (70–99% for Gemini and GPT-5 on VQA-RAD). Replacing model-predicted boxes with ground-truth boxes recovers/improves VQA accuracy, indicating the bottleneck is perception rather than the reasoning/decomposition. Grounding quality identified as the primary trustworthiness bottleneck in SLAKE bounding-box setting. Fine-tuning Qwen 2.5 VL on Med-VQA data yields the highest SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%) among comparable methods, suggesting domain adaptation can close the VQA-level gap, but it is unclear whether perception/trustworthiness bottlenecks are resolved by this alone.
AI.conclusion: Grounding/perception quality is a critical bottleneck for trustworthy medical VQA with frontier VLMs. Domain-adaptive fine-tuning improves task recall, but addressing perception (localization accuracy and overlap) remains essential for robust, auditable clinical deployment.

title: Machine Unlearning for Class Removal through SISA-based Deep Neural Network Architectures
summary: The rapid proliferation of image generation models and other artificial intelligence (AI) systems has intensified concerns regarding data privacy and user consent. As the availability of public datasets declines, major technology companies increasingly rely on proprietary or private user data for model training, raising ethical and legal challenges when users request the deletion of their data after it has influenced a trained model. Machine unlearning seeks to address this issue by enabling the removal of specific data from models without complete retraining. This study investigates a modified SISA (Sharded, Isolated, Sliced, and Aggregated) framework designed to achieve class-level unlearning in Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures. The proposed framework incorporates a reinforced replay mechanism and a gating network to enhance selective forgetting efficiency. Experimental evaluations across multiple image datasets and CNN configurations demonstrate that the modified SISA approach enables effective class unlearning while preserving model performance and reducing retraining overhead. The findings highlight the potential of SISA-based unlearning for deployment in privacy-sensitive AI applications. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/SiamFS/ sisa-class-unlearning.

AI.tldr: A modified SISA framework for class-level unlearning in CNNs is proposed, incorporating a reinforced replay mechanism and a gating network to enable selective forgetting. It demonstrates effective unlearning with preserved accuracy and reduced retraining overhead, across multiple datasets; code is released.
AI.motivation: Growing concerns about data privacy and post-hoc data deletion in AI models. As datasets shrink and legal/ethical pressures increase, there is a need to remove influence of specific data from trained models without full retraining.
AI.method: Enhances the Sharded, Isolated, Sliced, and Aggregated (SISA) unlearning framework by adding a reinforced replay mechanism and a gating network to support class-level forgetting in CNN architectures. The approach aims to selectively forget particular classes while preserving overall model performance and reducing retraining costs. Evaluation is performed on multiple image datasets and CNN configurations; implementation is publicly available.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations indicate the modified SISA approach achieves effective class-level unlearning while maintaining model performance and lowering retraining overhead across diverse CNN setups and image datasets.
AI.conclusion: SISA-based unlearning shows promise for privacy-sensitive AI applications and can be deployed with practical efficiency; public code release facilitates adoption and further development.

title: When Does Structure Matter in Continual Learning? Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Shapes Representational Geometry
summary: To preserve previously learned representations, continual learning systems must strike a balance between plasticity, the ability to acquire new knowledge, and stability. This stability-plasticity dilemma affects how representations can be reused across tasks: shared structure enables transfer when tasks are similar but may also induce interference when new learning disrupts existing representations. However, it remains unclear when and why structural separation influences this trade-off. In this study, we examine how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality jointly shape learning in a sequential task paradigm inspired by transfer-interference studies. We compare a task-partitioned modular recurrent network with a single-module baseline by systematically varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and the scale of weight initialization, which induces different learning regimes that we empirically characterize through the effective dimensionality of the learned representations. We find that architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations are sufficiently unconstrained to accommodate multiple tasks without strong interference. In contrast, in lower-dimensional (rich) regimes, architectural separation is decisive: modular networks exhibit graded alignment of task-specific subspaces with overlap for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and stronger separation for dissimilar tasks. This graded geometry is absent in the single network baseline. Our findings suggest that representational dimensionality acts as a key organizing variable governing when structural separation becomes functionally relevant, and highlight adaptive geometry as a central principle for designing continual learning systems.

AI.tldr: Representational dimensionality governs when architectural separation helps continual learning; high-dimensional regimes render architecture less critical, while low-dimensional regimes reveal graded, task-specific subspace geometry in modular networks that improves transfer and reduces interference.
AI.motivation: Address the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning by uncovering when architectural segregation of task representations is beneficial, and how task similarity and representation dimensionality shape learning dynamics.
AI.method: Compare a task-partitioned modular recurrent network with a single-module baseline across systematically varied task similarity (low/medium/high) and weight initialization scale to induce different learning regimes; characterize representations via effective dimensionality.
AI.result: In high-dimensional regimes, architecture has minimal impact; in low-dimensional regimes, modular networks exhibit graded subspace alignment: overlapping for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and strong separation for dissimilar tasks; the single network lacks this graded geometry.
AI.conclusion: Representational dimensionality is a key organizing variable determining when structural separation is functionally relevant; adaptive geometry should guide design of continual learning systems.

title: Iterative Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Medical Question Answering
summary: Medical retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems typically operate on text chunks extracted from biomedical literature, discarding the rich visual content (tables, figures, structured layouts) of original document pages. We propose MED-VRAG, an iterative multimodal RAG framework that retrieves and reasons over PMC document page images instead of OCR'd text. The system pairs ColQwen2.5 patch-level page embeddings with a sharded MapReduce LLM filter, scaling to ~350K pages while keeping Stage-1 retrieval under 30 ms via an offline coarse-to-fine index (C=8 centroids per page, ANN over centroids, exact two-way scoring on the top-R shortlist). A vision-language model (VLM) then iteratively refines its query and accumulates evidence in a memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds, with a single iteration costing ~15.9 s and the full three-round pipeline ~47.8 s on 4xA100. Across four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med), MEDVRAG reaches 78.6% average accuracy. Under controlled comparison with the same Qwen2.5-VL-32B backbone, retrieval contributes a +5.8 point gain over the no-retrieval baseline; we also note a +1.8 point edge over MedRAG + GPT-4 (76.8%), with the caveat that this is a cross-paper rather than head-to-head comparison. Ablations isolate +1.0 from page-image vs text-chunk retrieval, +1.5 from iteration, and +1.0 from the memory bank.

AI.tldr: A multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that uses PMC page images with patch-level embeddings and a MapReduce filter to perform iterative reasoning with a vision-language model, achieving strong medical QA performance and fast retrieval on a large document collection.
AI.motivation: Traditional RAG systems rely on OCR’d text and discard page visuals (tables, figures, layout), losing valuable evidentiary content. There is a need to leverage full-document page imagery at scale to improve biomedical question answering.
AI.method: MED-VRAG retrieves document pages via ColQwen2.5 patch-level embeddings, using an offline coarse-to-fine index (8 centroids per page; ANN over centroids; exact two-way scoring on top-R). A sharded MapReduce LLM filter selects relevant material. A vision-language model iteratively refines queries and accumulates evidence in a memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds; each iteration costs ~15.9s, ~47.8s total on 4x A100. Evaluation on four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med) yields 78.6% average accuracy. Ablations quantify contributions from page-image retrieval (+1.0), iteration (+1.5), and memory (+1.0); retrieval adds +5.8 points over no-retrieval baselines and +1.8 points vs MedRAG + GPT-4 (cross-paper comparison).
AI.result: MED-VRAG achieves 78.6% average accuracy across four medical QA benchmarks. Stage-1 retrieval runs under 30 ms; the full three-round pipeline costs ~47.8 s on 4x A100. Ablations indicate: page-image retrieval contributes ~1.0 point, iterative reasoning ~1.5 points, and memory bank ~1.0 point to the overall gain, with retrieval providing ~5.8 points over a no-retrieval baseline and ~1.8 points over a competitive MedRAG + GPT-4 setup (note cross-paper comparison).
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that incorporating page imagery and iterative multimodal reasoning yields meaningful gains in medical QA while maintaining scalable retrieval for large document collections. Strengths include fast Stage-1 retrieval, explicit use of visual content, and memory-enabled multi-round reasoning. Limitations include cross-paper comparability, computation cost, and potential sensitivity to image quality; future work could explore broader domain generalization, more efficient indexing, and deeper analysis of memory dynamics and failure modes.

title: Taming Noise-Induced Prototype Degradation for Privacy-Preserving Personalized Federated Fine-Tuning
summary: Prototype-based Personalized Federated Learning (ProtoPFL) enables efficient multi-domain adaptation by communicating compact class prototypes, but directly sharing them poses privacy risks. A common defense involves per-example $\ell_2$ clipping before prototype computation to bound sensitivity, followed by isotropic Gaussian noise to enforce Local Differential Privacy (LDP). However, Isotropic Gaussian Prototype Perturbation (IGPP) typically over-perturbs discriminative dimensions and struggles to balance the clipping threshold with representation fidelity. In this paper, we propose VPDR, a client-side privacy plug-in that seamlessly integrates into existing ProtoPFLs. Motivated by the observation that dimension-wise class variance reflects discriminability, we introduce Variance-adaptive Prototype Perturbation (VPP), which allocates less noise to discriminative subspaces, preserving semantic separability while ensuring privacy. We further develop Distillation-guided Clipping Regularization (DCR), which enables feature norms to adaptively concentrate near the predefined clipping threshold while maintaining prediction consistency. Theoretical analysis shows that our groupwise mechanism provides privacy guarantees no weaker than the isotropic baseline under the same privacy constraints. Extensive experiments on multi-domain benchmarks demonstrate that VPDR achieves a superior privacy-utility trade-off, outperforming IGPP in personalized federated fine-tuning without sacrificing robustness against realistic attacks.

AI.tldr: Variance-adaptive Prototype Perturbation (VPP) with Distillation-guided Clipping Regularization (DCR) as a client-side privacy plug-in for ProtoPFL; allocates less noise to discriminative subspaces based on dimension-wise variance, coupled with regularized clipping to preserve utility, yielding privacy guarantees at least as strong as isotropic baselines and improved privacy-utility over IGPP.
AI.motivation: ProtoPFL enables efficient multi-domain adaptation but sharing prototypes risks privacy. Isotropic LDP noise (IGPP) often over-perturbs informative dimensions and conflicts with clipping. A dimension-aware approach could preserve discriminability while maintaining privacy.
AI.method: Introduce VPDR consisting of Variance-adaptive Prototype Perturbation (VPP) that distributes noise non-uniformly across feature dimensions based on class-variance, and Distillation-guided Clipping Regularization (DCR) that steers feature norms toward the clipping threshold while maintaining predictive consistency. A groupwise privacy analysis shows guarantees not weaker than the isotropic baseline under the same constraints.
AI.result: Experimental results on multi-domain benchmarks show VPDR achieves better privacy-utility trade-off than IGPP, preserving robustness against realistic attacks and enabling effective personalized federated fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: VPDR provides a practical, privacy-preserving enhancement for ProtoPFL by tailoring perturbation to discriminative subspaces and enforcing adaptive clipping regularization, achieving strong privacy guarantees and improved utility.

title: Optimized Deferral for Imbalanced Settings
summary: Learning algorithms can be significantly improved by routing complex or uncertain inputs to specialized experts, balancing accuracy with computational cost. This approach, known as learning to defer, is essential in domains like natural language generation, medical diagnosis, and computer vision, where an effective deferral can reduce errors at low extra resource consumption. However, the two-stage learning to defer setting, which leverages existing predictors such as a collection of LLMs or other classifiers, often faces challenges due to an expert imbalance problem. This imbalance can lead to suboptimal performance, with deferral algorithms favoring the majority expert. We present a comprehensive study of two-stage learning to defer in expert imbalance settings. We cast the deferral loss optimization as a novel cost-sensitive learning problem over the input-expert domain. We derive new margin-based loss functions and guarantees tailored to this setting, and develop novel algorithms for cost-sensitive learning. Leveraging these results, we design principled deferral algorithms, MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), specifically suited for expert imbalance settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, showing clear improvements over existing baselines on both image classification and real-world Large Language Model (LLM) routing tasks.

AI.tldr: Two-stage learning to defer suffers from expert imbalance; the work reframes deferral as a cost-sensitive, margin-based learning problem, derives new loss functions and guarantees, and introduces MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), showing improvements on image classification and LLM routing.
AI.motivation: To address the expert imbalance problem in two-stage learning to defer, reducing errors with limited extra resources across domains like CV and NLP.
AI.method: Cast the deferral objective as a cost-sensitive learning problem over input-expert pairs, derive margin-based loss functions with theoretical guarantees, develop cost-sensitive learning algorithms, and propose MILD tailored for imbalanced expert settings.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate clear improvements over baselines on image classification and real-world LLM routing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Margin-based imbalanced learning to defer (MILD) offers a principled, scalable solution to expert imbalance, with strong empirical gains and broad applicability; future work could explore broader datasets and tighter theoretical bounds.

title: Consumer Attitudes Towards AI in Digital Health: A Mixed-Methods Survey in Australia
summary: AI applications are increasingly being introduced into digital health. While technical performance has advanced rapidly, successful deployment mainly depends on consumer attitudes, especially to patient-facing applications. However, most existing research examines consumer attitudes towards healthcare AI at an abstract level rather than in response to concrete artefacts. We report a mixed-methods survey study in Australia (N=275) examining consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI, combined with a scenario-based evaluation of an AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summary. Participants expressed moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness and ease of use, but also substantial concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, the AI-generated summary was strongly preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of the AI summary was near chance. Findings show that consumers judge AI through concrete communication quality and visible human governance, underscoring the need for clinically supervised deployment frameworks beyond technical performance alone.

AI.tldr: Mixed-methods Australian study finds moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness/ease of use for healthcare AI; AI-generated consultation summaries are preferred for quality and empathy, but participants struggle to identify AI output, highlighting the need for visible human governance and clinically supervised deployment beyond technical performance.
AI.motivation: To understand consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI at the artefact level, and to compare responses to an AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summary.
AI.method: Convergent mixed-methods study in Australia (N=275): a survey assessing readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions, combined with a scenario-based evaluation comparing an AI-generated summary to a clinician-written summary.
AI.result: Participants showed moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness and ease of use for healthcare AI, but had notable concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, the AI-generated summary was preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of AI output was near chance, indicating low awareness of artefact origin.
AI.conclusion: Consumers evaluate healthcare AI primarily through concrete communication quality and visible human governance; the findings advocate deployment frameworks that include clinical supervision and governance beyond purely technical performance.

title: Parameter-Efficient Architectural Modifications for Translation-Invariant CNNs
summary: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are widely assumed to be translation-invariant, yet standard architectures exhibit a startling fragility: even a single-pixel shift can drastically degrade performance due to their reliance on spatially dependent fully connected layers. In this work, we resolve this vulnerability by proposing a lightweight 'Online Architecture' strategy. By strategically inserting Global Average Pooling (GAP) layers at various network depths, we effectively decouple feature recognition from spatial location.
