AI.method: Build 2,209 instances across desktop, web, mobile; cover four interaction families and 23 UI component types; define explicit exact target states for fine-grained state setting; propose four-stage metric pipeline: Localization Success Rate (SR@Loc), Interaction Success Rate (SR@Int), Exact State Success Rate at Locate (ES-SR@Loc), Exact State Success Rate at Interact (ES-SR@Int); introduce Visual Diagnostic Assistant (VDA) that generates a textual Description and a bounding-box Localization Hint for visual grounding diagnosis; evaluate with and without VDA (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Flash).
AI.result: Exact-state success (ES-SR@Int) is low: 32.8% on Web, 22.8% average across platforms; VDA localization hints improve ES-SR@Int by +14.9 points, indicating substantial benefits from improved grounding, yet overall accuracy remains insufficient for reliable fine-grained state-conditioned interaction.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals nontrivial headroom in visual grounding and state grounding, suggesting that advances in grounding modules like VDA can yield meaningful gains; however, current LVLMs still struggle to achieve reliable fine-grained UI control, underscoring the need for targeted improvements and a standardized diagnostic pipeline.

title: Calibrating Attribution Proxies for Reward Allocation in Participatory Weather Sensing
summary: Large-scale IoT weather sensing networks require incentive mechanisms to sustain participation, yet determining how much value individual data contributions bring to the network remains an open problem. Existing approaches address data quality but not data valuation; in operational meteorology, adjoint-based methods derive value from the forecast model itself but require full data assimilation infrastructure. We propose to utilise differentiable AI weather models to fill this gap and characterise gradient-based attribution on gridded GFS analysis inputs as a candidate value signal, evaluating fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability across more than 400 configurations. Attribution captures near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonically faithful payments, but can be inflated by adversarial inputs, with detection requiring external baseline data. These findings establish gradient attribution as a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing.

AI.tldr: Gradient-based attribution from differentiable AI weather models offers a scalable data valuation signal for participatory weather sensing; near-optimal sensor placement with reliable payments, but vulnerable to adversarial inputs; external baselines needed for detection.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of data valuation in large-scale IoT weather networks and to avoid the infrastructure overhead of adjoint-based methods, enabling scalable incentive mechanisms.
AI.method: Utilize differentiable AI weather models on gridded GFS analysis inputs; perform gradient-based attribution; evaluate fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability across 400+ configurations.
AI.result: Attribution provides near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonic payments; however, adversarial inputs can inflate payments; detection requires external baseline data.
AI.conclusion: Gradient attribution is a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing.

title: KellyBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Sequential Decision Making
summary: Language models are saturating benchmarks for procedural tasks with narrow objectives. But they are increasingly being deployed in long-horizon, non-stationary environments with open-ended goals. In this paper we introduce KellyBench, an environment for evaluating sequential decision-making in sports betting markets. Agents are placed in a sequential simulation of the 2023-24 English Premier League season and tasked with maximising their long-term bankroll growth. They are given detailed historical data, including advanced statistics, lineups, and public odds. To succeed they must build machine learning models, identify edge in public markets, and adapt as the environment changes over time. We find that all frontier models evaluated lose money on average over the course of the season for five seeds. The best performing model achieves an average return of -8%, and many models experiencing ruin across seeds. To judge strategy sophistication, we use a human expert rubric to grade each model and find their approaches to be unsophisticated compared to human baselines; Claude Opus 4.6 achieves a rubric score of 26.5%, which means there is significant room for improvement. KellyBench is available as an open-access API endpoint at https://openreward.ai/GeneralReasoning/KellyBench.

AI.tldr: KellyBench evaluates sequential decision-making in sports betting by simulating the 2023-24 EPL season. Frontier models fail to beat the market, with average losses; best model at -8%; human rubric shows substantial room for improvement.
AI.motivation: To assess how language-model–driven agents perform in long-horizon, non-stationary decision tasks with open-ended goals, and to provide a benchmark for adaptive betting strategies using rich historical data.
AI.method: An open-ended environment that simulates the full EPL season. Agents receive historical data (advanced stats, lineups, odds) and must build ML models, identify edge in public markets, and adapt over time. The study runs multiple seeds (five) and evaluates long-term bankroll growth, using frontier models and a human expert rubric to assess strategy sophistication.
AI.result: All frontier models lose money on average across seeds; best average return is -8%, with multiple seeds resulting in ruin. A human expert rubric shows models are unsophisticated relative to human baselines; Claude Opus 4.6 scores 26.5% on the rubric.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier models struggle in long-horizon, non-stationary betting environments. KellyBench provides a challenging benchmark highlighting room for improvement in strategy sophistication and adaptation; open-access API is available for broader use.

title: TransVLM: A Vision-Language Framework and Benchmark for Detecting Any Shot Transitions
comment: This work has been deployed to production. For more related research, please visit HeyGen Research (https://www.heygen.com/research) and HeyGen Avatar-V (https://www.heygen.com/research/avatar-v-model). Project page: https://chence17.github.io/TransVLM/
summary: Traditional Shot Boundary Detection (SBD) inherently struggles with complex transitions by formulating the task around isolated cut points, frequently yielding corrupted video shots. We address this fundamental limitation by formalizing the Shot Transition Detection (STD) task. Rather than searching for ambiguous points, STD explicitly detects the continuous temporal segments of transitions. To tackle this, we propose TransVLM, a Vision-Language Model (VLM) framework for STD. Unlike regular VLMs that predominantly rely on spatial semantics and struggle with fine-grained inter-shot dynamics, our method explicitly injects optical flow as a critical motion prior at the input stage. Through a simple yet effective feature-fusion strategy, TransVLM directly processes concatenated color and motion representations, significantly enhancing its temporal awareness without incurring any additional visual token overhead on the language backbone. To overcome the severe class imbalance in public data, we design a scalable data engine to synthesize diverse transition videos for robust training, alongside a comprehensive benchmark for STD. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TransVLM achieves superior overall performance, outperforming traditional heuristic methods, specialized spatiotemporal networks, and top-tier VLMs. This work has been deployed to production. For more related research, please visit HeyGen Research (https://www.heygen.com/research) and HeyGen Avatar-V (https://www.heygen.com/research/avatar-v-model). Project page: https://chence17.github.io/TransVLM/

AI.tldr: Redefines Shot Boundary Detection as segmentation of transitions; introduces TransVLM that fuses color and optical-flow motion for robust STD, with synthetic data engine and benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art results and production deployment.
AI.motivation: SBD framed around isolated cut points leads to corrupted shots in complex transitions. STD reframes as detecting continuous transition segments, leveraging motion cues to capture fine-grained inter-shot dynamics.
AI.method: TransVLM uses a Vision-Language Model backbone; injects optical flow at input; simple feature fusion by concatenating color and motion; avoids adding visual tokens to language backbone; trains on synthetic diverse transition videos from a scalable data engine; evaluates on a comprehensive STD benchmark.
AI.result: Outperforms traditional heuristics, specialized spatiotemporal networks, and top-tier VLMs; robust under class imbalance; deployed in production.
AI.conclusion: Explicit temporal segmentation with motion priors and efficient fusion yields improved STD accuracy and practical deployment; data synthesis is key to robustness; encourages future STD benchmarking and production-ready VLM-based solutions.

title: Differentiable latent structure discovery for interpretable forecasting in clinical time series
comment: This manuscript is under review at BioData Mining
summary: Background: Timely, uncertainty-aware forecasting from irregular electronic health records (EHR) can support critical-care decisions, yet most approaches either impute to a grid or sacrifice interpretability. We introduce StructGP, a continuous-time multi-task Gaussian process that couples process convolutions with differentiable structure learning to uncover a sparse, ordered directed acyclic graph (DAG) of inter-variable dependencies while preserving principled uncertainty. We further propose LP-StructGP, which augments StructGP with latent pathways-shared, temporally shifted trajectories inferred via subject-specific coupling filters and a softmax gating mechanism-to capture cross-patient progression patterns. Both models are trained under sparsity and acyclicity constraints (augmented Lagrangian, Adam) using scalable low-rank updates. Results: In simulations, the approach reliably recovers ground-truth graphs (Structural Hamming Distance approaching 0 as cohorts grow) and pathway assignments (high Adjusted Rand Index). On a MIMIC-IV septic shock cohort (n=1,008; norepinephrine, creatinine, mean arterial pressure), StructGP improves short-horizon (6 h) forecasting over independent-task baselines (average RMSE 0.68 [95%CI: 0.63--0.74] vs. 0.88 [0.83-0.94]) and, with 15 additional inputs, markedly outperforms unstructured kernels (0.63 [0.58-0.69] vs. 3.02 [2.85-3.18]) with superior calibration (coverage 0.96 vs. 0.84). On the PhysioNet Challenge (12k patients, 41 variables), StructGP attains competitive accuracy (MAE 3.72e-2) relative to a state-of-the-art graph neural model while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. Conclusion: These results show that structured process convolutions with latent pathways deliver interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series.

AI.tldr: Structured, continuous-time multi-task GPs learn sparse DAGs and latent pathways for uncertainty-aware forecasting on irregular EHRs, yielding interpretable dependencies and improved accuracy/calibration over baselines.
AI.motivation: Provide timely, uncertainty-aware forecasts from irregular EHR data while preserving interpretability and avoiding imputation to grids.
AI.method: StructGP: continuous-time multi-task Gaussian process; process convolutions; differentiable structure learning to infer a sparse, ordered DAG. LP-StructGP: adds latent shared, temporally shifted pathways via coupling filters and softmax gating; trained with sparsity and acyclicity constraints using augmented Lagrangian and Adam; scalable with low-rank updates.
AI.result: Simulation: accurate recovery of ground-truth graphs (Structural Hamming Distance ->0 with larger cohorts) and good pathway clustering (Adjusted Rand Index). MIMIC-IV septic shock (n=1008): StructGP reduces short-horizon 6h RMSE to 0.68 vs 0.88; with 15 inputs, unstructured kernels performance drops; calibration improves (coverage 0.96 vs 0.84). PhysioNet Challenge (12k; 41 vars): StructGP MAE 0.0372, competitive with state-of-the-art GNN while preserving calibrated uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Structured process convolutions with latent pathways enable interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series.

title: Modeling Clinical Concern Trajectories in Language Model Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents deployed in clinical settings often exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior, offering little visibility into accumulating risk prior to escalation. In real-world care, however, clinicians act on gradually rising concern rather than instantaneous triggers. We study whether explicit state dynamics can expose such pre-escalation signals without delegating clinical authority to the agent. We introduce a lightweight agent architecture in which a memoryless clinical risk encoder is integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal. Across synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents exhibit sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories despite similar escalation timing. These trajectories surface sustained unease prior to escalation, enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring and more informed intervention. Our results suggest that explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed.

AI.tldr: Introducing second-order temporal dynamics into a lightweight LLM agent to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal, improving clinical legibility and enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring by avoiding abrupt, threshold-based escalations.
AI.motivation: LLM agents in clinical settings often exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior with little visibility into accumulating risk. Clinicians respond to gradually rising concern; explicit state dynamics could reveal pre-escalation signals without granting agent control.
AI.method: Integrates a memoryless clinical risk encoder over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal; evaluates stateless versus second-order dynamics across synthetic ward scenarios to assess pre-escalation visibility.
AI.result: Second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories with sustained unease prior to escalation, while stateless agents show sharp escalation cliffs; timing of escalation remains similar, but visibility of rising concern improves.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state dynamics enhance clinical legibility of LLM agents by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed, enabling safer human-in-the-loop monitoring.

title: Echo-α: Large Agentic Multimodal Reasoning Model for Ultrasound Interpretation
summary: Ultrasound interpretation requires both precise lesion localization and holistic clinical reasoning, yet existing methods typically excel at only one of these capabilities: specialized detectors offer strong localization but limited reasoning, whereas multimodal large language models (MLLMs) provide flexible reasoning but weak grounding in specialized medical domains. We present Echo-α, an agentic multimodal reasoning model for ultrasound interpretation that unifies these strengths within an invoke-and-reason framework. Echo-α is trained to coordinate organ-specific detector outputs, integrate them with global visual context, and convert the resulting evidence into grounded diagnostic decisions beyond detector-only inference. This behavior is established through a nine-task supervised curriculum and then refined by sequential reinforcement learning under different reward trade-offs, yielding Echo-α-Grounding for lesion anchoring and Echo-α-Diagnosis for final diagnosis. On multi-center renal and breast ultrasound benchmarks, Echo-α outperforms competitive baselines on both grounding and diagnosis. In particular, on cross-center test sets, Echo-α-Grounding attains 56.73%/43.78% F1@0.5 and Echo- α-Diagnosis reaches 74.90%/49.20% overall accuracy on renal/breast ultrasound. These results suggest that agentic multimodal reasoning can turn specialized detectors into verifiable clinical evidence, offering a practical route toward ultrasound AI systems that are more accurate, interpretable, and transferable. The repository is at https://github.com/MiliLab/Echo-Alpha.

AI.tldr: Echo-α fuses organ-specific detectors with global multimodal reasoning in an agentic framework to achieve grounded ultrasound interpretation, improving both lesion grounding and diagnostic accuracy on renal and breast ultrasound across multi-center datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome the typical split between precise localization (detectors) and flexible reasoning (MLLMs) by unifying them into an interpretable, evidence-based ultrasound interpretation system.
AI.method: A nine-task supervised curriculum trains coordination among organ detectors and global visual context, followed by sequential reinforcement learning with different reward trade-offs to yield Echo-α-Grounding (lesion anchoring) and Echo-α-Diagnosis (final diagnosis).
AI.result: On multi-center renal and breast ultrasound benchmarks, Echo-α outperforms baselines in grounding and diagnosis. Cross-center testing yields F1@0.5 of 56.73%/43.78% for grounding and overall accuracy of 74.90%/49.20% for diagnosis (renal/breast).
AI.conclusion: Agentic multimodal reasoning can convert specialized detectors into verifiable clinical evidence, offering a practical route toward more accurate, interpretable, and transferable ultrasound AI systems; code is available at the cited GitHub repository.

title: ITS-Mina: A Harris Hawks Optimization-Based All-MLP Framework with Iterative Refinement and External Attention for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting plays a pivotal role in numerous real-world applications, including financial analysis, energy management, and traffic planning. While Transformer-based architectures have gained popularity for this task, recent studies reveal that simpler MLP-based models can achieve competitive or superior performance with significantly reduced computational cost. In this paper, we propose ITS-Mina, a novel all-MLP framework for multivariate time series forecasting that integrates three key innovations: (1) an iterative refinement mechanism that progressively enhances temporal representations by repeatedly applying a shared-parameter residual mixer stack, effectively deepening the model's computational capacity without multiplying the number of distinct parameters; (2) an external attention module that replaces traditional self-attention with learnable memory units, capturing cross-sample global dependencies at linear computational complexity; and (3) a Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm for automatic dropout rate tuning, enabling adaptive regularization tailored to each dataset. Extensive experiments on six widely-used benchmark datasets demonstrate that ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.

AI.tldr: An all-MLP multivariate time series forecasting model, ITS-Mina, achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance by combining iterative refinement with a shared-parameter residual mixer, external attention via memory units for cross-sample global dependencies, and Harris Hawks Optimization for dataset-adaptive dropout tuning, validated on six benchmarks against 11 baselines.
AI.motivation: To improve accuracy and efficiency in multivariate time series forecasting by avoiding heavy Transformer architectures, leveraging a compact all-MLP design that can match or surpass Transformer-based models at lower computational cost.
AI.method: 1) Iterative refinement using a shared-parameter residual mixer stack to progressively deepen temporal representations without increasing parameter count. 2) External attention with learnable memory units replacing self-attention to capture cross-sample global dependencies at linear complexity. 3) Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) to automatically tune dropout rates per dataset for adaptive regularization. 4) End-to-end all-MLP architecture (ITS-Mina) evaluated on standard benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on six widely used benchmark datasets shows ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.
AI.conclusion: ITS-Mina demonstrates that an all-MLP approach, when equipped with iterative refinement, external memory-based attention, and dataset-specific regularization tuning, can closely approach or surpass Transformer-based methods while maintaining efficiency.

title: Building Persona-Based Agents On Demand: Tailoring Multi-Agent Workflows to User Needs
summary: Recent advances in agentic AI are shifting automation from discrete tools to proactive multi-agent systems that coordinate multi-specialized capabilities behind unified interfaces. However, today's agent systems typically rely on hard-coded agent architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows that limit end-user personalization and make adaptation to individual needs and contexts difficult. Given this limitation, we argue that on-demand persona-based agent generation offers a promising path towards more efficient and contextually appropriate interaction within agentic workflows. By dynamically crafting agents and personas at run-time to match user characteristics, task demands, and workflow context, agentic platforms can move beyond one-size-fits-all configurations. We present a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, detailing how real-time crafting of AI personas can be systematically integrated within agent systems, aiming to open new possibilities in agentic platform design paradigms.

AI.tldr: Proposes a runtime, persona-based generation pipeline to customize agentic AI systems beyond fixed architectures.
AI.motivation: Hard-coded agent architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows limit end-user personalization and adaptation to user needs and contexts.
AI.method: Presents a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, detailing real-time crafting of AI personas and their integration within multi-agent workflows and unified interfaces.
AI.result: A conceptual framework and pipeline for runtime persona creation, enabling flexible, personalized agentic interactions and adaptable system design.
AI.conclusion: On-demand persona generation offers a promising path to more efficient, contextually appropriate interaction in agentic workflows and suggests new design paradigms for agent platforms.

title: Faster 3D Gaussian Splatting Convergence via Structure-Aware Densification
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting has emerged as a powerful scene representation for real-time novel-view synthesis. However, its standard adaptive density control relies on screen-space positional gradients, which do not distinguish between geometric misplacement and frequency aliasing, often leading to either over-blurred high-frequency textures or inefficient over-densification. We present a structure-aware densification framework. Our key insight is that the decision to subdivide a Gaussian should be driven by an explicit comparison between its projected screen-space extent and the local structure of the texture it seeks to represent. We introduce a multi-scale frequency analysis combining structure tensors with Laplacian scale space analysis to estimate the dominant frequency at each pixel, enabling robust supervision across varying texture scales. Based on this analysis, we define $η$, a per-Gaussian, per-axis frequency violation metric that indicates when a primitive may be under-resolving local texture details. Unlike methods that perform isotropic splitting (e.g., splitting each Gaussian into two smaller ones with uniform shape), our approach performs anisotropic splitting. For each axis with high $η$, we compute a split factor to better resolve the local frequency content. We further introduce a multiview consistency criterion that aggregates $η$ observations across multiple views. By performing densification early and faster, we skip the lengthy iterative densification phases required by baseline methods and achieve significantly faster convergence. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method also achieves superior reconstruction quality, particularly in high-frequency regions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a structure-aware, anisotropic densification framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting using multi-scale frequency analysis to compute an η metric per Gaussian per axis, enabling early, fast, and accurate densification with multiview consistency and improved high-frequency reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Current density control in 3D Gaussian Splatting relies on screen-space gradients that conflate geometric misplacement with texture frequency, often causing over-blurring of high-frequency textures or inefficient over-densification and slow convergence.
AI.method: Compute dominant texture frequency per pixel via a multi-scale analysis that fuses structure tensors with Laplacian scale-space. Define η as a per-Gaussian, per-axis frequency-violation score. Perform anisotropic splitting guided by high-η axes. Aggregate η across views for multiview consistency. Densify early for faster convergence and evaluate on standard benchmarks against isotropic baselines.
AI.result: Experiments show faster convergence and superior reconstruction quality, particularly in high-frequency regions. Anisotropic splitting and multiview-consistent η lead to better detail preservation and efficiency compared to isotropic densification baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware densification effectively disentangles geometry misplacement from texture frequency, enabling per-axis adaptive splitting and multiview-consistent densification that yields faster convergence and improved high-frequency rendering in 3D Gaussian splatting.

title: Dynamic Scaled Gradient Descent for Stable Fine-Tuning for Classifications
summary: Fine-tuning pretrained models has become a standard approach to adapting pretrained knowledge to improve the accuracy on new sparse, imbalance datasets. However, issues arise when optimization falls into a collapsed state, where the model gets stuck, leading to degraded performance and unstable training. One possible reason for this is the cancellation of gradients across training examples. To address this problem, we propose a novel algorithm, dynamic scaled gradient descent (\mName), that directly modifies the gradients returned by training examples, specifically, scaling down the gradients of correctly classified examples using a dynamic scaler. This strategy offers both theoretical and empirical advantages in improving training stability. Experiments on a variety of benchmark datasets, spanning multiple tasks and large pretrained models, demonstrate that our method consistently reduces performance variance and surpasses the accuracy of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: Introduces dynamic scaled gradient descent (DSGD) to scale down gradients of correctly classified examples during fine-tuning, aiming to reduce gradient cancellation and collapse on sparse/imbalanced datasets; improves stability and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Gradient cancellation across training examples can cause optimization collapse during fine-tuning on imbalanced or sparse datasets; stabilizing the gradient signal is crucial.
AI.method: Apply per-example gradient modification by a dynamic scaler that reduces gradients from correctly classified instances; update model parameters using these scaled gradients (DSGD), applicable to large pretrained models and multiple tasks.
AI.result: Empirically reduces training variance and yields higher accuracy than baselines across diverse benchmarks and large pretrained models, indicating improved stability.
AI.conclusion: DSGD provides a principled approach to stabilize fine-tuning under data imbalance, with consistent performance gains and broad applicability.

title: In-Context Prompting Obsoletes Agent Orchestration for Procedural Tasks
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks -- LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, and others -- place an external orchestrator above the LLM, tracking state and injecting routing instructions at every turn. We present a controlled comparison showing that for procedural tasks, this architecture is dominated by a simpler alternative: putting the entire procedure in the system prompt and letting the model self-orchestrate. Across three domains -- travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom technical support (14 nodes), and insurance claims processing (55 nodes) -- we evaluate 200 conversations per condition using LLM-as-judge scoring on five quality criteria. The in-context approach scores 4.53--5.00 on a 5-point scale while a LangGraph orchestrator using the same model scores 4.17--4.84. The orchestrated system fails on 24% of travel, 9% of Zoom, and 17% of insurance conversations, compared to 11.5%, 0.5%, and 5% for the in-context baseline. While external orchestration may have been necessary for earlier models, advances in frontier model capabilities have made it unnecessary for multi-turn conversations following a defined procedure.

AI.tldr: Self-orchestration via the system prompt outperforms external orchestrators for procedural multi-turn tasks across three domains; external frameworks underperform in quality and reliability.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether external agent orchestration provides value with modern LLMs by directly comparing an external orchestrator (LangGraph) to an in-context self-orchestrating approach across multiple domains and conversation counts, using LLM-as-judge scoring.
AI.method: Controlled experiment with 200 conversations per condition across three domains (travel booking: 14 nodes; Zoom technical support: 14 nodes; insurance claims processing: 55 nodes). Two conditions: in-context self-orchestration (system-prompt-guided) vs LangGraph external orchestration. LLM-as-judge scores five quality criteria on a 5-point scale. Measure failure rates per domain.
AI.result: In-context scores: 4.53–5.00; LangGraph scores: 4.17–4.84. Failure rates: travel 24% vs 11.5%; Zoom 9% vs 0.5%; insurance 17% vs 5% (in-context better).
AI.conclusion: Advances in frontier models reduce the need for external orchestration in multi-turn procedural tasks; self-orchestrating prompts suffice and can outperform external frameworks. External orchestration may have been more relevant for earlier model capabilities.

title: Graph World Models: Concepts, Taxonomy, and Future Directions
summary: As one of the mainstream models of artificial intelligence, world models allow agents to learn the representation of the environment for efficient prediction and planning. However, classical world models based on flat tensors face several key problems, including noise sensitivity, error accumulation and weak reasoning. To address these limitations, many recent studies use graph structure to decompose the environment into entity nodes and interactive edges, and model virtual environments in a structured space. This paper systematically formalizes and unifies these emerging graph-based works under the concept of graph world models (GWMs). To the best of our knowledge, GWMs have not yet been explicitly defined and surveyed as a unified research paradigm. Furthermore, we propose a taxonomy based on relational inductive biases (RIB), categorizing GWMs by the specific structural priors they inject: (1) spatial RIB for topological abstraction; (2) physical RIB for dynamic simulation; and (3) logical RIB for causal and semantic reasoning. For each model category, we outline the key design principles, summarize representative models, and conduct comparative analyses. We further discuss open challenges and future directions, including dynamic graph adaptation, probabilistic relational dynamics, multi-granularity inductive biases, and the need for dedicated benchmarks and evaluation metrics for GWMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes graph-based world models (GWMs) as a unified paradigm, with a taxonomy guided by relational inductive biases (RIB). It surveys and unifies graph-centric approaches to environment modeling, analyzes design principles across three bias families, and outlines open challenges and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Classical flat-tensor world models suffer from noise sensitivity, error accumulation, and limited reasoning. Graph-based decompositions into entity nodes and interactive edges offer structured representations for robust prediction and planning. The field lacks a clear, unified definition and survey, motivating a formal treatment and taxonomy under GWMs.
AI.method: Formalize and unify existing graph-based environment models under the umbrella of graph world models (GWMs). Propose a taxonomy based on relational inductive biases (RIB) categorized into: (1) spatial RIB for topological abstraction, (2) physical RIB for dynamic simulation, and (3) logical RIB for causal and semantic reasoning. For each category, outline key design principles, summarize representative models, and perform comparative analyses. Discuss open challenges and future directions, including dynamic graph adaptation, probabilistic relational dynamics, multi-granularity inductive biases, and the need for dedicated benchmarks and evaluation metrics for GWMs.
AI.result: A formal definition and unified taxonomy of GWMs; a structured synthesis of design principles and representative models; comparative analyses across categories; and a clear mapping of open problems and evaluation needs within the GWMs framework.
AI.conclusion: GWMs offer a principled, graph-based paradigm for environment modeling that can improve robustness and reasoning by injecting appropriate relational inductive biases. Realizing this promise requires advances in dynamic graphs, probabilistic relational dynamics, and standardized benchmarks and metrics to evaluate GWMs.

title: Kernelized Advantage Estimation: From Nonparametric Statistics to LLM Reasoning
summary:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have increasingly relied on reinforcement learning (RL) to improve their reasoning capabilities. Three approaches have been widely adopted: (i) Proximal policy optimization and advantage actor-critic rely on a deep neural network to estimate the value function of the learning policy in order to reduce the variance of the policy gradient. However, estimating and maintaining such a value network incurs substantial computational and memory overhead. (ii) Group relative policy optimization (GRPO) avoids training a value network by approximating the value function using sample averages. However, GRPO samples a large number of reasoning traces per prompt to achieve accurate value function approximation, making it computationally expensive. (iii) REINFORCE-type algorithms sample only a single reasoning trajectory per prompt, which reduces computational cost but suffers from poor sample efficiency.
  In this work, we focus on a practical, resource-constrained setting in which only a small number of reasoning traces can be sampled per prompt, while low-variance gradient estimation remains essential for high-quality policy learning. To address this challenge, we bring classical nonparametric statistical methods, which are both computationally and statistically efficient, to LLM reasoning. We employ kernel smoothing as a concrete example for value function estimation and the subsequent policy optimization. Numerical and theoretical results demonstrate that our proposal achieves accurate value and gradient estimation, leading to improved policy optimization.

AI.tldr: Nonparametric kernel smoothing for value estimation in RL for LLMs under tight sample budgets; enables accurate value and gradient estimation and improves policy optimization without training a value network.
AI.motivation: Address the computational and memory overhead of value-function networks in PPO/AC and the high sampling cost of GRPO, while preserving low-variance gradient estimates under limited reasoning traces per prompt.
AI.method: Apply classical nonparametric kernel smoothing to estimate the value function from a small set of reasoning traces, integrate this into policy optimization to obtain value and gradient estimates without a parametric value network; provide theoretical guarantees and numerical validation.
AI.result: Accurate value and gradient estimation with very few traces per prompt, leading to improved policy optimization under resource constraints; demonstrated both analytically and numerically.
AI.conclusion: NonparametricKernel smoothing is a promising, resource-efficient alternative for RL-based LLM reasoning; offers computational/statistical efficiency and scalability under tight sampling budgets, with potential for extension to other nonparametric approaches; future work should address scalability to higher-dimensional state spaces and bandwidth selection.

title: Simulating clinical interventions with a generative multimodal model of human physiology
summary: Understanding how human health changes over time, and why responses to interventions vary between individuals, remains a central challenge in medicine. Here we present HealthFormer, a decoder-only transformer that models the human physiological trajectory generatively, by training on data from the Human Phenotype Project, a multi-visit cohort of over 15,000 deeply phenotyped individuals. We tokenise each participant's health trajectory across 667 measurements spanning seven domains: blood biomarkers, body composition, sleep physiology, continuous glucose monitoring, gut microbiome, wearable-derived physiology, and behaviour and medication exposure. We train HealthFormer to forecast individual physiological trajectories across these domains, and from this single generative objective a range of clinically relevant tasks can be expressed as queries on the model. We show that, without task-specific training, HealthFormer transfers to four independent cohorts and improves prediction for 27 of 30 incident-disease and mortality endpoints, exceeding established clinical risk scores in every comparison. We further show that the model can simulate interventions in silico: in a held-out personalised-nutrition trial, intervention-conditioned predictions recover individual six-month biomarker changes (e.g., Pearson r = 0.78 for diastolic blood pressure). Across 41 randomised intervention-outcome comparisons drawn from published trials, our results show that the predicted direction of effect agrees in every case, and the predicted mean falls within the reported 95% confidence interval in 30 cases. We position HealthFormer as an initial health world model, from which forecasting, risk stratification, and intervention-conditioned simulation arise as queries, providing a basis for clinical digital twins.

AI.tldr: HealthFormer is a decoder-only transformer that learns cross-domain, longitudinal human health trajectories from the Human Phenotype Project and can zero-shot forecast disease risk and mortality, surpass clinical risk scores in most cases, and simulate interventions in silico; positioned as a first health world model for forecasting, risk stratification, and digital-twin simulations.
AI.motivation: To capture the dynamics of human physiology over time and the heterogeneity of responses to interventions by learning a generative model over multi-domain health data, enabling task-agnostic queries for forecasting and simulated interventions.
AI.method: Train HealthFormer on the Human Phenotype Project dataset (≈15k individuals) with 667 measurements across seven domains. Use a decoder-only transformer optimized with a generative objective to forecast individual trajectories. Evaluate zero-shot on four external cohorts for incident disease/mortality endpoints, compare to clinical risk scores, and test intervention-conditioned predictions via in silico nutrition trial data across 41 randomized trials.
AI.result: HealthFormer improves predictions for 27 of 30 incident endpoints across four external cohorts, outperforming clinical risk scores in all comparisons. In a held-out nutrition trial, predicted six-month biomarker changes correlate with actual changes (e.g., Pearson r = 0.78 for diastolic BP). Across 41 randomized trials, the model correctly predicts the direction of effect in all cases, and the average predicted mean aligns with reported 95% CIs in 30 trials.
AI.conclusion: HealthFormer constitutes an initial health world model enabling forecasting, risk stratification, and intervention-conditioned simulations, laying groundwork for clinical digital twins.

title: TAFA-GSGC: Group-wise Scalable Point Cloud Geometry Compression with Progressive Residual Refinement
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2026
summary: Scalable compression is essential for bandwidth-adaptive transmission, yet most learned codecs are optimized for a fixed rate-distortion point, making rate adaptation costly due to re-encoding or maintaining multiple bitstreams. In this work, we propose TAFA-GSGC, a scalable learned point cloud geometry codec that enables multi-quality decoding from a single bitstream and a single trained model. TAFA-GSGC combines layered residual refinement with channel-group entropy coding, and introduces Target-Aligned Feature Aggregation module to reduce cross-layer redundancy in enhancement residuals. Our framework supports up to 9 decodable quality levels with monotonic quality improvement as more subbitstreams are received, while maintaining strong compression efficiency. Compared with the baseline PCGCv2, TAFA-GSGC attains comparable and slightly better RD performance, achieving average BD-Rate savings of -4.99% in D1 and -5.92% in D2.

AI.tldr: Scalable learned point cloud geometry codec TAFA-GSGC enabling multi-quality decoding from one bitstream and one model; uses layered residual refinement, channel-group entropy coding, and Target-Aligned Feature Aggregation; supports up to 9 decodable quality levels with monotonic quality increase and BD-rate gains over PCGCv2.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between fixed-rate learned codecs and bandwidth-adaptive transmission; enable scalable decoding from a single bitstream and model without re-encoding.
AI.method: TAFA-GSGC integrates layered residual refinement for progressive quality, channel-group entropy coding for efficient bitstream, and a Target-Aligned Feature Aggregation (TAFA) module to reduce cross-layer redundancy in enhancement residuals; supports up to 9 quality levels with monotonic improvement when additional subbitstreams are received; single trained model.
AI.result: Compared to baseline PCGCv2, TAFA-GSGC achieves comparable or slightly better rate-distortion performance, with average BD-Rate savings of -4.99% in D1 and -5.92% in D2.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that scalable, single-model, single-bitstream learned point cloud geometry coding is viable, offering monotonic quality growth and competitive compression efficiency across multiple quality levels.

title: Learning from Disagreement: Clinician Overrides as Implicit Preference Signals for Clinical AI in Value-Based Care
summary: We reframe clinician overrides of clinical AI recommendations as implicit preference data - the same signal structure exploited by reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), but richer: the annotator is a domain expert, the alternatives carry real consequences, and downstream outcomes are observable. We present a formal framework extending standard preference learning with three contributions: a five-category override taxonomy mapping override types to distinct model update targets; a preference formulation conditioned on patient state s, organizational context c, and clinician capability kappa, where kappa decomposes into execution capability kappa-exec and alignment capability kappa-align; and a dual learning architecture that jointly trains a reward model and a capability model via alternating optimization, preventing a failure mode we term suppression bias-the systematic suppression of correct-but-difficult recommendations when clinician capability falls below the execution threshold. We argue that chronic disease management under outcome-based payment contracts produces override data with uniquely favorable properties-longitudinal density, concentrated decision space, outcome labels, and natural capability variation-and that training environments combining longitudinal outcome measurement with aligned financial incentives are a necessary condition for learning a reward model aligned with patient trajectory rather than with encounter economics. This framework emerged from operational work to improve clinician capability in a live value-based care deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes a formal framework to treat clinician overrides as rich preference data for RLHF-like learning; introduces a five-category override taxonomy, a state- and context-conditioned preference model with decomposed clinician capability, and a dual-learning architecture to jointly train a reward and capability model, addressing suppression bias; grounds its relevance in outcome-based care data.
AI.motivation: To align AI clinical recommendations with patient trajectories under real-world constraints by leveraging expert overrides and observable outcomes, especially in value-based care with longitudinal data.
AI.method: Five-category override taxonomy mapping override types to model update targets; preference model conditioned on patient state s, organizational context c, and clinician capability kappa = kappa-exec + kappa-align; alternating optimization to train reward and capability models; analysis of suppression bias.
AI.result: Provides a formal framework and operational justification; argues that longitudinal, outcome-labeled overrides in value-based care offer favorable data properties; describes a live deployment context from which it emerged; no empirical results stated in abstract.
AI.conclusion: If deployed with longitudinal outcomes and matching incentives, the learned reward model can align with patient trajectories rather than encounter economics; framework serves to improve clinician capability in live value-based care settings.

title: From Unstructured Recall to Schema-Grounded Memory: Reliable AI Memory via Iterative, Schema-Aware Extraction
summary:
Persistent AI memory is often reduced to a retrieval problem: store prior interactions as text, embed them, and ask the model to recover relevant context later. This design is useful for thematic recall, but it is mismatched to the kinds of memory that agents need in production: exact facts, current state, updates and deletions, aggregation, relations, negative queries, and explicit unknowns. These operations require memory to behave less like search and more like a system of record.
  This paper argues that reliable external AI memory must be schema-grounded. Schemas define what must be remembered, what may be ignored, and which values must never be inferred. We present an iterative, schema-aware write path that decomposes memory ingestion into object detection, field detection, and field-value extraction, with validation gates, local retries, and stateful prompt control. The result shifts interpretation from the read path to the write path: reads become constrained queries over verified records rather than repeated inference over retrieved prose.
  We evaluate this design on structured extraction and end-to-end memory benchmarks. On the extraction benchmark, the judge-in-the-loop configuration reaches 90.42% object-level accuracy and 62.67% output accuracy, above all tested frontier structured-output baselines. On our end-to-end memory benchmark, xmemory reaches 97.10% F1, compared with 80.16%-87.24% across the third-party baselines. On the application-level task, xmemory reaches 95.2% accuracy, outperforming specialised memory systems, code-generated Markdown harnesses, and customer-facing frontier-model application harnesses. The results show that, for memory workloads requiring stable facts and stateful computation, architecture matters more than retrieval scale or model strength alone.

AI.tldr: Proposes schema-grounded external memory (xmemory) with an iterative, write-focused pipeline to enforce exact facts and stateful updates, outperforming retrieval-based methods on structured extraction, end-to-end memory, and application benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Persistent AI memory often treats memory as retrieval of prose, which fails for production needs that require exact facts, current state, updates, deletions, aggregation, and explicit unknowns. A schema-grounded approach can constrain and validate memory records.
AI.method: An iterative, schema-aware write path decomposes ingestion into object detection, field detection, and field-value extraction, with validation gates, local retries, and stateful prompt control. Reads are constrained queries over verified records rather than inference over retrieved text.
AI.result: Extraction benchmark: object-level 90.42%, output 62.67% (best among frontier structured-output baselines). End-to-end memory benchmark: 97.10% F1 (vs 80.16–87.24% for baselines). Application-level task: 95.2% accuracy, outperforming specialized memory systems and frontier-model harnesses.
AI.conclusion: For memory workloads requiring stable facts and stateful computation, architecture (schema-grounded, write-first memory) matters more than retrieval scale or model strength; the approach demonstrates strong gains across structured, end-to-end, and application tasks.

title: 3D Reconstruction Techniques in the Manufacturing Domain: Applications, Research Opportunities and Use Cases
summary: This comprehensive review examines the evolution and the current state of the art in three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction techniques in manufacturing applications. The analysis covers both traditional approaches and emerging deep learning methods, showing a critical research gap in unified 3d reconstruction frameworks. Through systematic review of 106 recent publications, we classify reconstruction techniques into three primary categories: data acquisition, point cloud generation, post-processing and applications. Non-contact methods, particularly structured light scanning and stereo vision, have shown significant adoption in manufacturing, with 47% of surveyed applications focusing on quality inspection. The integration of deep learning has enhanced reconstruction accuracy and processing speed, particularly in feature extraction and matching. Key applications span design and development (13%), machining (8%), process (17%), assembly (22%), and quality inspection (40%). While current technologies achieve sub-millimeter accuracy in controlled environments, challenges persist in handling reflective surfaces and dynamic environments. Our findings indicate a trend toward hybrid systems combining multiple sensor types and processing methods to overcome individual limitations. This survey provides a structured framework for understanding current capabilities and future directions in manufacturing-focused 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Systematic review of manufacturing-focused 3D reconstruction (106 papers) showing non-contact methods (structured light, stereo vision) dominate and deep learning enhances accuracy/speed; identifies a gap in unified reconstruction frameworks and a shift toward hybrid sensor-method systems. Quality inspection dominates applications (40%), with sub-millimeter accuracy in controlled settings but challenges for reflective/dynamic scenes.
AI.motivation: To synthesize current state-of-the-art in 3D reconstruction for manufacturing, identify gaps (notably unified frameworks), and guide future research and practice by comparing traditional and DL-based approaches across data acquisition, point cloud generation, post-processing, and applications.
AI.method: Systematic review of 106 recent publications in manufacturing 3D reconstruction. Classification into data acquisition, point cloud generation, post-processing, and applications. Quantitative and qualitative assessment of method adoption (e.g., non-contact methods) and application distribution; evaluation of accuracy and limitations reported in controlled environments.
AI.result: DL integration improves reconstruction accuracy and processing speed, especially in feature extraction/matching. Non-contact methods are widely adopted; ~47% of surveyed applications target quality inspection. Key application shares: design/development 13%, machining 8%, process 17%, assembly 22%, quality inspection 40%. Current technologies achieve sub-millimeter accuracy in controlled environments but struggle with reflective surfaces and dynamic scenes. A trend toward hybrid systems combining multiple sensors and processing approaches emerges. The study provides a structured framework and highlights the need for unified 3D reconstruction frameworks and future directions for manufacturing.
AI.conclusion: There is a clear gap in unified 3D reconstruction frameworks for manufacturing. Future work should emphasize hybrid sensor data fusion, standardized benchmarks, and integrated pipelines that balance accuracy, speed, and robustness across diverse manufacturing scenarios.

title: Cost-Aware Learning
summary: We consider the problem of Cost-Aware Learning, where sampling different component functions of a finite-sum objective incurs different costs. The objective is to reach a target error while minimizing the total cost. First, we propose the Cost-Aware Stochastic Gradient Descent algorithm for convex functions, and derive its cost complexity to attain an error of $ε$. Furthermore, we establish a lower bound for this setting and provide a subset selection algorithm to further reduce the cost of training. We apply our theoretical insights to reinforcement learning with language models, where the computational cost of policy gradients varies with sequence length. To this end, we introduce Cost-Aware GRPO, an algorithm designed to reduce the cost of policy optimization while preserving performance. Empirical results on 1.5B and 8B LLMs demonstrate that our approach reduces the tokens used in policy optimization by up to about 30% while matching or exceeding baseline accuracy.

AI.tldr: Cost-aware optimization for finite-sum objectives with varying sampling costs; proposes Cost-Aware SGD for convex functions, derives cost to achieve error ε, proves a lower bound, and introduces a subset-selection method. Extends to RL with language models via Cost-Aware GRPO to reduce policy-gradient computation cost; empirical results show up to ~30% token reduction with maintained or improved accuracy on 1.5B and 8B LLMs.
AI.motivation: In many learning tasks, different data samples or components incur different computation costs. The goal is to minimize total cost while attaining a target error, rather than simply minimizing iterations or gradient variance.
AI.method: 1) Cost-Aware SGD (CA-SGD) for convex finite-sum objectives; derive its cost complexity to reach error ε. 2) Prove a lower bound for the cost-aware setting. 3) Propose a subset-selection algorithm to further reduce training cost. 4) Apply insights to RL with language models by introducing Cost-Aware GRPO, reducing cost of policy optimization while preserving performance.
AI.result: Derives cost-dependent complexity bounds for CA-SGD; establishes fundamental lower bounds; shows subset selection reduces cost; introduces GRPO for cost-aware policy optimization; empirical experiments on 1.5B and 8B LLMs demonstrate up to ~30% reduction in tokens used in policy optimization with comparable or better accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Cost-aware learning strategies can substantially reduce computational cost in both convex optimization and RL with large LLMs, without sacrificing performance; subset selection and cost-aware policy optimization are effective tools for efficient training.

title: Taming the Centaur(s) with LAPITHS: a framework for a theoretically grounded interpretation of AI performances
summary: We introduce a framework called LAPITHS (Language model Analysis through Paradigm grounded Interpretations of Theses about Human likenesS) and use it to show that several major claims advanced by models such as CENTAUR, proposed as an artificial Unified Model of Cognition, are not theoretically or empirically justified. LAPITHS provides a principled reference point for counteracting the current behaviouristic tendency in AI research to interpret the human level performances of transformer based language models as evidence of human like underlying computation and, by extension, as signs of cognitive abilities. The novelty of LAPITHS lies in making explicit the arguments grounded in two quantitative assessments: (i) the Minimal Cognitive Grid, a theoretically motivated method for estimating the cognitive plausibility of artificial systems, and (ii) a behavioural comparison showing that results similar to those reported for CENTAUR like models can be reproduced by other systems that do not satisfy the structural constraints typically associated with cognitive plausibility, and whose outputs do not provide independent explanatory insight into human cognition.

AI.tldr: Introduces LAPITHS, a framework to critique CENTAUR-like claims about human-like cognition in transformer models; centers on cognitive plausibility with two quantitative assessments.
AI.motivation: Counteract the tendency to equate language-model performance with human-like cognition and provide a principled, quantitative basis for evaluating cognitive plausibility.
AI.method: Two quantitative assessments: (i) Minimal Cognitive Grid to estimate cognitive plausibility of artificial systems; (ii) behavioral comparison showing CENTAUR-like results can arise from systems lacking cognitive-structural constraints or explanatory power about human cognition.
AI.result: LAPITHS offers a reference framework that challenges the theoretical and empirical justification of CENTAUR-type claims and highlights that similar performance can be produced without cognitive plausibility, undermining assertions of human-like cognition.
AI.conclusion: Proposes LAPITHS as a safeguard against behaviorist bias in AI research, promoting explicit cognitive-grounding in model evaluation and advocating for more rigorous demonstrations of cognitive-level AI capabilities.

title: AesRM: Improving Video Aesthetics with Expert-Level Feedback
summary: Despite rapid advances in photorealistic video generation, real-world applications such as filmmaking require video aesthetics, e.g., harmonious colors and cinematic lighting, beyond visual fidelity. Prior work on visual aesthetics largely focuses on images, often reducing aesthetics to coarse definitions, e.g., visual pleasure, without a rigorous and systematic evaluation. To improve video aesthetics, we propose a hierarchical rubric that decomposes video aesthetics into three core dimensions, Visual Aesthetics (VA), Visual Fidelity (VF), and Visual Plausibility (VP), with 15 fine-grained criteria, e.g., shot composition. This framework enables a large-scale expert-annotated preference dataset and an evaluation benchmark, AesVideo-Bench, containing about 2500 video pairs with expert annotations on VA, VF, and VP. We then build a family of Video Aesthetic Reward Models (AesRM): AesRM-Base, which directly predicts pairwise preferences on these dimensions to provide efficient post-training rewards, and AesRM-CoT, which additionally generates CoT aligned with all 15 criteria to improve assessment interpretability. Specifically, we train AesRM with a three-stage progressive scheme: (1) Atomic Aesthetic Capability Learning, which strengthens AesRM's recognition of fundamental aesthetic concepts, e.g., accurately identifying centered composition; (2) Cold-Start, aligning the model with structured reasoning protocols; and (3) GRPO, further improving evaluation accuracy. To enhance AesRM-CoT, we additionally propose self-consistency-based CoT synthesis to improve CoT quality and design CoT-based process rewards during GRPO. Extensive experiments show AesRM outperforms baselines on multiple aesthetics benchmarks and is more robust, with lower position bias. Finally, we align Wan2.2 with AesRM and observe clear aesthetic gains over existing aesthetic reward models.

AI.tldr: Introduces a hierarchical 3D aesthetic rubric for videos (VA, VF, VP with 15 criteria), builds AesVideo-Bench dataset (~2500 video pairs), and proposes Video Aesthetic Reward Models (AesRM-Base and AesRM-CoT) with a three-stage training (Atomic, Cold-Start, GRPO) plus self-consistency CoT; demonstrates superior performance and robust, lower position bias; aligns Wan2.2 with AesRM for aesthetic gains.
AI.motivation: Video aesthetics require rigorous, scalable evaluation beyond image-centric methods. Existing benchmarks are limited and often lack expert-consensus on what constitutes aesthetics in video; there is a need for structured criteria, large expert-annotated data, and reward models to guide generation.
AI.method: Define a 3-dimensional, 15-criterion rubric (Visual Aesthetics, Visual Fidelity, Visual Plausibility). Create AesVideo-Bench with ~2500 expert-annotated video pairs across VA, VF, VP. Develop AesRM-Base to predict pairwise preferences on these dimensions; extend to AesRM-CoT that produces chain-of-thought aligned to all 15 criteria for interpretability. Train via a three-stage progressive scheme: (1) Atomic Aesthetic Capability Learning to recognize fundamental concepts (e.g., centered composition); (2) Cold-Start to align with structured reasoning; (3) GRPO to enhance evaluation accuracy. For AesRM-CoT, use self-consistency-based CoT synthesis and CoT-based process rewards during GRPO. Evaluate against baselines and align Wan2.2 with AesRM.
AI.result: AesRM outperforms baselines on multiple aesthetics benchmarks and shows robustness with lower position bias. Alignment with Wan2.2 yields clear aesthetic gains over existing aesthetic reward models.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable, interpretable framework for video aesthetics evaluation and reward modeling, enabling better post-training rewards and improved aesthetic quality in video generation; the approach demonstrates strong empirical gains and potential benefits for real-world filmmaking workflows.

title: FedHarmony: Harmonizing Heterogeneous Label Correlations in Federated Multi-Label Learning
summary: Federated Multi-Label Learning is a distributed paradigm where multiple clients possess heterogeneous multi-label data and perform collaborative learning under privacy constraints without sharing raw data. However, modeling label correlations under heterogeneous distributions remains challenging. Due to client-specific label spaces and varying co-occurrence patterns, correlations learned by individual clients inevitably deviate from the global structure, a phenomenon we term label correlation drift. To address this, we propose FedHarmony, a framework that harmonizes heterogeneous label correlations across clients. It introduces consensus correlation, capturing agreement among other clients and serving as a global teacher to correct biased local estimates. During aggregation, FedHarmony evaluates each client by both data size and correlation quality, assigning weights accordingly. Moreover, we develop an accelerated optimization algorithm for FedHarmony and theoretically establish faster convergence without sacrificing accuracy. Experiments on real-world federated multi-label datasets show that FedHarmony consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: FedHarmony aligns heterogeneous client label correlations in federated multi-label learning by using a global consensus correlation as a teacher, applying correlation-aware, data-size-weighted aggregation, and employing an accelerated optimization algorithm with convergence guarantees, achieving superior performance over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Label correlations in federated multi-label learning drift across clients due to heterogeneous label spaces and co-occurrence patterns. This misalignment impedes global models trained without sharing raw data, necessitating a mechanism to harmonize correlations while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Introduce consensus correlation as a global teacher representing agreement among clients. Use consensus to correct biased local estimates. During aggregation, weight clients by data size and correlation quality. Develop an accelerated optimization algorithm with theoretical convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on real-world federated multi-label datasets show FedHarmony consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FedHarmony effectively mitigates label correlation drift in federated multi-label learning, improves performance, and offers faster convergence with theoretical guarantees.

title: MM-StanceDet: Retrieval-Augmented Multi-modal Multi-agent Stance Detection
summary: Multimodal Stance Detection (MSD) is crucial for understanding public discourse, yet effectively fusing text and image, especially with conflicting signals, remains challenging. Existing methods often face difficulties with contextual grounding, cross-modal interpretation ambiguity, and single-pass reasoning fragility. To address these, we propose Retrieval-Augmented Multi-modal Multi-agent Stance Detection (MM-StanceDet), a novel multi-agent framework integrating Retrieval Augmentation for contextual grounding, specialized Multimodal Analysis agents for nuanced interpretation, a Reasoning-Enhanced Debate stage for exploring perspectives, and Self-Reflection for robust adjudication. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate MM-StanceDet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating the efficacy of its multi-agent architecture and structured reasoning stages in addressing complex multimodal stance challenges.

AI.tldr: A retrieval-augmented, multi-agent framework for multimodal stance detection (MM-StanceDet) with a multi-stage reasoning process, achieving state-of-the-art results on five datasets.
AI.motivation: Multimodal stance detection is crucial for understanding public discourse but faces context grounding, cross-modal ambiguity, and fragile single-pass reasoning, especially with conflicting signals.
AI.method: Introduce MM-StanceDet: Retrieval Augmentation for contextual grounding; specialized Multimodal Analysis agents for nuanced interpretation; a Reasoning-Enhanced Debate stage to explore perspectives; and Self-Reflection for robust adjudication. The architecture processes textual and visual inputs via agents, retrieves external context, conducts debated reasoning, and self-assesses conclusions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets show that MM-StanceDet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating the benefit of the multi-agent structure and structured reasoning stages.
AI.conclusion: A novel, structured multi-agent framework effectively addresses complex multimodal stance challenges, combining retrieval-grounded context, specialized multimodal analysis, debated reasoning, and self-reflection to improve accuracy and robustness.

title: UHR-Net: An Uncertainty-Aware Hypergraph Refinement Network for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Accurate lesion segmentation is crucial for clinical diagnosis and treatment planning. However, lesions often resemble surrounding tissues and exhibit ill-defined boundaries, leading to unstable predictions in boundary/transition regions. Moreover, small-lesion cues can be diluted by multi-scale feature extraction, causing under- or over-segmentation. To address these challenges, we propose an Uncertainty-Aware Hypergraph Refinement Network (UHR-Net). First, we introduce an Uncertainty-Oriented Instance Contrastive (UO-IC) pretraining strategy that couples geometry-aware copy-paste augmentation with hard-negative mining of lesion-like background regions to improve instance-level discrimination for small and visually ambiguous lesions. Second, we design an Uncertainty-Guided Hypergraph Refinement (UGHR) block, which derives an entropy-based uncertainty map from a coarse probability map to guide hypergraph refinement. By splitting hyperedge prototypes into foreground and background groups, UGHR decouples higher-order interactions and improves refinement in ambiguous regions. Experiments on five public benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines. Code is available at: https://github.com/CUGfreshman/UHR-Net.

AI.tldr: Proposes UHR-Net (Uncertainty-Aware Hypergraph Refinement Network) with Uncertainty-Oriented Instance Contrastive pretraining and Uncertainty-Guided Hypergraph Refinement to improve lesion segmentation, addressing boundary instability and small-lesion cues via entropy-guided refinement and hard-negative mining.
AI.motivation: Lesion segmentation suffers from ill-defined boundaries, unstable boundary predictions, and small lesions whose cues are overwhelmed by multi-scale features. There is a need for uncertainty modeling and higher-order context to improve discrimination and refinement.
AI.method: 1) Uncertainty-Oriented Instance Contrastive (UO-IC) pretraining with geometry-aware copy-paste augmentation and hard-negative mining of lesion-like backgrounds to boost instance-level discrimination, especially for small/ambiguous lesions. 2) Uncertainty-Guided Hypergraph Refinement (UGHR) block derives an entropy-based uncertainty map from a coarse probability map to guide hypergraph refinement; splits hyperedge prototypes into foreground/background groups to decouple higher-order interactions and improve refinement in ambiguous regions.
AI.result: Experiments on five public benchmarks show consistent gains over strong baselines, with improved handling of boundary regions and small lesions. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The proposed UHR-Net effectively tackles boundary-uncertainty and small-lesion challenges via a two-pronged strategy: uncertainty-aware contrastive pretraining and uncertainty-guided hypergraph refinement, yielding robust, cross-dataset gains.

title: MIFair: A Mutual-Information Framework for Intersectionality and Multiclass Fairness
summary: Fairness in machine learning remains challenging due to its ethical complexity, the absence of a universal definition, and the need for context-specific bias metrics. Existing methods still struggle with intersectionality, multiclass settings, and limited flexibility and generality. To address these gaps, we introduce MIFair, a unified framework for bias assessment and mitigation based on mutual information. MIFair provides a flexible metric template and an in-processing mitigation method inspired by the Prejudice Remover, defining group fairness as statistical independence between prediction-derived variables and sensitive attributes. We further strengthen its information-theoretic foundation by establishing equivalences with widely used fairness notions such as independence and separation. MIFair naturally supports intersectionality, complex subgroup structures, and multiclass classification and employs regularization-based training to reduce bias according to the selected metric. Its key advantage is its versatility: it consolidates diverse fairness requirements into a single coherent framework, enabling consistent benchmarking and simplifying practical use. Experiments on real-world tabular and image datasets show that MIFair effectively reduces bias, including previously unaddressed multi-attribute scenarios, while maintaining strong predictive performance across the evaluated settings.

AI.tldr: A mutual-information-based unified fairness framework (MIFair) for bias assessment and mitigation that handles intersectionality, multiclass settings, and complex subgroup structures; it uses in-processing mitigation inspired by Prejudice Remover, with regularization-based training to align with selected metric, and shows bias reduction with maintained accuracy on tabular and image data.
AI.motivation: Fair ML is ethically complex with no universal definition and context-dependent metrics; challenges include intersectionality, multiclass scenarios, and the need for flexible, generalizable bias measures. A unified, versatile framework would enable consistent benchmarking and practical deployment.
AI.method: MIFair defines a flexible metric template based on mutual information to assess bias. It includes an in-processing mitigation approach inspired by the Prejudice Remover, enforcing statistical independence between prediction-derived variables and sensitive attributes. It establishes equivalences with standard notions like independence (demographic parity) and separation (equalized odds), supports intersectionality and multiclass hypotheses, and uses regularization-based training to minimize bias per the chosen metric.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world tabular and image datasets shows that MIFair reduces bias, including in multi-attribute scenarios, while preserving strong predictive performance across tested settings.
AI.conclusion: MIFair offers a versatile, coherent framework that unifies diverse fairness notions, facilitates consistent benchmarking, and simplifies practical deployment for bias assessment and mitigation across complex data settings.

title: A Collective Variational Principle Unifying Bayesian Inference, Game Theory, and Thermodynamics
comment: Submitted to Nature. 21 pages, 4 figures. Code and data available at https://github.com/dbouchaffra/game-theoretic-free-energy-principle
summary: Collective intelligence emerges across biological, physical, and artificial systems without central coordination, yet a unifying principle governing such behaviour remains elusive. The Free Energy Principle explains how individual agents adapt through variational inference, while game theory formalises strategic interactions. Here we introduce the Game-Theoretic Free Energy Principle, a unified framework showing that multi-agent systems performing local free-energy minimisation implicitly implement a stochastic game. We prove that, under bounded rationality and local information constraints, stationary points of collective free energy correspond to approximate Nash equilibria of an induced game. Conversely, a broad class of cooperative games admits a variational representation in which equilibria arise as Gibbs distributions over coalitions, establishing a bridge between Bayesian inference and strategic interaction. To characterise higher-order effects, we introduce a free-energy formulation of the Harsanyi dividend, isolating irreducible multi-agent synergy. This yields a predictive theory of cooperation, including a falsifiable non-monotonic relationship between sensory precision and agent influence. We validate this prediction across neural, biological, and artificial multi-agent systems. These results identify a common variational principle underlying inference, thermodynamics, and game-theoretic equilibrium.

AI.tldr: A unifying variational framework—the Game-Theoretic Free Energy Principle (GTFEP)—ties the Free Energy Principle with multi-agent game theory. It shows that local free-energy minimisation yields stationary points that are approximate Nash equilibria under bounded rationality/local information. It represents cooperative games via Gibbs distributions over coalitions, introduces a free-energy-based Harsanyi dividend to capture irreducible multi-agent synergy, and yields testable predictions (e.g., non-monotonic sensor precision vs. agent influence) validated across neural, biological, and artificial systems. The work posits a common variational principle for inference, thermodynamics, and strategic interaction.
AI.motivation: To unify explanation of collective intelligent behavior across biological, physical, and artificial domains by linking variational inference (Free Energy Principle) with strategic interaction (game theory).
AI.method: Develop theoretical framework establishing correspondences: (i) stationary points of collective free energy correspond to approximate Nash equilibria under bounded rationality and local information; (ii) express a broad class of cooperative games as variational problems with Gibbs-distributed coalitions; (iii) formulate a free-energy version of the Harsanyi dividend to quantify higher-order synergy; (iv) derive predictions about cooperation (notably non-monotonic relation between sensory precision and influence); (v) validate predictions across neural, biological, and artificial multi-agent systems.
AI.result: Formal unification results: stationary points of multi-agent free-energy minimisation map to approximate Nash equilibria; cooperative games admit Gibbs-coalition variational representations; a free-energy Harsanyi dividend isolates irreducible multi-agent synergy; predictive theory of cooperation with falsifiable non-monotonic sensory precision–influence relation; empirical validation across diverse systems.
AI.conclusion: A common variational principle underpins inference, thermodynamics, and game-theoretic equilibrium, enabling a predictive theory of cooperation and a bridge between Bayesian inference and strategic interaction.

title: Beyond Gaussian Bottlenecks: Topologically Aligned Encoding of Vision-Transformer Feature Spaces
summary: Modern visual world modeling systems increasingly rely on high-capacity architectures and large-scale data to produce plausible motion, yet they often fail to preserve underlying 3D geometry or physically consistent camera dynamics. A key limitation lies not only in model capacity, but in the latent representations used to encode geometric structure. We propose S$^2$VAE, a geometry-first latent learning framework that focuses on compressing and representing the latent 3D state of a scene, including camera motion, depth, and point-level structure, rather than modeling appearance alone. Building on representations from a Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT), we introduce a novel type of variational autoencoder using a product of Power Spherical latent distributions, explicitly enforcing hyperspherical structure in the bottleneck to preserve directional and geometric semantics under strong compression. Across depth estimation, camera pose recovery, and point cloud reconstruction, we show that geometry-aligned hyperspherical latents consistently outperform conventional Gaussian bottlenecks, particularly in high-compression regimes. Our results highlight latent geometry as a first-class design choice for physically grounded visual and world models.
