AI.tldr: Geometry-first latent learning with a hyperspherical latent space (Power Spherical) in S^2VAE to compress 3D scene state (depth, pose, geometry) and improve high-compression depth/pose/point-cloud tasks.
AI.motivation: Current perceptual models are appearance-centric and rely on Gaussian bottlenecks, which can degrade 3D geometry and camera dynamics under compression. A latent representation aligned with geometric structure could preserve 3D semantics more faithfully.
AI.method: Propose S^2VAE built on VGGT representations. Introduce a product of Power Spherical latent distributions that enforce hyperspherical structure in the bottleneck to capture directional and geometric information. Apply to depth estimation, camera pose recovery, and point cloud reconstruction.
AI.result: Hyperspherical, geometry-aligned latents outperform conventional Gaussian bottlenecks, especially under high compression, across depth, pose, and geometry tasks.
AI.conclusion: Latent geometry should be a first-class design choice for physically grounded visual/world models; geometry-aware latent spaces enable better preservation of 3D structure under compression.

title: Shuffling-Aware Optimization for Private Vector Mean Estimation
summary: We study $d$-dimensional unbiased mean estimation in the single-message shuffle model, where each user sends a single privatized message and the analyzer only observes the shuffled multiset of reports. While minimax-optimal mechanisms are well understood in the local differential privacy setting, the corresponding notion of optimality after shuffling has remained largely unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce the recently proposed shuffle index and use it to formulate the post-shuffling mechanism design problem as an explicit optimization problem. We then establish a minimax lower bound on the achievable mean squared error in terms of the shuffle index, which implies that mechanisms that are optimal under LDP can become suboptimal once shuffling is applied. Finally, we construct an asymptotically minimax optimal mechanism in the high privacy regime, which as a consequence achieves a privacy-utility trade-off nearly identical to that of the central Gaussian mechanism.

AI.tldr: Introduces shuffle index-based framework for d-dimensional unbiased mean estimation in single-message shuffle model; proves minimax MSE lower bound in terms of shuffle index; shows LDP-optimal mechanisms can be suboptimal after shuffling; constructs an asymptotically minimax-optimal mechanism in the high-privacy regime achieving near-central Gaussian performance.
AI.motivation: To understand how a pre-processing shuffle alters the optimal privacy-utility trade-off for mean estimation, and to quantify the gap between LDP-optimal and post-shuffle optimum using a concrete index.
AI.method: Define the shuffle index; formulate post-shuffle design as a constrained optimization; derive a minimax lower bound on MSE using the index; design an asymptotically minimax-optimal mechanism in the high-privacy limit.
AI.result: Derived a MSE lower bound parameterized by the shuffle index; demonstrated suboptimality of LDP-optimal mechanisms after shuffling; produced an asymptotically minimax-optimal mechanism with performance matching the central Gaussian benchmark in the high-privacy regime.
AI.conclusion: Shuffling significantly changes optimal mechanisms; the shuffle index provides a principled tool to assess and design post-shuffle privatization; in strong privacy, one can achieve near-central Gaussian privacy-utility trade-offs.

title: The Effects of Visual Priming on Cooperative Behavior in Vision-Language Models
summary: As Vision-Language Models (VLMs) become increasingly integrated into decision-making systems, it is essential to understand how visual inputs influence their behavior. This paper investigates the effects of visual priming on VLMs' cooperative behavior using the Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma (IPD) as a test scenario. We examine whether exposure to images depicting behavioral concepts (kindness/helpfulness vs. aggressiveness/selfishness) and color-coded reward matrices alters VLM decision patterns. Experiments were conducted across multiple state-of-the-art VLMs. We further explore mitigation strategies including prompt modifications, Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning, and visual token reduction. Results show that VLM behavior can be influenced by both image content and color cues, with varying susceptibility and mitigation effectiveness across models. These findings not only underscore the importance of robust evaluation frameworks for VLM deployment in visually rich and safety-critical environments, but also highlight how architectural and training differences among models may lead to distinct behavioral responses-an area worthy of further investigation.

AI.tldr: Visual inputs and color cues can prime vision-language models to alter cooperative decisions in an Iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma; mitigation strategies (prompt design, Chain-of-Thought, visual token reduction) show model-dependent effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To understand how visual stimuli and color-coded cues influence the cooperative behavior and safety of vision-language models in decision-making tasks, and how architectural/training differences contribute to susceptibility.
AI.method: Cross-model experiments across multiple state-of-the-art VLMs. The study exposes models to images depicting behavioral concepts (kindness/helpfulness vs. aggressiveness/selfishness) and color-coded reward matrices, then measures decision patterns in the Iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma. It also evaluates mitigation strategies including prompt modifications, Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning, and visual token reduction.
AI.result: Visual content and color cues can influence decision patterns, with varying susceptibility across models. Mitigation strategies provide some mitigation but with model-dependent effectiveness. The work emphasizes the need for robust, visually grounded evaluation frameworks and notes that architectural and training differences drive distinct behavioral responses.
AI.conclusion: Understanding visual priors and their impact on LLM/VLM behavior is crucial for safe deployment in visually rich environments. The findings motivate further investigation into model-specific vulnerabilities and improved prompting/mitigation strategies to foster robust, safe decision-making.

title: Exponential families from a single KL identity
summary: Exponential families encompass the distributions central to modern machine learning -- softmax, Gaussians, and Boltzmann distributions -- and underlie the theory of variational inference, entropy-regularized reinforcement learning, and RLHF. We isolate a simple identity for exponential families that expresses the KL difference $\mathrm{KL}(q \| p_{λ_2}) - \mathrm{KL}(q \| p_{λ_1})$ in terms of the log-partition function $A(λ)$ and the moment $μ_q$. Remarkably, this identity together with the single fact that $\mathrm{KL} \geq 0$ (with equality iff $p = q$) suffices, by direct substitution and rearrangement, to derive a cluster of results that are classically obtained by separate, heavier arguments: a generalized three-point identity for arbitrary reference distributions, Pythagorean theorems for I-projections and reverse I-projections, convexity of the log-partition function, identification of its Legendre dual in KL terms, the Gibbs variational principle, and the explicit optimizer in KL-regularized reward maximization, including the exponential tilting formula underlying entropy-regularized control and RLHF. Beyond these purely algebraic consequences, standard analytic arguments recover the gradient formula for the log-partition function, the Bregman representation of within-family KL divergence, and the surjectivity of the moment map. The note is self-contained.

AI.tldr: A simple KL-difference identity for exponential families yields a unifying algebraic framework that derives classical results (three-point identity, I-projections, Pythagoras, convexity of log-partition, Legendre dual, Gibbs variational principle) and the optimizer for KL-regularized rewards, including entropy-regularized control and RLHF, from KL≥0 alone.
AI.motivation: To show that a single, elementary identity in exponential-family theory suffices to derive a wide cluster of results typically proved via disparate arguments, thereby unifying variational inference, entropy-regularized RL, and RLHF.
AI.method: Derive the KL difference KL(q||p_λ2) − KL(q||p_λ1) explicitly in terms of A(λ) and the q-moment μ_q for exponential families. Combine this identity with the nonnegativity of KL to rearrange and obtain multiple classical results. Extend to general reference distributions and explore consequences for the log-partition gradient, Bregman representations, and moment-map surjectivity. Apply framework to KL-regularized reward maximization and entropy-regularized control/RLHF.
AI.result: A cluster of results follows: (i) a generalized three-point identity for arbitrary references; (ii) Pythagorean theorems for I- and reverse I-projections; (iii) convexity of log-partition function and its Legendre dual in KL terms; (iv) Gibbs variational principle; (v) explicit KL-regularized optimizer and exponential tilting underpinning entropy-regularized control/RLHF; plus gradient formula for log-partition, Bregman representation of within-family KL, and surjectivity of the moment map. The note is self-contained.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a single, simple algebraic identity suffices to derive a broad suite of foundational results in exponential-family theory, providing a cohesive, self-contained account of connections among variational principles, convex duality, projection geometry, and KL-regularized optimization.

title: GUI Agents with Reinforcement Learning: Toward Digital Inhabitants
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents have emerged as a promising paradigm for intelligent systems that perceive and interact with graphical interfaces visually. Yet supervised fine-tuning alone cannot handle long-horizon credit assignment, distribution shifts, and safe exploration in irreversible environments, making Reinforcement Learning (RL) a central methodology for advancing automation. In this work, we present the first comprehensive overview of the intersection between RL and GUI agents, and examine how this research direction may evolve toward digital inhabitants. We propose a principled taxonomy that organizes existing methods into Offline RL, Online RL, and Hybrid Strategies, and complement it with analyses of reward engineering, data efficiency, and key technical innovations. Our analysis reveals several emerging trends: the tension between reliability and scalability is motivating the adoption of composite, multi-tier reward architectures; GUI I/O latency bottlenecks are accelerating the shift toward world-model-based training, which can yield substantial performance gains; and the spontaneous emergence of System-2-style deliberation suggests that explicit reasoning supervision may not be necessary when sufficiently rich reward signals are available. We distill these findings into a roadmap covering process rewards, continual RL, cognitive architectures, and safe deployment, aiming to guide the next generation of robust GUI automation and its agent-native infrastructure.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive overview of RL for GUI agents; proposes a taxonomy (Offline RL, Online RL, Hybrid) with analyses of reward engineering, data efficiency, and key innovations; identifies trends and provides a roadmap for robust GUI automation.
AI.motivation: Supervised fine-tuning struggles with long-horizon credit assignment, distribution shifts, and safe exploration in irreversible GUI environments, making RL essential for robust GUI automation.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature survey; develops a principled taxonomy organizing methods into Offline RL, Online RL, and Hybrid strategies; analyzes reward engineering, data efficiency, world-model-based training, and System-2-like deliberation.
AI.result: Identifies emerging trends: reliability vs. scalability drives multi-tier reward architectures; GUI I/O latency pushes adoption of world-model-based training with notable performance gains; spontaneous System-2-like deliberation suggests explicit reasoning supervision may be unnecessary with rich reward signals; provides a roadmap addressing process rewards, continual RL, cognitive architectures, and safe deployment.
AI.conclusion: Offers guidance for the next generation of robust GUI automation and agent-native infrastructure through a roadmap focusing on process rewards, continual RL, cognitive architectures, and safe deployment.

title: AdvDMD: Adversarial Reward Meets DMD For High-Quality Few-Step Generation
summary:
Diffusion models offer superior generation quality at the expense of extensive sampling steps.
  Distillation methods, with Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) as a popular example, can mitigate this issue, but performance degradation remains pronounced when sampling steps are limited.
  Reinforcement learning (RL) has been leveraged to improve the few-step generation quality during distillation, with the potential to even surpass the performance of the teacher model. However, existing approaches are combinatorial in nature, merely integrating an RL process with the distillation process, which introduces unnecessary complexities. To address this gap, we propose AdvDMD, a method that seamlessly unifies DMD distillation and RL.
  Specifically, AdvDMD employs the adversarially trained discriminator from DMD2 as the reward model, which assigns low scores to generated images and high scores to real ones.
  It is trained on both intermediate and final states of the denoising process and updated online with the distilled model, enabling a holistic supervision of the sampling trajectories and mitigating reward hacking.
  We adopt a unified SDE backward simulation and a different training schedule for DMD and RL to enable a more stable and efficient training.
  Experimental results demonstrate that the 4-step AdvDMD outperforms the original 40-step model for SD3.5 on DPG-Bench, while achieving significant performance gains for SD3 on the GenEval.
  On Qwen-Image, our 2-step AdvDMD achieves superior performance over TwinFlow.

AI.tldr: A unified AdvDMD method integrates distribution matching distillation (DMD) and reinforcement learning (RL) for diffusion models to enable high-quality generation with far fewer steps, by using an adversarial discriminator as the reward and a unified SDE backward simulation.
AI.motivation: Reduce sampling steps in diffusion models without sacrificing performance. Existing approaches blend RL with distillation but are complex and prone to reward hacking; a unified, stable training framework is needed to improve few-step generation.
AI.method: AdvDMD uses the adversarially trained discriminator from DMD2 as the reward model, training on intermediate and final denoising states and updating online with the distilled model. It employs a unified SDE backward simulation and a distinct training schedule for DMD and RL to stabilize training and provide holistic supervision of sampling trajectories.
AI.result: Empirical results show 4-step AdvDMD outperforms the original 40-step model for SD3.5 on DPG-Bench and yields significant gains for SD3 on GenEval; on Qwen-Image, 2-step AdvDMD surpasses TwinFlow.
AI.conclusion: A unified AdvDMD framework effectively merges DMD distillation and RL, enabling strong few-step diffusion model performance with substantially fewer sampling steps and robust training stability.

title: Early Detection of Water Stress by Plant Electrophysiology: Machine Learning for Irrigation Management
summary: Purpose: Fast detection of plant stress is key to plant phenotyping, precision agriculture, and automated crop management. In particular, efficient irrigation management requires early identification of water stress to optimize resource use while maintaining crop performance. Direct physiological sensing offers the potential to detect stress responses before visible symptoms appear. Methods: In this study, we recorded electrophysiological signals from greenhouse-grown tomato plants subjected to water stress and developed a framework based on machine learning for online stress detection. The recorded time-series data were processed using a processing pipeline that includes statistical feature extraction and selection, automated machine learning or alternatively deep learning, and probability calibration. Results: Across multiple input time horizons, we found that a 30-minute look-back window strikes the best balance between rapid decision-making and classification performance. Using automated machine learning, the framework achieved classification accuracies of up to 92%, outperforming deep learning approaches. Sequential backward selection reduced the feature set while maintaining performance. Importantly, the framework detects transitions from healthy to stressed states in recordings that were not included in the training set. Conclusion: Overall, we provide a decision-support tool for farmers and establish a foundation for biofeedback-driven irrigation control to improve resource efficiency in (semi-)autonomous crop production systems.

AI.tldr: ML-based online stress detection in tomato using electrophysiological signals; 30-min look-back window; AutoML achieves up to 92% accuracy, outperforming DL; feature selection reduces dimensionality; detects transitions to stress including unseen data; supports biofeedback-driven irrigation control.
AI.motivation: Need for rapid, early detection of water stress to enable efficient irrigation and resource management in precision agriculture; direct physiological sensing can reveal stress before visual symptoms.
AI.method: Time-series electrophysiological recordings from greenhouse-grown tomatoes under water stress; processing pipeline with statistical feature extraction and selection; automated machine learning or deep learning for classification; probability calibration; exploration of multiple input time horizons with a 30-minute look-back as optimal; sequential backward selection for feature reduction.
AI.result: Classification accuracies up to 92% with AutoML, outperforming deep learning approaches; a 30-minute look-back balances speed and performance; feature set reduced via backward selection while preserving accuracy; framework detects transitions from healthy to stressed states even in unseen data.
AI.conclusion: Provides a decision-support tool for farmers and lays groundwork for biofeedback-driven irrigation control in (semi-)autonomous crop production systems.

title: MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons
summary: Recent methods for arbitrary-skeleton motion capture from monocular video follow a factorized pipeline, where a Video-to-Pose network predicts joint positions and an analytical inverse-kinematics (IK) stage recovers joint rotations. While effective, this design is inherently limited, since joint positions do not fully determine rotations and leave degrees of freedom such as bone-axis twist ambiguous, and the non-differentiable IK stage prevents the system from adapting to noisy predictions or optimizing for the final animation objective. In this work, we present the first fully end-to-end framework in which both Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation are learnable and jointly optimized. We observe that the ambiguity in pose-to-rotation mapping arises from missing coordinate system information: the same joint positions can correspond to different rotations under different rest poses and local axis conventions. To resolve this, we introduce a reference pose-rotation pair from the target asset, which, together with the rest pose, not only anchors the mapping but also defines the underlying rotation coordinate system. This formulation turns rotation prediction into a well-constrained conditional problem and enables effective learning. In addition, our model predicts joint positions directly from video without relying on mesh intermediates, improving both robustness and efficiency. Both stages share a skeleton-aware Global-Local Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GL-GMHA) module for joint-level local reasoning and global coordination. Experiments on Truebones Zoo and Objaverse show that our method reduces rotation error from ~17 degrees to ~10 degrees, and to 6.54 degrees on unseen skeletons, while achieving ~20x faster inference than mesh-based pipelines. Project page: https://animotionlab.github.io/MoCapAnythingV2/

AI.tldr: End-to-end monocular motion capture with jointly learnable Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation, grounded by a reference pose–rotation pair to define the rotation coordinate system, plus direct joint-position prediction and a shared Global-Local Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GL-GMHA) module; achieves lower rotation error and faster inference than mesh-based pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current factorized pipelines predict joint positions first and then fit rotations via IK. The IK stage is non-differentiable and joint positions do not uniquely determine rotations, leaving axis twists and coordinate-system ambiguities unresolved. An end-to-end, learnable framework that also grounds rotations in a defined coordinate system is needed for robustness and optimization.
AI.method: Propose a fully end-to-end framework where both Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation are learnable and jointly optimized. Resolve rotation ambiguity by introducing a reference pose–rotation pair from the target asset, together with the rest pose to define the rotation coordinate system, turning rotation prediction into a conditioned, well-constrained problem. Predict joint positions directly from video without mesh intermediates. Employ a skeleton-aware GL-GMHA module shared by both stages for joint-level local reasoning and global coordination.
AI.result: Demonstrates rotation error reduction from ~17° to ~10°, and to 6.54° on unseen skeletons (Objaverse/Truebones Zoo), with approximately 20x faster inference than mesh-based pipelines.
AI.conclusion: First fully end-to-end learnable Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation MoCap framework that resolves rotation-coordinate ambiguity via a reference pose and rest pose, enables direct joint-position prediction, and delivers robust performance with significant speedups over mesh-based approaches.

title: PROMISE-AD: Progression-aware Multi-horizon Survival Estimation for Alzheimer's Disease Progression and Dynamic Tracking
summary: Individualized Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression prediction requires models that use irregular visits, account for censoring, avoid diagnostic leakage, and provide calibrated horizon risks. We propose PROgression-aware MultI-horizon Survival Estimation for Alzheimer's Disease (PROMISE-AD), a leakage-safe survival framework for predicting conversion from cognitively normal (CN) to mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and from MCI to AD dementia using ADNI/TADPOLE tabular histories. PROMISE-AD converts pre-index visits into tokens with standardized measurements, missingness masks, longitudinal changes, time-normalized slopes, visit timing, and non-diagnostic categorical attributes. A temporal Transformer fuses global, attention-pooled, and latest-visit representations to estimate a progression score and latent discrete-time mixture hazards. Training combines survival likelihood, horizon-specific focal risk loss, progression ranking, hazard smoothness, and mixture-balance regularization, followed by validation-set isotonic calibration for 1-, 2-, 3-, and 5-year risks. In held-out testing across three seeds, PROMISE-AD achieved an integrated Brier score (IBS) of 0.085 $\pm$ 0.012, C-index of 0.808 $\pm$ 0.015, and mean time-dependent AUC of 0.840 $\pm$ 0.081 for CN-to-MCI conversion, yielding the lowest IBS among compared methods. For MCI-to-AD conversion, PROMISE-AD achieved the highest C-index (0.894 $\pm$ 0.018) and near-ceiling 5-year discrimination (AUROC 0.997 $\pm$ 0.003; AUPRC 0.999 $\pm$ 0.001), although some baselines had lower IBS. Ablations and interpretability supported longitudinal change features, fused temporal representations, mixture hazards, cognitive and functional measures, APOE4 status, and recent conversion-proximal visits. These findings suggest that progression-aware survival modeling can provide interpretable multi-horizon AD conversion risk estimates.

AI.tldr: PROMISE-AD: a leakage-safe, multi-horizon survival model using a temporal Transformer to predict CN→MCI and MCI→AD conversion from irregular longitudinal histories; achieves strong discrimination and calibration (1–5 year) on ADNI/TADPOLE data.
AI.motivation: Address irregular visit timing, censoring, diagnostic leakage, and calibrated horizon risk in individualized AD progression prediction using tabular histories.
AI.method: Encode pre-index visits into tokens with measurements, masks, longitudinal changes, slopes, timing, and categorical attributes; fuse with a temporal Transformer to produce a progression score and latent discrete-time mixture hazards; train with survival likelihood, horizon-specific focal risk, ranking, hazard smoothness, mixture balance regularization; isotonic calibration on validation for 1–5 year risks.
AI.result: On held-out tests across three seeds: IBS 0.085±0.012; C-index 0.808±0.015 for CN→MCI; MCI→AD: C-index 0.894±0.018; AUROC 0.997±0.003; AUPRC 0.999±0.001; PROMISE-AD achieved lowest IBS among baselines; Ablations highlight importance of longitudinal changes, fused temporal representations, mixture hazards, cognitive/functional measures, APOE4, and recent visits.
AI.conclusion: Progression-aware survival modeling enables interpretable, multi-horizon AD conversion risk estimates with strong discrimination and calibrated horizons.

title: Language Models Refine Mechanical Linkage Designs Through Symbolic Reflection and Modular Optimisation
summary: Designing mechanical linkages involves combinatorial topology selection and continuous parameter fitting. We show that language models can systematically improve linkage designs through symbolic representations. Language model agents explore discrete topologies while numerical optimisers fit continuous parameters. A symbolic lifting operator translates simulator trajectories into qualitative descriptors, motion labels, temporal predicates, and structural diagnostics that models interpret across iterative design cycles. Across six engineering-relevant motion targets and three open-source models (Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 MoE 30B-A3B), the modular architecture reduces geometric error by up to 68% and improves structural validity by up to 134% over monolithic baselines. Critically, 78.6% of iterative refinement trajectories show measurable improvement, with the system correctly diagnosing overconstraint (56.3%) and underconstraint (35.6%) failure modes and proposing grounded corrections. Models across all three families acquire interpretable mechanical reasoning strategies without fine-tuning, demonstrating that principled symbolic abstraction bridges generative AI and the numerical precision required for engineering design.

AI.tldr: LM-driven modular design of mechanical linkages uses symbolic lifting to translate simulation traces into descriptors, enabling discrete topology search and continuous parameter fitting; achieves substantial reductions in geometric error and gains in structural validity across multiple models and tasks, with interpretable mechanical reasoning emerging without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between combinatorial topology search and continuous parameter optimization in linkage design; leverage language models to organize and reason about designs via symbolic abstractions to guide iterative refinement.
AI.method: A modular architecture combining LM agents for discrete topology exploration and numerical optimizers for continuous parameters; a symbolic lifting operator converts simulator trajectories into qualitative descriptors, motion labels, predicates, and structural diagnostics; iterative design cycles across six targets; evaluated on three open-source models (Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 MoE 30B-A3B).
AI.result: Geometric error reduced by up to 68%; structural validity improved by up to 134% versus monolithic baselines; 78.6% of refinement trajectories show measurable improvement; correct diagnosis of overconstraint (56.3%) and underconstraint (35.6%); grounding corrections proposed; interpretable mechanical reasoning acquired without fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic abstraction bridges generative AI and engineering-level numerical precision; modular, LM-assisted design improves performance and interpretability across model families.

title: 3D-ReGen: A Unified 3D Geometry Regeneration Framework
summary: We consider the problem of regenerating 3D objects from 2D images and initial 3D shapes. Most 3D generators operate in a one-shot fashion, converting text or images to a 3D object with limited controllability. We introduce instead 3D-ReGen, a 3D regenerator that is conditioned on an initial 3D shape. This conceptually simple formulation allows us to support numerous useful tasks, including 3D enhancement, reconstruction, and editing. 3D-ReGen uses a new conditioning mechanism based on VecSet, which allows the regenerator to update or improve the input geometry with consistent fine-grained details. 3D-ReGen learns a widely applicable regeneration prior from off-the-shelf 3D datasets via self-supervised pretext tasks and augmentations, without additional annotations. We evaluate both the geometric consistency and fine-grained quality of 3D-ReGen, achieving state-of-the-art performance in controllable 3D generation across several tasks.

AI.tldr: A 3D regenerator (3D-ReGen) conditioned on an initial 3D shape using VecSet enables regeneration with fine-grained details, trained via self-supervised pretext tasks on existing 3D data, achieving state-of-the-art controllable 3D generation across enhancement, reconstruction, and editing.
AI.motivation: Limitations of one-shot 3D generators and lack of controllable regeneration; need for a method that starts from an initial geometry and refines it.
AI.method: Propose 3D-ReGen: a regenerator conditioned on an initial 3D shape; introduce VecSet conditioning to update/input geometry; pretrain with self-supervised pretext tasks and augmentations on off-the-shelf 3D datasets; evaluate geometric consistency and fine-grained quality.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in controllable 3D generation across tasks; demonstrates improvements in geometric consistency and fine-grained detail; enables 3D enhancement, reconstruction, editing.
AI.conclusion: The regenerator paradigm with VecSet and self-supervised pretraining provides a versatile, controllable 3D generation tool that can be applied to multiple tasks, offering a broadly applicable regeneration prior.

title: A Unified Framework of Hyperbolic Graph Representation Learning Methods
summary: Hyperbolic geometry has emerged as an effective latent space for representing complex networks, owing to its ability to capture hierarchical organization and heterogeneous connectivity patterns using low-dimensional embeddings. As a result, numerous hyperbolic graph representation learning methods have been proposed in recent years. However, their practical adoption and systematic comparison remain challenging, as implementations are fragmented and shared tools for reproducible and fair evaluation are lacking. In this work, we introduce a unified open-source framework for hyperbolic graph representation learning that integrates several widely used embedding methods under a common optimization interface. The novel framework enables consistent training, visualization, and evaluation of hyperbolic embeddings, and interfaces seamlessly with standard network analysis tools. Leveraging this unified setup, we conduct an experimental study of hyperbolic embedding methods on real-world networks, focusing on two canonical downstream tasks: link prediction and node classification. Beyond predictive accuracy, the study offers practical insights into the strengths and limitations of existing approaches, thereby facilitating informed method selection and fostering reproducible research in hyperbolic graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: A unified, open-source framework for hyperbolic graph representation learning that standardizes training, visualization, and evaluation across multiple embedding methods, enabling reproducible comparisons on link prediction and node classification.
AI.motivation: Hyperbolic embeddings capture hierarchy and heterogeneity in complex networks, but fragmented implementations hinder reproducibility and fair benchmarking.
AI.method: Integrate widely used hyperbolic embedding methods under a single optimization interface within an open-source framework; provide training, visualization, evaluation tools, and compatibility with standard network analysis workflows.
AI.result: Empirical study on real-world networks assessing link prediction and node classification; yields comparative insights into strengths/limitations of existing methods and demonstrates the framework’s utility for reproducible research and method selection.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework facilitates fair, reproducible evaluation of hyperbolic graph representations and supports practical adoption by researchers and practitioners.

title: Splitting Assumption-Based Argumentation Frameworks
summary: Assumption-Based Argumentation (ABA) is a well-established formalism for modelling and reasoning over debates, with a wide range of applications. However, the high computational complexity of core reasoning tasks in ABA poses a significant challenge for its applicability. This issue is further aggravated when ABA frameworks (ABAFs) are instantiated into graph-based argumentation formalisms, such as Dung's Argumentation Frameworks (AFs) and Argumentation Frameworks with Collective Attacks (SETAFs). In knowledge representation and reasoning, a key strategy to address computational intractability is to optimise reasoning over a given knowledge base through divide-and-conquer algorithms. A paradigmatic example of this approach is splitting, where extensions of a given framework are computed incrementally, by restricting the search space to sub-frameworks only, and then combining the obtained results. This approach has been successfully applied to AFs, for which also a parametrised version has been introduced under stable semantics. However, the exponential growth produced by the instantiation might undermine the usefulness of splitting on the argument graphs induced by ABAFs. To address this issue, our work investigates the concept of splitting on the knowledge base rather than on its graph-based instantiation. Furthermore, we generalise splitting to its parametrised version for ABAFs.

AI.tldr: Proposes knowledge-base splitting for ABA-based frameworks and generalises the approach to a parametrised form to address complexity without relying on graph-instantiation, extending prior AF splitting to ABAFs.
AI.motivation: ABA-based reasoning is computationally hard; graph-based instantiations (AFs/SETAFs) can explode in size. Splitting has been effective for AFs, but applying it to ABAFs via graph instantiation risks exponential blow-up. The work shifts the splitting paradigm from the argument graph to the knowledge base and extends it to a parametrised version for ABAFs.
AI.method: Formal investigation of splitting on the knowledge base (not the graph) for ABAFs and development of a parametrised splitting framework. This includes theoretical definitions and analysis to enable incremental computation and combination of results.
AI.result: Introduction of a knowledge-base splitting approach for ABAFs and its parametrised generalisation. The abstract presents the conceptual framework and motivation, but does not report empirical results; it claims potential benefits for tractability.
AI.conclusion: Splitting on the knowledge base, with a parametrised extension for ABAFs, is proposed as a viable strategy to mitigate the complexity of ABA reasoning and to enhance the practicality of graph-based ABA formalisms.

title: Beyond Pixel Fidelity: Minimizing Perceptual Distortion and Color Bias in Night Photography Rendering
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, Accepted to 2026 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing
summary: Night Photography Rendering (NPR) poses a significant challenge due to the extreme contrast between dark and illuminated areas in scenes, stemming from concurrent capture of severely dark regions alongside intense point light sources. Existing methods, which are mainly tailored for fidelity metrics, reveal considerable perceptual gaps and often detract from visual quality. We introduce pHVI-ISPNet, a novel RAW-to-RGB framework built on the robust HVI color space. Our network integrates four distinct key refinements: RAW-domain feature processing and Wavelet-based feature propagation to mitigate high-frequency detail loss; sample-based dynamic loss coefficients to ensure stable learning across varying exposure levels; and loss term based on feature distributions to maintain rigorous color constancy. Evaluations on the dataset introduced in the NTIRE 2025 challenge on NPR confirm our approach achieves competitive fidelity while establishing new state-of-the-art results in both CIE2000 color difference and LPIPS. This validates our perceptually-driven design for high-quality nighttime imaging.

AI.tldr: Proposes pHVI-ISPNet, a RAW-to-RGB NPR framework in HVI color space with four refinements to boost perceptual quality: RAW-domain feature processing, Wavelet-based feature propagation, sample-based dynamic loss coefficients, and a feature-distribution loss for color constancy; achieves competitive fidelity and state-of-the-art CIE2000 and LPIPS on NTIRE 2025 NPR dataset.
AI.motivation: Night photography exhibits extreme luminance contrasts and perceptual gaps; existing fidelity-centric methods often degrade perceived quality. A perceptually-driven, color-accurate RAW-to-RGB approach in a robust color space is needed.
AI.method: A RAW-to-RGB network, pHVI-ISPNet, operating in the HVI color space. Four refinements are integrated: (1) RAW-domain feature processing; (2) Wavelet-based feature propagation to preserve high-frequency details; (3) sample-based dynamic loss coefficients across varying exposures; (4) a loss term based on feature distributions to enforce color constancy. Trained and evaluated on the NTIRE 2025 NPR dataset.
AI.result: Competitive fidelity metrics with new state-of-the-art performance in CIE2000 color difference and LPIPS on NTIRE 2025 NPR; demonstrates perceptual gains without sacrificing fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Perceptually driven design in the HVI color space with the four refinements yields high-quality nighttime imaging, aligning perceptual quality with traditional fidelity metrics and achieving superior color-constancy and perceptual similarity.

title: FiLMMeD: Feature-wise Linear Modulation for Cross-Problem Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing
summary: Solving practical multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRP) is a challenging optimization task central to modern logistics, increasingly driven by e-commerce. To address the MDVRP's computational complexity, neural-based combinatorial optimization methods offer a promising scalable alternative to traditional approaches. However, neural-based methods typically rely on rigid architectures and input encodings tailored to specific problem formulations. In real-world settings, heterogeneous constraints create multiple MDVRP variants, limiting the applicability of such models. While multi-task learning (MTL) has begun to accelerate the development of unified neural-based solvers, prior works focus almost exclusively on single-depot VRPs, leaving the MDVRP unaddressed. To bridge this gap, we propose Feature-wise Linear Modulation for Cross-Problem Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing (FiLMMeD), a novel unified neural-based model for 24 different MDVRP variants. We introduce three main contributions: (1) to improve the model's generalization, we augment the standard Transformer encoder with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM), which dynamically conditions learned internal representations based on the active set of constraints; (2) we provide an initial demonstration of Preference Optimization in the MTL setting, establishing it as a superior alternative to Reinforcement Learning for future MTL works; (3) to mitigate the generalization gap caused by the introduction of multi-depot constraints, we introduce a targeted curriculum learning strategy that progressively exposes the model to increasingly more complex constraint interactions. Extensive experiments on 24 MDVRP variants (including 8 novel formulations) and 16 single-depot VRPs confirm the effectiveness of FiLMMeD, which consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Our code is available at: https://github.com/AJ-Correa/FiLMMeD/tree/main

AI.tldr: Proposes FiLMMeD, a unified Transformer-based solver with FiLM conditioning for 24 MDVRP variants; leverages Multi-Task Learning with Preference Optimization and curriculum learning to generalize across constraints, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: MDVRP are computationally hard and diverse; existing neural solvers are often tailored to single formulations, hindering real-world applicability; a unified cross-problem approach is needed.
AI.method: Augment Transformer encoder with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to condition representations on active constraints; adopt Preference Optimization for MTL; employ curriculum learning to gradually increase constraint complexity; evaluate on 24 MDVRP variants (8 novel) and 16 single-depot VRPs; provide public code.
AI.result: FiLMMeD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all tested variants; demonstrates strong generalization and effectiveness for cross-problem MDVRP solving.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of unified neural solvers for MDVRP via FiLM conditioning, MTL with Preference Optimization, and curriculum learning; enables scalable deployment across diverse MDVRP formulations.

title: From LLM-Driven Trading Card Generation to Procedural Relatedness: A Pokémon Case Study
summary:
Since the dawn of Trading Card Games, the genre has grown into a multi-billion-dollar industry engaging millions of analog and digital players worldwide. Popular TCGs rely on regular updates, balance adjustments, and rotating constraints to sustain engagement. Yet, as metagames stabilize, predictable strategies dominate and viable card options diminish, often resulting in repetitive and impaired player experiences.
  This paper investigates the use of Large Language Models and Image Diffusion Models for Procedural Content Generation of TCG cards, addressing these challenges by enabling a personalized infinity of card designs. Modern generative AI not only enables large-scale content creation but could even introduce procedural relatedness, fostering unique connections between players and their cards. We present a pipeline combining player-centric co-creation, fine-tuned embeddings, local LLMs, and Diffusion Models to generate dynamic, personalized cards while potentially expanding creative range.
  We evaluated the pipeline in a user study with 49 participants who generated 196 Pokémon card samples. Participants rated aesthetics and representativeness of visuals and mechanics, and provided qualitative feedback. Results show high satisfaction and indicate that most participants successfully realized their own ideas through prompt adjustments. These findings lay groundwork for future content generation systems and alternatives to conventional metagame evolution through procedural relatedness.

AI.tldr: A multi-component pipeline using local LLMs, fine-tuned embeddings, and diffusion models enables personalized procedural content generation of TCG cards; a user study with 49 participants creating 196 Pokémon cards shows high aesthetic/representativeness satisfaction and feasible idea realization.
AI.motivation: Mitigate metagame stagnation in trading card games by expanding creative options and enabling procedural relatedness between players and cards; address repetitive strategies and shrinking viable card options.
AI.method: Develop pipeline combining player-centric co-creation, fine-tuned embeddings, local LLMs, and diffusion models to generate dynamic, personalized cards. Encourage iterative prompts and co-creation. Evaluate via a user study with 49 participants who generated 196 Pokémon card samples, rating aesthetics and representativeness of visuals and mechanics, plus qualitative feedback.
AI.result: Participants reported high satisfaction. Most could realize their own ideas through prompt adjustments, indicating feasibility of the approach and potential for procedural relatedness to enhance engagement.
AI.conclusion: The work lays groundwork for future content-generation systems in TCGs and presents a viable alternative to conventional metagame evolution through procedural relatedness; further work could explore scalability, robustness, and applicability to other IPs.

title: Continuous-tone Simple Points: An $\ell_0$-Norm of Cyclic Gradient for Topology-Preserving Data-Driven Image Segmentation
summary: Topological features play an essential role in ensuring geometric plausibility and structural consistency in image analysis tasks such as segmentation and skeletonization. However, integrating topology-preserving learning based on simple points into deep learning tasks remains challenging, as existing simple point detection methods are confined to binary images and are non-differentiable, rendering them incompatible with gradient-based optimization in modern deep learning. Moreover, morphological and purely data-driven approaches often fail to guaranty topological consistency. To address these limitations, we propose a novel method that directly computes simple points on continuous-valued images, enabling differentiable topological inference. Building on this theory, we develop an efficient skeleton extraction algorithm that preserves topological structures in binary and continuous-valued images. Furthermore, we design a variational model that enforces topological constraints by preserving topologically non-removable (i.e., non-simple) points, which can be seamlessly integrated into any deep neural network segmentation with softmax or sigmoid outputs. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively improves topological integrity and structural accuracy across multiple benchmarks. The codes are available in https://github.com/levnsio/CSP.

AI.tldr: A differentiable topology-preserving framework for simple points on continuous-valued images, enabling skeletonization and topology-aware segmentation within deep networks; demonstrates improved topology integrity on benchmarks; code available.
AI.motivation: Topological correctness is critical for segmentation and skeletonization; existing simple-point detectors are non-differentiable and limited to binary images, hindering integration with gradient-based learning; there is a need for differentiable methods to guarantee topological consistency.
AI.method: 1) Compute simple points on continuous-valued images to enable differentiable topological inference. 2) Develop an efficient skeleton extraction algorithm that preserves topology for binary and continuous-valued images. 3) Propose a variational model that preserves non-removable (non-simple) points to enforce topology constraints, compatible with neural networks using softmax or sigmoid outputs; can be integrated into deep segmentation models.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation across multiple benchmarks shows improved topological integrity and structural accuracy. Code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables differentiable topology-preserving processing for segmentation and skeletonization, facilitating more reliable topology-conscious deep learning pipelines.

title: Neural Aided Kalman Filtering for UAV State Estimation in Degraded Sensing Environments
summary: Accurate state estimation of nonlinear dynamical systems is fundamental to modern aerospace operations across air, sea, and space domains. Online tracking of adversarial unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is especially challenging due to agile nonlinear motion, noisy and sparse sensor measurements, and unknown control inputs; conditions that violate key assumptions of classical Kalman filter variants and degrade estimation performance. Neural networks (NNs) can learn complex nonlinear relationships from data, but lack principled uncertainty quantification, which is critical for state estimation tasks where confidence bounds drive downstream decisions. We address this with Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs), which model uncertainty through distributions over network weights and produce predictive means and uncertainties via Monte Carlo sampling. Building on this, we propose the Bayesian Neural Kalman Filter (BNKF): a hybrid framework coupling a trained BNN with a Kalman correction step for robust online UAV state estimation. Unlike related neural Kalman approaches, BNKF produces full state predictions and incorporates Bayesian uncertainty directly into covariance propagation, improving robustness under high noise conditions. We evaluate BNKF under varying radar noise levels and sampling rates using synthetic nonlinear UAV flight data. Five fold cross validation demonstrates that BNKF outperforms Extended and Unscented Kalman Filters in accuracy, precision, and truth containment under degraded sensing. An ensemble variant (BNKFe) further improves precision in high-noise edge cases at a slight accuracy tradeoff. Runtime analysis confirms minimal inference overhead, supporting real-time deployment feasibility.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian Neural Kalman Filter (BNKF) integrates a Bayesian Neural Network with a Kalman correction step for robust online UAV state estimation, improving accuracy and uncertainty handling under high noise; an ensemble BNKFe variant offers further precision at slight accuracy tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Accurate nonlinear state estimation for adversarial UAVs under noisy, sparse measurements and unknown inputs; classical Kalman filters assume linearity and Gaussian noise and lack uncertainty quantification, which impedes reliable decision-making.
AI.method: Train a Bayesian Neural Network to learn nonlinear dynamics and propagate predictive uncertainty; couple the BNN with a Kalman filter correction step so that Bayesian uncertainty informs covariance propagation; evaluate with synthetic nonlinear UAV flight data under varying radar noise and sampling rates; include an ensemble BNKFe variant for edge-case high-noise scenarios.
AI.result: BNKF outperforms Extended and Unscented Kalman Filters in accuracy, precision, and truth containment under degraded sensing; BNKFe enhances precision in high-noise edge cases with minor accuracy tradeoff; five-fold cross-validation on synthetic data confirms robustness; runtime overhead is minimal, enabling real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: The BNKF framework provides principled uncertainty-aware online state estimation for nonlinear UAV dynamics, improving robustness under noise and sparse measurements; the ensemble variant offers additional gains in challenging conditions; suitable for real-time aerospace sensing and potentially extendable to other nonlinear tracking tasks.

title: D3-Gym: Constructing Real-World Verifiable Environments for Data-Driven Discovery
summary: Despite recent progress in language models and agents for scientific data-driven discovery, further advancing their capabilities is held back by the absence of verifiable environments representing real-world scientific tasks.To fill this gap, we introduce D3-Gym, the first automatically constructed dataset with verifiable environments for scientific Data-Driven Discovery. D3-Gym comprises (1) 565 tasks sourced from 239 real scientific repositories across four disciplines where (2) each task is equipped with a natural language instruction, an executable environment with pre-installed dependencies, input dataset and artifact previews, a reference code solution, and an automatically synthesized evaluation script. Rigorous evaluation of the quality of the verification signal in D3-Gym confirms that our evaluation scripts achieve 87.5% agreement with human-annotated gold standards and strong alignment in domain-specific evaluation logic, showing their scientific soundness. Further, training on trajectories sampled from D3-Gym yields consistent and substantial gains across Qwen3 models of varying sizes on ScienceAgentBench, boosting Qwen3-32B by 7.8 absolute points and substantially shrinking the gap with strong proprietary models. All D3-Gym artifacts (environments, creation workflow, trajectories, and models) can be found at https://github.com/OSU-NLP-Group/D3-Gym.

AI.tldr: D3-Gym is a automatically constructed, verifiable dataset of 565 scientific tasks across four disciplines, each with NL instruction, executable environment, data/artifacts, reference solution, and evaluation script; its verification signal is robust (87.5% agreement with gold standards); training on D3-Gym trajectories improves Qwen3 models on ScienceAgentBench, notably +7.8 points for Qwen3-32B; code and artifacts available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Fill the lack of verifiable, real-world scientific task environments to benchmark data-driven discovery systems and agents, enabling reproducible evaluation and broader progress.
AI.method: Assemble D3-Gym with 565 tasks from 239 repositories; for each task provide NL instruction, executable environment with dependencies, input dataset/artifact previews, a reference solution, and an automatically synthesized evaluation script; validate evaluation scripts against human gold standards; train Qwen3 models on trajectories from D3-Gym and assess transfer on ScienceAgentBench.
AI.result: Quality assessment shows evaluation scripts achieve 87.5% agreement with gold standards and align with domain-specific evaluation logic; model training on D3-Gym trajectories yields consistent, substantial gains for Qwen3 models of various sizes on ScienceAgentBench, e.g., Qwen3-32B gains 7.8 absolute points and narrows the gap to strong proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: D3-Gym provides a verifiable, scalable benchmark suite for scientific data-driven discovery, enabling reproducible evaluation and demonstrated performance gains; artifacts and workflows are openly available at the project repository.

title: PhyCo: Learning Controllable Physical Priors for Generative Motion
summary: Modern video diffusion models excel at appearance synthesis but still struggle with physical consistency: objects drift, collisions lack realistic rebound, and material responses seldom match their underlying properties. We present PhyCo, a framework that introduces continuous, interpretable, and physically grounded control into video generation. Our approach integrates three key components: (i) a large-scale dataset of over 100K photorealistic simulation videos where friction, restitution, deformation, and force are systematically varied across diverse scenarios; (ii) physics-supervised fine-tuning of a pretrained diffusion model using a ControlNet conditioned on pixel-aligned physical property maps; and (iii) VLM-guided reward optimization, where a fine-tuned vision-language model evaluates generated videos with targeted physics queries and provides differentiable feedback. This combination enables a generative model to produce physically consistent and controllable outputs through variations in physical attributes-without any simulator or geometry reconstruction at inference. On the Physics-IQ benchmark, PhyCo significantly improves physical realism over strong baselines, and human studies confirm clearer and more faithful control over physical attributes. Our results demonstrate a scalable path toward physically consistent, controllable generative video models that generalize beyond synthetic training environments.

AI.tldr: PhyCo introduces physics-informed controllable video generation by (1) building a large physics-varied simulation video dataset, (2) physics-supervised fine-tuning of a diffusion model via ControlNet conditioned on pixel-aligned property maps, and (3) VLM-guided differentiable feedback to optimize physics realism, enabling physically consistent outputs without runtime simulators.
AI.motivation: Current video diffusion models struggle with physical realism and controllability; objects drift, collisions lack realistic responses, and material properties are seldom reflected in generated videos. A scalable, end-to-end approach is needed to enforce physical consistency and allow user control over physical attributes without expensive per-inference simulation.
AI.method: Three-pronged approach: (i) assemble a dataset of 100k+ photorealistic simulation videos with varying friction, restitution, deformation, and forces; (ii) physics-supervised fine-tuning of a pretrained diffusion model using a ControlNet conditioned on pixel-aligned physical property maps; (iii) VLM-guided reward optimization where a fine-tuned vision-language model evaluates generated videos on physics queries and provides differentiable feedback to steer generation.
AI.result: On Physics-IQ benchmark PhyCo significantly improves physical realism versus strong baselines; human studies show clearer, more faithful control of physical attributes. Demonstrates scalable path to physically consistent, controllable generative video models that generalize beyond synthetic training environments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating physics-aware data, supervised diffusion fine-tuning with property-conditioned control, and VLM-based feedback yields physically consistent, controllable video generation without on-the-fly simulation or geometry reconstruction, with demonstrated gains and promising generalization.

title: Auto-FlexSwitch: Efficient Dynamic Model Merging via Learnable Task Vector Compression
summary: Model merging has attracted attention as an effective path toward multi-task adaptation by integrating knowledge from multiple task-specific models. Among existing approaches, dynamic merging mitigates performance degradation caused by conflicting parameter updates across tasks by flexibly combining task-specific parameters at inference time, thereby maintaining high performance. However, these methods require storing independent parameters for each task, resulting in prohibitive storage overhead. To address this issue, we first experimentally demonstrate that the fine-tuned weight increments (referred to as task vectors) exhibit an impulse-like activation pattern and high robustness to low-bit representations. Driven by this insight, we propose T-Switch, which decomposes task vectors into three compact components: a binary sparse mask, a sign vector, and a scalar scaling factor, achieving high-fidelity approximation at high compression ratios. We then introduce Auto-Switch, a training-free merging scheme that automatically composes task vectors via feature similarity retrieval. Building on this, we develop Auto-Switch, a training-free merging scheme that automatically assembles task vectors through feature similarity retrieval. Furthermore, to transform task vector sparsification and quantization from static rules to adaptive learning, we propose FlexSwitch, a learnable framework which jointly optimizes the compression strategy for each model unit via Learnable Gating Sparsification (LGS) and Bit-width Adaptive Selection (BAS), while employing the Sparsity-Aware Storage Strategy (SASS) to select the optimal storage encoding structure. Finally, by incorporating a K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) inference scheme with a learnable low-rank metric, we present Auto-FlexSwitch, a dynamic model merging approach that supports highly efficient task vector compression.

AI.tldr: A cascade of compact, training-free dynamic model merging methods for multi-task adaptation, achieving high compression with minimal performance loss: T-Switch (binary mask, sign, scale), Auto-Switch (feature-similarity retrieval), FlexSwitch (LGS and BAS with SASS), and Auto-FlexSwitch (KNN with learnable metric).
AI.motivation: Dynamic merging improves multi-task performance but requires storing per-task parameters; need highly compact representations and adaptive compression.
AI.method: Decompose task vectors into three components (T-Switch); propose training-free Auto-Switch; introduce learnable FlexSwitch with LGS and BAS and storage encoding (SASS); extend to Auto-FlexSwitch with KNN inference and learnable low-rank metric.
AI.result: High-fidelity approximation at high compression; automatic assembly via similarity; per-unit adaptive compression; efficient storage; dynamic merging with KNN inference.
AI.conclusion: Offers scalable, storage-efficient dynamic model merging enabling robust multi-task adaptation with minimal performance loss.

title: Exploring Interaction Paradigms for LLM Agents in Scientific Visualization
summary: This paper examines how different types of large language model (LLM) agents perform on scientific visualization (SciVis) tasks, where users generate visualization workflows from natural-language instructions. We compare three primary interaction paradigms, including domain-specific agents with structured tool use, computer-use agents, and general-purpose coding agents, by evaluating eight representative agents across 15 benchmark tasks and measuring visualization quality, efficiency, robustness, and computational cost. We further analyze interaction modalities, including code scripts and model context protocol (MCP) or API calls for structured tool use, as well as command-line interfaces (CLI) and graphical user interfaces (GUI) for more general interaction, while additionally studying the effect of persistent memory in selected agents. The results reveal clear tradeoffs across paradigms and modalities. General-purpose coding agents achieve the highest task success rates but are computationally expensive, while domain-specific agents are more efficient and stable but less flexible. Computer-use agents perform well on individual steps but struggle with longer multi-step workflows, indicating that long-horizon planning is their primary limitation. Across both CLI- and GUI-based settings, persistent memory improves performance over repeated trials, although its benefits depend on the underlying interaction mode and the quality of feedback. These findings suggest that no single approach is sufficient, and future SciVis systems should combine structured tool use, interactive capabilities, and adaptive memory mechanisms to balance performance, robustness, and flexibility.

AI.tldr: Evaluates LLM agents for SciVis across three interaction paradigms; finds tradeoffs; hybrid systems with memory are recommended.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLM agent design choices affect SciVis workflow performance and identify complementary strengths/weaknesses.
AI.method: Benchmark study with eight agents, 15 tasks, measuring visualization quality, efficiency, robustness, and cost; analyze interaction modalities (code scripts, MCP/API calls, CLI/GUI) and memory effects.
AI.result: Coding agents highest success but high cost; domain-specific agents efficient/stable but less flexible; computer-use agents strong on single steps but poor long-horizon; persistent memory improves performance; benefits depend on mode and feedback.
AI.conclusion: No single approach suffices; future SciVis systems should integrate structured tool use, interactive capabilities, and adaptive memory to balance performance, robustness, and flexibility.

title: Do Sparse Autoencoders Capture Concept Manifolds?
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used to extract interpretable features from neural network representations, often under the implicit assumption that concepts correspond to independent linear directions. However, a growing body of evidence suggests that many concepts are instead organized along low-dimensional manifolds encoding continuous geometric relationships. This raises three basic questions: what does it mean for an SAE to capture a manifold, when do existing SAE architectures do so, and how? We develop a theoretical framework that answers these questions and show that SAEs can capture manifolds in two fundamentally different ways: globally, by allocating a compact group of atoms whose linear span contains the entire manifold, or locally, by distributing it across features that each selectively tile a restricted region of the underlying geometry. Empirically, we find that SAEs suboptimally recover continuous structures, mixing the global subspace and local tiling solutions in a fragmented regime we call dilution. This explains why manifold structure is rarely visible at the level of individual concepts and motivates post-hoc unsupervised discovery methods that search for coherent groups of atoms rather than isolated directions. More broadly, our results suggest that future representation learning methods should treat geometric objects, not just individual directions, as the basic units of interpretability.

AI.tldr: SAEs capture data manifolds either globally via a compact group of atoms spanning the manifold, or locally by tiling the geometry across features; empirically, manifold structure often appears diluted across atoms, making single directions poor indicators; advocates group-based interpretability and geometry-aware representations.
AI.motivation: Concepts in neural representations often lie on low-dimensional manifolds with continuous geometric relationships, not just independent directions; current SAE assumptions fail to capture this geometry, prompting a formal framework to understand if, when, and how SAEs encode manifolds.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework identifying two canonical modes for manifolds in SAEs (global subspace covering the manifold; local tiling across features); conduct empirical analyses showing a dilution regime where both modes mix; propose post-hoc unsupervised discovery methods to search for coherent groups of atoms rather than isolated directions; argue for geometry-aware interpretability units.
AI.result: SAEs can realize manifolds in two distinct ways (global subspaces and local tiling). In practice, these modes mix (dilution), making manifold structure rarely visible at the level of individual concepts. The findings motivate group-based, post-hoc discovery of coherent atom groups and suggest treating geometric objects as interpretability units.
AI.conclusion: Future representation learning should treat geometric objects, not just independent directions, as the basic units of interpretability and develop methods to identify coherent atom groups that encode underlying geometry.

title: A Pattern Language for Resilient Visual Agents
comment: Accepted to the 23rd International Conference on Software Architecture (ICSA 2026), New and Emerging Ideas Track. 5 pages, 1 figure
summary: Integrating multimodal foundation models into enterprise ecosystems presents a fundamental software architecture challenge. Architects must balance competing quality attributes: the high latency and non-determinism of vision language action (VLA) models versus the strict determinism and real-time performance required by enterprise control loops. In this study, we propose an architectural pattern language for visual agents that separates fast, deterministic reflexes from slow, probabilistic supervision. It consists of four architectural design patterns: (1) Hybrid Affordance Integration, (2) Adaptive Visual Anchoring, (3) Visual Hierarchy Synthesis, and (4) Semantic Scene Graph.

AI.tldr: A pattern language to architect visual agents in enterprise ecosystems, separating fast deterministic reflexes from slow probabilistic supervision via four design patterns.
AI.motivation: To address the integration challenge of multimodal foundation models in enterprise settings, balancing the latency/non-determinism of VLA with the determinism and real-time constraints of control loops.
AI.method: Proposes a pattern language consisting of four architectural design patterns: (1) Hybrid Affordance Integration, (2) Adaptive Visual Anchoring, (3) Visual Hierarchy Synthesis, (4) Semantic Scene Graph; outlines how to compose these patterns to separate fast reflexes from slow supervision.
AI.result: A conceptual architectural framework and four concrete design patterns for visual agents; enables decoupling of fast deterministic behavior from slower probabilistic supervision in enterprise contexts; no empirical evaluation reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The pattern language provides a structured approach to integrating VLA into enterprise ecosystems by partitioning fast reflexes and slow supervision, guided by the four patterns.

title: AEGIS: A Holistic Benchmark for Evaluating Forensic Analysis of AI-Generated Academic Images
summary: We introduce AEGIS, A holistic benchmark for Evaluating forensic analysis of AI-Generated academic ImageS. Compared to existing benchmarks, AEGIS features three key advances: (1) Domain-Specific Complexity: covering seven academic categories with 39 fine-grained subtypes, exposing intrinsic forensic difficulty, where even GPT-5.1 reaches 48.80% overall performance and expert models achieve only limited localization accuracy (IoU 30.09%); (2) Diverse Forgery Simulations: modeling four prevalent academic forgery strategies across 25 generative models, with 11 yielding average forensic accuracy below 50%, showing that forensics lag behind generative advances; and (3) Multi-Dimensional Forensic Evaluation: jointly assessing detection, reasoning, and localization, revealing complementary strengths between model families, with multimodal large language models (MLLMs) at 84.74% accuracy in textual artifact recognition and expert detectors peaking at 79.54% accuracy in binary authenticity detection. By evaluating 25 leading MLLMs, nine expert models, and one unified multimodal understanding and generation model, AEGIS serves as a diagnostic testbed exposing fundamental limitations in academic image forensics.

AI.tldr: AEGIS is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating forensic analysis of AI-generated academic images, revealing domain-specific difficulty, diverse forgery simulations, and multi-dimensional evaluation; current models lag behind generative advances, underscoring limitations in academic image forensics.
AI.motivation: As AI-generated content proliferates in academia, there is a need for systematic benchmarks that capture domain complexity, forgery diversity, and multi-faceted forensic tasks (detection, reasoning, localization).
AI.method: Constructed AEGIS covering seven academic categories and 39 subtypes; modeled four forgery strategies across 25 generative models; evaluated 25 leading MLLMs, nine expert detectors, and one unified model; metrics across detection, reasoning, localization; textual artifact recognition and binary authenticity.
AI.result: GPT-5.1 attains 48.80% overall performance and experts have IoU 30.09%; 11 of 25 forgery models yield average forensic accuracy below 50%; MLLMs achieve 84.74% in textual artifact recognition; expert detectors peak at 79.54% in binary authenticity.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS serves as a diagnostic testbed that reveals fundamental limitations in academic image forensics and highlights complementary strengths across model families, guiding future research toward improving robustness and localization.

title: Global Optimality for Constrained Exploration via Penalty Regularization
summary: Efficient exploration is a central problem in reinforcement learning and is often formalized as maximizing the entropy of the state-action occupancy measure. While unconstrained maximum-entropy exploration is relatively well understood, real-world exploration is often constrained by safety, resource, or imitation requirements. This constrained setting is particularly challenging because entropy maximization lacks additive structure, rendering Bellman-equation-based methods inapplicable. Moreover, scalable approaches require policy parameterization, inducing non-convexity in both the objective and the constraints. To our knowledge, the only prior model-free policy-gradient approach for this setting under general policy parameterization is due to Ying et al. (2025). Unfortunately, their guarantees are limited to weak regret and ergodic averages, which do not imply that the final output is a single deployable policy that is near-optimal and nearly feasible. In this work we take a different approach to this problem, and propose Policy Gradient Penalty (PGP) method, a single-loop policy-space method that enforces general convex occupancy-measure constraints via quadratic-penalty regularization. PGP constructs pseudo-rewards that yield gradient estimates of the penalized objective, subsequently exploiting the classical Policy Gradient Theorem. We further establish the regularity of the penalized objective, providing the smoothness properties needed to justify the convergence of PGP. Leveraging hidden convexity and strong duality, we then establish global last-iterate convergence guarantees, attaining an $ε$-optimal constrained entropy value with $ε$ bounded constraint violation despite policy-induced non-convexity. We validate PGP through ablations on a grid-world benchmark and further demonstrate scalability on two challenging continuous-control tasks.

AI.tldr: Policy Gradient Penalty (PGP) introduces a single-loop policy-gradient method that enforces convex occupancy-measure constraints via a quadratic penalty, achieving last-iterate convergence to a near-optimal, near-feasible constrained-entropy policy and scaling to continuous control.
AI.motivation: Constrained entropy maximization for exploration is practically relevant but difficult with general policy parameterizations; existing methods offer weak guarantees and do not ensure a deployable policy; a single-loop, provably convergent approach under convex occupancy constraints is needed.
AI.method: Formulate a penalized objective with a quadratic constraint penalty on occupancy measures. Generate pseudo-rewards that provide gradient estimates of the penalized objective, then apply the Policy Gradient Theorem within a single-loop optimization. Establish smoothness of the penalized objective, leverage hidden convexity and strong duality to prove global last-iterate convergence, achieving ε-optimal constrained entropy with ε-bounded constraint violation. Validate via grid-world ablations and two continuous-control tasks.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows PGP performs well on a grid-world benchmark and scales to challenging continuous-control tasks, supporting the theoretical guarantees with practical performance.
AI.conclusion: PGP provides a principled, scalable, single-loop policy-gradient method that achieves near-optimal constrained entropy with bounded violation, backed by global last-iterate convergence guarantees under convex occupancy constraints and non-convex policy parameterization.

title: SpecVQA: A Benchmark for Spectral Understanding and Visual Question Answering in Scientific Images
summary: Spectra are a prevalent yet highly information-dense form of scientific imagery, presenting substantial challenges to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) due to their unstructured and domain-specific characteristics. Here we introduce SpecVQA, a professional scientific-image benchmark for evaluating multimodal models on scientific spectral understanding, covering 7 representative spectrum types with expert-annotated question-answer pairs. The aim comprises two aspects: spectra scientific QA evaluation and corresponding underlying task evaluation. SpecVQA contains 620 figures and 3100 QA pairs curated from peer-reviewed literature, targeting both direct information extraction and domain-specific reasoning. To effectively reduce token length while preserving essential curve characteristics, we propose a spectral data sampling and interpolation reconstruction approach. Ablation studies further confirm that the approach achieves substantial performance improvements on the proposed benchmark. We test the capability of prominent MLLMs in scientific spectral understanding on our benchmark and present a leaderboard. This work represents an essential step toward enhancing spectral understanding in multimodal large models and suggests promising directions for extending visual-language models to broader scientific research and data analysis.

AI.tldr: SpecVQA introduces a specialized scientific-image benchmark for spectral understanding, covering 7 spectrum types with 620 figures and 3100 expert-annotated QA pairs. It also proposes a spectral data sampling and interpolation reconstruction technique to reduce token length while preserving curve characteristics. Ablation studies demonstrate performance gains, and a leaderboard evaluates prominent MLLMs on this task. The work aims to advance spectral understanding in multimodal LLMs and guide extending visual-language models to scientific data analysis.
AI.motivation: Spectral plots are information-dense, unstructured, and domain-specific, making it difficult for multimodal LLMs to extract precise information and perform reasoning. A dedicated benchmark and effective data representations are needed to evaluate and improve spectral understanding.
AI.method: Create SpecVQA from peer-reviewed literature, with expert-annotated QA pairs across 7 spectrum types (620 figures, 3100 QA pairs). Introduce a spectral data sampling and interpolation reconstruction approach to compress spectral curves while preserving key characteristics. Conduct ablation studies to validate the sampling method. Evaluate leading MLLMs on the benchmark and publish a leaderboard.
AI.result: The sampling/interpolation approach yields substantial performance gains on SpecVQA. Benchmark results across evaluated MLLMs establish a leaderboard and demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed data representation for spectral curves.
AI.conclusion: SpecVQA marks a crucial step in enabling spectral understanding in multimodal LLMs, providing dataset, evaluation protocol, and insights that can extend visual-language models to broader scientific data analysis.

title: Stop Holding Your Breath: CT-Informed Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Bronchoscopy
summary: Bronchoscopic navigation relies on registering endoscopic video to a preoperative CT scan, but respiratory motion deforms the airway by 5-20 mm, creating CT-to-body divergence that limits localization accuracy. In practice, this is mitigated through breath-hold protocols, which attempt to match the intraoperative anatomy to a static CT, but are difficult to reproduce and disrupt clinical workflow. We propose to eliminate the need for breath-hold protocols by leveraging patient-specific respiratory modeling. Paired inhale-exhale CT scans, already acquired for planning, implicitly define the patient-specific deformation space of the breathing airway.
